definition: root of a semisimple Lie algebra: : Let $\mathfrak{g}$ be a semisimple Lie algebra, $\mathfrak{h}$ be a Cartan subalgebra of $\mathfrak{g}$. For $\alpha \in \mathfrak{h}^*$, let $\mathfrak{g_\alpha} := \{ x \in \mathfrak{g} | [h,x] = \alpha(h) x \, \forall h \in \mathfrak{h} \} $. $\alpha$ is called a root of $\mathfrak{g}$ if it is nonzero and $\mathfrak{g_\alpha} \ne \{0\} $ : The set of all roots is denoted by $\Phi$ ; it forms a root system.
source_glossary: Glossary of Lie groups and Lie algebras

definition: A function f on a Riemannian manifold is a convex if for any geodesic $\gamma$ the function $f\circ\gamma$ is convex. A function f is called $\lambda$-convex if for any geodesic $\gamma$ with natural parameter $t$, the function $f\circ\gamma(t)-\lambda t^2$ is convex.
source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry
definition_raw: A function ''f'' on a Riemannian manifold is a convex if for any geodesic <math>\gamma</math> the function <math>f\circ\gamma</math> is [[Convex function|convex]]. A function ''f'' is called <math>\lambda</math>-convex if for any geodesic <math>\gamma</math> with natural parameter <math>t</math>, the function <math>f\circ\gamma(t)-\lambda t^2</math> is [[Convex function|convex]].

definition: A subset K of a Riemannian manifold M is called convex if for any two points in K there is a unique shortest path connecting them which lies entirely in K, see also totally convex.
source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry
definition_raw: A subset ''K'' of a Riemannian manifold ''M'' is called convex if for any two points in ''K'' there is a unique ''shortest path'' connecting them which lies entirely in ''K,'' see also ''totally convex''.

definition: at a point $p$ of a Riemannian manifold is the supremum of radii of balls centered at $p$ that are (totally) convex. The convexity radius of the manifold is the infimum of the convexity radii at its points; for a compact manifold this is a positive number. Sometimes the additional requirement is made that the distance function to $p$ in these balls is convex.
source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

definition: Given a simply connected nilpotent Lie group N acting on itself by left multiplication and a finite group of automorphisms F of N one can define an action of the semidirect product $N \rtimes F$ on N. An orbit space of N by a discrete subgroup of $N \rtimes F$ which acts freely on N is called an infranilmanifold. An infranilmanifold is finitely covered by a nilmanifold.

definition: A Jacobi field is a vector field on a geodesic γ which can be obtained on the following way: Take a smooth one parameter family of geodesics $\gamma_\tau$ with $\gamma_0=\gamma$, then the Jacobi field is described by $J(t)=\left. \frac{\partial\gamma_\tau(t)}{\partial \tau} \right|_{\tau=0}.$

definition: has two meanings; here we give the most common. A map $f: I \to Y$ (where $ I\subseteq \mathbb R$ is a subinterval) is called a quasigeodesic if there are constants $K \ge 1$ and $C \ge 0$ such that for every $ x,y\in I$ ${1\over K}d(x,y)-C\le d(f(x),f(y))\le Kd(x,y)+C.$ Note that a quasigeodesic is not necessarily a continuous curve.
source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry
definition_raw: has two meanings; here we give the most common. A map <math>f: I \to Y</math> (where <math> I\subseteq \mathbb R</math> is a subinterval) is called a ''quasigeodesic'' if there are constants <math>K \ge 1</math> and <math>C \ge 0</math> such that for every <math> x,y\in I</math> <math>{1\over K}d(x,y)-C\le d(f(x),f(y))\le Kd(x,y)+C.</math> Note that a quasigeodesic is not necessarily a continuous curve.

definition: A map $f:X\to Y$ is called a quasi-isometry if there are constants $K \ge 1$ and $C \ge 0$ such that ${1\over K}d(x,y)-C\le d(f(x),f(y))\le Kd(x,y)+C.$ and every point in Y has distance at most C from some point of f(X). Note that a quasi-isometry is not assumed to be continuous. For example, any map between compact metric spaces is a quasi isometry. If there exists a quasi-isometry from X to Y, then X and Y are said to be quasi-isometric.
source_glossary: Glossary of Riemannian and metric geometry

definition: Riemannian symmetric spaces are Riemannian manifolds in which the geodesic reflection at any point is an isometry. They turn out to be quotients of a real Lie group by a maximal compact subgroup whose Lie algebra is the fixed subalgebra of the involution obtained by differentiating the geodesic symmetry. This algebraic data is enough to provide a classification of the Riemannian symmetric spaces.

definition: A normal variety is $\mathbb{Q}$-factorial if every $\mathbb{Q}$-Weil divisor is $\mathbb{Q}$-Cartier.
definition_raw: A normal variety is <math>\mathbb{Q}</math>-factorial if every <math>\mathbb{Q}</math>-Weil divisor is <math>\mathbb{Q}</math>-Cartier.

definition: The set of all prime ideals in a ring R with Zariski topology; it is called the prime spectrum of R.
definition_raw: The set of all prime ideals in a ring ''R'' with Zariski topology; it is called the [[prime spectrum]] of ''R''.

definition: An abelian variety is a complete group variety. For example, consider the complex variety $\mathbb{C}^n/\mathbb{Z}^{2n} $ or an elliptic curve $E$ over a finite field $\mathbb{F}_q$.
definition_raw: An [[abelian variety]] is a complete group variety. For example, consider the complex variety <math>\mathbb{C}^n/\mathbb{Z}^{2n} </math> or an elliptic curve <math>E</math> over a finite field <math>\mathbb{F}_q</math>.

definition: If D is an effective Cartier divisor on an algebraic variety X, both admitting dualizing sheaves $\omega_D, \omega_X$, then the adjunction formula says: $\omega_D = (\omega_X \otimes \mathcal{O}_X(D))|_D$.
definition_raw:
If ''D'' is an effective Cartier divisor on an algebraic variety ''X'', both admitting [[dualizing sheaf|dualizing sheaves]] <math>\omega_D, \omega_X</math>, then the [[adjunction formula]] says:
<math>\omega_D = (\omega_X \otimes \mathcal{O}_X(D))|_D</math>.

definition: Affine space is roughly a vector space where one has forgotten which point is the origin
definition_raw: [[Affine space]] is roughly a vector space where one has forgotten which point is the origin

definition: Algebraic geometry is a branch of mathematics that studies solutions to algebraic equations.
definition_raw: [[Algebraic geometry]] is a branch of mathematics that studies solutions to algebraic equations.

term: algebraic geometry over the field with one element
definition: One goal is to prove the Riemann hypothesis. See also the field with one element and as well as .
definition_raw: One goal is to prove the [[Riemann hypothesis]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv|title = An essay on the Riemann Hypothesis|eprint = 1509.05576|date = 2015-09-18|first = Connes|last = Alain|class = math.NT}}</ref> See also the [[field with one element]] and {{Cite arXiv|title = Mapping F_1-land:An overview of geometries over the field with one element|eprint=0909.0069 |date = 2009-08-31|first1 = Javier López|last1 = Peña|first2 = Oliver|last2 = Lorscheid|class=math.AG }} as well as <ref>{{Cite arXiv|title = Re

definition: An algebraic group is an algebraic variety that is also a group in such a way the group operations are morphisms of varieties.
definition_raw: An [[algebraic group]] is an algebraic variety that is also a [[group (mathematics)|group]] in such a way the group operations are morphisms of varieties.

definition: A separated scheme of finite type over a field. For example, an algebraic variety is a reduced irreducible algebraic scheme.
definition_raw: A separated scheme of finite type over a field. For example, an algebraic variety is a reduced irreducible algebraic scheme.

definition: An algebraic set over a field k is a reduced separated scheme of finite type over $\operatorname{Spec}(k)$. An irreducible algebraic set is called an algebraic variety.
definition_raw: An [[algebraic set]] over a field ''k'' is a reduced separated scheme of finite type over <math>\operatorname{Spec}(k)</math>. An irreducible algebraic set is called an algebraic variety.

definition: An algebraic space is a quotient of a scheme by the étale equivalence relation.
definition_raw: An [[algebraic space]] is a quotient of a scheme by the [[étale equivalence relation]].

definition: An algebraic variety over a field k is an integral separated scheme of finite type over $\operatorname{Spec}(k)$. Note, not assuming k is algebraically closed causes some pathology; for example, <math>\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{C} \times_{\mathbb{R
definition_raw: An [[algebraic variety]] over a field ''k'' is an integral separated scheme of finite type over <math>\operatorname{Spec}(k)</math>. Note, not assuming ''k'' is algebraically closed causes some pathology; for example, <math>\operatorname{Spec} \mathbb{C} \times_{\mathbb{R

definition: A locally free sheaf of a finite rank.
definition_raw: A [[locally free sheaf]] of a finite rank.

definition: A line bundle on a projective variety is ample if some tensor power of it is very ample.
definition_raw: A line bundle on a projective variety is [[ample line bundle|ample]] if some tensor power of it is very ample.

definition: Algebraic geometry over the compactification of Spec of the ring of rational integers $\mathbb{Z}$. See Arakelov geometry.
definition_raw: Algebraic geometry over the compactification of Spec of the [[ring of rational integers]] <math>\mathbb{Z}</math>. See [[Arakelov geometry]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv|title = New Approach to Arakelov Geometry|eprint=0704.2030 |date = 2007-04-16|first = Nikolai|last = Durov|class=math.AG }}</ref>

definition: The arithmetic genus of a projective variety X of dimension r is $(-1)^r (\chi(\mathcal{O}_X) - 1)$.
definition_raw: The [[arithmetic genus]] of a projective variety ''X'' of dimension ''r'' is <math>(-1)^r (\chi(\mathcal{O}_X) - 1)</math>.

definition: The arithmetic scheme is a scheme over $\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbf{Z})$, the prime spectrum of the ring of rational integers.
definition_raw: The [[arithmetic scheme]] is a scheme over <math>\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbf{Z})</math>, the prime spectrum of the ring of rational integers.

definition: Another term for an algebraic stack.
definition_raw: Another term for an [[algebraic stack]].

definition: 0-dimensional and Noetherian. The definition applies both to a scheme and a ring.
definition_raw: 0-dimensional and Noetherian. The definition applies both to a scheme and a ring.

definition: The weighted Euler characteristic of a (nice) stack X with respect to the Behrend function is the degree of the virtual fundamental class of X.
definition_raw: The [[weighted Euler characteristic]] of a (nice) stack ''X'' with respect to the [[Behrend function]] is the degree of the [[virtual fundamental class]] of ''X''.

definition: Behrend's trace formula generalizes Grothendieck's trace formula; both formulas compute the trace of the Frobenius on l-adic cohomology.
definition_raw: [[Behrend's trace formula]] generalizes [[Grothendieck's trace formula]]; both formulas compute the trace of the [[Arithmetic and geometric Frobenius|Frobenius]] on ''l''-adic cohomology.

definition: A big line bundle L on X of dimension n is a line bundle such that $\displaystyle \limsup_{l \to \infty} \operatorname{dim} \Gamma(X, L^l) / l^n > 0$.
definition_raw: A [[big line bundle]] ''L'' on ''X'' of dimension ''n'' is a line bundle such that <math>\displaystyle \limsup_{l \to \infty} \operatorname{dim} \Gamma(X, L^l) / l^n > 0</math>.

definition: A birational morphism between schemes is a morphism that becomes an isomorphism after restricted to some open dense subset. One of the most common examples of a birational map is the map induced by a blowup.
definition_raw: A [[birational morphism]] between schemes is a morphism that becomes an isomorphism after restricted to some open dense subset. One of the most common examples of a birational map is the map induced by a blowup.

definition: A blow-up is a birational transformation that replaces a closed subscheme with an effective Cartier divisor. Precisely, given a noetherian scheme X and a closed subscheme $Z \subset X$, the blow-up of X along Z is a proper morphism $\pi: \widetilde{X} \to X$ such that (1) $\pi^{-1}(Z) \hookrightarrow \widetilde{X}$ is an effective Cartier divisor, called the exceptional divisor, and (2) $\pi$ is universal with respect to (1). Concretely, it is constructed as the relative Proj of the Rees algebra of $O_X$ with respect to the ideal sheaf determining Z.
definition_raw: A [[Blowing up|blow-up]] is a birational transformation that replaces a closed subscheme with an effective Cartier divisor. Precisely, given a noetherian scheme ''X'' and a closed subscheme <math>Z \subset X</math>, the blow-up of ''X'' along ''Z'' is a proper morphism <math>\pi: \widetilde{X} \to X</math> such that (1) <math>\pi^{-1}(Z) \hookrightarrow \widetilde{X}</math> is an effective Cartier divisor, called the [[exceptional divisor]], and (2) <math>\pi</math> is universal with respect to

definition: The Calabi–Yau metric is a Kähler metric whose Ricci curvature is zero.
definition_raw: The [[Calabi–Yau metric]] is a Kähler metric whose Ricci curvature is zero.

definition: The canonical sheaf on a normal variety X of dimension n is $\omega_X = i_* \Omega_U^n$ where i is the inclusion of the smooth locus U and $\Omega_U^n$ is the sheaf of differential forms on U of degree n. If the base field has characteristic zero instead of normality, then one may replace i by a resolution of singularities.
definition_raw: The [[canonical sheaf]] on a normal variety ''X'' of dimension ''n'' is <math>\omega_X = i_* \Omega_U^n</math> where ''i'' is the inclusion of the [[smooth locus]] ''U'' and <math>\Omega_U^n</math> is the sheaf of differential forms on ''U'' of degree ''n''. If the base field has characteristic zero instead of normality, then one may replace ''i'' by a resolution of singularities.

definition: The canonical model is the Proj of a canonical ring (assuming the ring is finitely generated.)
definition_raw: The [[canonical model (algebraic geometry)|canonical model]] is the [[Proj construction|Proj]] of a canonical ring (assuming the ring is finitely generated.)

definition: An effective Cartier divisor D on a scheme X over S is a closed subscheme of X that is flat over S and whose ideal sheaf is invertible (locally free of rank one).
definition_raw: An effective [[Cartier divisor]] ''D'' on a scheme ''X'' over ''S'' is a closed subscheme of ''X'' that is flat over ''S'' and whose ideal sheaf is invertible (locally free of rank one).

definition: The Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity of a coherent sheaf F on a projective space $f: \mathbf{P}^n_S \to S$ over a scheme S is the smallest integer r such that :$R^i f_*F(r-i) = 0$ for all i > 0.
definition_raw:
The [[Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity]] of a coherent sheaf ''F'' on a projective space <math>f: \mathbf{P}^n_S \to S</math> over a scheme ''S'' is the smallest integer ''r'' such that
:<math>R^i f_*F(r-i) = 0</math>
for all ''i'' > 0.

definition: A scheme is catenary, if all chains between two irreducible closed subschemes have the same length. Examples include virtually everything, e.g. varieties over a field, and it is hard to construct examples that are not catenary.
definition_raw:
A scheme is [[Catenary (ring theory)|catenary]], if all chains between two irreducible closed subschemes have the same length. 
Examples include virtually everything, e.g. varieties over a field, and it is hard to construct examples that are not catenary.

definition: A special fiber.
definition_raw: A special fiber.

definition: The k-th Chow group $A_k(X)$ of a smooth variety X is the free abelian group generated by closed subvarieties of dimension k (group of k-cycles) modulo rational equivalences.
definition_raw: The ''k''-th [[Chow group]] <math>A_k(X)</math> of a smooth variety ''X'' is the free abelian group generated by closed subvarieties of dimension ''k'' (group of ''k''-[[algebraic cycle|cycles]]) modulo [[rational equivalence]]s.

definition: Classification is a guiding principle in all of mathematics where one tries to describe all objects satisfying certain properties up to given equivalences by more accessible data such as invariants or even some constructive process. In algebraic geometry one distinguishes between discrete and continuous invariants. For continuous classifying invariants one additionally attempts to provide some geometric structure which leads to moduli spaces.
definition_raw: [[Classification theorem|Classification]] is a guiding principle in all of mathematics where one tries to describe all objects satisfying certain properties up to given equivalences by more accessible data such as [[Complete set of invariants|invariants]] or even some constructive process. In algebraic geometry one distinguishes between discrete and continuous invariants. For continuous classifying invariants one additionally attempts to provide some geometric structure which leads to [[moduli s

definition: An analog of a classifying space for torsors in algebraic geometry; see classifying stack.
definition_raw: An analog of a [[classifying space]] for [[torsor (algebraic geometry)|torsor]]s in algebraic geometry; see [[classifying stack]].

definition: Closed subschemes of a scheme X are defined to be those occurring in the following construction. Let J be a quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-ideals. The support of the quotient sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X/J$ is a closed subset Z of X and $(Z,(\mathcal{O}_X/J)|_Z)$ is a scheme called the closed subscheme defined by the quasi-coherent sheaf of ideals J. The reason the definition of closed subschemes relies on such a construction is that, unlike open subsets, a closed subset of a scheme does not have a unique structure as a subscheme.
definition_raw: '''Closed subschemes''' of a scheme ''X'' are defined to be those occurring in the following construction. Let ''J'' be a [[Quasi-coherent sheaf|quasi-coherent]] sheaf of <math>\mathcal{O}_X</math>-[[sheaf of ideals|ideals]]. The [[support of a sheaf|support]] of the [[quotient sheaf]] <math>\mathcal{O}_X/J</math> is a closed subset ''Z'' of ''X'' and <math>(Z,(\mathcal{O}_X/J)|_Z)</math> is a scheme called the '''closed subscheme defined by the quasi-coherent sheaf of ideals ''J'''''.<ref>{{har

definition: A scheme is called Cohen-Macaulay if all local rings are Cohen-Macaulay. For example, regular schemes, and Spec k[x,y]/(xy) are Cohen–Macaulay, but 50px is not.
definition_raw:
A scheme is called Cohen-Macaulay
if all local rings are [[Cohen-Macaulay ring|Cohen-Macaulay]].
For example,  regular schemes, and ''Spec k''[''x,y'']/(''xy'') are Cohen–Macaulay, but
[[Image:Non cohen macaulay scheme thumb.png|50px]] is not.

definition: A coherent sheaf on a Noetherian scheme X is a quasi-coherent sheaf that is finitely generated as OX-module.
definition_raw: A [[coherent sheaf]] on a Noetherian scheme ''X'' is a quasi-coherent sheaf that is finitely generated as ''O''<sub>''X''</sub>-module.

definition: An algebraic curve of degree two.
definition_raw: An [[algebraic curve]] of degree two.

definition: The scheme is connected as a topological space. Since the connected components refine the irreducible components any irreducible scheme is connected but not vice versa. An affine scheme Spec(R) is connected iff the ring R possesses no idempotents other than 0 and 1; such a ring is also called a connected ring. Examples of connected schemes include affine space, projective space, and an example of a scheme that is not connected is Spec(k[x]×k[x])
definition_raw:
The scheme is ''[[Connected space|connected]]'' as a topological space. Since the [[connected component (topology)|connected components]] refine the [[irreducible component]]s any irreducible scheme is connected but not vice versa. An [[affine scheme]] ''Spec(R)'' is connected [[iff]] the ring ''R'' possesses no [[idempotent]]s other than 0 and 1; such a ring is also called a '''connected ring'''.

Examples of connected schemes include [[affine space]], [[projective space]], and an example of a

definition: See for example Nagata's compactification theorem.
definition_raw: See for example [[Nagata's compactification theorem]].

definition: A generalization of a homogeneous coordinate ring. See Cox ring.
definition_raw: A generalization of a homogeneous coordinate ring. See [[Cox ring]].

definition: A crepant morphism $f: X \to Y$ between normal varieties is a morphism such that $f^* \omega_Y = \omega_X$.
definition_raw: A [[crepant morphism]] <math>f: X \to Y</math> between normal varieties is a morphism such that <math>f^* \omega_Y = \omega_X</math>.

definition: An algebraic variety of dimension one.
definition_raw: An algebraic variety of dimension one.

definition: Let $S \to S'$ be a morphism of schemes and X an S-scheme. Then a deformation X of X is an S-scheme together with a pullback square in which X is the pullback of X (typically X is assumed to be flat).
definition_raw: Let <math>S \to S'</math> be a morphism of schemes and ''X'' an ''S''-scheme. Then a deformation ''X''<nowiki>'</nowiki> of ''X'' is an ''S''<nowiki>'</nowiki>-scheme together with a pullback square in which ''X'' is the pullback of ''X''<nowiki>'</nowiki> (typically ''X''<nowiki>'</nowiki> is assumed to be [[flat morphism|flat]]).

definition: Given a vector-bundle map $f: E \to F$ over a variety X (that is, a scheme X-morphism between the total spaces of the bundles), the degeneracy locus is the (scheme-theoretic) locus $X_k(f) = \{ x \in X | \operatorname{rk}(f(x)) \le k \}$.
definition_raw:
Given a vector-bundle map <math>f: E \to F</math> over a variety ''X'' (that is, a scheme ''X''-morphism between the total spaces of the bundles), the [[degeneracy locus]] is the (scheme-theoretic) locus
<math>X_k(f) = \{ x \in X | \operatorname{rk}(f(x)) \le k \}</math>.

definition: A scheme X is said to degenerate to a scheme $X_0$ (called the limit of X) if there is a scheme $\pi: Y \to \mathbf{A}^1$ with generic fiber X and special fiber $X_0$.
definition_raw: A scheme ''X'' is said to [[degeneration (algebraic geometry)|degenerate]] to a scheme <math>X_0</math> (called the limit of ''X'') if there is a scheme <math>\pi: Y \to \mathbf{A}^1</math> with [[generic fiber]] ''X'' and [[special fiber]] <math>X_0</math>.

definition: The dimension, by definition the maximal length of a chain of irreducible closed subschemes, is a global property. It can be seen locally if a scheme is irreducible. It depends only on the topology, not on the structure sheaf. See also Global dimension. Examples: equidimensional schemes in dimension 0: Artinian schemes, 1: algebraic curves, 2: algebraic surfaces.
definition_raw:
The [[dimension of an algebraic variety|dimension]], by definition the maximal length of a chain of irreducible closed subschemes, is a global property. It can be seen locally if a scheme is irreducible. It depends only on the topology, not on the structure sheaf. See also [[Global dimension]].

Examples: [[equidimensional scheme]]s in dimension 0: [[Artinian ring|Artinian]] schemes, 1: [[algebraic curves]], 2: [[algebraic surfaces]].

definition: The degree of a line bundle L on a complete variety is an integer d such that $\chi(L^{\otimes m}) = {d \over n!} m^n + O(m^{n-1})$.
definition_raw: The degree of a line bundle ''L'' on a complete variety is an integer ''d'' such that <math>\chi(L^{\otimes m}) = {d \over n!} m^n + O(m^{n-1})</math>.

definition: An approach to algebraic geometry using (commutative) ring spectra instead of commutative rings; see derived algebraic geometry.
definition_raw: An approach to algebraic geometry using ([[commutative ring spectrum|commutative]]) [[ring spectrum|ring spectra]] instead of [[commutative ring]]s; see [[derived algebraic geometry]].

definition: A divisorial sheaf on a normal variety is a reflexive sheaf of the form OX(D) for some Weil divisor D.
definition_raw: A [[divisorial sheaf]] on a normal variety is a reflexive sheaf of the form ''O''<sub>''X''</sub>(''D'') for some [[Weil divisor]] ''D''.

definition: A morphism is called dominant, if the image f(X) is dense. A morphism of affine schemes Spec A → Spec B is dense if and only if the kernel of the corresponding map B → A is contained in the nilradical of B.
definition_raw: A morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''X'' → ''Y''}} is called ''[[dominant morphism|dominant]]'', if the image ''f''(''X'') is [[dense set|dense]]. A morphism of affine schemes ''Spec A'' → ''Spec B'' is dense if and only if the kernel of the corresponding map ''B'' → ''A'' is contained in the nilradical of ''B''.

definition: See Coherent duality.
definition_raw: See [[Coherent duality]].

definition: On a projective Cohen–Macaulay scheme of pure dimension n, the dualizing sheaf is a coherent sheaf $\omega$ on X such that $H^{n-i}(X, F^{\vee} \otimes \omega) \simeq H^i(X, F)^*$ holds for any locally free sheaf F on X; for example, if X is a smooth projective variety, then it is a canonical sheaf.
definition_raw:
On a projective [[Cohen–Macaulay scheme]] of pure dimension ''n'', the [[dualizing sheaf]] is a coherent sheaf <math>\omega</math> on ''X'' such that
<math>H^{n-i}(X, F^{\vee} \otimes \omega) \simeq H^i(X, F)^*</math>
holds for any locally free sheaf ''F'' on ''X''; for example, if ''X'' is a smooth projective variety, then it is a [[canonical sheaf]].

definition: The EGA was an incomplete attempt to lay a foundation of algebraic geometry based on the notion of scheme, a generalization of an algebraic variety. Séminaire de géométrie algébrique picks up where the EGA left off. Today it is one of the standard references in algebraic geometry.
definition_raw: The [[Éléments de géométrie algébrique|EGA]] was an incomplete attempt to lay a foundation of algebraic geometry based on the notion of [[scheme (mathematics)|scheme]], a generalization of an algebraic variety. ''[[Séminaire de géométrie algébrique]]'' picks up where the EGA left off. Today it is one of the standard references in algebraic geometry.

definition: An elliptic curve is a smooth projective curve of genus one.
definition_raw: An [[elliptic curve]] is a smooth [[projective curve]] of genus one.

definition: Localization of a finite type scheme.
definition_raw: Localization of a finite type scheme.

definition: A morphism is étale if it is flat and unramified. There are several other equivalent definitions. In the case of smooth varieties $ X $ and $ Y $ over an algebraically closed field, étale morphisms are precisely those inducing an isomorphism of tangent spaces $ df: T_{y} Y \rightarrow T_{f(y)} X$, which coincides with the usual notion of étale map in differential geometry. Étale morphisms form a very important class of morphisms; they are used to build the so-called étale topology and consequently the étale cohomology, which is nowadays one of the cornerstones of algebraic geometry.
definition_raw:
A morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} is [[étale morphism|étale]] if it is flat and unramified. There are several other equivalent definitions. In the case of smooth varieties <math> X </math> and <math> Y </math> over an algebraically closed [[field (mathematics)|field]], étale morphisms are precisely those inducing an isomorphism of tangent spaces <math> df: T_{y} Y \rightarrow T_{f(y)} X</math>, which coincides with the usual notion of étale map in differential geometry.

Étale morphism

definition: The exact sequence of sheaves: :$0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{P}^n} \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{P}^n}(1)^{\oplus (n+1)} \to T \mathbf{P}^n \to 0,$ where Pn is the projective space over a field and the last nonzero term is the tangent sheaf, is called the Euler sequence.
definition_raw:
The exact sequence of sheaves:
:<math>0 \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{P}^n} \to \mathcal{O}_{\mathbf{P}^n}(1)^{\oplus (n+1)} \to T \mathbf{P}^n \to 0,</math>
where '''P'''<sup>''n''</sup> is the projective space over a field and the last nonzero term is the [[tangent sheaf]], is called the [[Euler sequence]].

definition: See Chapter II of http://www.math.ubc.ca/~behrend/cet.pdf
definition_raw: See Chapter II of http://www.math.ubc.ca/~behrend/cet.pdf

definition: Related to Frobenius morphism.
definition_raw: Related to [[Frobenius morphism]].<ref>{{Cite arXiv|title = Frobenius Splitting in Commutative Algebra|eprint=1409.1169 |date = 2014-09-03|first1 = Karen E.|last1 = Smith|first2 = Wenliang|last2 = Zhang|class=math.AC }}</ref>

definition: A Fano variety is a smooth projective variety X whose anticanonical sheaf $\omega_X^{-1}$ is ample.
definition_raw: A [[Fano variety]] is a smooth [[projective variety]] ''X'' whose anticanonical sheaf <math>\omega_X^{-1}</math> is ample.

definition: Given $f: X \to Y$ between schemes, the fiber of f over y is, as a set, the pre-image $f^{-1}(y) = \{x \in X|f(x)=y\}$; it has the natural structure of a scheme over the residue field of y as the fiber product $X \times_Y \{y\}$, where $\{y\}$ has the natural structure of a scheme over Y as Spec of the residue field of y.
definition_raw: Given <math>f: X \to Y</math> between schemes, the fiber of ''f'' over ''y'' is, as a set, the pre-image <math>f^{-1}(y) = \{x \in X|f(x)=y\}</math>; it has the natural structure of a scheme over the [[residue field]] of ''y'' as the fiber product <math>X \times_Y \{y\}</math>, where <math>\{y\}</math> has the natural structure of a scheme over ''Y'' as Spec of the residue field of ''y''.

definition: Another term for the "pullback" in the category theory.
definition_raw: Another term for the "[[pullback (category theory)|pullback]]" in the category theory.

definition: One of Grothendieck's fundamental ideas is to emphasize relative notions, i.e. conditions on morphisms rather than conditions on schemes themselves. The category of schemes has a final object, the spectrum of the ring $ \mathbb{Z} $ of integers; so that any scheme $ S $ is over $ \textrm{Spec} (\mathbb{Z}) $, and in a unique way.
definition_raw: One of Grothendieck's fundamental ideas is to emphasize ''relative'' notions, i.e. conditions on morphisms rather than conditions on schemes themselves. The category of schemes has a [[final object]], the spectrum of the ring <math> \mathbb{Z} </math> of integers; so that any scheme <math> S </math> is ''over'' <math> \textrm{Spec} (\mathbb{Z}) </math>, and in a unique way.

definition: The morphism is finite if $X$ may be covered by affine open sets $ \text{Spec }B $ such that each $ f^{-1}(\text{Spec }B) $ is affine — say of the form $ \text{Spec }A $ — and furthermore $ A $ is finitely generated as a $ B $-module. See finite morphism. Finite morphisms are quasi-finite, but not all morphisms having finite fibers are quasi-finite, and morphisms of finite type are usually not quasi-finite.
definition_raw:
The morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} is '''finite''' if <math>X</math> may be covered by affine open sets <math> \text{Spec }B </math> such that each <math> f^{-1}(\text{Spec }B) </math> is affine — say of the form <math> \text{Spec }A </math> — and furthermore <math> A </math> is finitely generated as a <math> B </math>-module.  See [[finite morphism]].

Finite morphisms are quasi-finite, but not all morphisms having finite fibers are quasi-finite, and morphisms of finite type are usua

definition: The morphism is locally of finite type if $ X $ may be covered by affine open sets $ \text{Spec }B $ such that each inverse image $f^{-1}(\text{Spec }B)$ is covered by affine open sets $\text{Spec }A$ where each $ A $ is finitely generated as a $B$-algebra. The morphism is of finite type if $ X $ may be covered by affine open sets $ \text{Spec }B $ such that each inverse image $f^{-1}(\text{Spec }B)$ is covered by finitely many affine open sets $\text{Spec }A$ where each $ A $ is finitely generated as a $B$-algebra.
definition_raw:
The morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} is '''locally of finite type''' if <math> X </math> may be covered by affine open sets <math> \text{Spec }B </math> such that each inverse image <math>f^{-1}(\text{Spec }B)</math> is covered by affine open sets <math>\text{Spec }A</math> where each <math> A </math> is finitely generated as a <math>B</math>-algebra.

The morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} is [[Morphism of finite type|of finite type]] if <math> X </math> may be covered by affine

definition: The morphism has finite fibers if the fiber over each point $ x \in X $ is a finite set. A morphism is quasi-finite if it is of finite type and has finite fibers.
definition_raw: The morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} has '''finite fibers''' if the fiber over each point <math> x \in X </math> is a finite set. A morphism is '''[[quasi-finite morphism|quasi-finite]]''' if it is of finite type and has finite fibers.

definition: If y is a point of Y, then the morphism f is of finite presentation at y (or finitely presented at y) if there is an open affine neighborhood U of f(y) and an open affine neighbourhood V of y such that f(V) ⊆ U and $\mathcal{O}_Y(V)$ is a finitely presented algebra over $\mathcal{O}_X(U)$. The morphism f is locally of finite presentation if it is finitely presented at all points of Y. If X is locally Noetherian, then f is locally of finite presentation if, and only if, it is locally of finite type. The morphism is of finite presentation (or Y is finitely presented over X) if it is locally of finite presentation, quasi-compact, and quasi-separated. If X is locally Noetherian, then f is of finite presentation if, and only if, it is of finite type.
definition_raw: If ''y'' is a point of ''Y'', then the morphism ''f'' is '''of finite presentation at ''y''''' (or '''finitely presented at ''y''''') if there is an open affine neighborhood ''U'' of ''f(y)'' and an open affine neighbourhood ''V'' of ''y'' such that ''f''(''V'') ⊆ ''U'' and <math>\mathcal{O}_Y(V)</math> is a [[finitely presented algebra]] over <math>\mathcal{O}_X(U)</math>. The morphism ''f'' is '''locally of finite presentation''' if it is finitely presented at all points of ''Y''. If

definition: The flag variety parametrizes a flag of vector spaces.
definition_raw: The [[flag variety]] parametrizes a [[Flag (linear algebra)|flag]] of vector spaces.

definition: A morphism $ f $ is flat if it gives rise to a flat map on stalks. When viewing a morphism as a family of schemes parametrized by the points of $ X $, the geometric meaning of flatness could roughly be described by saying that the fibers $f^{-1}(x)$ do not vary too wildly.
definition_raw: A morphism <math> f </math> is [[flat morphism (ring theory)|flat]] if it gives rise to a [[Flat map (ring theory)|flat map]] on stalks. When viewing a morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} as a family of schemes parametrized by the points of <math> X </math>, the geometric meaning of flatness could roughly be described by saying that the fibers <math>f^{-1}(x)</math> do not vary too wildly.

definition: The Gabriel–Rosenberg reconstruction theorem states a scheme X can be recovered from the category of quasi-coherent sheaves on X. The theorem is a starting point for noncommutative algebraic geometry since, taking the theorem as an axiom, defining a noncommutative scheme amounts to defining the category of quasi-coherent sheaves on it. See also https://mathoverflow.net/q/16257

definition: The genus formula for a nodal curve in the projective plane says the genus of the curve is given as $g = (d-1)(d-2)/2 - \delta$ where d is the degree of the curve and δ is the number of nodes (which is zero if the curve is smooth).
definition_raw:
The [[genus formula]] for a nodal curve in the projective plane says the genus of the curve is given as
<math>g = (d-1)(d-2)/2 - \delta</math>
where ''d'' is the degree of the curve and δ is the number of nodes (which is zero if the curve is smooth).

definition: The geometric genus of a smooth projective variety X of dimension n is $\dim \Gamma(X, \Omega^n_X) = \dim \operatorname{H}^n(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$ (where the equality is Serre's duality theorem.)
definition_raw:
The [[geometric genus]] of a smooth projective variety ''X'' of dimension ''n'' is
<math>\dim \Gamma(X, \Omega^n_X) = \dim \operatorname{H}^n(X, \mathcal{O}_X)</math>
(where the equality is [[Serre's duality theorem]].)

definition: The prime spectrum of an algebraically closed field.
definition_raw: The prime spectrum of an algebraically closed field.

definition: A property of a scheme X over a field k is "geometric" if it holds for $X_E = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec} k} {\operatorname{Spec} E}$ for any field extension $E/k$.
definition_raw: A property of a scheme ''X'' over a field ''k'' is "[[geometrically (algebraic geometry)|geometric]]" if it holds for <math>X_E = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec} k} {\operatorname{Spec} E}</math> for any [[field extension]] <math>E/k</math>.

definition: The geometric quotient of a scheme X with the action of a group scheme G is a good quotient such that the fibers are orbits.
definition_raw: The [[geometric quotient]] of a scheme ''X'' with the action of a group scheme ''G'' is a good quotient such that the fibers are orbits.

definition: A gerbe is (roughly) a stack that is locally nonempty and in which two objects are locally isomorphic.
definition_raw: A [[gerbe]] is (roughly) a [[stack (mathematics)|stack]] that is locally nonempty and in which two objects are locally isomorphic.

definition: The GIT quotient $X / \! / G$ is $\operatorname{Spec}(A^G)$ when $X = \operatorname{Spec} A$ and $\operatorname{Proj}(A^G)$ when $X = \operatorname{Proj} A$.
definition_raw: The [[GIT quotient]] <math>X / \! / G</math> is <math>\operatorname{Spec}(A^G)</math> when <math>X = \operatorname{Spec} A</math> and <math>\operatorname{Proj}(A^G)</math> when <math>X = \operatorname{Proj} A</math>.

definition: The good quotient of a scheme X with the action of a group scheme G is an invariant morphism $f: X \to Y$ such that $(f_* \mathcal{O}_X)^G = \mathcal{O}_Y.$
definition_raw: The [[good quotient]] of a scheme ''X'' with the action of a group scheme ''G'' is an invariant morphism <math>f: X \to Y</math> such that <math>(f_* \mathcal{O}_X)^G = \mathcal{O}_Y.</math>

definition: A Gorenstein scheme is a locally Noetherian scheme whose local rings are Gorenstein rings.
definition_raw: A [[Gorenstein scheme]] is a locally Noetherian scheme whose local rings are [[Gorenstein ring]]s.

definition: The Grauert–Riemenschneider vanishing theorem extends the Kodaira vanishing theorem to higher direct image sheaves; see also https://arxiv.org/abs/1404.1827
definition_raw: The [[Grauert–Riemenschneider vanishing theorem]] extends the [[Kodaira vanishing theorem]] to higher direct image sheaves; see also https://arxiv.org/abs/1404.1827

definition: The Grothendieck ring of varieties is the free abelian group generated by isomorphism classes of varieties with the relation: $[X] = [Z] + [X - Z]$ where Z is a closed subvariety of a variety X and equipped with the multiplication $[X] \cdot [Y] = [X \times Y].$
definition_raw:
The [[Grothendieck ring of varieties]] is the free abelian group generated by isomorphism classes of varieties with the relation:
<math>[X] = [Z] + [X - Z]</math>
where ''Z'' is a closed subvariety of a variety ''X'' and equipped with the multiplication
<math>[X] \cdot [Y] = [X \times Y].</math>

definition: Grothendieck's vanishing theorem concerns local cohomology.
definition_raw: [[Grothendieck's vanishing theorem]] concerns [[local cohomology]].

definition: A group scheme is a scheme whose sets of points have the structures of a group.
definition_raw: A [[group scheme]] is a scheme whose sets of points have the structures of a [[group (mathematics)|group]].

definition_raw: An old term for a "smooth" algebraic group.

definition: The Hilbert polynomial of a projective scheme X over a field is the Euler characteristic $\chi(\mathcal{O}_X(s))$.
definition_raw: The [[Hilbert polynomial]] of a projective scheme ''X'' over a field is the Euler characteristic <math>\chi(\mathcal{O}_X(s))</math>.

definition: The Hodge bundle on the moduli space of curves (of fixed genus) is roughly a vector bundle whose fiber over a curve C is the vector space $\Gamma(C, \omega_C)$.
definition_raw: The [[Hodge bundle]] on the [[moduli space of curves]] (of fixed genus) is roughly a vector bundle whose fiber over a curve ''C'' is the vector space <math>\Gamma(C, \omega_C)</math>.

definition: A curve is hyperelliptic if it has a g12 (i.e., there is a linear system of dimension 1 and degree 2.)
definition_raw: A curve is [[hyperelliptic curve|hyperelliptic]] if it has a ''g''<sup>1</sup><sub>2</sub> (i.e., there is a linear system of dimension 1 and degree 2.)

definition: Another term for Serre's twisting sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X(1)$. It is the dual of the tautological line bundle (whence the term).
definition_raw: Another term for [[Serre's twisting sheaf]] <math>\mathcal{O}_X(1)</math>. It is the dual of the [[tautological line bundle]] (whence the term).

definition: If is any morphism of schemes, the scheme-theoretic image of f is the unique closed subscheme which satisfies the following universal property: #f factors through i, #if is any closed subscheme of X such that f factors through j, then i also factors through j. This notion is distinct from that of the usual set-theoretic image of f, f(Y). For example, the underlying space of Z always contains (but is not necessarily equal to) the Zariski closure of f(Y) in X, so if Y is any open (and not closed) subscheme of X and f is the inclusion map, then Z is different from f(Y). When Y is reduced, then Z is the Zariski closure of f(Y) endowed with the structure of reduced closed subscheme. But in general, unless f is quasi-compact, the construction of Z is not local on X.
definition_raw:
If {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} is any morphism of schemes, the '''scheme-theoretic image''' of ''f'' is the unique ''closed'' subscheme {{nowrap|''i'' : ''Z'' → ''X''}} which satisfies the following [[universal property]]:
#''f'' factors through ''i'',
#if {{nowrap|''j'' : ''Z''′ → ''X''}} is any closed subscheme of ''X'' such that ''f'' factors through ''j'', then ''i'' also factors through ''j''.<ref>{{harvnb|Hartshorne|1977|loc=Exercise II.3.11(d)}}</ref><ref>[http://www.math.columbia.ed

definition: Immersions are maps that factor through isomorphisms with subschemes. Specifically, an open immersion factors through an isomorphism with an open subscheme and a closed immersion factors through an isomorphism with a closed subscheme. Equivalently, f is a closed immersion if, and only if, it induces a homeomorphism from the underlying topological space of Y to a closed subset of the underlying topological space of X, and if the morphism $f^\sharp: \mathcal{O}_X \to f_* \mathcal{O}_Y$ is surjective. A composition of immersions is again an immersion. Some authors, such as Hartshorne in his book Algebraic Geometry and Q. Liu in his book Algebraic Geometry and Arithmetic Curves, define immersions as the composite of an open immersion followed by a closed immersion. These immersions are immersions in the sense above, but the converse is false. Furthermore, under this definition, the composite of two immersions is not necessarily an immersion. However, the two definitions are equivalent when f is quasi-compact. Note that an open immersion is completely described by its image in the sense of topological spaces, while a closed immersion is not: $\operatorname{Spec} A/I$ and $\operatorname{Spec} A/J$ may be homeomorphic but not isomorphic. This happens, for example, if I is the radical of J but J is not a radical ideal. When specifying a closed subset of a scheme without mentioning the scheme structure, usually the so-called reduced scheme structure is meant, that is, the scheme structure corresponding to the unique radical ideal consisting of all functions vanishing on that closed subset.
definition_raw: '''Immersions''' {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} are maps that factor through isomorphisms with subschemes. Specifically, an '''open immersion''' factors through an isomorphism with an open subscheme and a '''[[closed immersion]]''' factors through an isomorphism with a closed subscheme.<ref>{{harvnb|Grothendieck|Dieudonné|1960|loc=4.2.1}}</ref> Equivalently, ''f'' is a closed immersion if, and only if, it induces a homeomorphism from the underlying topological space of ''Y'' to a closed subset

definition: An ind-scheme is an inductive limit of closed immersions of schemes.
definition_raw: An [[ind-scheme]] is an [[inductive limit]] of closed immersions of schemes.

definition: A locally free sheaf of a rank one. Equivalently, it is a torsor for the multiplicative group $\mathbb{G}_m$ (i.e., line bundle).

definition: A scheme that is both reduced and irreducible is called integral. For locally Noetherian schemes, to be integral is equivalent to being a connected scheme that is covered by the spectra of integral domains. (Strictly speaking, this is not a local property, because the disjoint union of two integral schemes is not integral. However, for irreducible schemes, it is a local property.) For example, the scheme Spec k[t]/f, f irreducible polynomial is integral, while Spec A×B (A, B ≠ 0) is not.

definition: A scheme X is said to be irreducible when (as a topological space) it is not the union of two closed subsets except if one is equal to X. Using the correspondence of prime ideals and points in an affine scheme, this means X is irreducible iff X is connected and the rings Ai all have exactly one minimal prime ideal. (Rings possessing exactly one minimal prime ideal are therefore also called irreducible.) Any noetherian scheme can be written uniquely as the union of finitely many maximal irreducible non-empty closed subsets, called its irreducible components. Affine space and projective space are irreducible, while Spec k[x,y]/(xy) = 50px is not.

definition: Most important properties of schemes are local in nature, i.e. a scheme X has a certain property P if and only if for any cover of X by open subschemes Xi, i.e. X=$\cup$ Xi, every Xi has the property P. It is usually the case that it is enough to check one cover, not all possible ones. One also says that a certain property is Zariski-local, if one needs to distinguish between the Zariski topology and other possible topologies, like the étale topology. Consider a scheme X and a cover by affine open subschemes Spec Ai. Using the dictionary between (commutative) rings and affine schemes local properties are thus properties of the rings Ai. A property P is local in the above sense, iff the corresponding property of rings is stable under localization. For example, we can speak of locally Noetherian schemes, namely those which are covered by the spectra of Noetherian rings. The fact that localizations of a Noetherian ring are still noetherian then means that the property of a scheme of being locally Noetherian is local in the above sense (whence the name). Another example: if a ring is reduced (i.e., has no non-zero nilpotent elements), then so are its localizations. An example for a non-local property is separatedness (see below for the definition). Any affine scheme is separated, therefore any scheme is locally separated. However, the affine pieces may glue together pathologically to yield a non-separated scheme. The following is a (non-exhaustive) list of local properties of rings, which are applied to schemes. Let X = $\cup$ Spec Ai be a covering of a scheme by open affine subschemes. For definiteness, let k denote a field in the following. Most of the examples also work with the integers Z as a base, though, or even more general bases. Connected, irreducible, reduced, integral, normal, regular, Cohen-Macaulay, locally noetherian, dimension, catenary, Gorenstein.
definition_raw: Most important properties of schemes are ''local in nature'', i.e. a scheme ''X'' has a certain property ''P'' if and only if for any cover of ''X'' by open subschemes ''X<sub>i</sub>'', i.e. ''X''=<math>\cup</math> ''X<sub>i</sub>'', every ''X<sub>i</sub>'' has the property ''P''. It is usually the case that it is enough to check one cover, not all possible ones. definition: A scheme X covered by Spec Ai, where the Ai are Noetherian rings. If in addition a finite number of such affine spectra covers X, the scheme is called noetherian. While it is true that the spectrum of a noetherian ring is a noetherian topological space, the converse is false. For example, most schemes in finite-dimensional algebraic geometry are locally Noetherian, but $GL_\infty = \cup GL_n$ is not.

definition: A morphism of schemes is called open (closed), if the underlying map of topological spaces is open (closed, respectively), i.e. if open subschemes of Y are mapped to open subschemes of X (and similarly for closed). For example, finitely presented flat morphisms are open and proper maps are closed.

definition: A scheme $ S $ is a locally ringed space, so a fortiori a topological space, but the meanings of point of $ S $ are threefold: #a point $ P $ of the underlying topological space; #a $ T $-valued point of $S$ is a morphism from $ T $ to $ S $, for any scheme $ T $; #a geometric point, where $ S $ is defined over (is equipped with a morphism to) $ \textrm{Spec}(K) $, where $ K $ is a field, is a morphism from $ \textrm{Spec} (\overline{K}) $ to $ S $ where $ \overline{K} $ is an algebraic closure of $K$. Geometric points are what in the most classical cases, for example algebraic varieties that are complex manifolds, would be the ordinary-sense points. The points $ P $ of the underlying space include analogues of the generic points (in the sense of Zariski, not that of André Weil), which specialise to ordinary-sense points. The $ T $-valued points are thought of, via Yoneda's lemma, as a way of identifying $ S $ with the representable functor $ h_{S} $ it sets up. Historically there was a process by which projective geometry added more points (e.g. complex points, line at infinity) to simplify the geometry by refining the basic objects. The $ T $-valued points were a massive further step. As part of the predominating Grothendieck approach, there are three corresponding notions of fiber of a morphism: the first being the simple inverse image of a point. The other two are formed by creating fiber products of two morphisms. For example, a geometric fiber of a morphism $ S^{\prime} \to S $ is thought of as $ S^{\prime} \times_{S} \textrm{Spec}(\overline{K}) $. This makes the extension from affine schemes, where it is just the tensor product of R-algebras, to all schemes of the fiber product operation a significant (if technically anodyne) result.
an embedding into a projective space

definition: The projection formula says that, for a morphism $f:X \to Y$ of schemes, an $ \mathcal{O}_X$-module $\mathcal{F}$ and a locally free $\mathcal{O}_Y$-module $\mathcal{E}$ of finite rank, there is a natural isomorphism $f_* (F \otimes f^* E) = (f_* F) \otimes E$ (in short, $f_*$ is linear with respect to the action of locally free sheaves.)
definition_raw:
The [[projection formula]] says that, for a morphism <math>f:X \to Y</math> of schemes, an <math> \mathcal{O}_X</math>-module <math>\mathcal{F}</math> and a [[locally free sheaf|locally free]] <math>\mathcal{O}_Y</math>-module <math>\mathcal{E}</math> of finite rank, there is a natural isomorphism
<math>f_* (F \otimes f^* E) = (f_* F) \otimes E</math>
(in short, <math>f_*</math> is linear with respect to the action of locally free sheaves.)

definition: A projective variety is a closed subvariety of a projective space.

definition: If E is a locally free sheaf on a scheme X, the projective bundle P(E) of E is the global Proj of the symmetric algebra of the dual of E: $\mathbf{P}(E) = \mathbf{Proj}(\operatorname{Sym}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(E^{\vee})).$ Note this definition is standard nowadays (e.g., Fulton's Intersection theory) but differs from EGA and Hartshorne (they don't take a dual).
definition_raw:
If ''E'' is a locally free sheaf on a scheme ''X'', the [[projective bundle]] '''P'''(''E'') of ''E'' is the [[global Proj]] of the symmetric algebra of the dual of ''E'':
<math>\mathbf{P}(E) = \mathbf{Proj}(\operatorname{Sym}_{\mathcal{O}_X}(E^{\vee})).</math>
Note this definition is standard nowadays (e.g., Fulton's ''Intersection theory'') but differs from EGA and Hartshorne (they don't take a dual).

definition: A morphism is proper if it is separated, universally closed (i.e. such that fiber products with it are closed maps), and of finite type. Projective morphisms are proper; but the converse is not in general true. See also complete variety. A deep property of proper morphisms is the existence of a Stein factorization, namely the existence of an intermediate scheme such that a morphism can be expressed as one with connected fibres, followed by a finite morphism.
definition_raw: A morphism is '''[[proper morphism|proper]]''' if it is separated, ''universally closed'' (i.e. such that fiber products with it are closed maps), and of finite type. Projective morphisms are proper; but the converse is not in general true. See also [[complete variety]]. A deep property of proper morphisms is the existence of a ''[[Stein factorization]]'', namely the existence of an intermediate scheme such that a morphism can be expressed as one with connected fibres, followed by a finite morph

definition: Let P be a property of a scheme that is stable under base change (finite-type, proper, smooth, étale, etc.). Then a representable morphism $f: F \to G$ is said to have property P if, for any $B \to G$ with B a scheme, the base change $F \times_G B \to B$ has property P.
definition_raw: Let '''P''' be a property of a scheme that is stable under base change (finite-type, proper, smooth, étale, etc.). Then a representable morphism <math>f: F \to G</math> is said to have property '''P''' if, for any <math>B \to G</math> with ''B'' a scheme, the base change <math>F \times_G B \to B</math> has property '''P'''.

definition: Pseudoreductive generalizes reductive in the context of connected smooth linear algebraic group.

definition_wikilinks.target: Singular point of an algebraic variety

definition_raw: [[Pseudo-reductive group|Pseudoreductive]] generalizes [[Reductive group|reductive]] in the context of [[Connected space|connected]] [[Singular point of an algebraic variety|smooth]] [[linear algebraic group]].

definition: A scheme has pure dimension d if each irreducible component has dimension d.
definition_raw: A scheme has pure dimension ''d'' if each irreducible component has dimension ''d''.

definition: A quasi-coherent sheaf on a Noetherian scheme X is a sheaf of OX-modules that is locally given by modules.
definition_raw: A quasi-coherent sheaf on a Noetherian scheme ''X'' is a [[sheaf of modules|sheaf of ''O''<sub>''X''</sub>-modules]] that is locally given by modules.

definition: A morphism is called quasi-compact, if for some (equivalently: every) open affine cover of X by some Ui = Spec Bi, the preimages f−1(Ui) are quasi-compact.
definition_raw: A morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} is called ''[[quasi-compact morphism|quasi-compact]]'', if for some (equivalently: every) open affine cover of ''X'' by some ''U<sub>i</sub>'' = ''Spec B<sub>i</sub>'', the preimages ''f''<sup>−1</sup>(''U<sub>i</sub>'') are [[quasi-compact]].

definition: The morphism has finite fibers if the fiber over each point $ x \in X $ is a finite set. A morphism is quasi-finite if it is of finite type and has finite fibers.
definition_raw: The morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} has '''finite fibers''' if the fiber over each point <math> x \in X </math> is a finite set. A morphism is '''[[quasi-finite morphism|quasi-finite]]''' if it is of finite type and has finite fibers.

definition: A quasi-projective variety is a locally closed subvariety of a projective space.
definition_raw: A [[quasi-projective variety]] is a [[locally closed]] subvariety of a projective space.

definition: A morphism is called quasi-separated or (Y is quasi-separated over X) if the diagonal morphism is quasi-compact. A scheme Y is called quasi-separated if Y is quasi-separated over Spec(Z).
definition_raw: A morphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''Y'' → ''X''}} is called '''[[quasi-separated]]''' or ('''''Y'' is quasi-separated over ''X''''') if the diagonal morphism {{nowrap|''Y'' → ''Y'' ×<sub>''X''</sub>''Y''}} is quasi-compact. A scheme ''Y'' is called '''quasi-separated''' if ''Y'' is quasi-separated over Spec('''Z''').<ref>{{harvnb|Grothendieck|Dieudonné|1964|loc=1.2.1}}</ref>

definition: A reductive group $G$ defined over a field $k$ is quasi-split if and only if it admits a Borel subgroup $B\subseteq G$ defined over $k$. Any quasi-split reductive group is a split-reductive reductive group, but there are quasi-split reductive groups that are not split-reductive.
definition_raw: A [[reductive group]] <math>G</math> defined over a field <math>k</math> is [[Quasi-split group|quasi-split]] if and only if it admits a Borel subgroup <math>B\subseteq G</math> defined over <math>k</math>. Any quasi-split reductive group is a split-reductive reductive group, but there are quasi-split reductive groups that are not split-reductive.

definition: A Quot scheme parametrizes quotients of locally free sheaves on a projective scheme.
definition_raw: A [[Quot scheme]] parametrizes quotients of locally free sheaves on a projective scheme.

definition: Usually denoted by [X/G], a quotient stack generalizes a quotient of a scheme or variety.
definition_raw: Usually denoted by [''X''/''G''], a [[quotient stack]] generalizes a quotient of a scheme or variety.

definition: Over an algebraically closed field, a variety is rational if it is birational to a projective space. For example, rational curves and rational surfaces are those birational to $\mathbb{P}^1, \mathbb{P}^2$.
definition_raw: Over an algebraically closed field, a variety is [[rational variety|rational]] if it is birational to a projective space. For example, [[rational curve]]s and [[rational surface]]s are those birational to <math>\mathbb{P}^1, \mathbb{P}^2</math>.

definition: An element in the function field $k(X) = \varinjlim k[U]$ where the limit runs over all coordinates rings of open subsets U of an (irreducible) algebraic variety X. See also function field (scheme theory).

definition_wikilinks.target: function field of an algebraic variety

definition_raw: An element in the [[function field of an algebraic variety|function field]] <math>k(X) = \varinjlim k[U]</math> where the limit runs over all coordinates rings of open subsets ''U'' of an (irreducible) algebraic variety ''X''. See also [[function field (scheme theory)]].

definition: A rational normal curve is the image of $\mathbf{P}^1 \to \mathbf{P}^d, \, (s:t) \mapsto (s^d : s^{d-1} t : \cdots : t^d)$. If d = 3, it is also called the twisted cubic.
definition_raw:
A [[rational normal curve]] is the image of
<math>\mathbf{P}^1 \to \mathbf{P}^d, \, (s:t) \mapsto (s^d : s^{d-1} t : \cdots : t^d)</math>.
If ''d'' = 3, it is also called the [[twisted cubic]].

definition: A variety X over a field of characteristic zero has rational singularities if there is a resolution of singularities $f:X' \to X$ such that $f_*(\mathcal{O}_{X'}) = \mathcal{O}_X$ and $R^i f_*(\mathcal{O}_{X'}) = 0, \, i \ge 1$.
definition_raw: A variety ''X'' over a field of characteristic zero has [[rational singularities]] if there is a resolution of singularities <math>f:X' \to X</math> such that <math>f_*(\mathcal{O}_{X'}) = \mathcal{O}_X</math> and <math>R^i f_*(\mathcal{O}_{X'}) = 0, \, i \ge 1</math>.

definition: A commutative ring $R$ is reduced if it has no nonzero nilpotent elements, i.e., its nilradical is the zero ideal, $\sqrt{(0)} = (0)$. Equivalently, $R$ is reduced if $\operatorname{Spec}(R)$ is a reduced scheme.
definition_raw: A commutative ring <math>R</math> is [[reduced ring|reduced]] if it has no nonzero nilpotent elements, i.e., its nilradical is the zero ideal, <math>\sqrt{(0)} = (0)</math>. Equivalently, <math>R</math> is reduced if <math>\operatorname{Spec}(R)</math> is a reduced scheme.

definition: A connected linear algebraic group $G$ over a field $k$ is a reductive group if and only if the unipotent radical <math>R_u(G_{\overline{k
definition_raw: A [[Connected space|connected]] [[linear algebraic group]] <math>G</math> over a field <math>k</math> is a [[reductive group]] if and only if the unipotent radical <math>R_u(G_{\overline{k

definition: A coherent sheaf is reflexive if the canonical map to the second dual is an isomorphism.
definition_raw: A coherent sheaf is [[reflexive sheaf|reflexive]] if the canonical map to the second dual is an isomorphism.

definition: A regular scheme is a scheme where the local rings are regular local rings. For example, smooth varieties over a field are regular, while Spec k[x,y]/(x2+x3-y2)=50px is not.
definition_raw:
A [[regular scheme]] is a scheme where the local rings are [[regular local ring]]s. For example,  [[smooth scheme|smooth varieties]] over a field are regular, while
''Spec k''[''x,y'']/(''x''<sup>2</sup>+''x''<sup>3</sup>-''y''<sup>2</sup>)=[[Image:Non regular scheme thumb.png|50px]] is not.

definition: A closed immersion $i: X \hookrightarrow Y$ is a regular embedding if each point of X has an affine neighborhood in Y so that the ideal of X there is generated by a regular sequence. If i is a regular embedding, then the conormal sheaf of i, that is, $\mathcal{I}/\mathcal{I}^2$ when $\mathcal{I}$ is the ideal sheaf of X, is locally free.
definition_raw: A [[closed immersion]] <math>i: X \hookrightarrow Y</math> is a [[regular embedding]] if each point of ''X'' has an affine neighborhood in ''Y'' so that the ideal of ''X'' there is generated by a [[regular sequence]]. If ''i'' is a regular embedding, then the [[conormal sheaf]] of ''i'', that is, <math>\mathcal{I}/\mathcal{I}^2</math> when <math>\mathcal{I}</math> is the ideal sheaf of ''X'', is locally free.

definition: A morphism from an algebraic variety to the affine line.
definition_raw: A [[morphism of varieties|morphism]] from an algebraic variety to the [[affine line]].

definition: A morphism $F \to G$ of stacks such that, for any morphism $B \to G$ from a scheme B, the base change $F \times_G B$ is an algebraic space. If "algebraic space" is replaced by "scheme", then it is said to be strongly representable.
definition_raw: A morphism <math>F \to G</math> of stacks such that, for any morphism <math>B \to G</math> from a scheme ''B'', the base change <math>F \times_G B</math> is an algebraic space. If "algebraic space" is replaced by "scheme", then it is said to be strongly representable.

definition: A resolution of singularities of a scheme X is a proper birational morphism $\pi: Z \to X$ such that Z is smooth.
definition_raw: A [[resolution of singularities]] of a scheme ''X'' is a proper [[birational morphism]] <math>\pi: Z \to X</math> such that ''Z'' is [[smooth scheme|smooth]].

definition: Given a finite separable morphism $\pi:X \to Y$ between smooth projective curves, if $\pi$ is tamely ramified (no wild ramification), for example, over a field of characteristic zero, then the Riemann–Hurwitz formula relates the degree of π, the genera of X, Y and the ramification indices: $2g(X) - 2 = \operatorname{deg}(\pi) (2g(Y) - 2) + \sum_{y \in Y} (e_y - 1)$. Nowadays, the formula is viewed as a consequence of the more general formula (which is valid even if π is not tame): $K_X \sim \pi^*K_Y + R$ where $\sim$ means a linear equivalence and $R = \sum_{P \in X} \operatorname{length}_{\mathcal{O}_P} (\Omega_{X/Y})P$ is the divisor of the relative cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{X/Y}$ (called the different).
definition_raw:
Given a finite separable morphism <math>\pi:X \to Y</math> between smooth projective curves, if <math>\pi</math> is [[tamely ramified]] (no wild ramification), for example, over a field of characteristic zero, then the [[Riemann–Hurwitz formula]] relates the degree of π, the genera of ''X'', ''Y'' and the [[ramification index|ramification indices]]:
<math>2g(X) - 2 = \operatorname{deg}(\pi) (2g(Y) - 2) + \sum_{y \in Y} (e_y - 1)</math>.
Nowadays, the formula is viewed as a consequence of the mor

definition: If L is a line bundle of degree d on a smooth projective curve of genus g, then the Riemann–Roch formula computes the Euler characteristic of L: $\chi(L) = d - g + 1$. For example, the formula implies the degree of the canonical divisor K is 2g - 2.
definition_raw:
If ''L'' is a line bundle of degree ''d'' on a smooth projective curve of genus ''g'', then the [[Riemann–Roch formula]] computes the [[Euler characteristic]] of ''L'':
<math>\chi(L) = d - g + 1</math>.
For example, the formula implies the degree of the canonical divisor ''K'' is 2''g'' - 2.

definition: Every infinitesimal deformation is trivial. For example, the projective space is rigid since $\operatorname{H}^1(\mathbf{P}^n, T_{\mathbf{P}^n}) = 0$ (and using the Kodaira–Spencer map).
definition_raw: Every [[infinitesimal deformation]] is trivial. For example, the [[projective space]] is rigid since <math>\operatorname{H}^1(\mathbf{P}^n, T_{\mathbf{P}^n}) = 0</math> (and using the [[Kodaira–Spencer map]]).

definition: A heuristic term, roughly equivalent to "killing automorphisms". For example, one might say "we introduce level structures resp. marked points to rigidify the geometric situation."

definition_wikilinks.target: Moduli of algebraic curves#Moduli of marked curves

definition_raw: A heuristic term, roughly equivalent to "killing automorphisms". For example, one might say "we introduce [[Level structure (algebraic geometry)|level structure]]s resp. [[Moduli of algebraic curves#Moduli of marked curves|marked points]] to rigidify the geometric situation."

definition: A scheme is a locally ringed space that is locally a prime spectrum of a commutative ring.
definition_raw: A [[scheme (mathematics)|scheme]] is a [[locally ringed space]] that is locally a [[prime spectrum]] of a [[commutative ring]].

definition: A Schubert cell is a B-orbit on the Grassmannian $\operatorname{Gr}(d, n)$ where B is the standard Borel; i.e., the group of upper triangular matrices.
definition_raw: A [[Schubert cell]] is a ''B''-orbit on the Grassmannian <math>\operatorname{Gr}(d, n)</math> where ''B'' is the standard Borel; i.e., the group of upper triangular matrices.

definition: A rational normal scroll is a ruled surface which is of degree $n$ in a projective space $\mathbb{P}^{n+1}$ for some $n\in\mathbb{N}_{>1}$.

definition_wikilinks.target: Ruled surface#Ruled surfaces in algebraic geometry

definition_raw: A [[rational normal scroll]] is a [[Ruled surface#Ruled surfaces in algebraic geometry|ruled surface]] which is of [[Degree of an algebraic variety|degree]] <math>n</math> in a [[Projective space#Algebraic geometry|projective space]] <math>\mathbb{P}^{n+1}</math> for some <math>n\in\mathbb{N}_{>1}</math>.

definition: The secant variety to a projective variety $V \subset \mathbb{P}^r$ is the closure of the union of all secant lines to V in $\mathbb{P}^r$.
definition_raw: The [[secant variety]] to a projective variety <math>V \subset \mathbb{P}^r</math> is the closure of the union of all secant lines to ''V'' in <math>\mathbb{P}^r</math>.

definition: The section ring or the ring of sections of a line bundle L on a scheme X is the graded ring $\oplus_0^\infty \Gamma(X, L^n)$.
definition_raw: The [[section ring]] or the ring of sections of a line bundle ''L'' on a scheme ''X'' is the graded ring <math>\oplus_0^\infty \Gamma(X, L^n)</math>.

definition: See #dualizing sheaf
definition_raw: See [[#dualizing sheaf]]

definition: A separated morphism is a morphism $ f $ such that the fiber product of $ f $ with itself along $ f $ has its diagonal as a closed subscheme — in other words, the diagonal morphism is a closed immersion.
definition_raw: A [[separated morphism]] is a morphism <math> f </math> such that the [[fiber product]] of <math> f </math> with itself along <math> f </math> has its [[diagonal]] as a closed subscheme — in other words, the [[diagonal morphism (algebraic geometry)|diagonal morphism]] is a ''[[closed immersion]]''.

definition: A sheaf with a set of global sections that span the stalk of the sheaf at every point. See Sheaf generated by global sections.
definition_raw: A sheaf with a set of global sections that span the stalk of the sheaf at every point. See [[Sheaf generated by global sections]].

definition: The term "simple point" is an old term for a "smooth point".
definition_raw: The term "simple point" is an old term for a "smooth point".

definition: The higher-dimensional analog of étale morphisms are smooth morphisms. There are many different characterisations of smoothness. The following are equivalent definitions of smoothness of the morphism : # for any y ∈ Y, there are open affine neighborhoods V and U of y, x=f(y), respectively, such that the restriction of f to V factors as an étale morphism followed by the projection of affine n-space over U. # f is flat, locally of finite presentation, and for every geometric point $\bar{y}$ of Y (a morphism from the spectrum of an algebraically closed field $k(\bar{y})$ to Y), the geometric fiber <math>X_{\bar{y
A divisor D on a smooth curve C is special if $h^0(\mathcal{O}(K - D))$, which is called the index of speciality, is positive.
definition_raw: A divisor ''D'' on a smooth curve ''C'' is [[special divsor|special]] if <math>h^0(\mathcal{O}(K - D))</math>, which is called the index of speciality, is positive.

definition: A spherical variety is a normal G-variety (G connected reductive) with an open dense orbit by a Borel subgroup of G.
definition_raw: A [[spherical variety]] is a normal ''G''-variety (''G'' connected reductive) with an open dense orbit by a Borel subgroup of ''G''.

definition: In the context of an algebraic group $G$ for certain properties $P$ there is the derived property split-$P$. Usually $P$ is a property that is automatic or more common over algebraically closed fields $\overline{k}$. If this property holds already for $G$ defined over a not necessarily algebraically closed field $k$ then $G$ is said to satisfy split-$P$.
definition_raw: In the context of an [[algebraic group]] <math>G</math> for certain properties <math>P</math> there is the derived property split-<math>P</math>. Usually <math>P</math> is a property that is automatic or more common over algebraically closed fields <math>\overline{k}</math>. If this property holds already for <math>G</math> defined over a not necessarily algebraically closed field <math>k</math> then <math>G</math> is said to satisfy split-<math>P</math>.

definition: A stable curve is a curve with some "mild" singularity, used to construct a good-behaving moduli space of curves.
definition_raw: A [[stable curve]] is a curve with some "mild" singularity, used to construct a good-behaving [[moduli space of curves]].

definition: A stack parametrizes sets of points together with automorphisms.
definition_raw: A [[stack (mathematics)|stack]] parametrizes sets of points together with automorphisms.

definition: Given a blow-up $\pi: \widetilde{X} \to X$ along a closed subscheme Z and a morphism $f: Y \to X$, the strict transform of Y (also called proper transform) is the blow-up $\widetilde{Y} \to Y$ of Y along the closed subscheme $f^{-1} Z$. If f is a closed immersion, then the induced map $\widetilde{Y} \hookrightarrow \widetilde{X}$ is also a closed immersion.
definition_raw: Given a blow-up <math>\pi: \widetilde{X} \to X</math> along a closed subscheme ''Z'' and a morphism <math>f: Y \to X</math>, the [[strict transform]] of ''Y'' (also called proper transform) is the [[Blowing up|blow-up]] <math>\widetilde{Y} \to Y</math> of ''Y'' along the closed subscheme <math>f^{-1} Z</math>. If ''f'' is a closed immersion, then the induced map <math>\widetilde{Y} \hookrightarrow \widetilde{X}</math> is also a closed immersion.

definition: A subscheme, without qualifier, of X is a closed subscheme of an open subscheme of X.
definition_raw: A '''subscheme''', without qualifier, of ''X'' is a closed subscheme of an open subscheme of ''X''.

definition: An algebraic variety of dimension two.
definition_raw: An algebraic variety of dimension two.

definition: An analog of a symmetric space. See symmetric variety.
definition_raw: An analog of a [[symmetric space]]. See [[symmetric variety]].

definition: See Zariski tangent space.
definition_raw: See [[Zariski tangent space]].

definition: The tautological line bundle of a projective scheme X is the dual of Serre's twisting sheaf $\mathcal{O}_X(1)$; that is, $\mathcal{O}_X(-1)$.
definition_raw: The [[tautological line bundle]] of a projective scheme ''X'' is the dual of [[Serre's twisting sheaf]] <math>\mathcal{O}_X(1)</math>; that is, <math>\mathcal{O}_X(-1)</math>.

definition: See Zariski's main theorem, theorem on formal functions, cohomology base change theorem, :Category:Theorems in algebraic geometry.
definition_raw: See [[Zariski's main theorem]], [[theorem on formal functions]], [[cohomology base change theorem]], [[:Category:Theorems in algebraic geometry]].

definition: An old term for a toric variety
definition_raw: An old term for a [[toric variety]]

definition: A toric variety is a normal variety with the action of a torus such that the torus has an open dense orbit.
definition_raw: A [[toric variety]] is a normal variety with the action of a torus such that the torus has an open dense orbit.

definition: A kind of a piecewise-linear algebraic geometry. See tropical geometry.
definition_raw: A kind of a piecewise-linear algebraic geometry. See [[tropical geometry]].

definition: A split torus is a product of finitely many multiplicative groups $\mathbb{G}_m$.
definition_raw: A [[algebraic torus|split torus]] is a product of finitely many [[multiplicative group]]s <math>\mathbb{G}_m</math>.

definition: If a moduli functor F is represented by some scheme or algebraic space M, then a universal object is an element of F(M) that corresponds to the identity morphism M → M (which is an M-point of M). If the values of F are isomorphism classes of curves with extra structure, say, then a universal object is called a universal curve. A tautological bundle would be another example of a universal object.
definition_raw: If a [[moduli functor]] ''F'' is represented by some scheme or algebraic space ''M'', then a [[universal object]] is an element of ''F''(''M'') that corresponds to the identity morphism ''M'' → ''M'' (which is an ''M''-point of ''M''). If the values of ''F'' are isomorphism classes of curves with extra structure, say, then a universal object is called a [[Moduli of curves|universal curve]]. A [[tautological bundle]] would be another example of a universal object.

definition: A morphism has some property universally if all base changes of the morphism have this property. Examples include universally catenary, universally injective.
definition_raw: A morphism has some property universally if all base changes of the morphism have this property. Examples include [[universally catenary]], [[universally injective]].

definition: For a point $ y $ in $ Y $, consider the corresponding morphism of local rings $f^\# \colon \mathcal{O}_{X, f(y)} \to \mathcal{O}_{Y, y}$. Let $ \mathfrak{m} $ be the maximal ideal of $ \mathcal{O}_{X,f(y)} $, and let $\mathfrak{n} = f^\#(\mathfrak{m}) \mathcal{O}_{Y,y}$ be the ideal generated by the image of $ \mathfrak{m} $ in $\mathcal{O}_{Y,y} $. The morphism $ f $ is unramified (resp. G-unramified) if it is locally of finite type (resp. locally of finite presentation) and if for all $ y $ in $ Y $, $ \mathfrak{n} $ is the maximal ideal of $ \mathcal{O}_{Y,y} $ and the induced map $\mathcal{O}_{X,f(y)}/\mathfrak{m} \to \mathcal{O}_{Y,y}/\mathfrak{n} $ is a finite separable field extension. This is the geometric version (and generalization) of an unramified field extension in algebraic number theory.

definition: a scheme is weakly normal if any finite birational morphism to it is an isomorphism.
definition_raw: a scheme is weakly normal if any finite birational morphism to it is an isomorphism.

definition: Given chain maps $f, g: (C, d_C) \to (D, d_D)$ between chain complexes of modules, a chain homotopy s from f to g is a sequence of module homomorphisms $s_i: C_i \to D_{i+1}$ satisfying $f_i - g_i = d_D \circ s_i + s_{i-1} \circ d_C$. It is also called a homotopy operator.
definition_raw: Given chain maps <math>f, g: (C, d_C) \to (D, d_D)</math> between chain complexes of modules, a [[chain homotopy]] ''s'' from ''f'' to ''g'' is a sequence of module homomorphisms <math>s_i: C_i \to D_{i+1}</math> satisfying <math>f_i - g_i = d_D \circ s_i + s_{i-1} \circ d_C</math>. It is also called a [[homotopy operator]].

definition: A chain map $f: (C, d_C) \to (D, d_D)$ between chain complexes of modules is a sequence of module homomorphisms $f_i: C_i \to D_i$ that commutes with the differentials; i.e., $d_D \circ f_i = f_{i-1} \circ d_C$.
definition_raw: A chain map <math>f: (C, d_C) \to (D, d_D)</math> between chain complexes of modules is a sequence of module homomorphisms <math>f_i: C_i \to D_i</math> that commutes with the differentials; i.e., <math>d_D \circ f_i = f_{i-1} \circ d_C</math>.

definition: A chain map that is an isomorphism up to chain homotopy; that is, if ƒ:C→D is a chain map, then it is a chain homotopy equivalence if there is a chain map g:D→C such that gƒ and ƒg are chain homotopic to the identity homomorphisms on C and D, respectively.
definition_raw: A chain map that is an isomorphism up to chain homotopy; that is, if ''ƒ'':''C''→''D'' is a chain map, then it is a chain homotopy equivalence if there is a chain map ''g'':''D''→''C'' such that ''g''ƒ and ƒ''g'' are chain homotopic to the identity homomorphisms on ''C'' and ''D'', respectively.

definition: The change of fiber of a fibration p is a homotopy equivalence, up to homotopy, between the fibers of p induced by a path in the base.
definition_raw: The [[change of fiber]] of a fibration ''p'' is a homotopy equivalence, up to homotopy, between the fibers of ''p'' induced by a path in the base.

definition: The character variety of a group π and an algebraic group G (e.g., a reductive complex Lie group) is the geometric invariant theory quotient by G: :$\mathcal{X}(\pi, G) = \operatorname{Hom}(\pi, G)/ \! /G$.

definition: Let Vect(X) be the set of isomorphism classes of vector bundles on X. We can view $X \mapsto \operatorname{Vect}(X)$ as a contravariant functor from Top to Set by sending a map ƒ:X → Y to the pullback ƒ* along it. Then a characteristic class is a natural transformation from Vect to the cohomology functor H*. Explicitly, to each vector bundle E we assign a cohomology class, say, c(E). The assignment is natural in the sense that ƒ*c(E) = c(ƒ*E).

definition: A map $i: A \to B$ is a cofibration if it satisfies the property: given $h_0: B \to X$ and homotopy $g_t: A \to X$ such that $g_0=h_0\circ i$, there is a homotopy $h_t: B \to X$ such that $h_t \circ i = g_t$. A cofibration is injective and is a homeomorphism onto its image.

definition: A subspace $A \subset X$ is called a deformation retract of X if there is a homotopy $h_t : X \to X$ such that $h_0$ is the identity, $h_1(X) \subset A$ and ${h_1}|_A$ is the identity (i.e., $h_1$ is a retract of $A \hookrightarrow X$ in the sense in category theory). It is called a strong deformation retract if, in addition, $h_t$ satisfies the requirement that ${h_t}|_A$ is the identity.
