definition: Another term for multicategory, a generalized category where a morphism can have several domains. The notion of "colored operad" is more primitive than that of operad: in fact, an operad can be defined as a colored operad with a single object.
definition_raw: Another term for [[multicategory]], a generalized category where a morphism can have several domains. The notion of "colored operad" is more primitive than that of operad: in fact, an operad can be defined as a colored operad with a single object.

definition: Given functors $f: C \to B, g: D \to B$, the comma category $(f \downarrow g)$ is a category where (1) the objects are morphisms $f(c) \to g(d)$ and (2) a morphism from $\alpha: f(c) \to g(d)$ to $\beta: f(c') \to g(d')$ consists of $c \to c'$ and $d \to d'$ such that $f(c) \to f(c') \overset{\beta}\to g(d')$ is $f(c) \overset{\alpha}\to g(d) \to g(d').$ For example, if f is the identity functor and g is the constant functor with a value b, then it is the slice category of B over an object b.
definition_raw: Given functors <math>f: C \to B, g: D \to B</math>, the [[comma category]] <math>(f \downarrow g)</math> is a category where (1) the objects are morphisms <math>f(c) \to g(d)</math> and (2) a morphism from <math>\alpha: f(c) \to g(d)</math> to <math>\beta: f(c') \to g(d')</math> consists of <math>c \to c'</math> and <math>d \to d'</math> such that <math>f(c) \to f(c') \overset{\beta}\to g(d')</math> is <math>f(c) \overset{\alpha}\to g(d) \to g(d').</math> For example, if ''f'' is the identity fu

definition: A comonad in a category X is a comonoid in the monoidal category of endofunctors of X.
definition_raw: A [[comonad]] in a category ''X'' is a [[comonoid]] in the monoidal category of endofunctors of ''X''.

definition: Probably synonymous with #accessible.
definition_raw: Probably synonymous with [[#accessible]].

definition: A category is complete if all small limits exist.
definition_raw: A category is [[complete category|complete]] if all small limits exist.

definition: Deligne's completeness theorem; see .
definition_raw: [[Deligne's completeness theorem]]; see [https://www.math.ias.edu/~lurie/278xnotes/Lecture17X-Filtered.pdf].

definition: A composition of morphisms in a category is part of the datum defining the category.
definition_raw: A composition of morphisms in a category is part of the datum defining the category.

definition: computad.
definition_raw: [[computad]].

definition: A concrete category C is a category such that there is a faithful functor from C to Set; e.g., Vec, Grp and Top.
definition_raw: A [[concrete category]] ''C'' is a category such that there is a faithful functor from ''C'' to '''[[Category of sets|Set]]'''; e.g., '''[[category of vector spaces|Vec]]''', '''[[category of groups|Grp]]''' and '''[[category of topological spaces|Top]]'''.

definition: A cone is a way to express the universal property of a colimit (or dually a limit). One can show that the colimit $\varinjlim$ is the left adjoint to the diagonal functor $\Delta: C \to \operatorname{Fct}(I, C)$, which sends an object X to the constant functor with value X; that is, for any X and any functor $f: I \to C$, :$\operatorname{Hom}(\varinjlim f, X) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(f, \Delta_X),$ provided the colimit in question exists. The right-hand side is then the set of cones with vertex X.
definition_raw:
A [[cone (category theory)|cone]] is a way to express the [[universal property]] of a colimit (or dually a limit). One can show<ref>{{harvnb|Kashiwara|Schapira|2006|loc=Ch. 2, Exercise 2.8.}}</ref> that the colimit <math>\varinjlim</math> is the left adjoint to the diagonal functor <math>\Delta: C \to \operatorname{Fct}(I, C)</math>, which sends an object ''X'' to the constant functor with value ''X''; that is, for any ''X'' and any functor <math>f: I \to C</math>,
:<math>\operatorname{Hom}(\var

definition: A category is connected if, for each pair of objects x, y, there exists a finite sequence of objects zi such that $z_0 = x, z_n = y$ and either $\operatorname{Hom}(z_i, z_{i+1})$ or $\operatorname{Hom}(z_{i+1}, z_i)$ is nonempty for any i.
definition_raw: A category is [[connected category|connected]] if, for each pair of objects ''x'', ''y'', there exists a finite sequence of objects ''z''<sub>''i''</sub> such that <math>z_0 = x, z_n = y</math> and either <math>\operatorname{Hom}(z_i, z_{i+1})</math> or <math>\operatorname{Hom}(z_{i+1}, z_i)</math> is nonempty for any ''i''.

definition: A conservative functor is a functor that reflects isomorphisms. Many forgetful functors are conservative, but the forgetful functor from Top to Set is not conservative.
definition_raw: A [[conservative functor]] is a functor that reflects isomorphisms. Many forgetful functors are conservative, but the forgetful functor from '''Top''' to '''Set''' is not conservative.

definition: A functor is constant if it maps every object in a category to the same object A and every morphism to the identity on A. Put in another way, a functor $f: C \to D$ is constant if it factors as: $C \to \{ A \} \overset{i}\to D$ for some object A in D, where i is the inclusion of the discrete category { A }.
definition_raw: A functor is [[constant functor|constant]] if it maps every object in a category to the same object ''A'' and every morphism to the identity on ''A''. Put in another way, a functor <math>f: C \to D</math> is constant if it factors as: <math>C \to \{ A \} \overset{i}\to D</math> for some object ''A'' in ''D'', where ''i'' is the inclusion of the discrete category { ''A'' }.

definition: A contravariant functor F from a category C to a category D is a (covariant) functor from Cop to D. It is sometimes also called a presheaf especially when D is Set or the variants. For example, for each set S, let $\mathfrak{P}(S)$ be the power set of S and for each function $f: S \to T$, define :$\mathfrak{P}(f): \mathfrak{P}(T) \to \mathfrak{P}(S)$ by sending a subset A of T to the pre-image $f^{-1}(A)$. With this, $\mathfrak{P}: \mathbf{Set} \to \mathbf{Set}$ is a contravariant functor.
definition_raw:
A [[contravariant functor]] ''F'' from a category ''C'' to a category ''D'' is a (covariant) functor from ''C''<sup>op</sup> to ''D''. It is sometimes also called a [[presheaf (category theory)|presheaf]] especially when ''D'' is '''Set''' or the variants. For example, for each set ''S'', let <math>\mathfrak{P}(S)</math> be the power set of ''S'' and for each function <math>f: S \to T</math>, define
:<math>\mathfrak{P}(f): \mathfrak{P}(T) \to \mathfrak{P}(S)</math>
by sending a subset ''A'' of '

definition: The coproduct of a family of objects Xi in a category C indexed by a set I is the inductive limit $\varinjlim$ of the functor $I \to C, \, i \mapsto X_i$, where I is viewed as a discrete category. It is the dual of the product of the family. For example, a coproduct in Grp is a free product.
definition_raw: The [[coproduct]] of a family of objects ''X''<sub>''i''</sub> in a category ''C'' indexed by a set ''I'' is the inductive limit <math>\varinjlim</math> of the functor <math>I \to C, \, i \mapsto X_i</math>, where ''I'' is viewed as a discrete category. It is the dual of the product of the family. For example, a coproduct in '''[[category of groups|Grp]]''' is a [[free product]].

definition: The core of a category is the maximal groupoid contained in the category.
definition_raw: The [[core (category theory)|core]] of a category is the maximal groupoid contained in the category.

definition: A cubical set is an alternative for a simplicial set; a simplex is replaced by a cube.
definition_raw: A [[cubical set]] is an alternative for a simplicial set; a simplex is replaced by a cube.

definition: Given a group or monoid M, the Day convolution is the tensor product in $\mathbf{Fct}(M, \mathbf{Set})$.
definition_raw: Given a group or monoid ''M'', the [[Day convolution]] is the tensor product in <math>\mathbf{Fct}(M, \mathbf{Set})</math>.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Day+convolution|title = Day convolution in nLab}}</ref>

definition: Dendroidal set.
definition_raw: [[Dendroidal set]].

definition: The density theorem states that every presheaf (a set-valued contravariant functor) is a colimit of representable presheaves. Yoneda's lemma embeds a category C into the category of presheaves on C. The density theorem then says the image is "dense", so to say. The name "density" is because of the analogy with the Jacobson density theorem (or other variants) in abstract algebra.
definition_raw: The [[density theorem (category theory)|density theorem]] states that every presheaf (a set-valued contravariant functor) is a colimit of representable presheaves. Yoneda's lemma embeds a category ''C'' into the category of presheaves on ''C''. The density theorem then says the image is "dense", so to say. The name "density" is because of the analogy with the [[Jacobson density theorem]] (or other variants) in abstract algebra.

definition: Given categories I, C, the diagonal functor is the functor :$\Delta: C \to \mathbf{Fct}(I, C), \, A \mapsto \Delta_A$ that sends each object A to the constant functor with value A and each morphism $f: A \to B$ to the natural transformation $\Delta_{f, i}: \Delta_A(i) = A \to \Delta_B(i) =B$ that is f at each i.
definition_raw:
Given categories ''I'', ''C'', the [[diagonal functor]] is the functor
:<math>\Delta: C \to \mathbf{Fct}(I, C), \, A \mapsto \Delta_A</math>
that sends each object ''A'' to the constant functor with value ''A'' and each morphism <math>f: A \to B</math> to the natural transformation <math>\Delta_{f, i}: \Delta_A(i) = A \to \Delta_B(i) =B</math> that is ''f'' at each ''i''.

definition: Given a category C, a diagram in C is a functor $f: I \to C$ from a category I. For example, if $I = \mathbb{N}$ with no morphisms other than the identities, a diagram simply amounts to a sequence of objects. For a general I, typically there is a morphism between the images of objects in I under f (whence the term diagram).
definition_raw: Given a category ''C'', a [[diagram (category theory)|diagram]] in ''C'' is a functor <math>f: I \to C</math> from a category ''I''. For example, if <math>I = \mathbb{N}</math> with no morphisms other than  the identities, a diagram simply amounts to a sequence of objects. For a general ''I'', typically there is a morphism between the images of objects in ''I'' under ''f'' (whence the term diagram).

definition: A diagrammatic set is an alternative to a simplicial set or a cubical set.
definition_raw: A [[diagrammatic set]] is an alternative to a simplicial set or a cubical set.

definition: A differential graded category is a category whose Hom sets are equipped with structures of differential graded modules. In particular, if the category has only one object, it is the same as a differential graded module.
definition_raw: A [[differential graded category]] is a category whose Hom sets are equipped with structures of [[differential graded module]]s. In particular, if the category has only one object, it is the same as a differential graded module.

definition: A direct limit is the colimit of a direct system.
definition_raw: A [[direct limit]] is the [[colimit]] of a [[direct system]].

definition: A category is discrete if each morphism is an identity morphism (of some object). For example, a set can be viewed as a discrete category.
definition_raw: A category is [[discrete category|discrete]] if each morphism is an identity morphism (of some object). For example, a set can be viewed as a discrete category.

definition: Another term for "profunctor".
definition_raw: Another term for "profunctor".

definition: double category.
definition_raw: [[double category]].

definition: A Dwyer–Kan equivalence is a generalization of an equivalence of categories to the simplicial context.
definition_raw: A [[Dwyer–Kan equivalence]] is a generalization of an equivalence of categories to the simplicial context.<ref>{{cite arXiv|last=Hinich|first=V.|date=2013-11-17|title=Dwyer-Kan localization revisited|eprint=1311.4128|class=math.QA}}</ref>

term: Elementary Theory of the Category of Sets
definition: The Elementary Theory of the Category of Sets. The link is a redirect; for now, see https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/ETCS.

definition_wikilinks.target: Elementary Theory of the Category of Sets
definition_wikilinks.display: Elementary Theory of the Category of Sets

definition_raw: The [[Elementary Theory of the Category of Sets]]. The link is a redirect; for now, see https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/ETCS.

definition: Another name for the category of algebras for a given monad.
definition_raw: Another name for the category of [[algebra for a monad|algebras for a given monad]].

definition: Eilenberg–Zilber category.
definition_raw: [[Eilenberg–Zilber category]].

definition: The empty category is a category with no object. It is the same thing as the empty set when the empty set is viewed as a discrete category.
definition_raw: The [[empty category (category theory)|empty category]] is a category with no object. It is the same thing as the [[empty set]] when the empty set is viewed as a discrete category.

definition: The end of a functor <math>F: C^{\text{op
definition_raw: The [[end (category theory)|end]] of a functor <math>F: C^{\text{op

definition: A functor between the same category.
definition_raw: A functor between the same category.

definition: Given a monoidal category (C, ⊗, 1), a category enriched over C is, informally, a category whose Hom sets are in C. More precisely, a category D enriched over C is a data consisting of # A class of objects, # For each pair of objects X, Y in D, an object $\operatorname{Map}_D(X, Y)$ in C, called the mapping object from X to Y, # For each triple of objects X, Y, Z in D, a morphism in C, #:$\circ: \operatorname{Map}_D(Y, Z) \otimes \operatorname{Map}_D(X, Y) \to \operatorname{Map}_D(X, Z)$, #:called the composition, #For each object X in D, a morphism $1_X: 1 \to \operatorname{Map}_D(X, X)$ in C, called the unit morphism of X subject to the conditions that (roughly) the compositions are associative and the unit morphisms act as the multiplicative identity. For example, a category enriched over sets is an ordinary category.
definition_raw:
Given a monoidal category (''C'', ⊗, 1), a [[category enriched]] over ''C'' is, informally, a category whose Hom sets are in ''C''. More precisely, a category ''D'' enriched over ''C'' is a data consisting of 
# A class of objects,
# For each pair of objects ''X'', ''Y'' in ''D'', an object <math>\operatorname{Map}_D(X, Y)</math> in ''C'', called the [[mapping object]] from ''X'' to ''Y'',
# For each triple of objects ''X'', ''Y'', ''Z'' in ''D'', a morphism in ''C'',
#:<math>\circ: \operatornam

definition: A morphism f is an epimorphism if $g=h$ whenever $g\circ f=h\circ f$. In other words, f is the dual of a monomorphism.
definition_raw: A morphism ''f'' is an [[epimorphism]] if <math>g=h</math> whenever <math>g\circ f=h\circ f</math>. In other words, ''f'' is the dual of a monomorphism.

definition: The equalizer of a pair of morphisms $f, g: A \to B$ is the limit of the pair. It is the dual of a coequalizer.
definition_raw: The [[equalizer (mathematics)|equalizer]] of a pair of morphisms <math>f, g: A \to B</math> is the limit of the pair. It is the dual of a coequalizer.

definition: A functor is an equivalence if it is faithful, full and essentially surjective.
definition_raw: A functor is an [[equivalence of categories|equivalence]] if it is faithful, full and essentially surjective.

definition: A category is equivalent to another category if there is an equivalence between them.
definition_raw: A category is equivalent to another category if there is an [[equivalence of categories|equivalence]] between them.

definition: A functor F is called essentially surjective (or isomorphism-dense) if for every object B there exists an object A such that F(A) is isomorphic to B.
definition_raw: A functor ''F'' is called [[essentially surjective]] (or isomorphism-dense) if for every object ''B'' there exists an object ''A'' such that ''F''(''A'') is isomorphic to ''B''.

definition: Given categories C, D and an object A in C, the evaluation at A is the functor :$\mathbf{Fct}(C, D) \to D, \,\, F \mapsto F(A).$ For example, the Eilenberg–Steenrod axioms give an instance when the functor is an equivalence.
definition_raw:
Given categories ''C'', ''D'' and an object ''A'' in ''C'', the [[evaluation (category theory)|evaluation]] at ''A'' is the functor
:<math>\mathbf{Fct}(C, D) \to D, \,\, F \mapsto F(A).</math>
For example, the [[Eilenberg–Steenrod axioms]] give an instance when the functor is an equivalence.

definition: An exact sequence is typically a sequence (from arbitrary negative integers to arbitrary positive integers) of maps ::$\cdots \to E_1 \overset{f_1}\to E_2 \overset{f_2}\to E_3 \to \cdots$ :such that the image of $f_i$ is the kernel of $f_{i+1}$. The notion can be generalized in various ways.
definition_raw:
An [[exact sequence]] is typically a sequence (from arbitrary negative integers to arbitrary positive integers) of maps
::<math>\cdots \to E_1 \overset{f_1}\to E_2 \overset{f_2}\to E_3 \to \cdots</math>
:such that the image of <math>f_i</math> is the kernel of <math>f_{i+1}</math>. The notion can be generalized in various ways.

definition: exit-path category
definition_raw: [[exit-path category]]

definition: A functor is faithful if it is injective when restricted to each hom-set.
definition_raw: A functor is [[faithful functor|faithful]] if it is injective when restricted to each [[hom-set]].

definition: The fundamental category functor $\tau_1: s\mathbf{Set} \to \mathbf{Cat}$ is the left adjoint to the nerve functor N. For every category C, $\tau_1 NC = C$.
definition_raw: The [[fundamental category functor]] <math>\tau_1: s\mathbf{Set} \to \mathbf{Cat}</math> is the left adjoint to the nerve functor ''N''. For every category ''C'', <math>\tau_1 NC = C</math>.

definition: The fundamental groupoid $\Pi_1 X$ of a Kan complex X is the category where an object is a 0-simplex (vertex) $\Delta^0 \to X$, a morphism is a homotopy class of a 1-simplex (path) $\Delta^1 \to X$ and a composition is determined by the Kan property.
definition_raw: The [[fundamental groupoid]] <math>\Pi_1 X</math> of a Kan complex ''X'' is the category where an object is a 0-simplex (vertex) <math>\Delta^0 \to X</math>, a morphism is a homotopy class of a 1-simplex (path) <math>\Delta^1 \to X</math> and a composition is determined by the Kan property.

definition: A functor π: C → D is said to exhibit C as a category fibered over D if, for each morphism g: x → π(y) in D, there exists a π-cartesian morphism f: x → y in C such that π(f) = g. If D is the category of affine schemes (say of finite type over some field), then π is more commonly called a prestack. Note: π is often a forgetful functor and in fact the Grothendieck construction implies that every fibered category can be taken to be that form (up to equivalences in a suitable sense).
definition_raw: A functor π: ''C'' → ''D'' is said to exhibit ''C'' as a [[fibered category|category fibered over]] ''D'' if, for each morphism ''g'': ''x'' → π(''y'') in ''D'', there exists a π-cartesian morphism ''f'': ''x<nowiki>'</nowiki>'' → ''y'' in ''C'' such that π(''f'') = ''g''. If ''D'' is the category of affine schemes (say of finite type over some field), then π is more commonly called a [[prestack]]. '''Note''': π is often a forgetful functor and in fact the [[Grothendieck construction]] implies t

definition: Given a category C and a set I, the fiber product over an object S of a family of objects Xi in C indexed by I is the product of the family in the slice category $C_{/S}$ of C over S (provided there are $X_i \to S$). The fiber product of two objects X and Y over an object S is denoted by $X \times_S Y$ and is also called a Cartesian square.
definition_raw: Given a category ''C'' and a set ''I'', the [[fiber product]] over an object ''S'' of a family of objects ''X''<sub>''i''</sub> in ''C'' indexed by ''I'' is the product of the family in the [[slice category]] <math>C_{/S}</math> of ''C'' over ''S'' (provided there are <math>X_i \to S</math>). The fiber product of two objects ''X'' and ''Y'' over an object ''S'' is denoted by <math>X \times_S Y</math> and is also called a [[Cartesian square]].

definition: An object is fibrant if the unique morphism from it to the final object is a fibration, when there is a notion of a fibration.
definition_raw: An object is [[fibrant object|fibrant]] if the unique morphism from it to the final object is a fibration, when there is a notion of a fibration.

definition: A filtered category (also called a filtrant category) is a nonempty category with the properties (1) given objects i and j, there are an object k and morphisms i → k and j → k and (2) given morphisms u, v: i → j, there are an object k and a morphism w: j → k such that w ∘ u = w ∘ v. A category I is filtered if and only if, for each finite category J and functor f: J → I, the set $\varprojlim \operatorname{Hom}(f(j), i)$ is nonempty for some object i in I.
definition_raw: A [[filtered category]] (also called a filtrant category) is a nonempty category with the properties (1) given objects ''i'' and ''j'', there are an object ''k'' and morphisms ''i'' → ''k'' and ''j'' → ''k'' and (2) given morphisms ''u'', ''v'': ''i'' → ''j'', there are an object ''k'' and a morphism ''w'': ''j'' → ''k'' such that ''w'' ∘ ''u'' = ''w'' ∘ ''v''. A category ''I'' is filtered if and only if, for each finite category ''J'' and functor ''f'': ''J'' → ''I'', the set <math>\varprojlim

definition: Synonymous with terminal
definition_raw: Synonymous with terminal

definition: A finitary monad or an algebraic monad is a monad on Set whose underlying endofunctor commutes with filtered colimits.
definition_raw: A [[finitary monad]] or an algebraic monad is a monad on '''Set''' whose underlying endofunctor commutes with filtered colimits.

definition: A category is finite if it has only finitely many morphisms.
definition_raw: A category is finite if it has only finitely many morphisms.

definition: The forgetful functor is, roughly, a functor that loses some of data of the objects; for example, the functor $\mathbf{Grp} \to \mathbf{Set}$ that sends a group to its underlying set and a group homomorphism to itself is a forgetful functor.
definition_raw: The [[forgetful functor]] is, roughly, a functor that loses some of data of the objects; for example, the functor <math>\mathbf{Grp} \to \mathbf{Set}</math> that sends a group to its underlying set and a group homomorphism to itself is a forgetful functor.

definition: free category
definition_raw: [[free category]]

definition: free completion, free cocompletion.
definition_raw: [[free completion]], [[free cocompletion]].

definition: A free functor is a left adjoint to a forgetful functor. For example, for a ring R, the functor that sends a set X to the free R-module generated by X is a free functor (whence the name).
definition_raw: A [[free functor]] is a left adjoint to a forgetful functor. For example, for a ring ''R'', the functor that sends a set ''X'' to the [[free module|free ''R''-module]] generated by ''X'' is a free functor (whence the name).

definition: A Frobenius category is an exact category that has enough injectives and enough projectives and such that the class of injective objects coincides with that of projective objects.
definition_raw: A [[Frobenius category]] is an [[exact category]] that has enough injectives and enough projectives and such that the class of injective objects coincides with that of projective objects.

definition: See Fukaya category.
definition_raw: See [[Fukaya category]].

definition: A functor is full if it is surjective when restricted to each hom-set.
definition_raw: A functor is [[full functor|full]] if it is surjective when restricted to each [[hom-set]].

definition: Given categories C, D, a functor F from C to D is a structure-preserving map from C to D; i.e., it consists of an object F(x) in D for each object x in C and a morphism F(f) in D for each morphism f in C satisfying the conditions: (1) $F(f \circ g) = F(f) \circ F(g)$ whenever $f \circ g$ is defined and (2) $F(\operatorname{id}_x) = \operatorname{id}_{F(x)}$. For example, :$\mathfrak{P}: \mathbf{Set} \to \mathbf{Set}, \, S \mapsto \mathfrak{P}(S)$, where $\mathfrak{P}(S)$ is the power set of S is a functor if we define: for each function $f: S \to T$, $\mathfrak{P}(f): \mathfrak{P}(S) \to \mathfrak{P}(T)$ by $\mathfrak{P}(f)(A) = f(A)$.
definition_raw:
Given categories ''C'', ''D'', a [[functor]] ''F'' from ''C'' to ''D'' is a structure-preserving map from ''C'' to ''D''; i.e., it consists of an object ''F''(''x'') in ''D'' for each object ''x'' in ''C'' and a morphism ''F''(''f'') in ''D'' for each morphism ''f'' in ''C'' satisfying the conditions: (1) <math>F(f  \circ g) = F(f) \circ F(g)</math> whenever <math>f \circ g</math> is defined and (2) <math>F(\operatorname{id}_x) = \operatorname{id}_{F(x)}</math>. For example, 
:<math>\mathfrak{P}

definition: The functor category Fct(C, D) or $D^C$ from a category C to a category D is the category where the objects are all the functors from C to D and the morphisms are all the natural transformations between the functors.
definition_raw: The [[functor category]] '''Fct'''(''C'', ''D'') or <math>D^C</math> from a category ''C'' to a category ''D'' is the category where the objects are all the functors from ''C'' to ''D'' and the morphisms are all the natural transformations between the functors.

definition: The Gabriel–Popescu theorem says an abelian category is a quotient of the category of modules.
definition_raw: The [[Gabriel–Popescu theorem]] says an abelian category is a [[Serre quotient category|quotient]] of the category of modules.

definition: In SGA 1, Exposé V (Definition 5.1.), a category is called a Galois category if it is equivalent to the category of finite G-sets for some profinite group G.
definition_raw: In [[SGA 1]], Exposé V (Definition 5.1.), a category is called a [[Galois category]] if it is equivalent to the category of finite ''G''-sets for some profinite group ''G''.

definition: In a category C, a family of objects $G_i, i \in I$ is a system of generators of C if the functor $X \mapsto \prod_{i \in I} \operatorname{Hom}(G_i, X)$ is conservative. Its dual is called a system of cogenerators.
definition_raw: In a category ''C'', a family of objects <math>G_i, i \in I</math> is a [[generator (category theory)|system of generators]] of ''C'' if the functor <math>X \mapsto \prod_{i \in I} \operatorname{Hom}(G_i, X)</math> is conservative. Its dual is called a system of cogenerators.

definition: generalized metric space.
definition_raw: [[generalized metric space]].

definition: A Gray tensor product is a lax analog of a Cartesian product.
definition_raw: A [[Gray tensor product]] is a lax analog of a Cartesian product.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Gray+tensor+product | title=Gray tensor product in nLab | access-date=2024-07-01 | archive-date=2024-07-01 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240701190301/https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Gray+tensor+product | url-status=live }}</ref>

definition: The notion of a gros topos (of topological spaces) is due to Jean Giraud.
definition_raw: The notion of a [[gros topos]] (of topological spaces) is due to [[Jean Giraud]].

definition: A category-theoretic generalization of Galois theory; see Grothendieck's Galois theory.
definition_raw: A category-theoretic generalization of [[Galois theory]]; see [[Grothendieck's Galois theory]].

definition: A Grothendieck category is a certain well-behaved kind of an abelian category.
definition_raw: A [[Grothendieck category]] is a certain well-behaved kind of an abelian category.

definition: Given a functor $U: C \to \mathbf{Cat}$, let DU be the category where the objects are pairs (x, u) consisting of an object x in C and an object u in the category U(x) and a morphism from (x, u) to (y, v) is a pair consisting of a morphism f: x → y in C and a morphism U(f)(u) → v in U(y). The passage from U to DU is then called the Grothendieck construction.
definition_raw: Given a functor <math>U: C \to \mathbf{Cat}</math>, let ''D''<sub>''U''</sub> be the category where the objects are pairs (''x'', ''u'') consisting of an object ''x'' in ''C'' and an object ''u'' in the category ''U''(''x'') and a morphism from (''x'', ''u'') to (''y'', ''v'') is a pair consisting of a morphism ''f'': ''x'' → ''y'' in ''C'' and a morphism ''U''(''f'')(''u'') → ''v'' in ''U''(''y''). The passage from ''U'' to ''D''<sub>''U''</sub> is then called the [[Grothendieck construction]].

definition: A fibered category.
definition_raw: A [[fibered category]].

definition: A category is called a groupoid if every morphism in it is an isomorphism.
definition_raw: A category is called a [[groupoid]] if every morphism in it is an isomorphism.

definition: See Ringel–Hall algebra.
definition_raw: See [[Ringel–Hall algebra]].

definition: The heart of a t-structure ($D^{\ge 0}$, $D^{\le 0}$) on a triangulated category is the intersection $D^{\ge 0} \cap D^{\le 0}$. It is an abelian category.
definition_raw: The [[heart (category theory)|heart]] of a [[t-structure]] (<math>D^{\ge 0}</math>, <math>D^{\le 0}</math>) on a triangulated category is the intersection <math>D^{\ge 0} \cap D^{\le 0}</math>. It is an abelian category.

definition: Higher category theory is a subfield of category theory that concerns the study of n-categories and ∞-categories.
definition_raw: [[Higher category theory]] is a subfield of category theory that concerns the study of [[n-category|''n''-categories]] and [[∞-categories]].

definition: A higher stack is a generalization of a stack to higher categories.
definition_raw: A [[higher stack]] is a generalization of a stack to [[higher category|higher categories]].

definition: The homological dimension of an abelian category with enough injectives is the least non-negativer integer n such that every object in the category admits an injective resolution of length at most n. The dimension is ∞ if no such integer exists. For example, the homological dimension of ModR with a principal ideal domain R is at most one.
definition_raw: The [[homological dimension]] of an abelian category with enough injectives is the least non-negativer integer ''n'' such that every object in the category admits an injective resolution of length at most ''n''. The dimension is ∞ if no such integer exists. For example, the homological dimension of [[category of modules|Mod<sub>''R''</sub>]] with a principal ideal domain ''R'' is at most one.

definition: See homotopy category. It is closely related to a localization of a category.
definition_raw: See [[homotopy category]]. It is closely related to a [[localization of a category]].

definition: Thomason's homotopy colimit theorem.
definition_raw: [[Thomason's homotopy colimit theorem]].

definition: The homotopy hypothesis states an ∞-groupoid is a space (less equivocally, an n-groupoid can be used as a homotopy n-type.)
definition_raw: The [[homotopy hypothesis]] states an [[∞-groupoid]] is a space (less equivocally, an ''n''-groupoid can be used as a homotopy ''n''-type.)

definition: An endomorphism f is idempotent if $f \circ f = f$.
definition_raw: An endomorphism ''f'' is [[idempotent (category theory)|idempotent]] if <math>f \circ f = f</math>.

definition: The identity morphism f of an object A is a morphism from A to A such that for any morphisms g with domain A and h with codomain A, $g\circ f=g$ and $f\circ h=h$.
definition_raw: The [[identity morphism]] ''f'' of an object ''A'' is a morphism from ''A'' to ''A'' such that for any morphisms ''g'' with domain ''A'' and ''h'' with codomain ''A'', <math>g\circ f=g</math> and <math>f\circ h=h</math>.

definition: The image of a morphism f: X → Y is the equalizer of $Y \rightrightarrows Y \sqcup_X Y$.
definition_raw: The [[image of a morphism]] ''f'': ''X'' → ''Y'' is the equalizer of <math>Y \rightrightarrows Y \sqcup_X Y</math>.

definition: A colimit (or inductive limit) in <math>\mathbf{Fct}(C^{\text{op
definition_raw: A colimit (or inductive limit) in <math>\mathbf{Fct}(C^{\text{op

definition: Another name for colimit.
definition_raw: Another name for [[colimit]].

definition: An ∞-category is obtained from a category by replacing the class/set of objects and morphisms by the spaces of objects and morphisms. Precisely, an ∞-category C is a simplicial set satisfying the following condition: for each 0 < i < n, *every map of simplicial sets $f: \Lambda^n_i \to C$ extends to an n-simplex $f: \Delta^n \to C$ where Δn is the standard n-simplex and $\Lambda^n_i$ is obtained from Δn by removing the i-th face and the interior (see Kan fibration#Definitions). For example, the nerve of a category satisfies the condition and thus can be considered as an ∞-category.

|quote=[T]he theory of ∞-categories is a semantic interpretation of the formal language of category theory. This means that one can systematically make sense of any statement formulated in the language of category theory in the setting of ∞-categories.
|author=[[Denis-Charles Cisinski]]
|source=<ref>{{harvnb|Cisinski|2019|loc=Preface. A couple of perspectives and a tribute.}}</ref>

definition: An (∞, n)-category is obtained from an ∞-category by replacing the space of morphisms by the (∞, n - 1)-category of morphisms.
definition_raw: An [[(∞, n)-category|(∞, ''n'')-category]] is obtained from an ∞-category by replacing the space of morphisms by the (∞, ''n'' - 1)-category of morphisms.

definition: Another term for a homotopy sheaf.
definition_raw: Another term for a [[homotopy sheaf]].

definition: An object A is initial if there is exactly one morphism from A to each object; e.g., empty set in Set.
definition_raw: An object ''A'' is [[initial object|initial]] if there is exactly one morphism from ''A'' to each object; e.g., [[empty set]] in '''[[Category of sets|Set]]'''.

definition: An object A in an abelian category is injective if the functor $\operatorname{Hom}(-, A)$ is exact. It is the dual of a projective object.
definition_raw: An object ''A'' in an abelian category is [[injective object|injective]] if the functor <math>\operatorname{Hom}(-, A)</math> is exact. It is the dual of a projective object.

definition: Given a monoidal category (C, ⊗), the internal Hom is a functor <math>[-, -]: C^{\text{op
definition_raw: Given a [[monoidal category]] (''C'', ⊗), the [[internal Hom]] is a functor <math>[-, -]: C^{\text{op

definition: A morphism f is an inverse to a morphism g if $g\circ f$ is defined and is equal to the identity morphism on the codomain of g, and $f\circ g$ is defined and equal to the identity morphism on the domain of g. The inverse of g is unique and is denoted by g−1. f is a left inverse to g if $f\circ g$ is defined and is equal to the identity morphism on the domain of g, and similarly for a right inverse.
definition_raw: A morphism ''f'' is an [[inverse function|inverse]] to a morphism ''g'' if <math>g\circ f</math> is defined and is equal to the identity morphism on the codomain of ''g'', and <math>f\circ g</math> is defined and equal to the identity morphism on the domain of ''g''. The inverse of ''g'' is unique and is denoted by ''g''<sup>−1</sup>. ''f'' is a left inverse to ''g'' if <math>f\circ g</math> is defined and is equal to the identity morphism on the domain of ''g'', and similarly for a right invers

definition: Isbell duality/Isbell conjugacy
definition_raw: [[Isbell duality]]/[[Isbell conjugacy]]

definition: An object is isomorphic to another object if there is an isomorphism between them.
definition_raw: An object is [[isomorphic]] to another object if there is an isomorphism between them.

definition: A morphism f is an isomorphism if there exists an inverse of f.
definition_raw: A morphism ''f'' is an [[isomorphism]] if there exists an ''inverse'' of ''f''.

definition: A Kan complex is a fibrant object in the category of simplicial sets; i.e., the unique morphism from it to the final object is a fibration (Kan fibration). It is often taken as a model of an ∞-groupoid.
definition_raw: A [[Kan complex]] is a [[fibrant object]] in the category of simplicial sets; i.e., the unique morphism from it to the final object is a fibration (Kan fibration). It is often taken as a model of an [[∞-groupoid]].

definition: Given a category C, the left Kan extension functor along a functor $f: I \to J$ is the left adjoint (if it exists) to $f^* = - \circ f: \operatorname{Fct}(J, C) \to \operatorname{Fct}(I, C)$ and is denoted by $f_!$. For any $\alpha: I \to C$, the functor $f_! \alpha: J \to C$ is called the left Kan extension of α along f. One can show: :$(f_! \alpha)(j) = \varinjlim_{f(i) \to j} \alpha(i)$ where the colimit runs over all objects $f(i) \to j$ in the comma category.
definition_raw: Given a category ''C'', the left [[Kan extension]] functor along a functor <math>f: I \to J</math> is the left adjoint (if it exists) to <math>f^* = - \circ f: \operatorname{Fct}(J, C) \to \operatorname{Fct}(I, C)</math> and is denoted by <math>f_!</math>. For any <math>\alpha: I \to C</math>, the functor <math>f_! \alpha: J \to C</math> is called the left Kan extension of α along ''f''.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.math.harvard.edu/~lurie/282ynotes/LectureXI-Homological.pdf|title=Universal Hom

definition: Ken Brown's lemma gives a sufficient condition for a functor to preserve weak equivalences.
definition_raw: [[Ken Brown's lemma]] gives a sufficient condition for a functor to preserve weak equivalences.

definition: Given a monad T, the Kleisli category of T is the full subcategory of the category of T-algebras (called Eilenberg–Moore category) that consists of free T-algebras.
definition_raw: Given a monad ''T'', the [[Kleisli category]] of ''T'' is the full subcategory of the category of ''T''-algebras (called Eilenberg–Moore category) that consists of free ''T''-algebras.

definition: A lax functor is a generalisation of a pseudo-functor, in which the structural transformations associated to composition and identities are not required to be invertible.
definition_raw: A [[lax functor]] is a generalisation of a [[pseudo-functor]], in which the structural transformations associated to composition and identities are not required to be invertible.

definition: An object in an abelian category is said to have finite length if it has a composition series. The maximum number of proper subobjects in any such composition series is called the length of A.
definition_raw: An object in an abelian category is said to have finite length if it has a [[composition series]]. The maximum number of proper subobjects in any such composition series is called the '''length''' of ''A''.{{harvnb|Kashiwara|Schapira|2006|loc=exercise 8.20}}

definition: The limit (or projective limit) of a functor <math>f: I^{\text{op
definition_raw: The [[limit (category theory)|limit]] (or [[projective limit]]) of a functor <math>f: I^{\text{op

definition: See localization of a category and localization of an ∞-category.
definition_raw: See [[localization of a category]] and [[localization of an ∞-category]].

definition: An inverse system $\cdots \to X_2 \to X_1 \to X_0$ is said to satisfy the Mittag-Leffler condition if for each integer $n \ge 0$, there is an integer $m \ge n$ such that for each $l \ge m$, the images of $X_m \to X_n$ and $X_l \to X_n$ are the same.
definition_raw: An [[inverse system]] <math>\cdots \to X_2 \to X_1 \to X_0</math> is said to satisfy the [[Mittag-Leffler condition]] if for each integer <math>n \ge 0</math>, there is an integer <math>m \ge n</math> such that for each <math>l \ge m</math>, the images of <math>X_m \to X_n</math> and <math>X_l \to X_n</math> are the same.

definition: A modification is a morphism between natural transformations.
definition_raw: A [[modification (category theory)|modification]] is a morphism between natural transformations.

definition: modular category
definition_raw: [[modular category]]

definition: A monad in a category X is a monoid object in the monoidal category of endofunctors of X with the monoidal structure given by composition. For example, given a group G, define an endofunctor T on Set by $T(X) = G \times X$. Then define the multiplication μ on T as the natural transformation $\mu: T \circ T \to T$ given by :$\mu_X: G \times (G \times X) \to G \times X, \,\, (g, (h, x)) \mapsto (gh, x)$ and also define the identity map η in the analogous fashion. Then (T, μ, η) constitutes a monad in Set. More substantially, an adjunction between functors $F: X \rightleftarrows A : G$ determines a monad in X; namely, one takes $T = G \circ F$, the identity map η on T to be a unit of the adjunction and also defines μ using the adjunction.
definition_raw:
A [[monad (category theory)|monad]] in a category ''X'' is a [[monoid object]] in the monoidal category of endofunctors of ''X'' with the monoidal structure given by composition. For example, given a group ''G'', define an endofunctor ''T'' on '''Set''' by <math>T(X) = G \times X</math>. Then define the multiplication ''μ'' on ''T'' as the natural transformation <math>\mu: T \circ T \to T</math> given by
:<math>\mu_X: G \times (G \times X) \to G \times X, \,\, (g, (h, x)) \mapsto (gh, x)</math>

definition: An adjunction is said to be monadic if it comes from the monad that it determines by means of the Eilenberg–Moore category (the category of algebras for the monad).
definition_raw: An adjunction is said to be [[monadic adjunction|monadic]] if it comes from the monad that it determines by means of the [[Eilenberg–Moore category]] (the category of algebras for the monad).

definition: A monoidal category, also called a tensor category, is a category C equipped with (1) a bifunctor $\otimes: C \times C \to C$, (2) an identity object and (3) natural isomorphisms that make ⊗ associative and the identity object an identity for ⊗, subject to certain coherence conditions.
definition_raw: A [[monoidal category]], also called a tensor category, is a category ''C'' equipped with (1) a [[bifunctor]] <math>\otimes: C \times C \to C</math>, (2) an identity object and (3) natural isomorphisms that make ⊗ associative and the identity object an identity for ⊗, subject to certain coherence conditions.

definition: A monoid object in a monoidal category is an object together with the multiplication map and the identity map that satisfy the expected conditions like associativity. For example, a monoid object in Set is a usual monoid (unital semigroup) and a monoid object in R-mod is an associative algebra over a commutative ring R.
definition_raw: A [[monoid object]] in a monoidal category is an object together with the multiplication map and the identity map that satisfy the expected conditions like associativity. For example, a monoid object in '''Set''' is a usual monoid (unital semigroup) and a monoid object in '''[[category of modules|''R''-mod]]''' is an [[associative algebra]] over a commutative ring ''R''.

definition: A morphism f is a monomorphism (also called monic) if $g=h$ whenever $f\circ g=f\circ h$; e.g., an injection in Set. In other words, f is the dual of an epimorphism.
definition_raw: A morphism ''f'' is a [[monomorphism]] (also called monic) if <math>g=h</math> whenever <math>f\circ g=f\circ h</math>; e.g., an [[Injective function|injection]] in '''[[Category of sets|Set]]'''. In other words, ''f'' is the dual of an epimorphism.

definition: A multicategory is a generalization of a category in which a morphism is allowed to have more than one domain. It is the same thing as a colored operad.
definition_raw: A [[multicategory]] is a generalization of a category in which a morphism is allowed to have more than one domain. It is the same thing as a [[colored operad]].<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/multicategory|title=Multicategory in nLab|access-date=2019-01-03|archive-date=2019-01-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190103161234/https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/multicategory|url-status=live}}</ref>

definition: A strict n-category is defined inductively: a strict 0-category is a set and a strict n-category is a category whose Hom sets are strict (n-1)-categories. Precisely, a strict n-category is a category enriched over strict (n-1)-categories. For example, a strict 1-category is an ordinary category.
definition_raw: A [[strict n-category|strict ''n''-category]] is defined inductively: a strict 0-category is a set and a strict ''n''-category is a category whose Hom sets are strict (''n''-1)-categories. Precisely, a strict ''n''-category is a category enriched over strict (''n''-1)-categories. For example, a strict 1-category is an ordinary category.

definition: A natural transformation is, roughly, a map between functors. Precisely, given a pair of functors F, G from a category C to category D, a natural transformation φ from F to G is a set of morphisms in D :$\{ \phi_x: F(x) \to G(x) \mid x \in \operatorname{Ob}(C) \}$ satisfying the condition: for each morphism f: x → y in C, $\phi_y \circ F(f) = G(f) \circ \phi_x$. For example, writing $GL_n(R)$ for the group of invertible n-by-n matrices with coefficients in a commutative ring R, we can view $GL_n$ as a functor from the category CRing of commutative rings to the category Grp of groups. Similarly, $R \mapsto R^*$ is a functor from CRing to Grp. Then the determinant det is a natural transformation from $GL_n$ to -*.
definition_raw:
A natural transformation is, roughly, a map between functors. Precisely, given a pair of functors ''F'', ''G'' from a category ''C'' to category ''D'', a [[natural transformation]] φ from ''F'' to ''G'' is a set of morphisms in ''D''
:<math>\{ \phi_x: F(x) \to G(x) \mid x \in \operatorname{Ob}(C) \}</math>
satisfying the condition: for each morphism ''f'': ''x'' → ''y'' in ''C'', <math>\phi_y \circ F(f) = G(f) \circ \phi_x</math>. For example, writing <math>GL_n(R)</math> for the group of invert

definition: The nerve functor N is the functor from Cat to sSet given by <math>N(C)_n = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbf{Cat
definition_raw: The [[nerve functor]] ''N'' is the functor from '''Cat''' to ''s'''''Set''' given by <math>N(C)_n = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbf{Cat

definition: A monomorphism is normal if it is the kernel of some morphism, and an epimorphism is conormal if it is the cokernel of some morphism. A category is normal if every monomorphism is normal.
definition_raw: A monomorphism is normal if it is the kernel of some morphism, and an epimorphism is conormal if it is the cokernel of some morphism.  A category is [[normal category|normal]] if every monomorphism is normal.

definition: An object is part of a data defining a category.
definition_raw: An object is part of a data defining a category.

definition: A functor π:C → D is an op-fibration if, for each object x in C and each morphism g : π(x) → y in D, there is at least one π-coCartesian morphism f: x → y in C such that π(f) = g. In other words, π is the dual of a Grothendieck fibration.
definition_raw: A functor π:''C'' → ''D'' is an [[op-fibration]] if, for each object ''x'' in ''C'' and each morphism ''g'' : π(''x'') → ''y'' in ''D'', there is at least one π-coCartesian morphism ''f'': ''x'' → ''y<nowiki>'</nowiki>'' in ''C'' such that π(''f'') = ''g''. In other words, π is the dual of a [[Grothendieck fibration]].

definition: The opposite category of a category is obtained by reversing the arrows. For example, if a partially ordered set is viewed as a category, taking its opposite amounts to reversing the ordering.
definition_raw: The [[opposite category]] of a category is obtained by reversing the arrows. For example, if a partially ordered set is viewed as a category, taking its opposite amounts to reversing the ordering.

definition: Sometimes synonymous with "compact". See perfect complex.
definition_raw: Sometimes synonymous with "compact". See [[perfect complex]].

definition: A category (or ∞-category) is called pointed if it has a zero object.
definition_raw: A category (or ∞-category) is called pointed if it has a zero object.

definition: A polygraph is a generalization of a directed graph.
definition_raw: A [[polygraph (mathematics)|polygraph]] is a generalization of a directed graph.

definition: A functor from the category of finite-dimensional vector spaces to itself is called a polynomial functor if, for each pair of vector spaces V, W, is a polynomial map between the vector spaces. A Schur functor is a basic example.
definition_raw: A functor from the category of finite-dimensional vector spaces to itself is called a [[polynomial functor]] if, for each pair of vector spaces ''V'', ''W'', {{nowrap|''F'': Hom(''V'', ''W'') → Hom(''F''(''V''), ''F''(''W''))}} is a polynomial map between the vector spaces. A [[Schur functor]] is a basic example.

definition: A pre-abelian category is an additive category that has all kernels and cokernels.
definition_raw: A [[pre-abelian category]] is an additive category that has all kernels and cokernels.

definition: A category is preadditive if it is enriched over the monoidal category of abelian groups. More generally, it is R-linear if it is enriched over the monoidal category of R-modules, for R a commutative ring.
definition_raw: A category is [[preadditive category|preadditive]] if it is [[enriched category|enriched]] over the [[monoidal category]] of [[abelian group]]s. More generally, it is [[preadditive category#R-linear categories|''R''-linear]] if it is enriched over the monoidal category of [[module (mathematics)|''R''-modules]], for ''R'' a [[commutative ring]].

definition: Given a regular cardinal κ, a category is κ-presentable if it admits all small colimits and is κ-accessible. A category is presentable if it is κ-presentable for some regular cardinal κ (hence presentable for any larger cardinal). Note: Some authors call a presentable category a locally presentable category.
definition_raw: Given a [[regular cardinal]] κ, a category is [[presentabl category|κ-presentable]] if it admits all small colimits and is [[#accessible|κ-accessible]]. A category is presentable if it is κ-presentable for some regular cardinal κ (hence presentable for any larger cardinal). '''Note''': Some authors call a presentable category a [[locally presentable category]].

definition: Another term for a contravariant functor: a functor from a category Cop to Set is a presheaf of sets on C and a functor from Cop to sSet is a presheaf of simplicial sets or simplicial presheaf, etc. A topology on C, if any, tells which presheaf is a sheaf (with respect to that topology).
definition_raw: Another term for a contravariant functor: a functor from a category ''C''<sup>op</sup> to '''Set''' is a presheaf of sets on ''C'' and a functor from ''C''<sup>op</sup> to ''s'''''Set''' is a presheaf of simplicial sets or [[simplicial presheaf]], etc. A [[Grothendieck topology|topology]] on ''C'', if any, tells which presheaf is a sheaf (with respect to that topology).

definition: The product of a family of objects Xi in a category C indexed by a set I is the projective limit $\varprojlim$ of the functor $I \to C, \, i \mapsto X_i$, where I is viewed as a discrete category. It is denoted by $\prod_i X_i$ and is the dual of the coproduct of the family.
definition_raw: The [[product (category theory)|product]] of a family of objects ''X''<sub>''i''</sub> in a category ''C'' indexed by a set ''I'' is the projective limit <math>\varprojlim</math> of the functor <math>I \to C, \, i \mapsto X_i</math>, where ''I'' is viewed as a discrete category. It is denoted by <math>\prod_i X_i</math> and is the dual of the coproduct of the family.

definition: Given categories C and D, a profunctor (or a distributor) from C to D is a functor of the form <math>D^{\text{op
definition_raw: Given categories ''C'' and ''D'', a [[profunctor]] (or a distributor) from ''C'' to ''D'' is a functor of the form <math>D^{\text{op

definition: An object A in an abelian category is projective if the functor $\operatorname{Hom}(A, -)$ is exact. It is the dual of an injective object.
definition_raw: An object ''A'' in an abelian category is [[projective object|projective]] if the functor <math>\operatorname{Hom}(A, -)</math> is exact. It is the dual of an injective object.

definition: A PROP is a symmetric strict monoidal category whose objects are natural numbers and whose tensor product addition of natural numbers.
definition_raw: A [[PROP (category theory)|PROP]] is a symmetric strict monoidal category whose objects are natural numbers and whose tensor product [[addition]] of natural numbers.

definition: A pseudoalgebra is a 2-category-version of an algebra for a monad (with a monad replaced by a 2-monad).
definition_raw: A [[pseudoalgebra]] is a 2-category-version of an algebra for a monad (with a monad replaced by a 2-monad).

definition: The pseudo-abelian envelope of an additive category is the smallest pseudo-abelian category containing the given category.
definition_raw: The [[pseudo-abelian envelope]] of an additive category is the smallest [[pseudo-abelian category]] containing the given category.

definition: pseudo-tensor category.
definition_raw: [[pseudo-tensor category]].

definition: Q-category.
definition_raw: [[Q-category]].

definition: Quillen’s theorem A provides a criterion for a functor to be a weak equivalence.
definition_raw: [[Quillen’s theorem A]] provides a criterion for a functor to be a weak equivalence.

definition: a quasi-abelian category.
definition_raw: a [[quasi-abelian category]].

definition: a quasitopos.
definition_raw: a [[quasitopos]].

definition: A Reedy category. A prototypical example is the simplex category $\Delta$.
definition_raw: A [[Reedy category]]. A prototypical example is the simplex category <math>\Delta</math>.

definition: A functor is said to reflect identities if it has the property: if F(k) is an identity then k is an identity as well.
definition_raw: A functor is said to reflect identities if it has the property: if ''F''(''k'') is an identity then ''k'' is an identity as well.

definition: A regular category.
definition_raw: A [[regular category]].

definition: A rig category is a category with two monoidal structures, one distributing over the other.
definition_raw: A [[rig category]] is a category with two monoidal structures, one distributing over the other.

definition: A Schur functor.
definition_raw: A [[Schur functor]].

definition: A morphism is a section if it has a left inverse. For example, the axiom of choice says that any surjective function admits a section.
definition_raw: A morphism is a [[section (category theory)|section]] if it has a left inverse. For example, the [[axiom of choice]] says that any surjective function admits a section.

definition: Segal condition. For now, see https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Segal+condition
definition_raw: [[Segal condition]]. For now, see https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Segal+condition

definition: A semi-abelian category.
definition_raw: A [[semi-abelian category]].

definition: An abelian category is semisimple if every short exact sequence splits. For example, a ring is semisimple if and only if the category of modules over it is semisimple.
definition_raw: An abelian category is [[semisimple category|semisimple]] if every short exact sequence splits. For example, a ring is [[semisimple ring|semisimple]] if and only if the category of modules over it is semisimple.

definition: Given a k-linear category C over a field k, a Serre functor $f: C \to C$ is an auto-equivalence such that $\operatorname{Hom}(A, B) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(B,f(A))^*$ for any objects A, B.
definition_raw: Given a ''k''-linear category ''C'' over a field ''k'', a [[Serre functor]] <math>f: C \to C</math> is an auto-equivalence such that <math>\operatorname{Hom}(A, B) \simeq \operatorname{Hom}(B,f(A))^*</math> for any objects ''A'', ''B''.

definition: A setoid is an object in the free exact completion of the category of sets. If the axiom of choice is valid, it is the same as a set.
definition_raw: A [[setoid]] is an object in the [[free exact completion]] of the category of sets. If the axiom of choice is valid, it is the same as a set.

definition: In a category, a sieve on an object x is a set S of some morphisms with the target x such that, for each $f$ in $S$ and each morphism $g$ in the category, $f \circ g$ is in $S$ if the composition is defined.
definition_raw: In a category, a [[sieve (category theory)|sieve]] on an object ''x'' is a set ''S'' of some morphisms with the target ''x'' such that, for each <math>f</math> in <math>S</math> and each morphism <math>g</math> in the category, <math>f \circ g</math> is in <math>S</math> if the composition is defined.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00YW | title=Section 7.47 (00YW): Topologies—The Stacks project | access-date=2025-04-15 | archive-date=2025-08-18 | archive-url=https://we

definition: A simple object in an abelian category is an object A that is not isomorphic to the zero object and whose every subobject is isomorphic to zero or to A. For example, a simple module is precisely a simple object in the category of (say left) modules.
definition_raw: A simple object in an abelian category is an object ''A'' that is not isomorphic to the zero object and whose every [[subobject]] is isomorphic to zero or to ''A''. For example, a [[simple module]] is precisely a simple object in the category of (say left) modules.

definition: The simplex category Δ is the category where an object is a set [n] = { 0, 1, …, n }, n ≥ 0, totally ordered in the standard way and a morphism is an order-preserving function.
definition_raw: The [[simplex category]] Δ is the category where an object is a set [''n''] = { 0, 1, …, ''n'' }, n ≥ 0, totally ordered in the standard way and a morphism is an order-preserving function.

definition: A category enriched over simplicial sets.
definition_raw: A category enriched over simplicial sets.

definition: Simplicial localization is a method of localizing a category.
definition_raw: [[Simplicial localization]] is a method of localizing a category.

definition: A simplicial object in a category C is roughly a sequence of objects $X_0, X_1, X_2, \dots$ in C that forms a simplicial set. In other words, it is a covariant or contravariant functor Δ → C. For example, a simplicial presheaf is a simplicial object in the category of presheaves.
definition_raw: A [[simplicial object]] in a category ''C'' is roughly a sequence of objects <math>X_0, X_1, X_2, \dots</math> in ''C'' that forms a simplicial set. In other words, it is a covariant or contravariant functor Δ → ''C''. For example, a [[simplicial presheaf]] is a simplicial object in the category of presheaves.

definition: Simpson's semi-strictification conjecture (as this is a red link, for now, see ).
definition_raw: [[Simpson's semi-strictification conjecture]] (as this is a red link, for now, see [https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/Simpson%27s+conjecture]).

definition: A simplicial set is a contravariant functor from Δ to Set, where Δ is the simplex category, a category whose objects are the sets [n] = { 0, 1, …, n } and whose morphisms are order-preserving functions. One writes $X_n = X([n])$ and an element of the set $X_n$ is called an n-simplex. For example, $\Delta^n = \operatorname{Hom}_{\Delta}(-, [n])$ is a simplicial set called the standard n-simplex. By Yoneda's lemma, $X_n \simeq \operatorname{Nat}(\Delta^n, X)$.
definition_raw: A [[simplicial set]] is a contravariant functor from Δ to '''Set''', where Δ is the [[simplex category]], a category whose objects are the sets [''n''] = { 0, 1, …, ''n'' } and whose morphisms are order-preserving functions. One writes <math>X_n = X([n])</math> and an element of the set <math>X_n</math> is called an ''n''-simplex. For example, <math>\Delta^n = \operatorname{Hom}_{\Delta}(-, [n])</math> is a simplicial set called the standard ''n''-simplex. By Yoneda's lemma, <math>X_

definition: A category equipped with a Grothendieck topology.
definition_raw: A category equipped with a [[Grothendieck topology]].

definition: A category is skeletal if isomorphic objects are necessarily identical.
definition_raw: A category is [[Skeleton (category theory)|skeletal]] if isomorphic objects are necessarily identical.

definition: Given a category C and an object A in it, the slice category C/A of C over A is the category whose objects are all the morphisms in C with codomain A, whose morphisms are morphisms in C such that if f is a morphism from $p_X: X \to A$ to $p_Y: Y \to A$, then $p_Y \circ f = p_X$ in C and whose composition is that of C.
definition_raw: Given a category ''C'' and an object ''A'' in it, the [[slice category]] ''C''<sub>/''A''</sub> of ''C'' over ''A'' is the category whose objects are all the morphisms in ''C'' with codomain ''A'', whose morphisms are morphisms in ''C'' such that if ''f'' is a morphism from <math>p_X: X \to A</math> to <math>p_Y: Y \to A</math>, then <math>p_Y \circ f = p_X</math> in ''C'' and whose composition is that of ''C''.

definition: A small category is a category in which the class of all morphisms is a set (i.e., not a proper class); otherwise large. A category is locally small if the morphisms between every pair of objects A and B form a set. Some authors assume a foundation in which the collection of all classes forms a "conglomerate", in which case a quasicategory is a category whose objects and morphisms merely form a conglomerate. (NB: some authors use the term "quasicategory" with a different meaning.)
definition_raw: A [[small category]] is a category in which the class of all morphisms is a [[Set (mathematics)|set]] (i.e., not a [[proper class]]); otherwise '''large'''. A category is '''locally small''' if the morphisms between every pair of objects ''A'' and ''B'' form a set. Some authors assume a foundation in which the collection of all classes forms a "conglomerate", in which case a '''quasicategory''' is a category whose objects and morphisms merely form a [[conglomerate (mathematics)|conglomerate]].<r

definition: A (combinatorial) species is an endofunctor on the groupoid of finite sets with bijections. It is categorically equivalent to a symmetric sequence.
definition_raw: A [[combinatorial species|(combinatorial) species]] is an endofunctor on the groupoid of finite sets with bijections. It is categorically equivalent to a [[symmetric sequence]].

definition: Spherical category.
definition_raw: [[Spherical category]].

definition: An ∞-category is stable if (1) it has a zero object, (2) every morphism in it admits a fiber and a cofiber and (3) a triangle in it is a fiber sequence if and only if it is a cofiber sequence.
definition_raw: An ∞-category is [[stable ∞-category|stable]] if (1) it has a zero object, (2) every morphism in it admits a fiber and a cofiber and (3) a triangle in it is a fiber sequence if and only if it is a cofiber sequence.

definition: A morphism f in a category admitting finite limits and finite colimits is strict if the natural morphism $\operatorname{Coim}(f) \to \operatorname{Im}(f)$ is an isomorphism.
definition_raw: A morphism ''f'' in a category admitting finite limits and finite colimits is [[strict morphism|strict]] if the natural morphism <math>\operatorname{Coim}(f) \to \operatorname{Im}(f)</math> is an isomorphism.

definition: A strict 0-category is a set and for any integer n > 0, a strict n-category is a category enriched over strict (n-1)-categories. For example, a strict 1-category is an ordinary category. Note: the term "n-category" typically refers to "weak n-category"; not strict one.
definition_raw: A strict 0-category is a set and for any integer ''n'' > 0, a [[strict n-category|strict ''n''-category]] is a category enriched over strict (''n''-1)-categories. For example, a strict 1-category is an ordinary category. '''Note''': the term "''n''-category" typically refers to "[[weak n-category|weak ''n''-category]]"; not strict one.

definition: A strictification is a process of replacing equalities holding weakly (i.e., up to coherent isomorphisms) by actual equalities.
definition_raw: A [[strictification]] is a process of replacing equalities holding weakly (i.e., up to coherent isomorphisms) by actual equalities.

definition: A topology on a category is subcanonical if every representable contravariant functor on C is a sheaf with respect to that topology. Generally speaking, some flat topology may fail to be subcanonical; but flat topologies appearing in practice tend to be subcanonical.
definition_raw: A topology on a category is [[subcanonical]] if every representable contravariant functor on ''C'' is a sheaf with respect to that topology.<ref>{{harvnb|Vistoli|2004|loc=Definition 2.57.}}</ref> Generally speaking, some [[flat topology]] may fail to be subcanonical; but flat topologies appearing in practice tend to be subcanonical.

definition: A category A is a subcategory of a category B if there is an inclusion functor from A to B.
definition_raw: A category ''A'' is a [[subcategory]] of a category ''B'' if there is an inclusion functor from ''A'' to ''B''.

definition: Given an object A in a category, a subobject of A is an equivalence class of monomorphisms to A; two monomorphisms f, g are considered equivalent if f factors through g and g factors through f.
definition_raw: Given an object ''A'' in a category, a [[subobject]] of ''A'' is an equivalence class of monomorphisms to ''A''; two monomorphisms ''f'', ''g'' are considered equivalent if ''f'' factors through ''g'' and ''g'' factors through ''f''.

definition: A subquotient is a quotient of a subobject.
definition_raw: A [[subquotient]] is a quotient of a subobject.

definition: A subterminal object is an object X such that every object has at most one morphism into X.
definition_raw: A [[subterminal object]] is an object ''X'' such that every object has at most one morphism into ''X''.

definition: A symmetric monoidal category is a monoidal category (i.e., a category with ⊗) that has maximally symmetric braiding.
definition_raw: A [[symmetric monoidal category]] is a [[monoidal category]] (i.e., a category with ⊗) that has maximally symmetric braiding.

definition: A symmetric sequence is a sequence of objects with actions of symmetric groups. It is categorically equivalent to a (combinatorial) species.
definition_raw: A [[symmetric sequence]] is a sequence of objects with actions of [[symmetric group]]s. It is categorically equivalent to a [[combinatorial species|(combinatorial) species]].

definition: syntactic category
definition_raw: [[syntactic category (mathematics)|syntactic category]]

definition: A t-structure is an additional structure on a triangulated category (more generally stable ∞-category) that axiomatizes the notions of complexes whose cohomology concentrated in non-negative degrees or non-positive degrees.
definition_raw: A [[t-structure]] is an additional structure on a [[triangulated category]] (more generally [[stable ∞-category]]) that axiomatizes the notions of complexes whose cohomology concentrated in non-negative degrees or non-positive degrees.

definition: The Tannakian duality states that, in an appropriate setup, to give a morphism $f: X \to Y$ is to give a pullback functor $f^*$ along it. In other words, the Hom set $\operatorname{Hom}(X, Y)$ can be identified with the functor category $\operatorname{Fct}(D(Y), D(X))$, perhaps in the derived sense, where $D(X)$ is the category associated to X (e.g., the derived category).
definition_raw: The [[Tannakian duality]] states that, in an appropriate setup, to give a morphism <math>f: X \to Y</math> is to give a pullback functor <math>f^*</math> along it. In other words, the Hom set <math>\operatorname{Hom}(X, Y)</math> can be identified with the functor category <math>\operatorname{Fct}(D(Y), D(X))</math>, perhaps in the [[derived algebraic geometry|derived sense]], where <math>D(X)</math> is the category associated to ''X'' (e.g., the derived category).<ref>Jacob Lurie. Tannaka duali

definition: Usually synonymous with monoidal category (though some authors distinguish between the two concepts.)
definition_raw: Usually synonymous with [[monoidal category]] (though some authors distinguish between the two concepts.)

definition: A tensor triangulated category is a category that carries the structure of a symmetric monoidal category and that of a triangulated category in a compatible way.
definition_raw: A [[tensor triangulated category]] is a category that carries the structure of a symmetric monoidal category and that of a triangulated category in a compatible way.

definition: Given a monoidal category B, the tensor product of functors <math>F: C^{\text{op
definition_raw: Given a monoidal category ''B'', the '''tensor product of functors''' <math>F: C^{\text{op

definition: An object A is terminal (also called final) if there is exactly one morphism from each object to A; e.g., singletons in Set. It is the dual of an initial object.
definition_raw: An object ''A'' is [[terminal object|terminal]] (also called final) if there is exactly one morphism from each object to ''A''; e.g., [[singleton (mathematics)|singleton]]s in '''[[Category of sets|Set]]'''. It is the dual of an [[initial object]].

definition: Joyal's theta category is an alternative to the simplex category, that can be also used to defined an ∞-category.
definition_raw: [[Joyal's theta category]] is an alternative to the simplex category, that can be also used to defined an ∞-category.

definition: A full subcategory of an abelian category is thick if it is closed under extensions.
definition_raw: A full subcategory of an abelian category is [[thick subcategory|thick]] if it is closed under extensions.

definition: A thin category is a category where there is at most one morphism between any pair of objects.
definition_raw: A [[thin category]] is a category where there is at most one morphism between any pair of objects.

definition: A tiny object. For now, see https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/tiny+object
definition_raw: A [[tiny object]]. For now, see https://ncatlab.org/nlab/show/tiny+object

definition: A topological topos, a possible substitute for the category of topological spaces. See https://golem.ph.utexas.edu/category/2014/04/on_a_topological_topos.html
definition_raw: A [[topological topos]], a possible substitute for the category of topological spaces. See https://golem.ph.utexas.edu/category/2014/04/on_a_topological_topos.html

definition: A transfinite composition is a composition involving infinitely many morphisms. See direct limit and inverse limit
definition_raw: A [[transfinite composition]] is a composition involving infinitely many morphisms. See [[direct limit]] and [[inverse limit]]

definition: A triangulated category is a category where one can talk about distinguished triangles, generalization of exact sequences. An abelian category is a prototypical example of a triangulated category. A derived category is a triangulated category that is not necessary an abelian category.
definition_raw: A [[triangulated category]] is a category where one can talk about distinguished triangles, generalization of exact sequences. An abelian category is a prototypical example of a triangulated category. A [[derived category]] is a triangulated category that is not necessary an abelian category.

definition: Given a functor $f: C \to D$ and an object X in D, a universal morphism from X to f is an initial object in the comma category $(X \downarrow f)$. (Its dual is also called a universal morphism.) For example, take f to be the forgetful functor $\mathbf{Vec}_k \to \mathbf{Set}$ and X a set. An initial object of $(X \downarrow f)$ is a function $j: X \to f(V_X)$. That it is initial means that if $k: X \to f(W)$ is another morphism, then there is a unique morphism from j to k, which consists of a linear map $V_X \to W$ that extends k via j; that is to say, $V_X$ is the free vector space generated by X.
definition_raw: Given a functor <math>f: C \to D</math> and an object ''X'' in ''D'', a [[universal morphism]] from ''X'' to ''f'' is an initial object in the [[comma category]] <math>(X \downarrow f)</math>. (Its dual is also called a universal morphism.) For example, take ''f'' to be the forgetful functor <math>\mathbf{Vec}_k \to \mathbf{Set}</math> and ''X'' a set. An initial object of <math>(X \downarrow f)</math> is a function <math>j: X \to f(V_X)</math>. That it is initial means that if <math>k: X \to f(

definition: Verdier localization
definition_raw: [[Verdier localization]]

definition: A Waldhausen category is, roughly, a category with families of cofibrations and weak equivalences.
definition_raw: A [[Waldhausen category]] is, roughly, a category with families of cofibrations and weak equivalences.

definition: A category is wellpowered if for each object there is only a set of pairwise non-isomorphic subobjects.
definition_raw: A category is wellpowered if for each object there is only a set of pairwise non-isomorphic [[subobject]]s.

definition: The Yoneda lemma says: for each set-valued contravariant functor F on C and an object X in C, there is a natural bijection :$F(X) \simeq \operatorname{Nat}(\operatorname{Hom}_C(-, X), F)$ where Nat means the set of natural transformations. In particular, the functor :<math>y: C \to \mathbf{Fct}(C^{\text{op

definition: A zero object is an object that is both initial and terminal, such as a trivial group in Grp.
definition_raw: A [[zero object]] is an object that is both initial and terminal, such as a [[trivial group]] in '''[[Category of groups|Grp]]'''.

definition: Projective space of dimension $1, 2, \ldots, n$. This notation was introduced by .
definition_raw: Projective space of dimension <math>1, 2, \ldots, n</math>. This notation was introduced by {{harvs|txt|last=Schubert|year=1886|authorlink=Hermann Schubert}}.

definition: A family of dimension 1, 2, ...
