definition: The characteristic of the field F is the smallest positive integer n such that ; here n·1 stands for n summands . If no such n exists, we say the characteristic is zero. Every non-zero characteristic is a prime number. For example, the rational numbers, the real numbers and the p-adic numbers have characteristic 0, while the finite field Zp with p being prime has characteristic p.
definition_raw: The ''characteristic'' of the field ''F'' is the smallest positive [[integer]] ''n'' such that {{nowrap|1=''n''·1 = 0}}; here ''n''·1 stands for ''n'' summands {{nowrap|1 + 1 + 1 + ... + 1}}. If no such ''n'' exists, we say the characteristic is zero. Every non-zero characteristic is a [[prime number]]. For example, the [[rational number]]s, the [[real number]]s and the [[p-adic numbers|''p''-adic numbers]] have characteristic 0, while the finite field '''Z'''<sub>''p''</sub> with ''p'' being pr

definition: A subfield of a field F is a subset of F which is closed under the field operation + and * of F and which, with these operations, forms itself a field.
definition_raw: A ''subfield'' of a field ''F'' is a [[subset]] of ''F'' which is closed under the field operation + and * of ''F'' and which, with these operations, forms itself a field.

definition: The prime field of the field F is the unique smallest subfield of F.
definition_raw: The ''prime field'' of the field ''F'' is the unique smallest subfield of ''F''.

definition: If F is a subfield of E then E is an extension field of F. We then also say that E/F is a field extension.
definition_raw: If ''F'' is a subfield of ''E'' then ''E'' is an ''extension field'' of ''F''. We then also say that ''E''/''F'' is a ''field extension''.

definition: Given an extension E/F, the field E can be considered as a vector space over the field F, and the dimension of this vector space is the degree of the extension, denoted by [E : F].
definition_raw: Given an extension ''E''/''F'', the field ''E'' can be considered as a [[vector space]] over the field ''F'', and the [[dimension (vector space)|dimension]] of this vector space is the ''degree'' of the extension, denoted by [''E'' : ''F''].

definition: A finite extension is a field extension whose degree is finite.
definition_raw: A ''finite extension'' is a field extension whose degree is finite.

definition: If an element α of an extension field E over F is the root of a non-zero polynomial in F[x], then α is algebraic over F. If every element of E is algebraic over F, then E/F is an algebraic extension.
definition_raw: If an element ''α'' of an extension field ''E'' over ''F'' is the [[root]] of a non-zero polynomial in ''F''[''x''], then ''α'' is ''algebraic'' over ''F''.  If every element of ''E'' is algebraic over ''F'', then ''E''/''F'' is an ''algebraic extension''.

definition: Given a field extension E/F and a subset S of E, we write F(S) for the smallest subfield of E that contains both F and S. It consists of all the elements of E that can be obtained by repeatedly using the operations +, −, *, / on the elements of F and S. If , we say that E is generated by S over F.
definition_raw: Given a field extension ''E''/''F'' and a subset ''S'' of ''E'', we write ''F''(''S'') for the smallest subfield of ''E'' that contains both ''F'' and ''S''. It consists of all the elements of ''E'' that can be obtained by repeatedly using the operations +, −, *, / on the elements of ''F'' and ''S''. If {{nowrap|1=''E'' = ''F''(''S'')}}, we say that ''E'' is generated by ''S'' over ''F''.

definition: An element α of an extension field E over a field F is called a primitive element if E=F(α), the smallest extension field containing α. Such an extension is called a simple extension.
definition_raw: An element ''α'' of an extension field ''E'' over a field ''F'' is called a ''primitive element'' if ''E''=''F''(''α''), the smallest extension field containing ''α''. Such an extension is called a '''[[simple extension]]'''.

definition: A field extension generated by the complete factorisation of a polynomial.
definition_raw: A field extension generated by the complete factorisation of a polynomial.

definition: A field extension generated by the complete factorisation of a set of polynomials.
definition_raw: A field extension generated by the complete factorisation of a set of polynomials.

definition: An extension generated by roots of separable polynomials.
definition_raw: An extension generated by roots of [[separable polynomial]]s.

definition: A field such that every finite extension is separable. All fields of characteristic zero, and all finite fields, are perfect.
definition_raw: A field such that every finite extension is separable. All fields of characteristic zero, and all finite fields, are perfect.

definition: Let F be a field of characteristic ; then Fp is a subfield. The degree is called the imperfect degree of F. The field F is perfect if and only if its imperfect degree is 1. For example, if F is a function field of n variables over a finite field of characteristic , then its imperfect degree is pn.
definition_raw: Let ''F'' be a field of characteristic {{nowrap|''p'' > 0}}; then ''F''<sup>''p''</sup> is a subfield. The degree {{nowrap|[''F'' : ''F''<sup>''p''</sup>]}} is called the ''imperfect degree'' of ''F''. The field ''F'' is perfect if and only if its imperfect degree is ''1''. For example, if ''F'' is a function field of ''n'' variables over a finite field of characteristic {{nowrap|''p'' > 0}}, then its imperfect degree is ''p''<sup>''n''</sup>.{{sfn|Fried|Jarden|2008|p=45|ps=none}}

definition: A field F is algebraically closed if every polynomial in F[x] has a root in F; equivalently: every polynomial in F[x] is a product of linear factors.
definition_raw: A field ''F'' is ''algebraically closed'' if every polynomial in ''F''[''x''] has a root in ''F''; equivalently: every polynomial in ''F''[''x''] is a product of linear factors.

definition: An algebraic closure of a field F is an algebraic extension of F which is algebraically closed. Every field has an algebraic closure, and it is unique up to an isomorphism that fixes F.
definition_raw: An ''algebraic closure'' of a field ''F'' is an algebraic extension of ''F'' which is algebraically closed. Every field has an algebraic closure, and it is unique up to an isomorphism that fixes ''F''.

definition: Those elements of an extension field of F that are not algebraic over F are transcendental over F.
definition_raw: Those elements of an extension field of ''F'' that are not algebraic over ''F'' are ''transcendental'' over ''F''.

definition: Elements of an extension field of F are algebraically independent over F if they don't satisfy any non-zero polynomial equation with coefficients in F.
definition_raw: Elements of an extension field of ''F'' are ''algebraically independent'' over ''F'' if they don't satisfy any non-zero polynomial equation with coefficients in ''F''.

definition: The number of algebraically independent transcendental elements in a field extension. It is used to define the dimension of an algebraic variety.
definition_raw: The number of algebraically independent transcendental elements in a field extension. It is used to define the [[dimension of an algebraic variety]].

definition: A field homomorphism between two fields E and F is a ring homomorphism, i.e., a function : f : E → F such that, for all x, y in E, : f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y) : f(xy) = f(x) f(y) : f(1) = 1. For fields E and F, these properties imply that , for x in E×, and that f is injective. Fields, together with these homomorphisms, form a category. Two fields E and F are called isomorphic if there exists a bijective homomorphism : f : E → F. The two fields are then identical for all practical purposes; however, not necessarily in a unique way. See, for example, Complex conjugate.
definition_raw: A ''field homomorphism'' between two fields ''E'' and ''F'' is a [[ring homomorphism]], i.e., a [[function (mathematics)|function]] : ''f'' : ''E'' → ''F'' such that, for all ''x'', ''y'' in ''E'', : ''f''(''x'' + ''y'') = ''f''(''x'') + ''f''(''y'') : ''f''(''xy'') = ''f''(''x'') ''f''(''y'') : ''f''(1) = 1. For fields ''E'' and ''F'', these properties imply that {{nowrap|1=''f''(0) = 0}}, {{nowrap|1=''f''(''x''<sup>−1</sup>) = ''f''(''x'')<sup>−1</sup>}} for ''x'' in ''E''<sup>×</sup>, and tha

definition: A field with finitely many elements, a.k.a. Galois field.
definition_raw: A field with finitely many elements, a.k.a. '''Galois field'''.

definition: A field with a total order compatible with its operations.
definition_raw: A field with a [[total order]] compatible with its operations.

definition: Finite extension of the field of rational numbers.
definition_raw: Finite extension of the field of rational numbers.

definition: The field of algebraic numbers is the smallest algebraically closed extension of the field of rational numbers. Their detailed properties are studied in algebraic number theory.
definition_raw: The field of algebraic numbers is the smallest algebraically closed extension of the field of rational numbers. Their detailed properties are studied in [[algebraic number theory]].

definition: A degree-two extension of the rational numbers.
definition_raw: A degree-two extension of the rational numbers.

definition: An extension of the rational numbers generated by a root of unity.
definition_raw: An extension of the rational numbers generated by a [[root of unity]].

definition: A number field generated by a root of a polynomial, having all its roots real numbers.
definition_raw: A number field generated by a root of a polynomial, having all its roots real numbers.

definition: A number field or a function field of one variable over a finite field.
definition_raw: A number field or a function field of one variable over a finite field.

definition: A completion of some global field (w.r.t. a prime of the integer ring).
definition_raw: A completion of some global field ([[w.r.t.]] a prime of the integer ring).

definition: A field complete w.r.t. to some valuation.
definition_raw: A field complete w.r.t. to some valuation.

definition: A field in which every variety has a rational point.
definition_raw: A field in which every variety has a [[rational point]].{{sfn|Fried|Jarden|2008|p=214|ps=none}}

definition: A field satisfying Hensel lemma w.r.t. some valuation. A generalization of complete fields.
definition_raw: A field satisfying [[Hensel lemma]] w.r.t. some valuation. A generalization of complete fields.

definition: A field satisfying Hilbert's irreducibility theorem: formally, one for which the projective line is not thin in the sense of Serre.

definition_wikilinks.display: thin in the sense of Serre

definition_raw: A field satisfying [[Hilbert's irreducibility theorem]]: formally, one for which the [[projective line]] is not [[Thin set (Serre)|thin in the sense of Serre]].{{sfn|Serre|1992|p=19|ps=none}}{{sfn|Schinzel|2000|p=298|ps=none}}

definition: A totally real algebraic number field or a totally imaginary quadratic extension of a totally real field.
definition_raw: A totally real algebraic number field or a totally imaginary quadratic extension of a totally real field.{{sfn|Schinzel|2000|p=5|ps=none}}

definition: An algebraic number field which is a totally imaginary quadratic extension of a totally real field.
definition_raw: An algebraic number field which is a totally imaginary quadratic extension of a totally real field.{{sfn|Washington|1996|ps=none}}

definition: A field over which no biquaternion algebra is a division algebra.
definition_raw: A field over which no [[biquaternion algebra]] is a [[division algebra]].{{sfn|Lam|2005|p=342|ps=none}}

definition: A pseudo algebraically closed field whose absolute Galois group has the embedding property.
definition_raw: A [[pseudo algebraically closed field]] whose [[absolute Galois group]] has the embedding property.{{sfn|Fried|Jarden|2008|p=564|ps=none}}

definition: An extension which is generated by a single element, called a primitive element, or generating element. The primitive element theorem classifies such extensions.
definition_raw: An extension which is generated by a single element, called a '''primitive element''', or '''generating element'''.{{sfn|Roman|2007|p=46|ps=none}}  The [[primitive element theorem]] classifies such extensions.{{sfn|Lang|2002|p=243|ps=none}}

definition: A normal, separable field extension.
definition_raw: A normal, separable field extension.

definition: An extension E/F such that the algebraic closure of F in E is purely inseparable over F; equivalently, E is linearly disjoint from the separable closure of F.
definition_raw: An extension ''E''/''F'' such that the algebraic closure of ''F'' in ''E'' is [[purely inseparable]] over ''F''; equivalently, ''E'' is [[linearly disjoint]] from the [[separable closure]] of ''F''.{{sfn|Fried|Jarden|2008|p=44|ps=none}}

definition: An extension E/F in which every element of E not in F is transcendental over F.
definition_raw: An extension ''E''/''F'' in which every element of ''E'' not in ''F'' is transcendental over ''F''.{{sfn|Roman|2007|p=102|ps=none}}{{sfn|Isaacs|1994|p=389|ps=none}}

definition: An extension E/F such that E is separable over F and F is algebraically closed in E.
definition_raw: An extension ''E''/''F'' such that ''E'' is separable over ''F'' and ''F'' is algebraically closed in ''E''.{{sfn|Fried|Jarden|2008|p=44|ps=none}}

definition: A simple extension E/F generated by a single element α satisfying for an element b of F. In characteristic p, we also take an extension by a root of an Artin–Schreier polynomial to be a simple radical extension.
definition_raw: A [[simple extension]] ''E''/''F''  generated by a single element ''α'' satisfying {{nowrap|1=''α''<sup>''n''</sup> = ''b''}} for an element ''b'' of ''F''.  In [[Characteristic (algebra)|characteristic]] ''p'', we also take an extension by a root of an [[Artin–Schreier theory|Artin–Schreier polynomial]] to be a simple radical extension.{{sfn|Roman|2007|p=273|ps=none}}

definition: A tower where each extension is a simple radical extension.
definition_raw: A tower {{nowrap|1=''F'' = ''F''<sub>0</sub> < ''F''<sub>1</sub> < ⋅⋅⋅ < ''F''<sub>''k''</sub> = ''E''}} where each extension {{nowrap|''F''<sub>''i''</sub> / ''F''<sub>''i''−1</sub>}} is a simple radical extension.{{sfn|Roman|2007|p=273|ps=none}}

definition: An extension E/F such that is an integral domain.
definition_raw: An extension ''E''/''F'' such that {{nowrap|''E'' ⊗<sub>''F''</sub> ''E''}} is an integral domain.{{sfn|Cohn|2003|p=427|ps=none}}

definition: An extension E/F such that F is algebraically closed in F.
definition_raw: An extension ''E''/''F'' such that ''F'' is algebraically closed in ''F''.{{sfn|Isaacs|1994|p=389|ps=none}}

definition: A class C of field extensions with the three properties # If E is a C-extension of F and F is a C-extension of K then E is a C-extension of K. # If E and F are C-extensions of K in a common overfield M, then the compositum EF is a C-extension of K. # If E is a C-extension of F and then E is a C-extension of K.
definition_raw: A class ''C'' of field extensions with the three properties{{sfn|Lang|2002|p=228|ps=none}} # If ''E'' is a C-extension of ''F'' and ''F'' is a C-extension of ''K'' then ''E'' is a C-extension of ''K''. # If ''E'' and ''F'' are C-extensions of ''K'' in a common overfield ''M'', then the [[compositum]] ''EF'' is a C-extension of ''K''. # If ''E'' is a C-extension of ''F'' and {{nowrap|''E'' > ''K'' > ''F''}} then ''E'' is a C-extension of ''K''.

definition: The automorphism group of a Galois extension. When it is a finite extension, this is a finite group of order equal to the degree of the extension. Galois groups for infinite extensions are profinite groups.
definition_raw: The [[automorphism group]] of a Galois extension. When it is a finite extension, this is a finite group of order equal to the degree of the extension. Galois groups for infinite extensions are [[profinite group]]s.

definition: The Galois theory of taking nth roots, given enough roots of unity. It includes the general theory of quadratic extensions.
definition_raw: The Galois theory of taking ''n''th roots, given enough [[roots of unity]]. It includes the general theory of [[quadratic extension]]s.

definition: Covers an exceptional case of Kummer theory, in characteristic p.
definition_raw: Covers an exceptional case of Kummer theory, in characteristic ''p''.

definition: A basis in the vector space sense of L over K, on which the Galois group of L over K acts transitively.
definition_raw: A basis in the vector space sense of ''L'' over ''K'', on which the Galois group of ''L'' over ''K'' acts transitively.

definition: A different foundational piece of algebra, including the compositum operation (join of fields).
definition_raw: A different foundational piece of algebra, including the [[compositum]] operation ([[join (mathematics)|join]] of fields).

definition: Given a group G, find an extension of the rational number or other field with G as Galois group.
definition_raw: Given a group ''G'', find an extension of the rational number or other field with ''G'' as Galois group.

definition: The subject in which symmetry groups of differential equations are studied along the lines traditional in Galois theory. This is actually an old idea, and one of the motivations when Sophus Lie founded the theory of Lie groups. It has not, probably, reached definitive form.
definition_raw: The subject in which symmetry groups of [[differential equation]]s are studied along the lines traditional in Galois theory. This is actually an old idea, and one of the motivations when [[Sophus Lie]] founded the theory of [[Lie group]]s. It has not, probably, reached definitive form.

definition: A very abstract approach from algebraic geometry, introduced to study the analogue of the fundamental group.
definition_raw: A very abstract approach from [[algebraic geometry]], introduced to study the analogue of the [[fundamental group]].

definition: -homomorphism between involutive Banach algebras is an algebra homomorphism preserving *.
definition_raw: [[*-homomorphism]] between [[involutive Banach algebra]]s is an algebra homomorphism preserving *.

definition: Synonymous with "commutative"; e.g., an abelian Banach algebra means a commutative Banach algebra.
definition_raw: Synonymous with "commutative"; e.g., an abelian Banach algebra means a commutative Banach algebra.

definition: The Anderson–Kadec theorem says a separable infinite-dimensional Fréchet space is isomorphic to <math>\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N
definition_raw: The [[Anderson–Kadec theorem]] says a separable infinite-dimensional Fréchet space is isomorphic to <math>\mathbb{R}^{\mathbb{N

definition: Alaoglu's theorem states that the closed unit ball in a normed space is compact in the weak-* topology.
definition_raw: [[Alaoglu's theorem]] states that the closed unit ball in a normed space is compact in the [[weak-* topology]].

definition: The adjoint of a bounded linear operator $T: H_1 \to H_2$ between Hilbert spaces is the bounded linear operator $T^* : H_2 \to H_1$ such that $\langle Tx, y \rangle = \langle x, T^* y \rangle$ for each $x \in H_1, y \in H_2$.
definition_raw: The [[adjoint operator|adjoint]] of a bounded linear operator <math>T: H_1 \to H_2</math> between Hilbert spaces is the bounded linear operator <math>T^* : H_2 \to H_1</math> such that <math>\langle Tx, y \rangle = \langle x, T^* y \rangle</math> for each <math>x \in H_1, y \in H_2</math>.

definition: In a not-necessarily-unital Banach algebra, an approximate identity is a sequence or a net $\{ u_i \}$ of elements such that $u_i x \to x, x u_i \to x$ as $i \to \infty$ for each x in the algebra.
definition_raw: In a not-necessarily-unital Banach algebra, an [[approximate identity]] is a sequence or a net <math>\{ u_i \}</math> of elements such that <math>u_i x \to x, x u_i \to x</math> as <math>i \to \infty</math> for each ''x'' in the algebra.

definition: A Banach space is said to have the approximation property if every compact operator is a limit of finite-rank operators.
definition_raw: A Banach space is said to have the [[approximation property]] if every compact operator is a limit of finite-rank operators.

definition: The Baire category theorem states that a complete metric space is a Baire space; if $U_i$ is a sequence of open dense subsets, then $\cap_1^{\infty} U_i$ is dense.
definition_raw: The [[Baire category theorem]] states that a [[complete metric space]] is a [[Baire space]]; if <math>U_i</math> is a sequence of open dense subsets, then <math>\cap_1^{\infty} U_i</math> is dense.

definition: A Banach space is a normed vector space that is complete as a metric space.
definition_raw: A [[Banach space]] is a normed vector space that is complete as a metric space.

definition: A subset S of a vector space over real or complex numbers is balanced if $\lambda S \subset S$ for every scalar $\lambda$ of length at most one.
definition_raw: A subset ''S'' of a vector space over real or complex numbers is [[balanced subset|balanced]] if <math>\lambda S \subset S</math> for every scalar <math>\lambda</math> of length at most one.

definition: A barrel in a topological vector space is a subset that is closed, convex, balanced and absorbing.
definition_raw: A [[Barrelled set|barrel]] in a topological vector space is a subset that is closed, convex, balanced and absorbing.

definition: Bessel's inequality states: given an orthonormal set S and a vector x in a Hilbert space, :$\sum_{u \in S} |\langle x, u \rangle|^2 \le \|x\|^2$, where the equality holds if and only if S is an orthonormal basis; i.e., maximal orthonormal set.
definition_raw:
[[Bessel's inequality]] states: given an orthonormal set ''S'' and a vector ''x'' in a Hilbert space,
:<math>\sum_{u \in S} |\langle x, u \rangle|^2 \le \|x\|^2</math>,<ref name="sum convention" />
where the equality holds if and only if ''S'' is an orthonormal basis; i.e., maximal orthonormal set.

definition: bipolar theorem.
definition_raw: [[bipolar theorem]].

definition: A bounded operator is a linear operator between Banach spaces for which the image of the unit ball is bounded.
definition_raw: A [[bounded operator]] is a linear operator between Banach spaces for which the image of the unit ball is bounded.

definition: A bornological space.
definition_raw: A [[bornological space]].

definition: Two vectors x and y in a normed linear space are said to be Birkhoff orthogonal if $\| x + \lambda y \| \ge \|x\|$ for all scalars λ. If the normed linear space is a Hilbert space, then it is equivalent to the usual orthogonality.
definition_raw: Two vectors  ''x'' and ''y'' in a [[normed linear space]] are said to be '''Birkhoff orthogonal''' if <math>\| x + \lambda y \| \ge \|x\|</math> for all scalars λ. If the normed linear space is a Hilbert space, then it is equivalent to the usual orthogonality.

definition: Borel functional calculus
definition_raw: [[Borel functional calculus]]

definition: c space.
definition_raw: [[c space]].

definition: The Calkin algebra on a Hilbert space is the quotient of the algebra of all bounded operators on the Hilbert space by the ideal generated by compact operators.
definition_raw: The [[Calkin algebra]] on a Hilbert space is the quotient of the algebra of all bounded operators on the Hilbert space by the ideal generated by compact operators.

definition: The Cauchy–Schwarz inequality states: for each pair of vectors $x, y$ in an inner-product space, :$|\langle x, y \rangle| \le \|x\| \|y\|$.
definition_raw:
The [[Cauchy–Schwarz inequality]] states: for each pair of vectors <math>x, y</math> in an inner-product space,
:<math>|\langle x, y \rangle| \le \|x\| \|y\|</math>.

definition: The closed graph theorem states that a linear operator between Banach spaces is continuous (bounded) if and only if it has closed graph.
definition_raw: The [[closed graph theorem]] states that a linear operator between Banach spaces is continuous (bounded) if and only if it has closed graph.

definition: Another name for "centralizer"; i.e., the commutant of a subset S of an algebra is the algebra of the elements commuting with each element of S and is denoted by $S'$.
definition_raw: Another name for "[[centralizer (ring theory)|centralizer]]"; i.e., the commutant of a subset ''S'' of an algebra is the algebra of the elements commuting with each element of ''S'' and is denoted by <math>S'</math>.

definition: A compact operator is a linear operator between Banach spaces for which the image of the unit ball is precompact.
definition_raw: A [[compact operator]] is a linear operator between Banach spaces for which the image of the unit ball is precompact.

definition: Connes fusion.
definition_raw: [[Connes fusion]].

definition: A C* algebra is an involutive Banach algebra satisfying $\|x^* x\| = \|x^*\| \|x\|$.
definition_raw: A [[C* algebra]] is an involutive Banach algebra satisfying <math>\|x^* x\| = \|x^*\| \|x\|</math>.

definition: A locally convex space is a topological vector space whose topology is generated by convex subsets.
definition_raw: A [[locally convex space]] is a topological vector space whose topology is generated by convex subsets.

definition: Given a representation $(\pi, V)$ of a Banach algebra $A$, a cyclic vector is a vector $v \in V$ such that $\pi(A)v$ is dense in $V$.
definition_raw: Given a representation <math>(\pi, V)</math> of a Banach algebra <math>A</math>, a [[cyclic vector]] is a vector <math>v \in V</math> such that <math>\pi(A)v</math> is dense in <math>V</math>.

definition: dilation (operator theory).
definition_raw: [[dilation (operator theory)]].

definition: Philosophically, a direct integral is a continuous analog of a direct sum.
definition_raw: Philosophically, a [[direct integral]] is a continuous analog of a direct sum.

definition: Douglas' lemma
definition_raw: [[Douglas' lemma]]

definition: Dunford–Schwartz theorem
definition_raw: [[Dunford–Schwartz theorem]]

definition: The continuous dual of a topological vector space is the vector space of all the continuous linear functionals on the space.
definition_raw: The [[continuous dual]] of a topological vector space is the vector space of all the continuous linear functionals on the space.

definition: A theorem of Eidelheit.
definition_raw: [[A theorem of Eidelheit]].

definition: An essentially selfadjoint operator.
definition_raw: An [[essentially selfadjoint operator]].

definition: A factor is a von Neumann algebra with trivial center.
definition_raw: A [[factor (functional analysis)|factor]] is a von Neumann algebra with trivial center.

definition: A linear functional $\omega$ on an involutive algebra is faithful if $\omega(x^*x) \ne 0$ for each nonzero element $x$ in the algebra.
definition_raw: A linear functional <math>\omega</math> on an involutive algebra is [[faithful linear functional|faithful]] if <math>\omega(x^*x) \ne 0</math> for each nonzero element <math>x</math> in the algebra.

definition: A Fréchet space is a topological vector space whose topology is given by a countable family of seminorms (which makes it a metric space) and that is complete as a metric space.
definition_raw: A [[Fréchet space]] is a topological vector space whose topology is given by a countable family of seminorms (which makes it a metric space) and that is complete as a metric space.

definition: A Fredholm operator is a bounded operator such that it has closed range and the kernels of the operator and the adjoint have finite-dimension.
definition_raw: A [[Fredholm operator]] is a bounded operator such that it has closed range and the kernels of the operator and the adjoint have finite-dimension.

definition: The Gelfand–Mazur theorem states that a Banach algebra that is a division ring is the field of complex numbers.
definition_raw: The [[Gelfand–Mazur theorem]] states that a Banach algebra that is a division ring is the field of complex numbers.

definition: Grothendieck's inequality.
definition_raw: [[Grothendieck's inequality]].

definition: The Hahn–Banach theorem states: given a linear functional $\ell$ on a subspace of a complex vector space V, if the absolute value of $\ell$ is bounded above by a seminorm on V, then it extends to a linear functional on V still bounded by the seminorm. Geometrically, it is a generalization of the hyperplane separation theorem.
definition_raw: The [[Hahn–Banach theorem]] states: given a linear functional <math>\ell</math> on a subspace of a complex vector space ''V'', if the absolute value of <math>\ell</math> is bounded above by a seminorm on ''V'', then it extends to a linear functional on ''V'' still bounded by the seminorm. Geometrically, it is a generalization of the [[hyperplane separation theorem]].

definition: A topological vector space is said to have the Heine–Borel property if every closed and bounded subset is compact. Riesz's lemma says a Banach space with the Heine–Borel property must be finite-dimensional.
definition_raw: A topological vector space is said to have the [[Heine–Borel property]] if every closed and bounded subset is compact. Riesz's lemma says a Banach space with the Heine–Borel property must be finite-dimensional.

definition: A Hilbert space is an inner product space that is complete as a metric space.
definition_raw: A [[Hilbert space]] is an inner product space that is complete as a metric space.

definition: The Hilbert–Schmidt norm of a bounded operator $T$ on a Hilbert space is $\sum_i \|T e_i \|^2$ where $\{ e_i \}$ is an orthonormal basis of the Hilbert space.
definition_raw: The [[Hilbert–Schmidt norm]] of a bounded operator <math>T</math> on a Hilbert space is <math>\sum_i \|T e_i \|^2</math> where <math>\{ e_i \}</math> is an orthonormal basis of the Hilbert space.

definition: The index of a Fredholm operator $T : H_1 \to H_2$ is the integer $\operatorname{dim}(\operatorname{ker}(T^*)) - \operatorname{dim}(\operatorname{ker}(T))$.
definition_raw: The index of a Fredholm operator <math>T : H_1 \to H_2</math> is the integer <math>\operatorname{dim}(\operatorname{ker}(T^*)) - \operatorname{dim}(\operatorname{ker}(T))</math>.

definition: The index group of a unital Banach algebra is the quotient group $G(A)/G_0(A)$ where $G(A)$ is the unit group of A and $G_0(A)$ the identity component of the group.
definition_raw: The [[index group]] of a unital Banach algebra is the quotient group <math>G(A)/G_0(A)</math> where <math>G(A)</math> is the unit group of ''A'' and <math>G_0(A)</math> the identity component of the group.

definition: infra-barrelled
definition_raw: [[infra-barrelled]]

definition: An inner product on a real or complex vector space $V$ is a function $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{R}$ such that for each $v, w \in V$, (1) $x \mapsto \langle x, v \rangle$ is linear and (2) $\langle v, w \rangle = \overline{\langle w, v\rangle}$ where the bar means complex conjugate.
definition_raw: An [[inner product]] on a real or complex vector space <math>V</math> is a function <math>\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle : V \times V \to \mathbb{R}</math> such that for each <math>v, w \in V</math>, (1) <math>x \mapsto \langle x, v \rangle</math> is linear and (2) <math>\langle v, w \rangle = \overline{\langle w, v\rangle}</math> where the bar means complex conjugate.

definition: An involution of a Banach algebra A is an isometric endomorphism $A \to A, \, x \mapsto x^*$ that is conjugate-linear and such that $(xy)^* = (yx)^*$.
definition_raw: An [[Involution (mathematics)|involution]] of a Banach algebra ''A'' is an isometric endomorphism <math>A \to A, \, x \mapsto x^*</math> that is conjugate-linear and such that <math>(xy)^* = (yx)^*</math>.

definition: A linear isometry between normed vector spaces is a linear map preserving norm.
definition_raw: A [[linear isometry]] between normed vector spaces is a linear map preserving norm.

definition: The Kato–Rellich theorem
definition_raw: The [[Kato–Rellich theorem]]

definition: A Köthe sequence space. For now, see https://mathoverflow.net/questions/361048/on-k%C3%B6the-sequence-spaces
definition_raw: A [[Köthe sequence space]]. For now, see https://mathoverflow.net/questions/361048/on-k%C3%B6the-sequence-spaces

definition: The Krein–Milman theorem states: a nonempty compact convex subset of a locally convex space has an extremal point.
definition_raw: The [[Krein–Milman theorem]] states: a nonempty compact convex subset of a locally convex space has an extremal point.

definition: Krein–Smulian theorem
definition_raw: [[Krein–Smulian theorem]]

definition: Linear Operators is a three-value book by Dunford and Schwartz.
definition_raw: [[Linear Operators]] is a three-value book by Dunford and Schwartz.

definition: A locally convex algebra is an algebra whose underlying vector space is a locally convex space and whose multiplication is continuous with respect to the locally convex space topology.
definition_raw: A [[locally convex algebra]] is an algebra whose underlying vector space is a locally convex space and whose multiplication is continuous with respect to the locally convex space topology.

definition: Mazur–Ulam theorem.
definition_raw: [[Mazur–Ulam theorem]].

definition: Montel space.
definition_raw: [[Montel space]].

definition: A representation $(\pi, V)$ of an algebra $A$ is said to be nondegenerate if for each vector $v \in V$, there is an element $a \in A$ such that $\pi(a) v \ne 0$.
definition_raw: A representation <math>(\pi, V)</math> of an algebra <math>A</math> is said to be nondegenerate if for each vector <math>v \in V</math>, there is an element <math>a \in A</math> such that <math>\pi(a) v \ne 0</math>.

definition: noncommutative integration
definition_raw: [[noncommutative integration]]

definition: A norm on a vector space X is a real-valued function $\| \cdot \| : X \to \mathbb{R}$ such that for each scalar $a$ and vectors $x, y$ in $X$, (1) $\| ax\| = |a| \| x \|$, (2) (triangular inequality) $\| x + y \| \le \| x \| + \| y \|$ and (3) $\| x \| \ge 0$ where the equality holds only for $x = 0$.
definition_raw: A [[norm (mathematics)|norm]] on a vector space ''X'' is a real-valued function <math>\| \cdot \| : X \to \mathbb{R}</math> such that for each scalar <math>a</math> and vectors <math>x, y</math> in <math>X</math>, (1) <math>\| ax\| = |a| \| x \|</math>, (2) (triangular inequality) <math>\| x + y \| \le \| x \| + \| y \|</math> and (3) <math>\| x \| \ge 0</math> where the equality holds only for <math>x = 0</math>.

definition: An operator is normal if it and its adjoint commute.
definition_raw: An operator is [[normal operator|normal]] if it and its adjoint commute.

definition: nuclear operator.
definition_raw: [[nuclear operator]].

definition: A one parameter group of a unital Banach algebra A is a continuous group homomorphism from $(\mathbb{R}, +)$ to the unit group of A.
definition_raw: A [[one parameter group]] of a unital Banach algebra ''A'' is a continuous group homomorphism from <math>(\mathbb{R}, +)</math> to the unit group of ''A''.

definition: The open mapping theorem says a surjective continuous linear operator between Banach spaces is an open mapping.
definition_raw: The [[open mapping theorem (functional analysis)|open mapping theorem]] says a surjective continuous linear operator between Banach spaces is an open mapping.

definition: A subset S of a Hilbert space is orthonormal if, for each u, v in the set, $\langle u, v \rangle$ = 0 when $u \ne v$ and $= 1$ when $u = v$.
definition_raw: A subset ''S'' of a Hilbert space is [[orthonormal]] if, for each ''u'', ''v'' in the set, <math>\langle u, v \rangle</math> = 0 when <math>u \ne v</math> and <math>= 1</math> when <math>u = v</math>.

definition: Given a Hilbert space H and a closed subspace M, the orthogonal complement of M is the closed subspace $M^{\bot} = \{ x \in H | \langle x, y \rangle = 0, y \in M \}$.
definition_raw: Given a Hilbert space ''H'' and a closed subspace ''M'', the [[orthogonal complement]] of ''M'' is the closed subspace <math>M^{\bot} = \{ x \in H | \langle x, y \rangle = 0, y \in M \}</math>.

definition: Parseval's identity states: given an orthonormal basis S in a Hilbert space, $\| x \|^2 = \sum_{u \in S} |\langle x, u \rangle|^2$.
definition_raw: [[Parseval's identity]] states: given an orthonormal basis ''S'' in a Hilbert space, <math>\| x \|^2 = \sum_{u \in S} |\langle x, u \rangle|^2</math>.<ref name="sum convention">Here, the part of the assertion is <math>\sum_{u \in S} \cdots</math> is well-defined; i.e., when ''S'' is infinite, for countable totally ordered subsets <math>S' \subset S</math>, <math>\sum_{u \in S'} \cdots</math> is independent of <math>S'</math> and <math>\sum_{u \in S} \cdots</math> denotes the common value.</ref>

definition: A linear functional $\omega$ on an involutive Banach algebra is said to be positive if $\omega(x^* x) \ge 0$ for each element $x$ in the algebra.
definition_raw: A linear functional <math>\omega</math> on an involutive Banach algebra is said to be [[positive linear functional|positive]] if <math>\omega(x^* x) \ge 0</math> for each element <math>x</math> in the algebra.

definition: predual.
definition_raw: [[predual]].

definition: An operator T is called a projection if it is an idempotent; i.e., $T^2 = T$.
definition_raw: An operator ''T'' is called a [[projection (functional analysis)|projection]] if it is an idempotent; i.e., <math>T^2 = T</math>.

definition: Quasitrace.
definition_raw: [[Quasitrace]].

definition: See Radon measure.

definition: Riesz decomposition.

definition: Riesz's lemma.
definition_raw: [[Riesz's lemma]].

definition: A reflexive space is a topological vector space such that the natural map from the vector space to the second (topological) dual is an isomorphism.
definition_raw: A [[reflexive space]] is a topological vector space such that the natural map from the vector space to the second (topological) dual is an isomorphism.

definition: The resolvent of an element x of a unital Banach algebra is the complement in $\mathbb{C}$ of the spectrum of x.
definition_raw: The [[Resolvent set|resolvent]] of an element ''x'' of a unital Banach algebra is the complement in <math>\mathbb{C}</math> of the spectrum of ''x''.

definition: Ryll-Nardzewski fixed-point theorem.
definition_raw: [[Ryll-Nardzewski fixed-point theorem]].

definition: Schauder basis.

definition: Schatten class
definition_raw: [[Schatten class]]

definition: Michael selection theorem.
definition_raw: [[Michael selection theorem]].

definition: A self-adjoint operator is a bounded operator whose adjoint is itself. More generally, a closed densely defined operator is called self-adjoint if it coincides with the adjoint including the domain.
definition_raw: A [[self-adjoint operator]] is a bounded operator whose adjoint is itself. More generally, a closed densely defined operator is called self-adjoint if it coincides with the adjoint including the domain.

definition: A locally convex space is called semi-reflexive space if the canonical map to the second continuous dual is surjective.
definition_raw: A locally convex space is called [[semi-reflexive|semi-reflexive space]] if the canonical map to the second continuous dual is surjective.

definition: A separable Hilbert space is a Hilbert space admitting a finite or countable orthonormal basis.
definition_raw: A [[separable Hilbert space]] is a Hilbert space admitting a finite or countable orthonormal basis.

definition: The spectrum of an element x of a unital Banach algebra is the set of complex numbers $\lambda$ such that $x - \lambda$ is not invertible.
definition_raw: The spectrum of an element ''x'' of a unital Banach algebra is the set of complex numbers <math>\lambda</math> such that <math>x - \lambda</math> is not invertible.

definition: The spectral radius of an element x of a unital Banach algebra is $\sup_{\lambda} |\lambda|$ where the sup is over the spectrum of x.
definition_raw: The [[spectral radius]] of an element ''x'' of a unital Banach algebra is <math display="inline">\sup_{\lambda} |\lambda|</math> where the sup is over the spectrum of ''x''.

definition: A state is a positive linear functional of norm one.
definition_raw: A [[state (functional analysis)|state]] is a positive linear functional of norm one.

definition: Stone lemma.
definition_raw: [[Stone lemma]].

definition: A linear operator T on a pre-Hilbert space is symmetric if $(Tx, y) = (x, Ty).$
definition_raw: A linear operator ''T'' on a pre-Hilbert space is [[symmetric operator|symmetric]] if <math>(Tx, y) = (x, Ty).</math>

definition: See topological tensor product. Note it is still somewhat of an open problem to define or work out a correct tensor product of topological vector spaces, including Banach spaces.
definition_raw: See [[topological tensor product]]. Note it is still somewhat of an open problem to define or work out a correct tensor product of topological vector spaces, including Banach spaces.

definition: A topological vector space is a vector space equipped with a topology such that (1) the topology is Hausdorff and (2) the addition $(x, y) \mapsto x + y$ as well as scalar multiplication $(\lambda, x) \mapsto \lambda x$ are continuous.
definition_raw: A [[topological vector space]] is a vector space equipped with a [[topology (mathematics)|topology]] such that (1) the topology is [[Hausdorff topology|Hausdorff]] and (2) the addition <math>(x, y) \mapsto x + y</math> as well as scalar multiplication <math>(\lambda, x) \mapsto \lambda x</math> are continuous.

definition: ultraweak topology.
definition_raw: [[ultraweak topology]].

definition: An unbounded operator is a partially defined linear operator, usually defined on a dense subspace.
definition_raw: An [[unbounded operator]] is a partially defined linear operator, usually defined on a dense subspace.

definition: The uniform boundedness principle states: given a set of operators between Banach spaces, if $\sup_T |Tx| < \infty$, sup over the set, for each x in the Banach space, then $\sup_T \|T\| < \infty$.
definition_raw: The [[uniform boundedness principle]] states: given a set of operators between Banach spaces, if <math display="inline">\sup_T |Tx| < \infty</math>, sup over the set, for each ''x'' in the Banach space, then <math display="inline">\sup_T \|T\| < \infty</math>.

definition: A unitary operator between Hilbert spaces is an invertible bounded linear operator such that the inverse is the adjoint of the operator.
definition_raw: A [[unitary operator]] between Hilbert spaces is an invertible bounded linear operator such that the inverse is the adjoint of the operator.

definition: A von Neumann algebra.
definition_raw: A [[von Neumann algebra]].

definition: A W*-algebra is a C*-algebra that admits a faithful representation on a Hilbert space such that the image of the representation is a von Neumann algebra.
definition_raw: A W*-algebra is a C*-algebra that admits a faithful representation on a Hilbert space such that the image of the representation is a von Neumann algebra.

definition: Given a preference ν on the outcome space, an outcome a is a condorcet winner if all non-dummy players prefer a to all other outcomes.
definition_raw: Given a '''preference''' ''ν'' on the '''outcome space''', an outcome '''a''' is a condorcet winner if all non-dummy players prefer '''a''' to all other outcomes.

definition: In relation to game theory, refers to the question of the existence of an algorithm that can and will return an answer as to whether a game can be solved or not.
definition_raw: In relation to game theory, refers to the question of the existence of an algorithm that can and will return an answer as to whether a game can be solved or not.<ref>[https://mathoverflow.net/q/27967 Mathoverflow.net/Decidability-of-chess-on-an-infinite-board] Decidability-of-chess-on-an-infinite-board</ref>

definition: A subfield of set theory that examines the conditions under which one or the other player of a game has a winning strategy, and the consequences of the existence of such strategies. Games studied in set theory are Gale–Stewart games – two-player games of perfect information in which the players make an infinite sequence of moves and there are no draws.

Games studied in set theory are Gale–Stewart games – two-player games of perfect information in which the players make an infinite sequence of moves and there are no draws.

definition: In game theory, a strictly determined game is a two-player zero-sum game that has at least one Nash equilibrium with both players using pure strategies.
definition_raw: In game theory, a strictly determined game is a two-player [[zero-sum]] game that has at least one [[Nash equilibrium]] with both players using [[pure strategy|pure strategies]].<ref>{{cite book|title=A gentle introduction to game theory|author=Saul Stahl|page=[https://archive.org/details/gentleintroducti0000stah/page/54 54]|chapter=Solutions of zero-sum games|publisher=AMS Bookstore|year=1999|isbn=9780821813393|chapter-url=https://archive.org/details/gentleintroducti0000stah/page/54}}</ref><ref

definition: A player is a strong dictator if he can guarantee any outcome regardless of the other players. $m \in \mathbb{N}$ is a weak dictator if he can guarantee any outcome, but his strategies for doing so might depend on the complement strategy vector. Naturally, every strong dictator is a weak dictator. Formally: n is a Strong dictator if: $ \forall a \in \mathrm{A}, \; \exist \sigma\ _n \in \Sigma\ ^n \; s.t. \; \forall \sigma\ _{-n} \in \Sigma\ ^{-n}: \; \Gamma\ (\sigma\ _{-n},\sigma\ _n) = a $ m is a Weak dictator if: $ \forall a \in \mathrm{A}, \; \forall \sigma\ _{-m} \in \Sigma\ ^{-m} \; \exist \sigma\ _m \in \Sigma\ ^m \; s.t. \; \Gamma\ (\sigma\ _{-m},\sigma\ _m) = a $ Another way to put it is: :A strong dictator is $\alpha$-effective for every possible outcome. :A weak dictator is $\beta$-effective for every possible outcome. :A game can have no more than one strong dictator. Some games have multiple weak dictators (in rock-paper-scissors both players are weak dictators but none is a strong dictator). Also see Effectiveness. Antonym: dummy.

definition: A player i is a dummy if he has no effect on the outcome of the game. I.e. if the outcome of the game is insensitive to player is strategy. Antonyms: say, veto, dictator.
definition_raw: A player '''i''' is a dummy if he has no effect on the outcome of the game. I.e. if the outcome of the game is insensitive to player '''i''''s strategy. Antonyms: ''say'', ''veto'', ''dictator''.

definition: A coalition (or a single player) S is effective for a if it can force a to be the outcome of the game. S is α-effective if the members of S have strategies s.t. no matter what the complement of S does, the outcome will be a. S is β-effective if for any strategies of the complement of S, the members of S can answer with strategies that ensure outcome a.
definition_raw: A coalition (or a single player) '''S''' is ''effective for '' '''a''' if it can force '''a''' to be the outcome of the game. '''S''' is α-effective if the members of '''S''' have strategies s.t. no matter what the complement of '''S''' does, the outcome will be '''a'''. '''S''' is β-effective if for any strategies of the complement of '''S''', the members of '''S''' can answer with strategies that ensure outcome '''a'''.

definition: is a game with finitely many players, each of which has a finite set of strategies.
definition_raw: is a game with finitely many players, each of which has a finite set of '''strategies'''.

definition: refers to the coalition containing all players. In cooperative games it is often assumed that the grand coalition forms and the purpose of the game is to find stable imputations.

definition: An outcome a of game form π is (strongly) pareto efficient if it is undominated under all preference profiles.
definition_raw: An '''outcome''' ''a'' of '''game form''' ''π'' is (strongly) '''pareto efficient''' if it is '''undominated''' under all '''preference profiles'''.

definition: A game whose outcome (win, lose or draw) can be correctly predicted assuming perfect play from all players.

definition: A veto denotes the ability (or right) of some player to prevent a specific alternative from being the outcome of the game. A player who has that ability is called a veto player. Antonym: Dummy.
definition_raw: A veto denotes the ability (or right) of some player to prevent a specific alternative from being the outcome of the game. A player who has that ability is called '''a veto player'''. Antonym: ''Dummy''.

definition: See limit point.

definition: The topology of a space X is an Alexandrov topology (or is finitely generated) if arbitrary intersections of open sets in X are open, or equivalently, if arbitrary unions of closed sets are closed, or, again equivalently, if the open sets are the upper sets of a poset.
definition_raw: The topology of a space ''X'' is an [[Alexandrov topology]] (or is '''finitely generated''') if arbitrary intersections of open sets in ''X'' are open, or equivalently, if arbitrary unions of closed sets are closed, or, again equivalently, if the open sets are the [[upper set]]s of a [[poset]].<ref>Vickers (1989) p.22</ref>

definition: A space is almost discrete if every open set is closed (hence clopen). The almost discrete spaces are precisely the finitely generated zero-dimensional spaces.
definition_raw: A space is almost discrete if every open set is closed (hence clopen). The almost discrete spaces are precisely the finitely generated zero-dimensional spaces.

definition: A subset A of a topological space X is α-open if $A \subseteq \operatorname{Int}_X \left( \operatorname{Cl}_X \left( \operatorname{Int}_X A \right) \right)$, and the complement of such a set is α-closed.
definition_raw: A subset ''A'' of a topological space ''X'' is α-open if <math>A \subseteq \operatorname{Int}_X \left( \operatorname{Cl}_X \left( \operatorname{Int}_X A \right) \right)</math>, and the complement of such a set is α-closed.{{sfn|Hart|Nagata|Vaughan|2004|p=9}}

definition: An approach space is a generalization of metric space based on point-to-set distances, instead of point-to-point.
definition_raw: An [[approach space]] is a generalization of metric space based on point-to-set distances, instead of point-to-point.

definition: This has two distinct common meanings: #A space is a Baire space if the intersection of any countable collection of dense open sets is dense; see Baire space. #Baire space is the set of all functions from the natural numbers to the natural numbers, with the topology of pointwise convergence; see Baire space (set theory).
definition_raw: This has two distinct common meanings: #A space is a '''Baire space''' if the intersection of any [[countable]] collection of dense open sets is dense; see [[Baire space]]. #'''Baire space''' is the set of all functions from the natural numbers to the natural numbers, with the topology of pointwise convergence; see [[Baire space (set theory)]].

definition: A collection B of open sets is a base (or basis) for a topology $\tau$ if every open set in $\tau$ is a union of sets in $ B $. The topology $\tau$ is the smallest topology on $X$ containing $B$ and is said to be generated by $B$.
definition_raw: A collection ''B'' of open sets is a [[base (topology)|base]] (or '''basis''') for a topology <math>\tau</math> if every open set in <math>\tau</math> is a union of sets in <math> B </math>. The topology <math>\tau</math> is the smallest topology on <math>X</math> containing <math>B</math> and is said to be generated by <math>B</math>.

definition: A subset $A$ of a topological space $X$ is b-open if $A \subseteq \operatorname{Int}_X \left( \operatorname{Cl}_X A \right) \cup \operatorname{Cl}_X \left( \operatorname{Int}_X A \right)$. The complement of a b-open set is b-closed.
definition_raw: A subset <math>A</math> of a topological space <math>X</math> is b-open if <math>A \subseteq \operatorname{Int}_X \left( \operatorname{Cl}_X A \right) \cup \operatorname{Cl}_X \left( \operatorname{Int}_X A \right)</math>. The complement of a b-open set is b-closed.{{sfn|Hart|Nagata|Vaughan|2004|p=9}}

definition: The Borel algebra on a topological space $ (X,\tau)$ is the smallest $\sigma$-algebra containing all the open sets. It is obtained by taking intersection of all $\sigma$-algebras on $ X $ containing $ \tau $.
definition_raw: The [[Borel algebra]] on a topological space <math> (X,\tau)</math> is the smallest [[Sigma-algebra|<math>\sigma</math>-algebra]] containing all the open sets. It is obtained by taking intersection of all <math>\sigma</math>-algebras on <math> X </math> containing <math> \tau </math>.

definition: A Borel set is an element of a Borel algebra.
definition_raw: A Borel set is an element of a Borel algebra.

definition: The boundary (or frontier) of a set is the set's closure minus its interior. Equivalently, the boundary of a set is the intersection of its closure with the closure of its complement. Boundary of a set $ A $ is denoted by $ \partial A$ or $bd$ $A$.
definition_raw: The [[boundary (topology)|boundary]] (or '''frontier''') of a set is the set's closure minus its interior.  Equivalently, the boundary of a set is the intersection of its closure with the closure of its complement. Boundary of a set <math> A </math> is denoted by <math> \partial A</math> or <math>bd</math> <math>A</math>.

definition: A set in a metric space is bounded if it has finite diameter. Equivalently, a set is bounded if it is contained in some open ball of finite radius. A function taking values in a metric space is bounded if its image is a bounded set.
definition_raw: A set in a metric space is [[bounded set|bounded]] if it has [[finite set|finite]] diameter. Equivalently, a set is bounded if it is contained in some open ball of finite radius. A [[function (mathematics)|function]] taking values in a metric space is [[bounded function|bounded]] if its [[image (functions)|image]] is a bounded set.

definition: The category Top has topological spaces as objects and continuous maps as morphisms.
definition_raw: The [[category theory|category]] '''[[Category of topological spaces|Top]]''' has [[topological space]]s as [[object (category theory)|objects]] and [[continuous map]]s as [[morphism]]s.

definition: A sequence {xn} in a metric space (M, d) is a Cauchy sequence if, for every positive real number r, there is an integer N such that for all integers m, n > N, we have d(xm, xn) < r.
definition_raw: A [[sequence]] {''x''<sub>''n''</sub>} in a metric space (''M'', ''d'') is a [[Cauchy sequence]] if, for every [[positive number|positive]] [[real number]] ''r'', there is an [[integer]] ''N'' such that for all integers ''m'', ''n'' > ''N'', we have ''d''(''x''<sub>''m''</sub>, ''x''<sub>''n''</sub>) < ''r''.

definition: A set is clopen if it is both open and closed.
definition_raw: A set is [[clopen set|clopen]] if it is both open and closed.

definition: If (M, d) is a metric space, a closed ball is a set of the form D(x; r) := {y in M : d(x, y) ≤ r}, where x is in M and r is a positive real number, the radius of the ball. A closed ball of radius r is a closed r-ball. Every closed ball is a closed set in the topology induced on M by d. Note that the closed ball D(x; r) might not be equal to the closure of the open ball B(x; r).
definition_raw: If (''M'', ''d'') is a [[metric space]], a closed ball is a set of the form ''D''(''x''; ''r'') := {''y'' in ''M'' : ''d''(''x'', ''y'') ≤ ''r''}, where ''x'' is in ''M'' and ''r'' is a [[positive number|positive]] [[real number]], the '''radius''' of the ball. A closed ball of radius ''r'' is a '''closed ''r''-ball'''. Every closed ball is a closed set in the topology induced on ''M'' by ''d''. Note that the closed ball ''D''(''x''; ''r'') might not be equal to the [[closure (topology)|closure]

definition: A set is closed if its complement is a member of the topology.
definition_raw: A set is [[Closed set|closed]] if its complement is a member of the topology.

definition: A function from one space to another is closed if the image of every closed set is closed.
definition_raw: A function from one space to another is closed if the [[image (mathematics)|image]] of every closed set is closed.

definition: The closure of a set is the smallest closed set containing the original set. It is equal to the intersection of all closed sets which contain it. An element of the closure of a set S is a point of closure of S.
definition_raw: The [[closure (topology)|closure]] of a set is the smallest closed set containing the original set. It is equal to the intersection of all closed sets which contain it. An element of the closure of a set ''S'' is a '''point of closure''' of ''S''.

definition: See Kuratowski closure axioms.
definition_raw: See '''[[Kuratowski closure axioms]]'''.

definition: If X is a set, and if T1 and T2 are topologies on X, then T1 is coarser (or smaller, weaker) than T2 if T1 is contained in T2. Beware, some authors, especially analysts, use the term stronger.
definition_raw: If ''X'' is a set, and if ''T''<sub>1</sub> and ''T''<sub>2</sub> are topologies on ''X'', then ''T''<sub>1</sub> is [[coarser topology|coarser]] (or '''smaller''', '''weaker''') than ''T''<sub>2</sub> if ''T''<sub>1</sub> is contained in ''T''<sub>2</sub>. Beware, some authors, especially [[mathematical analysis|analyst]]s, use the term '''stronger'''.

definition: A subset A of a space X is comeagre (comeager) if its complement X\A is meagre. Also called residual.
definition_raw: A subset ''A'' of a space ''X'' is '''comeagre''' ('''comeager''') if its [[complement (set theory)|complement]] ''X''\''A'' is [[meagre set|meagre]]. Also called '''residual'''.

definition: A space is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover. Every compact space is Lindelöf and paracompact. Therefore, every compact Hausdorff space is normal. See also quasicompact.
definition_raw: A space is [[compact space|compact]] if every open cover has a [[finite set|finite]] subcover. Every compact space is Lindelöf and paracompact. Therefore, every compact [[Hausdorff space]] is normal. See also '''quasicompact'''.

definition: The compact-open topology on the set C(X, Y) of all continuous maps between two spaces X and Y is defined as follows: given a compact subset K of X and an open subset U of Y, let V(K, U) denote the set of all maps f in C(X, Y) such that f(K) is contained in U. Then the collection of all such V(K, U) is a subbase for the compact-open topology.
definition_raw: The [[compact-open topology]] on the set ''C''(''X'', ''Y'') of all continuous maps between two spaces ''X'' and ''Y'' is defined as follows: given a compact subset ''K'' of ''X'' and an open subset ''U'' of ''Y'', let ''V''(''K'', ''U'') denote the set of all maps ''f'' in ''C''(''X'', ''Y'') such that ''f''(''K'') is contained in ''U''. Then the collection of all such ''V''(''K'', ''U'') is a subbase for the compact-open topology.

definition: A metric space is complete if every Cauchy sequence converges.
definition_raw: A metric space is [[complete space|complete]] if every Cauchy sequence converges.

definition: See complete space.
definition_raw: See '''[[complete space]]'''.

definition: A space is completely normal if any two separated sets have disjoint neighbourhoods.
definition_raw: A space is completely normal if any two separated sets have [[Disjoint sets|disjoint]] neighbourhoods.

definition: A completely normal Hausdorff space (or T5 space) is a completely normal T1 space. (A completely normal space is Hausdorff if and only if it is T1, so the terminology is consistent.) Every completely normal Hausdorff space is normal Hausdorff.
definition_raw: A completely normal Hausdorff space (or [[T5 space|'''T<sub>5</sub>''' space]]) is a completely normal T<sub>1</sub> space. (A completely normal space is Hausdorff [[if and only if]] it is T<sub>1</sub>, so the terminology is [[consistent]].) Every completely normal Hausdorff space is normal Hausdorff.

definition: A space is completely regular if, whenever C is a closed set and x is a point not in C, then C and {x} are functionally separated.
definition_raw: A space is [[Completely regular space|completely regular]] if, whenever ''C'' is a closed set and ''x'' is a point not in ''C'', then ''C'' and {''x''} are functionally separated.

definition: See Tychonoff.
definition_raw: See '''[[Tychonoff space|Tychonoff]]'''.

definition: See Connected component/Path-connected component.
definition_raw: See '''[[Connected space|Connected component]]'''/'''Path-connected component'''.

definition: A space is connected if it is not the union of a pair of disjoint nonempty open sets. Equivalently, a space is connected if the only clopen sets are the whole space and the empty set.
definition_raw: A space is [[connected (topology)|connected]] if it is not the union of a pair of [[Disjoint sets|disjoint]] nonempty open sets. Equivalently, a space is connected if the only clopen sets are the whole space and the empty set.

definition: A connected component of a space is a maximal nonempty connected subspace. Each connected component is closed, and the set of connected components of a space is a partition of that space.
definition_raw: A [[connected space|connected component]] of a space is a maximal nonempty connected subspace. Each connected component is closed, and the set of connected components of a space is a [[partition of a set|partition]] of that space.

definition: A function from one space to another is continuous if the preimage of every open set is open.
definition_raw: A function from one space to another is [[continuity (topology)|continuous]] if the [[preimage]] of every open set is open.

definition: A space is called a continuum if it a compact, connected Hausdorff space.
definition_raw: A space is called a continuum if it a compact, connected Hausdorff space.

definition: A space X is contractible if the identity map on X is homotopic to a constant map. Every contractible space is simply connected.
definition_raw: A space ''X'' is contractible if the [[identity function|identity map]] on ''X'' is homotopic to a constant map. Every contractible space is simply connected.

definition: If {Xi} is a collection of spaces and X is the (set-theoretic) disjoint union of {Xi}, then the coproduct topology (or disjoint union topology, topological sum of the Xi) on X is the finest topology for which all the injection maps are continuous.
definition_raw: If {''X''<sub>''i''</sub>} is a collection of spaces and ''X'' is the (set-theoretic) [[disjoint union]] of {''X''<sub>''i''</sub>}, then the coproduct topology (or '''disjoint union topology''', '''topological sum''' of the ''X''<sub>''i''</sub>) on ''X'' is the finest topology for which all the injection maps are continuous.

definition: A continuous image of some separable metric space.
definition_raw: A [[continuous function|continuous]] [[Image (mathematics)|image]] of some [[Separable space|separable]] [[metric space]].<ref>{{cite book | title=Encyclopedia of Distances | first1=Michel Marie | last1=Deza | first2=Elena | last2=Deza |author2-link=Elena Deza| publisher=[[Springer-Verlag]] | year=2012 | isbn=978-3642309588 | page=64 }}</ref>

definition: A space X satisfies the countable chain condition if every family of non-empty, pairswise disjoint open sets is countable.
definition_raw: A space ''X'' satisfies the countable chain condition if every family of non-empty, pairswise disjoint open sets is countable.

definition: A space is countably compact if every countable open cover has a finite subcover. Every countably compact space is pseudocompact and weakly countably compact.
definition_raw: A space is countably compact if every [[countable]] open cover has a [[finite set|finite]] subcover. Every countably compact space is pseudocompact and weakly countably compact.

definition: A collection of subsets of a space X is countably locally finite (or σ-locally finite) if it is the union of a countable collection of locally finite collections of subsets of X.
definition_raw: A collection of subsets of a space ''X'' is '''countably locally finite''' (or '''σ-locally finite''') if it is the union of a [[countable]] collection of locally finite collections of subsets of ''X''.

definition: A collection of subsets of a space is a cover (or covering) of that space if the union of the collection is the whole space.
definition_raw: A collection of subsets of a space is a cover (or '''covering''') of that space if the union of the collection is the whole space.

definition: See Cover.
definition_raw: See '''Cover'''.

definition: If X is a connected space with more than one point, then a point x of X is a cut point if the subspace X − {x} is disconnected.
definition_raw: If ''X'' is a connected space with more than one point, then a point ''x'' of ''X'' is a cut point if the subspace ''X'' − {''x''} is disconnected.

definition: A point x of a topological space X is a δ-cluster point of a subset A if $A \cap \operatorname{Int}_X\left( \operatorname{Cl}_X(U) \right) \neq \emptyset$ for every open neighborhood U of x in X. The subset A is δ-closed if it is equal to the set of its δ-cluster points, and δ-open if its complement is δ-closed.
definition_raw: A point ''x'' of a topological space ''X'' is a δ-cluster point of a subset ''A'' if <math>A \cap \operatorname{Int}_X\left( \operatorname{Cl}_X(U) \right) \neq \emptyset</math> for every open neighborhood ''U'' of ''x'' in ''X''.  The subset ''A'' is δ-closed if it is equal to the set of its δ-cluster points, and δ-open if its complement is δ-closed.{{sfn|Hart|Nagata|Vaughan|2004|pp=8–9}}

definition: A set is dense if it has nonempty intersection with every nonempty open set. Equivalently, a set is dense if its closure is the whole space.
definition_raw: A set is dense if it has nonempty intersection with every nonempty open set. Equivalently, a set is dense if its closure is the whole space.

definition: A set is dense-in-itself if it has no isolated point.
definition_raw: A set is dense-in-itself if it has no [[isolated point]].

definition: the minimal cardinality of a dense subset of a topological space. A set of density ℵ0 is a separable space.
definition_raw: the minimal cardinality of a dense subset of a topological space.  A set of density ℵ<sub>0</sub> is a [[separable space]].<ref>Nagata (1985) p.104</ref>

definition: If X is a space and S is a subset of X, the derived set of S in X is the set of limit points of S in X.
definition_raw: If ''X'' is a space and ''S'' is a subset of ''X'', the derived set of ''S'' in ''X'' is the set of limit points of ''S'' in ''X''.

definition: A topological space with a development.
definition_raw: A topological space with a [[Development (topology)|development]].<ref name=ss163/>

definition: A countable collection of open covers of a topological space, such that for any closed set C and any point p in its complement there exists a cover in the collection such that every neighbourhood of p in the cover is disjoint from C.
definition_raw: A [[countable set|countable]] collection of [[open cover]]s of a topological space, such that for any closed set ''C'' and any point ''p'' in its complement there exists a cover in the collection such that every neighbourhood of ''p'' in the cover is [[disjoint sets|disjoint]] from ''C''.<ref name=ss163/>

definition: If (M, d) is a metric space and S is a subset of M, the diameter of S is the supremum of the distances d(x, y), where x and y range over S.
definition_raw: If (''M'', ''d'') is a metric space and ''S'' is a subset of ''M'', the diameter of ''S'' is the [[supremum]] of the distances ''d''(''x'', ''y''), where ''x'' and ''y'' range over ''S''.

definition: The discrete metric on a set X is the function d : X × X → R such that for all x, y in X, d(x, x) = 0 and d(x, y) = 1 if x ≠ y. The discrete metric induces the discrete topology on X.
definition_raw: The discrete metric on a set ''X'' is the function ''d'' : ''X'' × ''X''  →  '''[[real number|R]]''' such that for all ''x'', ''y'' in ''X'', ''d''(''x'', ''x'') = 0 and ''d''(''x'', ''y'') = 1 if ''x'' ≠ ''y''. The discrete metric induces the discrete topology on ''X''.

definition: A space X is discrete if every subset of X is open. We say that X carries the discrete topology.
definition_raw: A space ''X'' is [[discrete space|discrete]] if every subset of ''X'' is open. We say that ''X'' carries the '''discrete topology'''.<ref name=ss41>Steen & Seebach (1978) p.41</ref>

definition: See discrete space.
definition_raw: See '''[[discrete space]]'''.

definition: See Coproduct topology.
definition_raw: See '''Coproduct topology'''.

definition: If X is a connected space with more than one point, then a point x of X is a dispersion point if the subspace X − {x} is hereditarily disconnected (its only connected components are the one-point sets).
definition_raw: If ''X'' is a connected space with more than one point, then a point ''x'' of ''X'' is a dispersion point if the subspace ''X'' − {''x''} is hereditarily disconnected (its only connected components are the one-point sets).

definition: See metric space.
definition_raw: See '''[[metric space]]'''.

definition: See Uniform space.
definition_raw: See '''[[Uniform space]]'''.

definition: The exterior of a set is the interior of its complement.
definition_raw: The exterior of a set is the interior of its complement.

definition: The Eilenberg–Wojdysławski theorem says every bounded metric space can be embedded into a Banach space as a closed subset of the convex hull of the image.
definition_raw: The [[Eilenberg–Wojdysławski theorem]] says every bounded metric space can be embedded into a Banach space as a closed subset of the convex hull of the image.

definition: An Fσ set is a countable union of closed sets.
definition_raw: An [[F-sigma set|''F''<sub>σ</sub> set]] is a [[countable]] union of closed sets.<ref name=ss162/>

definition: See also: Filters in topology. A filter on a space X is a nonempty family F of subsets of X such that the following conditions hold: # The empty set is not in F. # The intersection of any finite number of elements of F is again in F. # If A is in F and if B contains A, then B is in F.
definition_raw: See also: [[Filters in topology]]. A filter on a space ''X'' is a nonempty family ''F'' of subsets of ''X'' such that the following conditions hold: # The [[empty set]] is not in ''F''. # The intersection of any [[finite set|finite]] number of elements of ''F'' is again in ''F''. # If ''A'' is in ''F'' and if ''B'' contains ''A'', then ''B'' is in ''F''.

definition: On a set X with respect to a family of functions into $X$, is the finest topology on X which makes those functions continuous.
definition_raw: On a set ''X'' with respect to a family of functions into <math>X</math>, is the [[finest topology]] on ''X'' which makes those functions [[continuous function (topology)|continuous]].<ref>{{cite book | first=Stephen | last=Willard | title=General Topology | url=https://archive.org/details/generaltopology00will_0 | url-access=registration | year=1970 | publisher=Addison-Wesley | location=Reading, MA | zbl=0205.26601 | series=Addison-Wesley Series in Mathematics| isbn=9780201087079 }}</ref>

definition: On Euclidean space $\R^n$, the coarsest topology making all subharmonic functions (equivalently all superharmonic functions) continuous.
definition_raw: On [[Euclidean space]] <math>\R^n</math>, the coarsest topology making all [[subharmonic function]]s (equivalently all superharmonic functions) continuous.<ref>{{cite book | first=John B. | last=Conway | author-link=John B. Conway | isbn=0-387-94460-5 | series=[[Graduate Texts in Mathematics]] | volume=159 | title=Functions of One Complex Variable II | publisher=[[Springer-Verlag]] | year=1995 | zbl=0887.30003 | pages=367–376 }}</ref>

definition: If X is a set, and if T1 and T2 are topologies on X, then T2 is finer (or larger, stronger) than T1 if T2 contains T1. Beware, some authors, especially analysts, use the term weaker.
definition_raw: If ''X'' is a set, and if ''T''<sub>1</sub> and ''T''<sub>2</sub> are topologies on ''X'', then ''T''<sub>2</sub> is [[finer topology|finer]] (or '''larger''', '''stronger''') than ''T''<sub>1</sub> if ''T''<sub>2</sub> contains ''T''<sub>1</sub>. Beware, some authors, especially [[mathematical analysis|analyst]]s, use the term '''weaker'''.

definition: See Alexandrov topology.
definition_raw: See '''[[Alexandrov topology]]'''.

definition: See Meagre.
definition_raw: See '''[[Meagre set|Meagre]]'''.

definition: A space is first-countable if every point has a countable local base.
definition_raw: A space is [[First-countable space|first-countable]] if every point has a [[countable]] local base.

definition: See T1.
definition_raw: See '''[[T1 space|T<sub>1</sub>]]'''.

definition: See Boundary.
definition_raw: See '''[[Boundary (topology)|Boundary]]'''.

definition: A compact subset K of the complex plane is called full if its complement is connected. For example, the closed unit disk is full, while the unit circle is not.
definition_raw: A [[compact space|compact]] subset ''K'' of the [[complex plane]] is called '''full''' if its [[complement (set theory)|complement]] is connected. For example, the [[closed unit disk]] is full, while the [[unit circle]] is not.

definition: Two sets A and B in a space X are functionally separated if there is a continuous map f: X → [0, 1] such that f(A) = 0 and f(B) = 1.
definition_raw: Two sets ''A'' and ''B'' in a space ''X'' are functionally separated if there is a continuous map ''f'': ''X''  →  [0, 1] such that ''f''(''A'') = 0 and ''f''(''B'') = 1.

definition: A Gδ set or inner limiting set is a countable intersection of open sets.
definition_raw: A [[G-delta set|''G''<sub>δ</sub> set]] or '''inner limiting set''' is a [[countable]] intersection of open sets.<ref name=ss162>Steen & Seebach (1978) p.162</ref>

definition: A space in which every closed set is a Gδ set.
definition_raw: A space in which every closed set is a ''G''<sub>δ</sub> set.<ref name=ss162/>

definition: A generic point for a closed set is a point for which the closed set is the closure of the singleton set containing that point.
definition_raw: A [[generic point]] for a closed set is a point for which the closed set is the closure of the singleton set containing that point.<ref>Vickers (1989) p.65</ref>

definition: A Hausdorff space (or T2 space) is one in which every two distinct points have disjoint neighbourhoods. Every Hausdorff space is T1.
definition_raw: A [[Hausdorff space]] (or '''[[T2 space|T<sub>2</sub>]] space''') is one in which every two distinct points have [[Disjoint sets|disjoint]] neighbourhoods. Every Hausdorff space is T<sub>1</sub>.

definition: A space is H-closed, or Hausdorff closed or absolutely closed, if it is closed in every Hausdorff space containing it.
definition_raw: A space is H-closed, or '''Hausdorff closed''' or '''absolutely closed''', if it is closed in every Hausdorff space containing it.

definition: A space is hemicompact, if there is a sequence of compact subsets so that every compact subset is contained in one of them.
definition_raw: A space is hemicompact, if there is a sequence of compact subsets so that every compact subset is contained in one of them.

definition: A space is hereditarily P for some property P if every subspace is also P.
definition_raw: A space is hereditarily ''P'' for some property ''P'' if every subspace is also ''P''.

definition: A property of spaces is said to be hereditary if whenever a space has that property, then so does every subspace of it. For example, second-countability is a hereditary property.
definition_raw: A property of spaces is said to be hereditary if whenever a space has that property, then so does every subspace of it.<ref name=ss4>Steen & Seebach p.4</ref> For example, second-countability is a hereditary property.

definition: If X and Y are spaces, a homeomorphism from X to Y is a bijective function f : X → Y such that f and f−1 are continuous. The spaces X and Y are then said to be homeomorphic. From the standpoint of topology, homeomorphic spaces are identical.
definition_raw: If ''X'' and ''Y'' are spaces, a [[homeomorphism]] from ''X'' to ''Y'' is a [[bijection|bijective]] function ''f'' : ''X'' → ''Y'' such that ''f'' and ''f''<sup>−1</sup> are continuous. The spaces ''X'' and ''Y'' are then said to be '''homeomorphic'''. From the standpoint of topology, homeomorphic spaces are identical.

definition: A space X is homogeneous if, for every x and y in X, there is a homeomorphism f : X → X such that f(x) = y. Intuitively, the space looks the same at every point. Every topological group is homogeneous.
definition_raw: A space ''X'' is [[Homogeneous space|homogeneous]] if, for every ''x'' and ''y'' in ''X'', there is a homeomorphism ''f'' : ''X''  →  ''X'' such that ''f''(''x'') = ''y''. Intuitively, the space looks the same at every point. Every [[topological group]] is homogeneous.

definition: Two continuous maps f, g : X → Y are homotopic (in Y) if there is a continuous map H : X × [0, 1] → Y such that H(x, 0) = f(x) and H(x, 1) = g(x) for all x in X. Here, X × [0, 1] is given the product topology. The function H is called a homotopy (in Y) between f and g.
definition_raw: Two continuous maps  ''f'', ''g'' : ''X''  →  ''Y'' are [[homotopic]] (in ''Y'') if there is a continuous map ''H'' : ''X'' × [0, 1]  →  ''Y'' such that ''H''(''x'', 0) = ''f''(''x'') and ''H''(''x'', 1) = ''g''(''x'') for all ''x'' in ''X''. Here, ''X'' × [0, 1] is given the product topology. The function ''H'' is called a '''homotopy''' (in ''Y'') between ''f'' and ''g''.

definition: See Homotopic maps.
definition_raw: See '''[[homotopic|Homotopic maps]]'''.

definition: A space is hyperconnected if no two non-empty open sets are disjoint Every hyperconnected space is connected.
definition_raw: A space is hyperconnected if no two non-empty open sets are disjoint<ref name=ss29/>  Every hyperconnected space is connected.<ref name=ss29/>

definition: See Quotient map.
definition_raw: See '''[[Quotient map (topology)|Quotient map]]'''.

definition: See Quotient space.
definition_raw: See '''[[Quotient space (topology)|Quotient space]]'''.

definition: See Trivial topology.
definition_raw: See '''[[Trivial topology]]'''.

definition: See Hilbert manifold and Q-manifolds, i.e. (generalized) manifolds modelled on the Hilbert space and on the Hilbert cube respectively.
definition_raw: See '''[[Hilbert manifold]]''' and '''[[Q-manifolds]]''', i.e. (generalized) manifolds modelled on the Hilbert space and on the Hilbert cube respectively.

definition: A Gδ set.
definition_raw: A ''G''<sub>δ</sub> set.<ref name=ss162/>

definition: The interior of a set is the largest open set contained in the original set. It is equal to the union of all open sets contained in it. An element of the interior of a set S is an interior point of S.
definition_raw: The [[interior (topology)|interior]] of a set is the largest open set contained in the original set. It is equal to the union of all open sets contained in it. An element of the interior of a set ''S'' is an '''interior point''' of ''S''.

definition: See Interior.
definition_raw: See '''[[Interior (topology)|Interior]]'''.

definition: A point x is an isolated point if the singleton {x} is open. More generally, if S is a subset of a space X, and if x is a point of S, then x is an isolated point of S if {x} is open in the subspace topology on S.
