definition_raw: A point ''x'' is an [[isolated point]] if the [[singleton (mathematics)|singleton]] {''x''} is open. More generally, if ''S'' is a subset of a space ''X'', and if ''x'' is a point of ''S'', then ''x'' is an isolated point of ''S'' if {''x''} is open in the subspace topology on ''S''.

definition: If M1 and M2 are metric spaces, an isometric isomorphism from M1 to M2 is a bijective isometry f : M1 → M2. The metric spaces are then said to be isometrically isomorphic. From the standpoint of metric space theory, isometrically isomorphic spaces are identical.
definition_raw: If ''M''<sub>1</sub> and ''M''<sub>2</sub> are metric spaces, an isometric isomorphism from ''M''<sub>1</sub> to ''M''<sub>2</sub> is a [[bijection|bijective]] isometry ''f'' : ''M''<sub>1</sub>  →  ''M''<sub>2</sub>. The metric spaces are then said to be '''isometrically isomorphic'''. From the standpoint of metric space theory, isometrically isomorphic spaces are identical.

definition: If (M1, d1) and (M2, d2) are metric spaces, an isometry from M1 to M2 is a function f : M1 → M2 such that d2(f(x), f(y)) = d1(x, y) for all x, y in M1. Every isometry is injective, although not every isometry is surjective.
definition_raw: If (''M''<sub>1</sub>, ''d''<sub>1</sub>) and (''M''<sub>2</sub>, ''d''<sub>2</sub>) are metric spaces, an isometry from ''M''<sub>1</sub> to ''M''<sub>2</sub> is a function ''f'' : ''M''<sub>1</sub>  →  ''M''<sub>2</sub> such that ''d''<sub>2</sub>(''f''(''x''), ''f''(''y'')) = ''d''<sub>1</sub>(''x'', ''y'') for all ''x'', ''y'' in ''M''<sub>1</sub>. Every isometry is [[Injective function|injective]], although not every isometry is [[surjection|surjective]].

definition: See T0.
definition_raw: See '''[[T0 space|T<sub>0</sub>]]'''.

definition: The Kuratowski closure axioms is a set of axioms satisfied by the function which takes each subset of X to its closure: # Isotonicity: Every set is contained in its closure. # Idempotence: The closure of the closure of a set is equal to the closure of that set. # Preservation of binary unions: The closure of the union of two sets is the union of their closures. # Preservation of nullary unions: The closure of the empty set is empty. If c is a function from the power set of X to itself, then c is a closure operator if it satisfies the Kuratowski closure axioms. The Kuratowski closure axioms can then be used to define a topology on X by declaring the closed sets to be the fixed points of this operator, i.e. a set A is closed if and only if c(A) = A.
definition_raw: The [[Kuratowski closure axioms]] is a set of [[axiom]]s satisfied by the function which takes each subset of ''X'' to its closure: # ''[[Isotone function|Isotonicity]]'': Every set is contained in its closure. # ''[[Idempotent function|Idempotence]]'': The closure of the closure of a set is equal to the closure of that set. # ''Preservation of binary unions'': The closure of the union of two sets is the union of their closures. # ''Preservation of nullary unions'': The closure of the empty set

definition: TKol = {R, $\varnothing$}∪{(a,∞): a is real number}; the pair (R,TKol) is named Kolmogorov Straight.
definition_raw: T<sub>''Kol''</sub> = {R, <math>\varnothing</math>}∪{(a,∞): a is real number}; the pair (R,T<sub>''Kol''</sub>) is named ''Kolmogorov Straight''.

definition: An L-space is a hereditarily Lindelöf space which is not hereditarily separable. A Suslin line would be an L-space.
definition_raw: An ''L-space'' is a [[Hereditary property#In topology|hereditarily]] [[Lindelöf space]] which is not hereditarily [[Separable space|separable]].  A [[Suslin line]] would be an L-space.<ref name=GKW290>{{cite book | title=Sets and Extensions in the Twentieth Century | editor1-first=Dov M. | editor1-last=Gabbay | editor2-first=Akihiro | editor2-last=Kanamori | editor3-first=John Hayden | editor3-last=Woods | publisher=Elsevier | year=2012 | isbn=978-0444516213 | page=290 }}</ref>

definition: See Finer topology.
definition_raw: See '''[[Finer topology]]'''.

definition: A point x in a space X is a limit point of a subset S if every open set containing x also contains a point of S other than x itself. This is equivalent to requiring that every neighbourhood of x contains a point of S other than x itself.
definition_raw: A point ''x'' in a space ''X'' is a [[limit point]] of a subset ''S'' if every open set containing ''x'' also contains a point of ''S'' other than ''x'' itself. This is equivalent to requiring that every neighbourhood of ''x'' contains a point of ''S'' other than ''x'' itself.

definition: See Weakly countably compact.
definition_raw: See '''Weakly countably compact'''.

definition: A space is Lindelöf if every open cover has a countable subcover.
definition_raw: A space is [[Lindelöf space|Lindelöf]] if every open cover has a [[countable]] subcover.

definition: A set B of neighbourhoods of a point x of a space X is a local base (or local basis, neighbourhood base, neighbourhood basis) at x if every neighbourhood of x contains some member of B.
definition_raw: A set ''B'' of neighbourhoods of a point ''x'' of a space ''X'' is a local base (or '''local basis''', '''neighbourhood base''', '''neighbourhood basis''') at ''x'' if every neighbourhood of ''x'' contains some member of ''B''.

definition: See Local base.
definition_raw: See '''Local base'''.

definition: There are two definitions for a space to be "locally (P)" where (P) is a topological or set-theoretic property: that each point has a neighbourhood with property (P), or that every point has a neighourbood base for which each member has property (P). The first definition is usually taken for locally compact, countably compact, metrizable, separable, countable; the second for locally connected.
definition_raw: There are two definitions for a space to be "locally (P)" where (P) is a topological or set-theoretic property: that each point has a neighbourhood with property (P), or that every point has a neighourbood base for which each member has property (P).  The first definition is usually taken for locally compact, countably compact, metrizable, separable, countable; the second for locally connected.<ref name=EGT65>Hart et al (2004) p.65</ref>

definition: A subset of a topological space that is the intersection of an open and a closed subset. Equivalently, it is a relatively open subset of its closure.
definition_raw: A subset of a topological space that is the intersection of an open and a closed subset.  Equivalently, it is a relatively open subset of its closure.

definition: A space is locally compact if every point has a compact neighbourhood: the alternative definition that each point has a local base consisting of compact neighbourhoods is sometimes used: these are equivalent for Hausdorff spaces. Every locally compact Hausdorff space is Tychonoff.
definition_raw: A space is [[Locally compact space|locally compact]] if every point has a compact neighbourhood: the alternative definition that each point has a local base consisting of compact neighbourhoods is sometimes used: these are equivalent for Hausdorff spaces.<ref name=EGT65/>  Every locally compact Hausdorff space is Tychonoff.

definition: A space is locally connected if every point has a local base consisting of connected neighbourhoods.
definition_raw: A space is [[locally connected]] if every point has a local base consisting of connected neighbourhoods.<ref name=EGT65/>

definition: see Preopen.
definition_raw: see ''Preopen''.

definition: A collection of subsets of a space is locally finite if every point has a neighbourhood which has nonempty intersection with only finitely many of the subsets. See also countably locally finite, point finite.
definition_raw: A collection of subsets of a space is [[Locally finite collection|locally finite]] if every point has a neighbourhood which has nonempty intersection with only [[finite set|finite]]ly many of the subsets.  See also '''countably locally finite''', '''[[point finite]]'''.

definition: A space is locally metrizable if every point has a metrizable neighbourhood.
definition_raw: A space is locally metrizable if every point has a metrizable neighbourhood.<ref name=EGT65/>

definition: A space is locally path-connected if every point has a local base consisting of path-connected neighbourhoods. A locally path-connected space is connected if and only if it is path-connected.
definition_raw: A space is [[locally path-connected]] if every point has a local base consisting of path-connected neighbourhoods.<ref name=EGT65/>  A locally path-connected space is connected [[if and only if]] it is path-connected.

definition: A space is locally simply connected if every point has a local base consisting of simply connected neighbourhoods.
definition_raw: A space is locally simply connected if every point has a local base consisting of simply connected neighbourhoods.

definition: If x is a point in a space X, a loop at x in X (or a loop in X with basepoint x) is a path f in X, such that f(0) = f(1) = x. Equivalently, a loop in X is a continuous map from the unit circle S1 into X.
definition_raw: If ''x'' is a point in a space ''X'', a [[loop (topology)|loop]] at ''x'' in ''X'' (or a loop in ''X'' with basepoint ''x'') is a path ''f'' in ''X'', such that ''f''(0) = ''f''(1) = ''x''. Equivalently, a loop in ''X'' is a continuous map from the [[unit circle]] ''S''<sup>1</sup> into ''X''.

definition: If X is a space and A is a subset of X, then A is meagre in X (or of first category in X) if it is the countable union of nowhere dense sets. If A is not meagre in X, A is of second category in X.
definition_raw: If ''X'' is a space and ''A'' is a subset of ''X'', then ''A'' is meagre in ''X'' (or of '''first category''' in ''X'') if it is the [[countable]] union of nowhere dense sets.  If ''A'' is not meagre in ''X'', ''A'' is of '''second category''' in ''X''.<ref name=ss7>Steen & Seebach (1978) p.7</ref>

definition: A space is metacompact if every open cover has a point finite open refinement.
definition_raw: A space is metacompact if every open cover has a point finite open refinement.

definition: See Metric space.
definition_raw: See '''[[Metric space]]'''.

definition: A metric invariant is a property which is preserved under isometric isomorphism.
definition_raw: A metric invariant is a property which is preserved under isometric isomorphism.

definition: If X and Y are metric spaces with metrics dX and dY respectively, then a metric map is a function f from X to Y, such that for any points x and y in X, dY(f(x), f(y)) ≤ dX(x, y). A metric map is strictly metric if the above inequality is strict for all x and y in X.
definition_raw: If ''X'' and ''Y'' are metric spaces with metrics ''d''<sub>''X''</sub> and ''d''<sub>''Y''</sub> respectively, then a [[metric map]] is a function ''f'' from ''X'' to ''Y'', such that for any points ''x'' and ''y'' in ''X'', ''d''<sub>''Y''</sub>(''f''(''x''), ''f''(''y'')) ≤ ''d''<sub>''X''</sub>(''x'', ''y''). A metric map is [[metric map|strictly metric]] if the above inequality is strict for all ''x'' and ''y'' in ''X''.

definition: A metric space (M, d) is a set M equipped with a function d : M × M → R satisfying the following axioms for all x, y, and z in M: # d(x, y) ≥ 0 # d(x, x) = 0 # if d(x, y) = 0 then x = y (identity of indiscernibles) # d(x, y) = d(y, x) (symmetry) # d(x, z) ≤ d(x, y) + d(y, z) (triangle inequality)
definition_raw: A [[metric space]] (''M'', ''d'') is a set ''M'' equipped with a function ''d'' : ''M'' × ''M'' → '''[[real number|R]]''' satisfying the following axioms for all ''x'', ''y'', and ''z'' in ''M'': # ''d''(''x'', ''y'') ≥ 0 # ''d''(''x'', ''x'') = 0 # if   ''d''(''x'', ''y'') = 0   then   ''x'' = ''y''     (''identity of indiscernibles'') # ''d''(''x'', ''y'') = ''d''(''y'', ''x'')      (''symmetry'') # ''d''(''x'', ''z'') ≤ ''d

definition: A space is metrizable if it is homeomorphic to a metric space. Every metrizable space is Hausdorff and paracompact (and hence normal and Tychonoff). Every metrizable space is first-countable.
definition_raw: A space is [[metrizable]] if it is homeomorphic to a metric space. Every metrizable space is Hausdorff and paracompact (and hence normal and Tychonoff). Every metrizable space is first-countable.

definition: Every non-empty ultra-connected compact space X has a largest proper open subset; this subset is called a monolith.
definition_raw: Every non-empty ultra-connected compact space ''X'' has a largest proper open subset; this subset is called a '''monolith'''.

definition: A Moore space is a developable regular Hausdorff space.
definition_raw: A [[Moore space (topology)|Moore space]] is a [[developable space|developable]] [[regular Hausdorff space]].<ref name=ss163>Steen & Seebach (1978) p.163</ref>

definition: see preopen. See also: almost open map.
definition_raw: see ''preopen''. See also: [[almost open map]].

definition: A neighbourhood of a point x is a set containing an open set which in turn contains the point x. More generally, a neighbourhood of a set S is a set containing an open set which in turn contains the set S. A neighbourhood of a point x is thus a neighbourhood of the singleton set {x}. (Note that under this definition, the neighbourhood itself need not be open. Many authors require that neighbourhoods be open; be careful to note conventions.)
definition_raw: A neighbourhood of a point ''x'' is a set containing an open set which in turn contains the point ''x''. More generally, a neighbourhood of a set ''S'' is a set containing an open set which in turn contains the set ''S''. A neighbourhood of a point ''x'' is thus a neighbourhood of the [[singleton (mathematics)|singleton]] set {''x''}. (Note that under this definition, the neighbourhood itself need not be open. Many authors require that neighbourhoods be open; be careful to note conventions.)

definition: See Local base.
definition_raw: See '''[[Local base]]'''.

term: Neighbourhood system for a point x
definition: A neighbourhood system at a point x in a space is the collection of all neighbourhoods of x.
definition_raw: A [[neighbourhood system]] at a point ''x'' in a space is the collection of all neighbourhoods of ''x''.

definition: A net in a space X is a map from a directed set A to X. A net from A to X is usually denoted (xα), where α is an index variable ranging over A. Every sequence is a net, taking A to be the directed set of natural numbers with the usual ordering.
definition_raw: A [[net (mathematics)|net]] in a space ''X'' is a map from a [[directed set]] ''A'' to ''X''. A net from ''A'' to ''X'' is usually denoted (''x''<sub>α</sub>), where α is an [[index set|index variable]] ranging over ''A''. Every [[sequence]] is a net, taking ''A'' to be the directed set of [[natural number]]s with the usual ordering.

definition: A space is normal if any two disjoint closed sets have disjoint neighbourhoods. Every normal space admits a partition of unity.
definition_raw: A space is [[normal space|normal]] if any two disjoint closed sets have disjoint neighbourhoods.<ref name=ss162/>  Every normal space admits a [[partition of unity]].

definition: A normal Hausdorff space (or T4 space) is a normal T1 space. (A normal space is Hausdorff if and only if it is T1, so the terminology is consistent.) Every normal Hausdorff space is Tychonoff.
definition_raw: A [[T4 space|normal Hausdorff]] space (or [[T4 space|'''T<sub>4</sub>''' space]]) is a normal T<sub>1</sub> space. (A normal space is Hausdorff [[if and only if]] it is T<sub>1</sub>, so the terminology is consistent.) Every normal Hausdorff space is Tychonoff.

definition: A nowhere dense set is a set whose closure has empty interior.
definition_raw: A [[nowhere dense set]] is a set whose closure has empty interior.

definition: An open cover is a cover consisting of open sets.
definition_raw: An [[open cover]] is a cover consisting of open sets.<ref name=ss163/>

definition: If (M, d) is a metric space, an open ball is a set of the form B(x; r) := {y in M : d(x, y) < r}, where x is in M and r is a positive real number, the radius of the ball. An open ball of radius r is an open r-ball. Every open ball is an open set in the topology on M induced by d.
definition_raw: If (''M'', ''d'') is a metric space, an open ball is a set of the form ''B''(''x''; ''r'') := {''y'' in ''M'' : ''d''(''x'', ''y'') < ''r''}, where ''x'' is in ''M'' and ''r'' is a [[positive number|positive]] [[real number]], the '''radius''' of the ball. An open ball of radius ''r'' is an '''open ''r''-ball'''. Every open ball is an open set in the topology on ''M'' induced by ''d''.

definition: See open property.
definition_raw: See '''open property'''.

definition: An open set is a member of the topology.
definition_raw: An [[open set]] is a member of the topology.

definition: A function from one space to another is open if the image of every open set is open.
definition_raw: A function from one space to another is [[open map|open]] if the [[image (mathematics)|image]] of every open set is open.

definition: A property of points in a topological space is said to be "open" if those points which possess it form an open set. Such conditions often take a common form, and that form can be said to be an open condition; for example, in metric spaces, one defines an open ball as above, and says that "strict inequality is an open condition".
definition_raw: A property of points in a [[topological space]] is said to be "open" if those points which possess it form an [[open set]].  Such conditions often take a common form, and that form can be said to be an ''open condition''; for example, in [[metric space]]s, one defines an open ball as above, and says that "strict inequality is an open condition".

definition: A space is orthocompact, if every open cover has an interior-preserving open refinement.
definition_raw: A space is orthocompact, if every [[open cover]] has an interior-preserving open [[Refinement (topology)|refinement]].

definition: A space is paracompact if every open cover has a locally finite open refinement. Paracompact implies metacompact. Paracompact Hausdorff spaces are normal.
definition_raw: A space is [[paracompact space|paracompact]] if every open cover has a locally finite open refinement.  Paracompact implies metacompact.<ref name=ss23>Steen & Seebach (1978) p.23</ref>  Paracompact Hausdorff spaces are normal.<ref name=ss25>Steen & Seebach (1978) p.25</ref>

definition: A partition of unity of a space X is a set of continuous functions from X to [0, 1] such that any point has a neighbourhood where all but a finite number of the functions are identically zero, and the sum of all the functions on the entire space is identically 1.
definition_raw: A partition of unity of a space ''X'' is a set of continuous functions from ''X'' to [0, 1] such that any point has a neighbourhood where all but a [[finite set|finite]] number of the functions are identically zero, and the sum of all the functions on the entire space is identically 1.

definition: A path in a space X is a continuous map f from the closed unit interval [0, 1] into X. The point f(0) is the initial point of f; the point f(1) is the terminal point of f.
definition_raw: A [[Path (topology)|path]] in a space ''X'' is a continuous map ''f'' from the closed unit [[interval (mathematics)|interval]] [0, 1] into ''X''. The point ''f''(0) is the initial point of ''f''; the point ''f''(1) is the terminal point of ''f''.<ref name=ss29>Steen & Seebach (1978) p.29</ref>

definition: A space X is path-connected if, for every two points x, y in X, there is a path f from x to y, i.e., a path with initial point f(0) = x and terminal point f(1) = y. Every path-connected space is connected.
definition_raw: A space ''X'' is [[path-connected space|path-connected]] if, for every two points ''x'', ''y'' in ''X'', there is a path ''f'' from ''x'' to ''y'', i.e., a path with initial point ''f''(0) = ''x'' and terminal point ''f''(1) = ''y''. Every path-connected space is connected.<ref name=ss29/>

definition: A path-connected component of a space is a maximal nonempty path-connected subspace. The set of path-connected components of a space is a partition of that space, which is finer than the partition into connected components. The set of path-connected components of a space X is denoted π0(X).
definition_raw: A path-connected component of a space is a maximal nonempty path-connected subspace. The set of path-connected components of a space is a [[partition of a set|partition]] of that space, which is [[partition of a set|finer]] than the partition into connected components.<ref name=ss29/> The set of path-connected components of a space ''X'' is denoted [[homotopy groups|π<sub>0</sub>(''X'')]].

definition: a normal space which is also a Gδ.
definition_raw: a normal space which is also a G<sub>δ</sub>.<ref name=ss162/>

definition: A collection B of nonempty open sets is a π-base for a topology τ if every nonempty open set in τ includes a set from B.
definition_raw: A collection ''B'' of nonempty open sets is a π-base for a topology τ if every nonempty open set in τ includes a set from ''B''.<ref>Hart, Nagata, Vaughan Sect. d-22, page 227</ref>

definition: A point is an element of a topological space. More generally, a point is an element of any set with an underlying topological structure; e.g. an element of a metric space or a topological group is also a "point".
definition_raw: A point is an element of a topological space. More generally, a point is an element of any set with an underlying topological structure; e.g. an element of a metric space or a topological group is also a "point".

definition: See Closure.
definition_raw: See '''[[Closure (topology)|Closure]]'''.

definition: A space is Polish if it is separable and completely metrizable, i.e. if it is homeomorphic to a separable and complete metric space.
definition_raw: A space is Polish if it is separable and completely metrizable, i.e. if it is homeomorphic to a separable and complete metric space.

definition: A space is polyadic if it is the continuous image of the power of a one-point compactification of a locally compact, non-compact Hausdorff space.
definition_raw: A space is polyadic if it is the continuous image of the power of a [[one-point compactification]] of a locally compact, non-compact Hausdorff space.

definition: A polytopological space is a set $X$ together with a family $\{\tau_i\}_{i\in I}$ of topologies on $X$ that is linearly ordered by the inclusion relation where $I$ is an arbitrary index set.
definition_raw: A polytopological space is a [[set (mathematics)|set]] <math>X</math> together with a [[family of sets|family]] <math>\{\tau_i\}_{i\in I}</math> of [[Topological_space#Definition_via_open_sets|topologies]] on <math>X</math> that is [[Total order|linearly ordered]] by the [[Subset|inclusion relation]] where <math>I</math> is an arbitrary [[index set]].

definition: A point of a topological space is a P-point if its filter of neighbourhoods is closed under countable intersections.
definition_raw: A point of a topological space is a P-point if its [[Neighbourhood system|filter of neighbourhoods]] is closed under countable intersections.

definition: See Relatively compact.
definition_raw: See '''[[Relatively compact]]'''.

definition: The prodiscrete topology on a product AG is the product topology when each factor A is given the discrete topology.
definition_raw: The prodiscrete topology on a product ''A''<sup>''G''</sup> is the product topology when each factor ''A'' is given the discrete topology.<ref>{{cite book | last1=Ceccherini-Silberstein | first1=Tullio | last2=Coornaert | first2=Michel | title=Cellular automata and groups | zbl=1218.37004 | series=Springer Monographs in Mathematics | location=Berlin | publisher=[[Springer-Verlag]] | isbn=978-3-642-14033-4 | year=2010 | page=3 }}</ref>

definition: If $\left(X_i\right)$ is a collection of spaces and X is the (set-theoretic) Cartesian product of $\left(X_i\right),$ then the product topology on X is the coarsest topology for which all the projection maps are continuous.
definition_raw: If <math>\left(X_i\right)</math> is a collection of spaces and ''X'' is the (set-theoretic) [[Cartesian product]] of <math>\left(X_i\right),</math> then the [[product topology]] on ''X'' is the coarsest topology for which all the projection maps are continuous.

definition: A continuous function f from a space X to a space Y is proper if $f^{-1}(C)$ is a compact set in X for any compact subspace C of Y.
definition_raw: A continuous function ''f'' from a space ''X'' to a space ''Y'' is proper if <math>f^{-1}(C)</math> is a compact set in ''X'' for any compact subspace ''C'' of ''Y''.

definition: A proximity space (X, d) is a set X equipped with a binary relation d between subsets of X satisfying the following properties:
definition_raw: A proximity space (''X'', '''d''') is a set ''X'' equipped with a [[binary relation]] '''d''' between subsets of ''X'' satisfying the following properties:

definition: A space is pseudocompact if every real-valued continuous function on the space is bounded.
definition_raw: A space is pseudocompact if every [[real number|real-valued]] continuous function on the space is bounded.

definition: See Pseudometric space.
definition_raw: See '''Pseudometric space'''.

definition: A pseudometric space (M, d) is a set M equipped with a real-valued function $d : M \times M \to \R$ satisfying all the conditions of a metric space, except possibly the identity of indiscernibles. That is, points in a pseudometric space may be "infinitely close" without being identical. The function d is a pseudometric on M. Every metric is a pseudometric.
definition_raw: A pseudometric space (''M'', ''d'') is a set ''M'' equipped with a [[Real number|real]]-valued function <math>d : M \times M \to \R</math> satisfying all the conditions of a metric space, except possibly the identity of indiscernibles. That is, points in a pseudometric space may be "infinitely close" without being identical.  The function ''d'' is a '''pseudometric''' on ''M''. Every metric is a pseudometric.

definition: A punctured neighbourhood of a point x is a neighbourhood of x, minus {x}. For instance, the interval (−1, 1) = {y : −1 < y < 1} is a neighbourhood of x = 0 in the real line, so the set $(-1, 0) \cup (0, 1) = (-1, 1) - \{ 0 \}$ is a punctured neighbourhood of 0.
definition_raw: A punctured neighbourhood of a point ''x'' is a neighbourhood of ''x'', [[Set subtraction|minus]] {''x''}. For instance, the [[interval (mathematics)|interval]] (−1, 1) = {''y'' : −1 < ''y'' < 1} is a neighbourhood of ''x'' = 0 in the [[real line]], so the set <math>(-1, 0) \cup (0, 1) = (-1, 1) - \{ 0 \}</math> is a punctured neighbourhood of 0.

definition: See compact. Some authors define "compact" to include the Hausdorff separation axiom, and they use the term quasicompact to mean what we call in this glossary simply "compact" (without the Hausdorff axiom). This convention is most commonly found in French, and branches of mathematics heavily influenced by the French.
definition_raw: See '''[[compact space|compact]]'''. Some authors define "compact" to include the [[Hausdorff space|Hausdorff]] separation axiom, and they use the term '''quasicompact''' to mean what we call in this glossary simply "compact" (without the Hausdorff axiom). This convention is most commonly found in French, and branches of mathematics heavily influenced by the French.

definition: If X and Y are spaces, and if f is a surjection from X to Y, then f is a quotient map (or identification map) if, for every subset U of Y, U is open in Y if and only if f 1(U) is open in X. In other words, Y has the f-strong topology. Equivalently, $f$ is a quotient map if and only if it is the transfinite composition of maps $X\rightarrow X/Z$, where $Z\subset X$ is a subset. Note that this does not imply that f is an open function.
definition_raw: If ''X'' and ''Y'' are spaces, and if ''f'' is a [[surjection]] from ''X'' to ''Y'', then ''f'' is a quotient map (or '''identification map''') if, for every subset ''U'' of ''Y'', ''U'' is open in ''Y'' [[if and only if]] ''f''<sup> {{mono|-}}1</sup>(''U'') is open in ''X''.  In other words, ''Y'' has the ''f''-strong topology.  Equivalently, <math>f</math> is a quotient map if and only if it is the transfinite composition of maps <math>X\rightarrow X/Z</math>, where <math>Z\subset X</math

definition: If X is a space, Y is a set, and f : X → Y is any surjective function, then the Quotient topology on Y induced by f is the finest topology for which f is continuous. The space X is a quotient space or identification space. By definition, f is a quotient map. The most common example of this is to consider an equivalence relation on X, with Y the set of equivalence classes and f the natural projection map. This construction is dual to the construction of the subspace topology.
definition_raw: If ''X'' is a space, ''Y'' is a set, and ''f'' : ''X'' → ''Y'' is any [[surjection|surjective]] function, then the [[Quotient topology]] on ''Y'' induced by ''f'' is the finest topology for which ''f'' is continuous. The space ''X'' is a quotient space or '''identification space'''. By definition, ''f'' is a quotient map. The most common example of this is to consider an [[equivalence relation]] on ''X'', with ''Y'' the set of [[equivalence class]]es and ''f'' the natural projecti

definition: A cover K is a refinement of a cover L if every member of K is a subset of some member of L.
definition_raw: A cover ''K'' is a [[refinement (topology)|refinement]] of a cover ''L'' if every member of ''K'' is a subset of some member of ''L''.

definition: A space is regular if, whenever C is a closed set and x is a point not in C, then C and x have disjoint neighbourhoods.
definition_raw: A space is [[regular space|regular]] if, whenever ''C'' is a closed set and ''x'' is a point not in ''C'', then ''C'' and ''x'' have [[Disjoint sets|disjoint]] neighbourhoods.

definition: A space is regular Hausdorff (or T3) if it is a regular T0 space. (A regular space is Hausdorff if and only if it is T0, so the terminology is consistent.)
definition_raw: A space is [[T3 space|regular Hausdorff]] (or '''T<sub>3</sub>''') if it is a regular T<sub>0</sub> space.  (A regular space is Hausdorff [[if and only if]] it is T<sub>0</sub>, so the terminology is consistent.)

definition: A subset Y of a space X is relatively compact in X if the closure of Y in X is compact.
definition_raw: A subset ''Y'' of a space ''X'' is [[relatively compact]] in ''X'' if the closure of ''Y'' in ''X'' is compact.

definition: If X is a space and A is a subset of X, then A is residual in X if the complement of A is meagre in X. Also called comeagre or comeager.
definition_raw: If ''X'' is a space and ''A'' is a subset of ''X'', then ''A'' is residual in ''X'' if the complement of ''A'' is meagre in ''X''. Also called '''comeagre''' or '''comeager'''.

definition: A topological space is called resolvable if it is expressible as the union of two disjoint dense subsets.
definition_raw: A [[topological space]] is called [[resolvable space|resolvable]] if it is expressible as the union of two [[disjoint sets|disjoint]] [[dense subset]]s.

definition: A space is rim-compact if it has a base of open sets whose boundaries are compact.
definition_raw: A space is rim-compact if it has a base of open sets whose boundaries are compact.

definition: An S-space is a hereditarily separable space which is not hereditarily Lindelöf.
definition_raw: An ''S-space'' is a [[Hereditary property#In topology|hereditarily]] [[separable space]] which is not hereditarily [[Lindelöf space|Lindelöf]].<ref name=GKW290/>

definition: A space X is scattered if every nonempty subset A of X contains a point isolated in A.
definition_raw: A space ''X'' is [[scattered space|scattered]] if every nonempty subset ''A'' of ''X'' contains a point isolated in ''A''.

definition: The Scott topology on a poset is that in which the open sets are those Upper sets inaccessible by directed joins.
definition_raw: The [[Scott topology]] on a [[poset]] is that in which the open sets are those [[Upper set]]s inaccessible by directed joins.<ref>Vickers (1989) p.95</ref>

definition: See Meagre.
definition_raw: See '''Meagre'''.

definition: A space is second-countable or perfectly separable if it has a countable base for its topology. Every second-countable space is first-countable, separable, and Lindelöf.
definition_raw: A space is [[second-countable space|second-countable]] or '''perfectly separable''' if it has a [[countable]] base for its topology.<ref name=ss162/> Every second-countable space is first-countable, separable, and Lindelöf.

definition: A space X is semilocally simply connected if, for every point x in X, there is a neighbourhood U of x such that every loop at x in U is homotopic in X to the constant loop x. Every simply connected space and every locally simply connected space is semilocally simply connected. (Compare with locally simply connected; here, the homotopy is allowed to live in X, whereas in the definition of locally simply connected, the homotopy must live in U.)
definition_raw: A space ''X'' is [[semilocally simply connected]] if, for every point ''x'' in ''X'', there is a neighbourhood ''U'' of ''x'' such that every loop at ''x'' in ''U'' is homotopic in ''X'' to the constant loop ''x''. Every simply connected space and every locally simply connected space is semilocally simply connected. (Compare with locally simply connected; here, the homotopy is allowed to live in ''X'', whereas in the definition of locally simply connected, the homotopy must live in ''U''.)

definition: A subset A of a topological space X is called semi-open if $A \subseteq \operatorname{Cl}_X \left( \operatorname{Int}_X A \right)$.
definition_raw: A subset ''A'' of a topological space ''X'' is called semi-open if <math>A \subseteq \operatorname{Cl}_X \left( \operatorname{Int}_X A \right)</math>.{{sfn|Hart|Nagata|Vaughan|2004|p=8}}

definition: A subset A of a topological space X is called semi-preopen if $A \subseteq \operatorname{Cl}_X \left( \operatorname{Int}_X \left( \operatorname{Cl}_X A \right) \right)$
definition_raw: A subset ''A'' of a topological space ''X'' is called semi-preopen if <math>A \subseteq \operatorname{Cl}_X \left( \operatorname{Int}_X \left( \operatorname{Cl}_X A \right) \right)</math>{{sfn|Hart|Nagata|Vaughan|2004|p=9}}

definition: A space is semiregular if the regular open sets form a base.
definition_raw: A space is semiregular if the regular open sets form a base.

definition: A space is separable if it has a countable dense subset.
definition_raw: A space is [[separable (topology)|separable]] if it has a [[countable]] dense subset.<ref name=ss162/><ref name=ss7/>

definition: Two sets A and B are separated if each is disjoint from the other's closure.
definition_raw: Two sets ''A'' and ''B'' are [[separated sets|separated]] if each is [[Disjoint sets|disjoint]] from the other's closure.

definition: A space is sequentially compact if every sequence has a convergent subsequence. Every sequentially compact space is countably compact, and every first-countable, countably compact space is sequentially compact.
definition_raw: A space is sequentially compact if every [[sequence]] has a convergent subsequence. Every sequentially compact space is countably compact, and every first-countable, countably compact space is sequentially compact.

definition: See metric map
definition_raw: See '''[[metric map]]'''

definition: A space is simply connected if it is path-connected and every loop is homotopic to a constant map.
definition_raw: A space is [[simply connected space|simply connected]] if it is path-connected and every loop is homotopic to a constant map.

definition: See Coarser topology.
definition_raw: See '''[[Coarser topology]]'''.

definition: In a sober space, every irreducible closed subset is the closure of exactly one point: that is, has a unique generic point.
definition_raw: In a [[sober space]], every [[hyperconnected space|irreducible]] closed subset is the [[closure (topology)|closure]] of exactly one point: that is, has a unique [[generic point]].<ref>Vickers (1989) p.66</ref>

definition: The star of a point in a given cover of a topological space is the union of all the sets in the cover that contain the point. See star refinement.
definition_raw: The star of a point in a given [[cover (topology)|cover]] of a [[topological space]] is the union of all the sets in the cover that contain the point. See '''[[star refinement]]'''.

definition: Let $f\colon X\rightarrow Y$ be a map of topological spaces. We say that $Y$ has the $f$-strong topology if, for every subset $U\subset Y$, one has that $U$ is open in $Y$ if and only if $f^{-1}(U)$ is open in $X$
definition_raw: Let <math>f\colon X\rightarrow Y</math> be a map of topological spaces.  We say that <math>Y</math> has the <math>f</math>-strong topology if, for every subset <math>U\subset Y</math>, one has that <math>U</math> is open in <math>Y</math> if and only if <math>f^{-1}(U)</math> is open in <math>X</math>

definition: See Finer topology. Beware, some authors, especially analysts, use the term weaker topology.
definition_raw: See '''[[Finer topology]]'''. Beware, some authors, especially [[mathematical analysis|analyst]]s, use the term '''weaker topology'''.

definition: A collection of open sets is a subbase (or subbasis) for a topology if every non-empty proper open set in the topology is the union of a finite intersection of sets in the subbase. If $\mathcal B$ is any collection of subsets of a set X, the topology on X generated by $\mathcal B$ is the smallest topology containing $\mathcal B;$ this topology consists of the empty set, X and all unions of finite intersections of elements of $\mathcal B.$ Thus $\mathcal B$ is a subbase for the topology it generates.
definition_raw: A collection of open sets is a [[subbase]] (or '''subbasis''') for a topology if every non-empty proper open set in the topology is the union of a [[finite set|finite]] intersection of sets in the subbase. If <math>\mathcal B</math> is ''any'' collection of subsets of a set ''X'', the topology on ''X'' generated by <math>\mathcal B</math> is the smallest topology containing <math>\mathcal B;</math> this topology consists of the empty set, ''X'' and all unions of finite intersections of elements

definition: See Subbase.
definition_raw: See '''[[Subbase]]'''.

definition: A cover K is a subcover (or subcovering) of a cover L if every member of K is a member of L.
definition_raw: A cover ''K'' is a subcover (or '''subcovering''') of a cover ''L'' if every member of ''K'' is a member of ''L''.

definition: See Subcover.
definition_raw: See '''Subcover'''.

definition: A topological space is said to be submaximal if every subset of it is locally closed, that is, every subset is the intersection of an open set and a closed set.
definition_raw: A [[topological space]] is said to be submaximal if every subset of it is locally closed, that is, every subset is the intersection of an [[open set]] and a [[closed set]].

definition: If T is a topology on a space X, and if A is a subset of X, then the subspace topology on A induced by T consists of all intersections of open sets in T with A. This construction is dual to the construction of the quotient topology.
definition_raw: If ''T'' is a topology on a space ''X'', and if ''A'' is a subset of ''X'', then the [[subspace topology]] on ''A'' induced by ''T'' consists of all intersections of open sets in ''T'' with ''A''. This construction is dual to the construction of the quotient topology.

definition: A space is T0 (or Kolmogorov) if for every pair of distinct points x and y in the space, either there is an open set containing x but not y, or there is an open set containing y but not x.
definition_raw: A space is [[T0 space|T<sub>0</sub>]] (or '''Kolmogorov''') if for every pair of distinct points ''x'' and ''y'' in the space, either there is an open set containing ''x'' but not ''y'', or there is an open set containing ''y'' but not ''x''.

definition: A space is T1 (or Fréchet or accessible) if for every pair of distinct points x and y in the space, there is an open set containing x but not y. (Compare with T0; here, we are allowed to specify which point will be contained in the open set.) Equivalently, a space is T1 if all its singletons are closed. Every T1 space is T0.
definition_raw: A space is [[T1 space|T<sub>1</sub>]] (or '''Fréchet''' or '''accessible''') if for every pair of distinct points ''x'' and ''y'' in the space, there is an open set containing ''x'' but not ''y''. (Compare with T<sub>0</sub>; here, we are allowed to specify which point will be contained in the open set.) Equivalently, a space is T<sub>1</sub> if all its [[singleton (mathematics)|singleton]]s are closed. Every T<sub>1</sub> space is T<sub>0</sub>.

definition: See Hausdorff space.
definition_raw: See '''[[Hausdorff space]]'''.

definition: See Regular Hausdorff.
definition_raw: See '''[[T3 space|Regular Hausdorff]]'''.

definition: See Tychonoff space.
definition_raw: See '''[[Tychonoff space]]'''.

definition: See Normal Hausdorff.
definition_raw: See '''[[T4 space|Normal Hausdorff]]'''.

definition: See Completely normal Hausdorff.
definition_raw: See '''[[T5 space|Completely normal Hausdorff]]'''.

definition: See Category of topological spaces.
definition_raw: See '''[[Category of topological spaces]]'''.

definition: A point x of a topological space X is a θ-cluster point of a subset A if $A \cap \operatorname{Cl}_X(U) \neq \emptyset$ for every open neighborhood U of x in X. The subset A is θ-closed if it is equal to the set of its θ-cluster points, and θ-open if its complement is θ-closed.
definition_raw: A point ''x'' of a topological space ''X'' is a θ-cluster point of a subset ''A'' if <math>A \cap \operatorname{Cl}_X(U) \neq \emptyset</math> for every open neighborhood ''U'' of ''x'' in ''X''.  The subset ''A'' is θ-closed if it is equal to the set of its θ-cluster points, and θ-open if its complement is θ-closed.{{sfn|Hart|Nagata|Vaughan|2004|p=8}}

definition: A topological invariant is a property which is preserved under homeomorphism. For example, compactness and connectedness are topological properties, whereas boundedness and completeness are not. Algebraic topology is the study of topologically invariant abstract algebra constructions on topological spaces.
definition_raw: A topological invariant is a property which is preserved under homeomorphism. For example, compactness and connectedness are topological properties, whereas boundedness and completeness are not. [[Algebraic topology]] is the study of topologically invariant [[abstract algebra]] constructions on topological spaces.

definition: A topological space (X, T) is a set X equipped with a collection T of subsets of X satisfying the following axioms:
definition_raw: A [[topological space]] (''X'', ''T'') is a set ''X'' equipped with a collection ''T'' of subsets of ''X'' satisfying the following [[axiom]]s:

definition: See Coproduct topology.
definition_raw: See '''Coproduct topology'''.

definition: Completely metrizable spaces (i. e. topological spaces homeomorphic to complete metric spaces) are often called topologically complete; sometimes the term is also used for Čech-complete spaces or completely uniformizable spaces.
definition_raw: [[Completely metrizable space]]s (i. e. topological spaces homeomorphic to complete metric spaces) are often called ''topologically complete''; sometimes the term is also used for [[Čech-complete space]]s or [[completely uniformizable space]]s.

definition: See Topological space.
definition_raw: See '''[[Topological space]]'''.

definition: A metric space M is totally bounded if, for every r > 0, there exist a finite cover of M by open balls of radius r. A metric space is compact if and only if it is complete and totally bounded.
definition_raw: A metric space ''M'' is totally bounded if, for every ''r'' > 0, there exist a [[finite set|finite]] cover of ''M'' by open balls of radius ''r''. A metric space is compact if and only if it is complete and totally bounded.

definition: A space is totally disconnected if it has no connected subset with more than one point.
definition_raw: A space is totally disconnected if it has no connected subset with more than one point.

definition: The trivial topology (or indiscrete topology) on a set X consists of precisely the empty set and the entire space X.
definition_raw: The [[trivial topology]] (or '''indiscrete topology''') on a set ''X'' consists of precisely the empty set and the entire space ''X''.

definition: A Tychonoff space (or completely regular Hausdorff space, completely T3 space, T3.5 space) is a completely regular T0 space. (A completely regular space is Hausdorff if and only if it is T0, so the terminology is consistent.) Every Tychonoff space is regular Hausdorff.
definition_raw: A [[Tychonoff space]] (or '''completely regular Hausdorff''' space, '''completely T<sub>3</sub>''' space, '''T<sub>3.5</sub>''' space) is a completely regular T<sub>0</sub> space.  (A completely regular space is Hausdorff [[if and only if]] it is T<sub>0</sub>, so the terminology is consistent.) Every Tychonoff space is regular Hausdorff.

definition: A space is ultra-connected if no two non-empty closed sets are disjoint. Every ultra-connected space is path-connected.
definition_raw: A space is ultra-connected if no two non-empty closed sets are disjoint.<ref name="ss29"/> Every ultra-connected space is path-connected.

definition: A metric is an ultrametric if it satisfies the following stronger version of the triangle inequality: for all x, y, z in M, d(x, z) ≤ max(d(x, y), d(y, z)).
definition_raw: A metric is an ultrametric if it satisfies the following stronger version of the [[triangle inequality]]: for all ''x'', ''y'', ''z'' in ''M'', ''d''(''x'', ''z'') ≤ max(''d''(''x'', ''y''), ''d''(''y'', ''z'')).

definition: If X and Y are uniform spaces, a uniform isomorphism from X to Y is a bijective function f : X → Y such that f and f−1 are uniformly continuous. The spaces are then said to be uniformly isomorphic and share the same uniform properties.
definition_raw: If ''X'' and ''Y'' are [[uniform space]]s, a uniform isomorphism from ''X'' to ''Y'' is a bijective function ''f'' : ''X'' → ''Y'' such that ''f'' and ''f''<sup>−1</sup> are [[uniformly continuous]]. The spaces are then said to be uniformly isomorphic and share the same [[uniform properties]].

definition: A space is uniformizable if it is homeomorphic to a uniform space.
definition_raw: A space is uniformizable if it is homeomorphic to a uniform space.

definition: A uniform space is a set X equipped with a nonempty collection Φ of subsets of the Cartesian product X × X satisfying the following axioms:
definition_raw: A [[uniform space]] is a set ''X'' equipped with a nonempty collection Φ of subsets of the [[Cartesian product]] ''X'' × ''X'' satisfying the following [[axiom]]s:

definition: See Uniform space.
definition_raw: See '''[[Uniform space]]'''.

definition: Vietoris topology
definition_raw: Vietoris topology

definition: The weak topology on a set, with respect to a collection of functions from that set into topological spaces, is the coarsest topology on the set which makes all the functions continuous.
definition_raw: The [[weak topology]] on a set, with respect to a collection of functions from that set into topological spaces, is the coarsest topology on the set which makes all the functions continuous.

definition: See Coarser topology. Beware, some authors, especially analysts, use the term stronger topology.
definition_raw: See '''[[Coarser topology]]'''. Beware, some authors, especially [[mathematical analysis|analyst]]s, use the term '''stronger topology'''.

definition: A space is weakly countably compact (or limit point compact) if every infinite subset has a limit point.
definition_raw: A space is weakly countably compact (or '''limit point compact''') if every [[Infinity|infinite]] subset has a limit point.

definition: A property of spaces is said to be weakly hereditary if whenever a space has that property, then so does every closed subspace of it. For example, compactness and the Lindelöf property are both weakly hereditary properties, although neither is hereditary.
definition_raw: A property of spaces is said to be weakly hereditary if whenever a space has that property, then so does every closed subspace of it. For example, compactness and the Lindelöf property are both weakly hereditary properties, although neither is hereditary.

definition: The weight of a space X is the smallest cardinal number κ such that X has a base of cardinal κ. (Note that such a cardinal number exists, because the entire topology forms a base, and because the class of cardinal numbers is well-ordered.)
definition_raw: The [[weight of a space]] ''X'' is the smallest [[cardinal number]] κ such that ''X'' has a base of cardinal κ. (Note that such a cardinal number exists, because the entire topology forms a base, and because the class of cardinal numbers is [[well order|well-ordered]].)

definition: See Ultra-connected. (Some authors use this term strictly for ultra-connected compact spaces.)
definition_raw: See '''Ultra-connected'''. (Some authors use this term strictly for ultra-connected compact spaces.)

definition: A space is zero-dimensional if it has a base of clopen sets.
definition_raw: A space is [[zero-dimensional]] if it has a base of clopen sets.<ref name=ss33>Steen & Seebach (1978) p.33</ref>

definition: G[S] is the induced subgraph of a graph G for vertex subset S.
definition_raw: {{math|''G''[''S'']}} is the {{gli|induced|induced subgraph}} of a graph {{mvar|G}} for vertex subset {{mvar|S}}.

definition: The prime symbol is often used to modify notation for graph invariants so that it applies to the line graph instead of the given graph. For instance, α(G) is the independence number of a graph; α′(G) is the matching number of the graph, which equals the independence number of its line graph. Similarly, χ(G) is the chromatic number of a graph; χ is the chromatic index of the graph, which equals the chromatic number of its line graph.
definition_raw: The [[Prime (symbol)|prime symbol]] is often used to modify notation for graph invariants so that it applies to the [[line graph]] instead of the given graph. For instance, {{math|''α''(''G'')}} is the independence number of a graph; {{math|''α''′(''G'')}} is the matching number of the graph, which equals the independence number of its line graph. Similarly, {{math|''χ''(''G'')}} is the chromatic number of a graph; {{math|''χ''{{hsp}}′(''G'')}} is the chromatic index of the g

definition: An absorbing set $A$ of a directed graph $G$ is a set of vertices such that for any vertex $v \in G \setminus A$, there is an edge from $v$ towards a vertex of $A$.
definition_raw: An absorbing set <math>A</math> of a directed graph <math>G</math> is a set of vertices such that for any vertex <math>v \in G \setminus A</math>, there is an edge from <math>v</math> towards a vertex of <math>A</math>.

definition: The achromatic number of a graph is the maximum number of colors in a complete coloring.
definition_raw: The [[achromatic number]] of a graph is the maximum number of colors in a complete coloring.<ref>{{citation|last1=Farber|first1=M.|last2=Hahn|first2=G.|last3=Hell|first3=P.|author3-link=Pavol Hell|last4=Miller|first4=D. J.|title=Concerning the achromatic number of graphs|journal=Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B|volume=40|issue=1|year=1986|pages=21–39|doi=10.1016/0095-8956(86)90062-6|doi-access=free}}.</ref>

definition: A graph is acyclic if it has no cycles. An undirected acyclic graph is the same thing as a forest. An acyclic directed graph, which is a digraph without directed cycles, is often called a directed acyclic graph, especially in computer science.
definition_raw: A graph is acyclic if it has no cycles. An undirected acyclic graph is the same thing as a [[Tree (graph theory)|forest]]. An acyclic directed graph, which is a digraph without directed cycles, is often called a [[directed acyclic graph]], especially in computer science.<ref name="clrs">{{citation|title=Introduction to Algorithms|year=2001|publisher=MIT Press and McGraw-Hill|first1=Thomas H.|last1=Cormen|author1-link=Thomas H. Cormen|first2=Charles E.|last2=Leiserson|author2-link=Charles E. Leis

definition: The adjacency matrix of a graph is a matrix whose rows and columns are both indexed by vertices of the graph, with a one in the cell for row i and column j when vertices i and j are adjacent, and a zero otherwise.
definition_raw: The [[adjacency matrix]] of a graph is a matrix whose rows and columns are both indexed by vertices of the graph, with a one in the cell for row {{mvar|i}} and column {{mvar|j}} when vertices {{mvar|i}} and {{mvar|j}} are adjacent, and a zero otherwise.<ref>{{harvtxt|Cormen|Leiserson|Rivest|Stein|2001}}, p. 529.</ref>

definition: The relation between two vertices that are both endpoints of the same edge.
definition_raw: The relation between two vertices that are both endpoints of the same edge.<ref name="clrs"/>

definition: For a graph G, α(G) (using the Greek letter alpha) is its independence number (see independent), and α′(G) is its matching number (see matching).
definition_raw: For a graph {{mvar|G}}, {{math|''α''(''G'')}} (using the Greek letter alpha) is its independence number (see {{gli|independent}}), and {{math|''α''′(''G'')}} is its matching number (see {{gli|matching}}).

definition: In a graph with a matching, an alternating path is a path whose edges alternate between matched and unmatched edges. An alternating cycle is, similarly, a cycle whose edges alternate between matched and unmatched edges. An augmenting path is an alternating path that starts and ends at unsaturated vertices. A larger matching can be found as the symmetric difference of the matching and the augmenting path; a matching is maximum if and only if it has no augmenting path.
definition_raw: In a graph with a matching, an alternating path is a path whose edges alternate between matched and unmatched edges. An alternating cycle is, similarly, a cycle whose edges alternate between matched and unmatched edges. An augmenting path is an alternating path that starts and ends at unsaturated vertices. A larger matching can be found as the [[symmetric difference]] of the matching and the augmenting path; a matching is maximum if and only if it has no augmenting path.

definition: In a directed acyclic graph, a subset S of vertices that are pairwise incomparable, i.e., for any $x \leq y $ in S, there is no directed path from x to y or from y to x. Inspired by the notion of antichains in partially ordered sets.
definition_raw: In a [[directed acyclic graph]], a subset {{mvar|S}} of vertices that are pairwise incomparable, i.e., for any <math>x \leq y </math> in {{mvar|S}}, there is no directed path from {{mvar|x}} to {{mvar|y}} or from {{mvar|y}} to {{mvar|x}}. Inspired by the notion of [[antichain]]s in [[partially ordered set]]s.

definition: Synonym for non-edge, a pair of non-adjacent vertices.
definition_raw: Synonym for ''non-edge'', a pair of non-adjacent vertices.

definition: A three-vertex independent set, the complement of a triangle.
definition_raw: A three-vertex independent set, the complement of a triangle.

definition: An apex graph is a graph in which one vertex can be removed, leaving a planar subgraph. The removed vertex is called the apex. A k-apex graph is a graph that can be made planar by the removal of k vertices.
definition_raw: An [[apex graph]] is a graph in which one vertex can be removed, leaving a {{gli|planar}} subgraph. The removed vertex is called the apex. A {{mvar|k}}-apex graph is a graph that can be made planar by the removal of {{mvar|k}} vertices.

definition: Synonym for a rooted and directed tree; see tree.
definition_raw: Synonym for a rooted and directed tree; see {{gli|tree}}.

definition: See edge.
definition_raw: See {{gli|edge}}.

definition: An ordered pair of vertices, such as an edge in a directed graph. An arrow (x, y) has a tail x, a head y, and a direction from x to y; y is said to be the direct successor to x and x the direct predecessor to y. The arrow (y, x) is the inverted arrow of the arrow (x, y).
definition_raw: An [[ordered pair]] of {{gli|vertex|vertices}}, such as an {{gli|edge}} in a {{gli|directed graph}}. An arrow {{math|(''x'', ''y'')}} has a {{gli|tail}} {{math|''x''}}, a {{gli|head}} {{math|''y''}}, and a {{gli|direction}} {{math|from ''x'' to ''y''}}; {{math|''y''}} is said to be the {{gli|direct successor}} to {{math|''x''}} and {{math|''x''}} the {{gli|direct predecessor}} to {{math|''y''}}. The arrow {{math|(''y'', ''x'')}} is the {{gli|inverted arrow}} of the arrow {{math|(''x'', ''y'')}}.

definition: A vertex in a connected graph whose removal would disconnect the graph. More generally, a vertex whose removal increases the number of components.
definition_raw: A {{gli|vertex}} in a {{gli|connected graph}} whose removal would {{gli|disconnect}} the graph. More generally, a vertex whose removal increases the number of {{gli|component}}s.

definition: A k-ary tree is a rooted tree in which every internal vertex has no more than k children. A 1-ary tree is just a path. A 2-ary tree is also called a binary tree, although that term more properly refers to 2-ary trees in which the children of each node are distinguished as being left or right children (with at most one of each type). A k-ary tree is said to be complete if every internal vertex has exactly k children.
definition_raw: A [[K-ary tree|{{mvar|k}}-ary tree]] is a rooted tree in which every internal vertex has no more than {{mvar|k}} children. A 1-ary tree is just a path. A 2-ary tree is also called a [[binary tree]], although that term more properly refers to 2-ary trees in which the children of each node are distinguished as being left or right children (with at most one of each type).  A {{mvar|k}}-ary tree is said to be complete if every internal vertex has exactly {{mvar|k}} children.

definition: A special type of alternating path; see alternating.
definition_raw: A special type of alternating path; see {{gli|alternating}}.

definition: A graph automorphism is a symmetry of a graph, an isomorphism from the graph to itself.
definition_raw: A [[graph automorphism]] is a symmetry of a graph, an isomorphism from the graph to itself.

definition: One of the sets of vertices in a tree decomposition.
definition_raw: One of the sets of vertices in a {{gli|tree decomposition}}.

definition: A bipartite or multipartite graph is balanced if each two subsets of its vertex partition have sizes within one of each other.
definition_raw: A bipartite or multipartite graph is balanced if each two subsets of its vertex partition have sizes within one of each other.

definition: A ball (also known as a neighborhood ball or distance ball) is the set of all vertices that are at most distance r from a vertex. More formally, for a given vertex v and radius r, the ball B(v,r) consists of all vertices whose shortest path distance to v is less than or equal to r.
definition_raw: A ball (also known as a neighborhood ball or distance ball) is the set of all vertices that are at most distance r from a vertex. More formally, for a given vertex v and radius r, the ball B(v,r) consists of all vertices whose shortest path distance to v is less than or equal to r.

definition: The bandwidth of a graph G is the minimum, over all orderings of vertices of G, of the length of the longest edge (the number of steps in the ordering between its two endpoints). It is also one less than the size of the maximum clique in a proper interval completion of G, chosen to minimize the clique size.
definition_raw: The [[Graph bandwidth|bandwidth]] of a graph {{mvar|G}} is the minimum, over all orderings of vertices of {{mvar|G}}, of the length of the longest edge (the number of steps in the ordering between its two endpoints). It is also one less than the size of the maximum clique in a proper interval completion of {{mvar|G}}, chosen to minimize the clique size.

definition: Synonym for complete bipartite graph or complete bipartite subgraph; see complete.
definition_raw: Synonym for [[complete bipartite graph]] or complete bipartite subgraph; see {{gli|complete}}.

definition: Usually a synonym for 2-vertex-connected, but sometimes includes K2 though it is not 2-connected. See connected; for biconnected components, see component.
definition_raw: Usually a synonym for [[k-vertex-connected graph|{{math|2}}-vertex-connected]], but sometimes includes ''K''<sub>2</sub> though it is not 2-connected. See {{gli|connected}}; for [[biconnected component]]s, see {{gli|component}}.

definition: The smallest possible ratio of the number of neighbors of a proper subset of vertices to the size of the subset.
definition_raw: The smallest possible ratio of the number of neighbors of a proper subset of vertices to the size of the subset.<ref>{{Citation | first = D. R. | last = Woodall | title = The Binding Number of a Graph and its Anderson Number | journal= J. Combin. Theory Ser. B| year = 1973 | volume = 15 | issue = 3 |pages=225–255 | doi=10.1016/0095-8956(73)90038-5| doi-access = free }}</ref>

definition: A bipartite graph is a graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that the vertices in one set are not connected to each other, but may be connected to vertices in the other set. Put another way, a bipartite graph is a graph with no odd cycles; equivalently, it is a graph that may be properly colored with two colors. Bipartite graphs are often written G = (U,V,E) where U and V are the subsets of vertices of each color. However, unless the graph is connected, it may not have a unique 2-coloring.
definition_raw: A [[bipartite graph]] is a graph whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that the vertices in one set are not connected to each other, but may be connected to vertices in the other set. Put another way, a bipartite graph is a graph with no odd cycles; equivalently, it is a graph that may be properly colored with two colors. Bipartite graphs are often written {{math|1=''G'' = (''U'',''V'',''E'')}} where {{mvar|U}} and {{mvar|V}} are the subsets of vertices of each color. However

definition: A biregular graph is a bipartite graph in which there are only two different vertex degrees, one for each set of the vertex bipartition.
definition_raw: A [[biregular graph]] is a {{gli|bipartite}} graph in which there are only two different vertex degrees, one for each set of the vertex bipartition.

definition: A block of a graph G is a maximal subgraph which is either an isolated vertex, a bridge edge, or a 2-connected subgraph. If a block is 2-connected, every pair of vertices in it belong to a common cycle. Every edge of a graph belongs in exactly one block.
definition_raw: A block of a graph {{mvar|G}} is a maximal subgraph which is either an isolated vertex, a bridge edge, or a 2-connected subgraph. If a block is 2-connected, every pair of vertices in it belong to a common cycle. Every edge of a graph belongs in exactly one block.

definition: A minimal cut-set: a set of edges whose removal disconnects the graph, for which no proper subset has the same property.
definition_raw: A {{gli|minimal}} {{gli|cut-set}}: a set of edges whose removal disconnects the graph, for which no proper subset has the same property.

definition: A book, book graph, or triangular book is a complete tripartite graph K1,1,n; a collection of n triangles joined at a shared edge.
definition_raw: A [[Book (graph theory)|book]], book graph, or triangular book is a complete tripartite graph {{math|''K''<sub>1,1,''n''</sub>}}; a collection of {{mvar|n}} triangles joined at a shared edge.

definition: In a graph embedding, a boundary walk is the subgraph containing all incident edges and vertices to a face.
definition_raw: In a [[graph embedding]], a boundary walk is the subgraph containing all incident edges and vertices to a {{gli|face}}.

definition: A bramble is a collection of mutually touching connected subgraphs, where two subgraphs touch if they share a vertex or each includes one endpoint of an edge. The order of a bramble is the smallest size of a set of vertices that has a nonempty intersection with all of the subgraphs. The treewidth of a graph is the maximum order of any of its brambles.
definition_raw: A [[Bramble (graph theory)|bramble]] is a collection of mutually touching connected subgraphs, where two subgraphs touch if they share a vertex or each includes one endpoint of an edge. The order of a bramble is the smallest size of a set of vertices that has a nonempty intersection with all of the subgraphs. The treewidth of a graph is the maximum order of any of its brambles.

definition: A path of degree-two vertices, ending at vertices whose degree is unequal to two.
definition_raw:
A path of degree-two vertices, ending at vertices whose degree is unequal to two.<ref>{{citation
 | last = van der Holst | first = Hein
 | date = March 2009
 | doi = 10.1016/j.jctb.2008.10.002
 | issue = 2
 | journal = Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B
 | pages = 512–530
 | publisher = Elsevier BV
 | title = A polynomial-time algorithm to find a linkless embedding of a graph
 | volume = 99
 | url = https://research.tue.nl/en/publications/5df93dc5-c2bd-4c47-81a5-9d8d111eabbe
 }}</ref>

definition: A branch-decomposition of G is a hierarchical clustering of the edges of G, represented by an unrooted binary tree with its leaves labeled by the edges of G. The width of a branch-decomposition is the maximum, over edges e of this binary tree, of the number of shared vertices between the subgraphs determined by the edges of G in the two subtrees separated by e. The branchwidth of G is the minimum width of any branch-decomposition of G.
definition_raw: A [[branch-decomposition]] of {{mvar|G}} is a hierarchical clustering of the edges of {{mvar|G}}, represented by an unrooted binary tree with its leaves labeled by the edges of {{mvar|G}}. The width of a branch-decomposition is the maximum, over edges {{mvar|e}} of this binary tree, of the number of shared vertices between the subgraphs determined by the edges of {{mvar|G}} in the two subtrees separated by {{mvar|e}}. The branchwidth of {{mvar|G}} is the minimum width of any branch-decomposition

definition: See branch-decomposition.
definition_raw: See {{gli|branch-decomposition}}.

definition: A bridge, isthmus, or cut edge is an edge whose removal would disconnect the graph. A bridgeless graph is one that has no bridges; equivalently, a 2-edge-connected graph.
definition_raw: A [[Bridge (graph theory)|bridge]], isthmus, or cut edge is an edge whose removal would disconnect the graph. A bridgeless graph is one that has no bridges; equivalently, a 2-edge-connected graph.

definition: A bridgeless or isthmus-free graph is a graph that has no bridge edges (i.e., isthmi); that is, each connected component is a 2-edge-connected graph.
definition_raw: A [[bridgeless graph|bridgeless]] or isthmus-free graph is a graph that has no bridge edges (i.e., isthmi); that is, each connected component is a [[k-edge-connected graph|2-edge-connected graph]].

definition: The butterfly graph has five vertices and six edges; it is formed by two triangles that share a vertex.
definition_raw: The [[butterfly graph]] has five vertices and six edges; it is formed by two triangles that share a vertex.

definition: Cn is an n-vertex cycle graph; see cycle.
definition_raw: {{math|''C''<sub>''n''</sub>}} is an {{mvar|n}}-vertex [[cycle graph]]; see {{gli|cycle}}.

definition: A cactus graph, cactus tree, cactus, or Husimi tree is a connected graph in which each edge belongs to at most one cycle. Its blocks are cycles or single edges. If, in addition, each vertex belongs to at most two blocks, then it is called a Christmas cactus.
definition_raw: A [[cactus graph]], cactus tree, cactus, or Husimi tree is a connected graph in which each edge belongs to at most one cycle. Its blocks are cycles or single edges. If, in addition, each vertex belongs to at most two blocks, then it is called a Christmas cactus.

definition: A cage is a regular graph with the smallest possible order for its girth.
definition_raw: A [[Cage (graph theory)|cage]] is a regular graph with the smallest possible order for its girth.

definition: A canonical form of a graph is an invariant such that two graphs have equal invariants if and only if they are isomorphic. Canonical forms may also be called canonical invariants or complete invariants, and are sometimes defined only for the graphs within a particular family of graphs. Graph canonization is the process of computing a canonical form.
definition_raw: A [[canonical form]] of a graph is an invariant such that two graphs have equal invariants if and only if they are isomorphic. Canonical forms may also be called canonical invariants or complete invariants, and are sometimes defined only for the graphs within a particular family of graphs. [[Graph canonization]] is the process of computing a canonical form.

definition: A canonical form of a graph is an invariant such that two graphs have equal invariants if and only if they are isomorphic. Canonical forms may also be called canonical invariants or complete invariants, and are sometimes defined only for the graphs within a particular family of graphs. Graph canonization is the process of computing a canonical form.
definition_raw: A [[canonical form]] of a graph is an invariant such that two graphs have equal invariants if and only if they are isomorphic. Canonical forms may also be called canonical invariants or complete invariants, and are sometimes defined only for the graphs within a particular family of graphs. [[Graph canonization]] is the process of computing a canonical form.

definition: A graph formed from a given graph by deleting one vertex, especially in the context of the reconstruction conjecture. See also deck, the multiset of all cards of a graph.
definition_raw: A graph formed from a given graph by deleting one vertex, especially in the context of the [[reconstruction conjecture]]. See also {{gli|deck}}, the multiset of all cards of a graph.

definition: Carving width is a notion of graph width analogous to branchwidth, but using hierarchical clusterings of vertices instead of hierarchical clusterings of edges.
definition_raw: Carving width is a notion of graph width analogous to branchwidth, but using hierarchical clusterings of vertices instead of hierarchical clusterings of edges.

definition: A caterpillar tree or caterpillar is a tree in which the internal nodes induce a path.
definition_raw: A [[caterpillar tree]] or caterpillar is a tree in which the internal nodes induce a path.

definition: The center of a graph is the set of vertices of minimum eccentricity.
definition_raw: The [[Graph center|center]] of a graph is the set of vertices of minimum {{gli|eccentricity}}.

definition: A centroid of a tree is a vertex v such that if rooted at v, no other vertex has subtree size greater than half the size of the tree.
definition_raw: A [[Centroid (graph theory)|centroid]] of a tree is a vertex {{mvar|v}} such that if rooted at {{mvar|v}}, no other vertex has subtree size greater than half the size of the tree.

definition: Synonym for walk.
definition_raw: Synonym for {{gli|walk}}.

definition: See expansion.
definition_raw: See {{gli|expansion}}.

definition: A cherry is a path on three vertices.
definition_raw: A cherry is a path on three vertices.<ref>{{citation|last1=Sudakov|first1=Benny|last2=Volec|first2=Jan|title=Properly colored and rainbow copies of graphs with few cherries|journal=Journal of Combinatorial Theory, Series B|volume=122|issue=1|year=2017|pages=391–416|doi=10.1016/j.jctb.2016.07.001|doi-access=free|arxiv=1504.06176}}.</ref>

definition: χ(G) (using the Greek letter chi) is the chromatic number of G and χ is its chromatic index; see chromatic and coloring.
definition_raw: {{math|''χ''(''G'')}} (using the Greek letter chi) is the chromatic number of {{mvar|G}} and {{math|''χ''{{hsp}}′(''G'')}} is its chromatic index; see {{gli|chromatic}} and {{gli|coloring}}.

definition: In a rooted tree, a child of a vertex v is a neighbor of v along an outgoing edge, one that is directed away from the root.
definition_raw: In a rooted tree, a child of a vertex {{mvar|v}} is a neighbor of {{mvar|v}} along an outgoing edge, one that is directed away from the root.

definition: A chord of a cycle is an edge that does not belong to the cycle, for which both endpoints belong to the cycle.
definition_raw: A chord of a cycle is an edge that does not belong to the cycle, for which both endpoints belong to the cycle.

definition: A chord of a cycle is an edge that does not belong to the cycle, for which both endpoints belong to the cycle.
definition_raw: A chord of a cycle is an edge that does not belong to the cycle, for which both endpoints belong to the cycle.

definition: Having to do with coloring; see color. Chromatic graph theory is the theory of graph coloring. The chromatic number χ(G) is the minimum number of colors needed in a proper coloring of G. χ is the chromatic index of G, the minimum number of colors needed in a proper edge coloring of G.
definition_raw: Having to do with coloring; see {{gli|color}}. Chromatic graph theory is the theory of graph coloring. The [[chromatic number]] {{math|''χ''(''G'')}} is the minimum number of colors needed in a proper coloring of {{mvar|G}}. {{math|''χ''{{hsp}}′(''G'')}} is the [[chromatic index]] of {{mvar|G}}, the minimum number of colors needed in a proper [[edge coloring]] of {{mvar|G}}.

definition: A graph is k-choosable if it has a list coloring whenever each vertex has a list of k available colors. The choosability of the graph is the smallest k for which it is k-choosable.
definition_raw: A graph is {{mvar|k}}-choosable if it has a [[list coloring]] whenever each vertex has a list of {{mvar|k}} available colors. The choosability of the graph is the smallest {{mvar|k}} for which it is {{mvar|k}}-choosable.

definition: A graph is k-choosable if it has a list coloring whenever each vertex has a list of k available colors. The choosability of the graph is the smallest k for which it is k-choosable.
definition_raw: A graph is {{mvar|k}}-choosable if it has a [[list coloring]] whenever each vertex has a list of {{mvar|k}} available colors. The choosability of the graph is the smallest {{mvar|k}} for which it is {{mvar|k}}-choosable.

definition: A circle graph is the intersection graph of chords of a circle.
definition_raw: A [[circle graph]] is the [[intersection graph]] of chords of a circle.

definition: A circuit may refer to a closed trail or an element of the cycle space (an Eulerian spanning subgraph). The circuit rank of a graph is the dimension of its cycle space.
definition_raw: A circuit may refer to a closed trail or an element of the [[cycle space]] (an Eulerian spanning subgraph). The [[circuit rank]] of a graph is the dimension of its cycle space.

definition: The circumference of a graph is the length of its longest simple cycle. The graph is Hamiltonian if and only if its circumference equals its order.
definition_raw: The [[Circumference (graph theory)|circumference]] of a graph is the length of its longest simple cycle. The graph is Hamiltonian if and only if its circumference equals its order.

definition: A class of graphs or family of graphs is a (usually infinite) collection of graphs, often defined as the graphs having some specific property. The word "class" is used rather than "set" because, unless special restrictions are made (such as restricting the vertices to be drawn from a particular set, and defining edges to be sets of two vertices) classes of graphs are usually not sets when formalized using set theory.
definition_raw: A [[Class (set theory)|class]] of graphs or family of graphs is a (usually infinite) collection of graphs, often defined as the graphs having some specific property. The word "class" is used rather than "set" because, unless special restrictions are made (such as restricting the vertices to be drawn from a particular set, and defining edges to be sets of two vertices) classes of graphs are usually not sets when formalized using set theory.

definition: A claw is a tree with one internal vertex and three leaves, or equivalently the complete bipartite graph K1,3. A claw-free graph is a graph that does not have an induced subgraph that is a claw.
definition_raw: A [[Claw (graph theory)|claw]] is a tree with one internal vertex and three leaves, or equivalently the complete bipartite graph {{math|''K''<sub>1,3</sub>}}. A [[claw-free graph]] is a graph that does not have an induced subgraph that is a claw.

definition: A clique is a set of mutually adjacent vertices (or the complete subgraph induced by that set). Sometimes a clique is defined as a maximal set of mutually adjacent vertices (or maximal complete subgraph), one that is not part of any larger such set (or subgraph). A k-clique is a clique of order k. The clique number ω(G) of a graph G is the order of its largest clique. The clique graph of a graph G is the intersection graph of the maximal cliques in G. See also biclique, a complete bipartite subgraph.
definition_raw: A [[Clique (graph theory)|clique]] is a set of mutually adjacent vertices (or the complete subgraph induced by that set).  Sometimes a clique is defined as a maximal set of mutually adjacent vertices (or maximal complete subgraph), one that is not part of any larger such set (or subgraph). A {{mvar|k}}-clique is a clique of order {{mvar|k}}. The [[clique number]] {{math|''ω''(''G'')}} of a graph {{mvar|G}} is the order of its largest clique. The [[clique graph]] of a graph ''G'' is the [[i

definition: A synonym for a block graph.
definition_raw: A synonym for a {{gli|block graph}}.

definition: The clique-width of a graph G is the minimum number of distinct labels needed to construct G by operations that create a labeled vertex, form the disjoint union of two labeled graphs, add an edge connecting all pairs of vertices with given labels, or relabel all vertices with a given label. The graphs of clique-width at most 2 are exactly the cographs.
definition_raw: The [[clique-width]] of a graph {{mvar|G}} is the minimum number of distinct labels needed to construct {{mvar|G}} by operations that create a labeled vertex, form the disjoint union of two labeled graphs, add an edge connecting all pairs of vertices with given labels, or relabel all vertices with a given label. The graphs of clique-width at most {{math|2}} are exactly the [[cograph]]s.

definition: A closed neighborhood is one that includes its central vertex; see neighbourhood.
definition_raw: A closed neighborhood is one that includes its central vertex; see {{gli|neighbourhood}}.

definition: For the transitive closure of a directed graph, see transitive.
definition_raw: For the transitive closure of a directed graph, see {{gli|transitive}}.

definition: This prefix has various meanings usually involving complement graphs. For instance, a cograph is a graph produced by operations that include complementation; a cocoloring is a coloring in which each vertex induces either an independent set (as in proper coloring) or a clique (as in a coloring of the complement).
