definition: A joint relative invariant of several forms of the same degree, that is unchanged if a multiple of one of the forms is added to another. Essentially a relative invariant of a product of two general linear groups.
definition_raw: A joint relative invariant of several forms of the same degree, that is unchanged if a multiple of one of the forms is added to another. Essentially a relative invariant of a product of two general linear groups. {{harv|Elliott|1895|loc=p.340}} {{harvs|txt|last=Sylvester|year=1853|loc=Glossary p. 543–548}} {{harv|Salmon|1885|loc=p.161}}

definition: Related to invariants of a product of groups. For example a combinative covariant is a covariant of a product of two groups.
definition_raw: Related to invariants of a product of groups. For example a combinative covariant is a covariant of a product of two groups.

definition: A generalization of the determinant to arrays of dimension greater than 2.
definition_raw: A generalization of the determinant to arrays of dimension greater than 2. {{harv|Cayley|1860}}

definition: A complete system of invariants is a set of generators for the ring of invariants.
definition_raw: A complete system of invariants is a set of generators for the ring of invariants.

definition_raw: A relative invariant of GL(''V'') acting on the polynomials over ''S''<sup>''n''</sup>(''V'')⊕''V''⊕V*.

definition_raw: A form in two sets of variables, one set corresponding to a vector space and the other to its dual, or in other words an element of the symmetric algebra of ''V''⊕''V''* for a vector space ''V''. Introduced by Clebsch.

definition: The discriminant of a form in n variables is the multivariate resultant of the n differentials with respect to each of the variables. For binary forms the discriminant vanishes if the form has multiple roots and is essentially the same as the discriminant of a polynomial of 1 variable. The discriminant of a form vanishes when the corresponding hypersurface has singularities (as a scheme).

The discriminant of a form vanishes when the corresponding hypersurface has singularities (as a scheme).

definition: A skew invariant is a relative invariant of a group G that changes sign under an element of order 2 in its abelianization. In particular for the general linear group it changes sign under elements of determinant –1, and for the symmetric group it changes sign under odd permutations. For binary forms skew invariants are the invariants of odd weight. They do not exist for binary quadrics, cubics, or quartics, but do for binary quintics.
definition_raw: A skew invariant is a relative invariant of a group ''G'' that changes sign under an element of order 2 in its abelianization. In particular for the general linear group it changes sign under elements of determinant –1, and for the symmetric group it changes sign under odd permutations. For binary forms skew invariants are the invariants of odd weight. They do not exist for binary quadrics, cubics, or quartics, but do for binary quintics. {{harv|Elliott|1895|loc=p.112}}

definition: An operator (x, y) → (y, –x), rotating the plane 90° counterclockwise
definition_raw: An operator (''x, y'') → (''y, –x''), rotating the plane 90° counterclockwise

definition: Of a matrix $A$, another matrix $B$ such that $A$ multiplied by $B$ and $B$ multiplied by $A$ both equal the identity matrix.
definition_raw: Of a matrix <math>A</math>, another matrix <math>B</math> such that <math>A</math> multiplied by <math>B</math> and <math>B</math> multiplied by <math>A</math> both equal the identity matrix.{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=263}}

definition: In a vector space with a quadratic form, a non-zero vector for which the form is zero.
definition_raw: In a vector space with a [[quadratic form]], a non-zero vector for which the form is zero.

definition: A vector space with a quadratic form which has a null vector.
definition_raw: A vector space with a quadratic form which has a {{gli|null vector}}.

definition: The branch of mathematics that deals with vectors, vector spaces, linear transformations and systems of linear equations.
definition_raw: The branch of mathematics that deals with vectors, vector spaces, linear transformations and systems of linear equations.

definition: A sum, each of whose summands is an appropriate vector times an appropriate scalar (or ring element).
definition_raw: A sum, each of whose summands is an appropriate vector times an appropriate scalar (or ring element).{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=251}}

definition: A linear dependence of a tuple of vectors $\vec v_1,\ldots,\vec v_n$ is a nonzero tuple of scalar coefficients $c_1,\ldots,c_n$ for which the linear combination $c_1\vec v_1+\cdots+c_n\vec v_n$ equals $\vec0$.
definition_raw: A linear dependence of a tuple of vectors <math display=inline>\vec v_1,\ldots,\vec v_n</math> is a nonzero tuple of scalar coefficients <math display=inline>c_1,\ldots,c_n</math> for which the linear combination <math display=inline>c_1\vec v_1+\cdots+c_n\vec v_n</math> equals <math display=inline>\vec0</math>.

definition: A polynomial equation of degree one (such as $x = 2y - 7$).
definition_raw: A [[polynomial equation]] of degree one (such as <math>x = 2y - 7</math>).{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=252}}

definition: A linear map from a vector space to its field of scalars
definition_raw: A {{gli|linear map}} from a {{gli|vector space}} to its field of scalars{{sfn|Bourbaki|1989|page=232}}

definition: Property of being not linearly dependent.
definition_raw: Property of being not {{gli|linear dependence|linearly dependent}}.{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=111}}

definition: A function between vector spaces which respects addition and scalar multiplication.
definition_raw: A [[function (mathematics)|function]] between {{gli|vector space}}s which respects addition and scalar multiplication.

definition: A linear map whose domain and codomain are equal; it is generally supposed to be invertible.
definition_raw: A {{gli|linear map}} whose [[domain of a function|domain]] and [[codomain]] are equal; it is generally supposed to be [[inverse function|invertible]].

definition: Rectangular arrangement of numbers or other mathematical objects. A matrix is written A = (ai, j), where ai, j is the entry at row i and column j.
definition_raw: Rectangular arrangement of numbers or other [[mathematical object]]s.{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=263}} A matrix is written ''A'' = (a<sub>i, j</sub>), where a<sub>i, j</sub> is the entry at row i and column j.

definition: If a matrix A has the same number of columns as does matrix B of rows, then a product C = AB may be formed with ci, j equal to the dot product of row i of A with column j of B.
definition_raw: If a matrix ''A'' has the same number of columns as does matrix ''B'' of rows, then a product ''C'' = ''AB'' may be formed with ''c''<sub>i, j</sub> equal to the {{gli|dot product}} of row i of ''A'' with column j of ''B''.

definition: Two vectors u and v are orthogonal with respect to a bilinear form B when B(u,v) = 0.
definition_raw: Two vectors ''u'' and ''v'' are orthogonal with respect to a {{gli|bilinear form}} ''B'' when ''B''(''u,v'') = 0.

definition: Two vectors u and v are orthogonal with respect to a bilinear form B when B(u,v) = 0.
definition_raw: Two vectors ''u'' and ''v'' are orthogonal with respect to a {{gli|bilinear form}} ''B'' when ''B''(''u,v'') = 0.

definition: A set of vectors is orthonormal when they are all unit vectors and are pairwise orthogonal.
definition_raw: A set of vectors is orthonormal when they are all {{gli|unit vector}}s and are pairwise orthogonal.

definition: A real square matrix with rows (or columns) that form an orthonormal set.
definition_raw: A real square matrix with rows (or columns) that form an orthonormal set.

definition: A matrix with only one row.
definition_raw: A matrix with only one row.{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=263}}

definition: A scalar is an element of a field used in the definition of a vector space.
definition_raw: A scalar is an element of a [[field (mathematics)|field]] used in the definition of a {{gli|vector space}}.

definition: a factorization of an $m \times n$ complex matrix M as $\mathbf{U\Sigma V^*}$, where U is an $m \times m$ complex unitary matrix, $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is an $m \times n$ rectangular diagonal matrix with non-negative real numbers on the diagonal, and V is an $n \times n$ complex unitary matrix.
definition_raw: a factorization of an <math>m \times n</math> complex matrix {{math|'''M'''}} as <math>\mathbf{U\Sigma V^*}</math>, where {{math|'''U'''}} is an <math>m \times m</math> complex [[unitary matrix]], <math>\mathbf{\Sigma}</math> is an <math>m \times n</math> [[rectangular diagonal matrix]] with non-negative real numbers on the diagonal, and {{math|'''V'''}} is an <math>n \times n</math> complex unitary matrix.{{sfn|Williams|2014|page=407}}

definition: Set of the eigenvalues of a matrix.
definition_raw: Set of the [[eigenvalue]]s of a matrix.{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=389}}

definition: An element of a split-complex plane
definition_raw: An element of a split-complex plane

definition: A linear algebra over the real numbers with basis {1, j }, where j is a hyperbolic unit
definition_raw: A {{gli|linear algebra}} over the real numbers with {{gli|basis}} {1, j }, where j is a {{gli|hyperbolic unit}}

definition: A matrix having the same number of rows as columns.
definition_raw: A matrix having the same number of rows as columns.{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=263}}

definition: The transpose of an n × m matrix M is an m × n matrix M T obtained by using the rows of M for the columns of M T.
definition_raw: The transpose of an n × m matrix ''M'' is an m × n matrix ''M ''<sup>T</sup> obtained by using the rows of ''M'' for the columns of ''M ''<sup>T</sup>.

definition: a vector in a normed vector space whose norm is 1, or a Euclidean vector of length one.
definition_raw: a vector in a normed vector space whose [[Norm (mathematics)|norm]] is 1, or a [[Euclidean vector]] of length one.{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=463}}

definition: A directed quantity, one with both magnitude and direction.
definition_raw: A directed quantity, one with both magnitude and direction.

definition: A set, whose elements can be added together, and multiplied by elements of a field (this is scalar multiplication); the set must be an abelian group under addition, and the scalar multiplication must be a linear map.
definition_raw: A [[set (mathematics)|set]], whose elements can be added together, and multiplied by elements of a [[field (mathematics)|field]] (this is [[scalar multiplication]]); the set must be an [[abelian group]] under addition, and the scalar multiplication must be a [[linear map]].{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=442}}

definition: The additive identity in a vector space. In a normed vector space, it is the unique vector of norm zero. In a Euclidean vector space, it is the unique vector of length zero.
definition_raw: The [[additive identity]] in a vector space. In a [[normed vector space]], it is the unique vector of norm zero. In a [[Euclidean vector space]], it is the unique vector of length zero.{{sfn|James|James|1992|page=452}}

definition: A tongue-in-cheek reference to category theory, using which one can employ arguments that establish a (possibly concrete) result without reference to any specifics of the present problem. For that reason, it is also known as general abstract nonsense or generalized abstract nonsense.
definition_raw: A [[tongue-in-cheek]] reference to [[category theory]], using which one can employ arguments that establish a (possibly concrete) result without reference to any specifics of the present problem. For that reason, it is also known as ''general abstract nonsense'' or ''generalized abstract nonsense''.

definition: A reference to a standard or choice-free presentation of some mathematical object (e.g., canonical map, canonical form, or canonical ordering). The same term can also be used more informally to refer to something "standard" or "classic". For example, one might say that Euclid's proof is the "canonical proof" of the infinitude of primes.
definition_raw: A reference to a standard or choice-free presentation of some [[mathematical object]] (e.g., canonical map, canonical form, or canonical ordering). The same term can also be used more informally to refer to something "standard" or "classic". For example, one might say that [[Euclid's Theorem#Euclid's proof|Euclid's proof]] is the "canonical proof" of [[Euclid's theorem|the infinitude of primes]].

definition: A result is called "deep" if its proof requires concepts and methods that are advanced beyond the concepts needed to formulate the result. For example, the prime number theorem – originally proved using techniques of complex analysis – was once thought to be a deep result until elementary proofs were found. On the other hand, the fact that π is irrational is usually known to be a deep result, because it requires a considerable development of real analysis before the proof can be established – even though the claim itself can be stated in terms of simple number theory and geometry.
definition_raw: A result is called "deep" if its proof requires concepts and methods that are advanced beyond the concepts needed to formulate the result. For example, the [[prime number theorem]] – originally proved using techniques of [[complex analysis]] – was once thought to be a deep result until [[elementary proof]]s were found.<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.math.columbia.edu/~goldfeld/ErdosSelbergDispute.pdf|title=The Elementary Proof of the Prime Number Theorem: An Historical Perspective|last=Goldfeld|f

definition: An aesthetic term referring to the ability of an idea to provide insight into mathematics, whether by unifying disparate fields, introducing a new perspective on a single field, or by providing a technique of proof which is either particularly simple, or which captures the intuition or imagination as to why the result it proves is true. In some occasions, the term "beautiful" can also be used to the same effect, though Gian-Carlo Rota distinguished between elegance of presentation and beauty of concept, saying that for example, some topics could be written about elegantly although the mathematical content is not beautiful, and some theorems or proofs are beautiful but may be written about inelegantly.
definition_raw: An aesthetic term referring to the ability of an idea to provide insight into mathematics, whether by unifying disparate fields, introducing a new perspective on a single field, or by providing a technique of proof which is either particularly simple, or which captures the intuition or imagination as to why the result it proves is true. In some occasions, the term "beautiful" can also be used to the same effect, though [[Gian-Carlo Rota]] distinguished between ''elegance of presentation'' and ''

definition: A proof or a result is called "elementary" if it only involves basic concepts and methods in the field, and is to be contrasted with deep results which require more development within or outside the field. The concept of "elementary proof" is used specifically in number theory, where it usually refers to a proof that does not resort to methods from complex analysis.
definition_raw: A proof or a result is called "elementary" if it only involves basic concepts and methods in the field, and is to be contrasted with [[#deep|deep]] results which require more development within or outside the field. The concept of "elementary proof" is used specifically in [[number theory]], where it usually refers to a proof that does not resort to methods from [[complex analysis]].

definition: A result is called "folklore" if it is non-obvious and non-published, yet generally known to the specialists within a field. In many scenarios, it is unclear as to who first obtained the result, though if the result is significant, it may eventually find its way into the textbooks, whereupon it ceases to be folklore.
definition_raw: A result is called "folklore" if it is non-obvious and non-published, yet generally known to the specialists within a field. In many scenarios, it is unclear as to who first obtained the result, though if the result is significant, it may eventually find its way into the textbooks, whereupon it ceases to be folklore.

definition: Similar to "canonical" but more specific, and which makes reference to a description (almost exclusively in the context of transformations) which holds independently of any choices. Though long used informally, this term has found a formal definition in category theory.
definition_raw: Similar to "canonical" but more specific, and which makes reference to a description (almost exclusively in the context of [[transformation (mathematics)|transformation]]s) which holds independently of any choices. Though long used informally, this term has found a formal definition in category theory.

definition: An object behaves pathologically (or, somewhat more broadly used, in a degenerated way) if it either fails to conform to the generic behavior of such objects, fails to satisfy certain context-dependent regularity properties, or simply disobeys mathematical intuition. In many occasions, these can be and often are contradictory requirements, while in other occasions, the term is more deliberately used to refer to an object artificially constructed as a counterexample to these properties. A simple example is that from the definition of a triangle having angles which sum to π radians, a single straight line conforms to this definition pathologically.
definition_raw: An object behaves pathologically (or, somewhat more broadly used, in a ''degenerated'' way) if it either fails to conform to the generic behavior of such objects, fails to satisfy certain context-dependent regularity properties, or simply disobeys [[mathematical intuition]]. In many occasions, these can be and often are contradictory requirements, while in other occasions, the term is more deliberately used to refer to an object artificially constructed as a counterexample to these properties. A

definition: The act of establishing a mathematical result using indisputable logic, rather than informal descriptive argument. Rigor is a cornerstone quality of mathematics, and can play an important role in preventing mathematics from degenerating into fallacies.
definition_raw: The act of establishing a mathematical result using indisputable logic, rather than informal descriptive argument. Rigor is a cornerstone quality of mathematics, and can play an important role in preventing mathematics from degenerating into fallacies.

definition: The act of establishing a mathematical result using indisputable logic, rather than informal descriptive argument. Rigor is a cornerstone quality of mathematics, and can play an important role in preventing mathematics from degenerating into fallacies.
definition_raw: The act of establishing a mathematical result using indisputable logic, rather than informal descriptive argument. Rigor is a cornerstone quality of mathematics, and can play an important role in preventing mathematics from degenerating into fallacies.

definition: An object is well-behaved (in contrast with being Pathological) if it satisfies certain prevailing regularity properties, or if it conforms to mathematical intuition (even though intuition can often suggest opposite behaviors as well). In some occasions (e.g., analysis), the term "smooth" can also be used to the same effect.
definition_raw: An object is well-behaved (in contrast with being ''[[#pathological|Pathological]]'') if it satisfies certain prevailing regularity properties, or if it conforms to mathematical intuition (even though intuition can often suggest opposite behaviors as well). In some occasions (e.g., [[mathematical analysis|analysis]]), the term "[[List of mathematical jargon#smooth|smooth]]''"'' can also be used to the same effect.

definition: A shorthand term for "all except for a set of measure zero", when there is a measure to speak of, with the phrases almost surely and almost everywhere having related meanings. For example, "almost all real numbers are transcendental" because the algebraic real numbers form a countable subset of the real numbers with measure zero. One can also speak of "almost all" integers having a property to mean "all except finitely many", despite the integers not admitting a measure for which this agrees with the previous usage, such as "almost all prime numbers are odd". There is a more complicated meaning for integers as well, discussed in the main article. Finally, this term is sometimes used synonymously with generic, below.
definition_raw: A shorthand term for "all except for a [[set (mathematics)|set]] of [[measure zero]]", when there is a [[measure (mathematics)|measure]] to speak of, with the phrases ''[[almost surely]]'' and ''[[almost everywhere]]'' having related meanings.  For example, "almost all [[real number]]s are [[transcendental number|transcendental]]" because the [[algebraic numbers|algebraic real numbers]] form a [[countable]] [[subset]] of the real numbers with measure zero.  One can also speak of "almost all" [[i

definition: Notions which arise mostly in the context of limits, referring to the recurrence of a phenomenon as the limit is approached. A statement such as that predicate P is satisfied by arbitrarily large values, can be expressed in more formal notation by . See also frequently. The statement that quantity f(x) depending on x "can be made" arbitrarily large, corresponds to .

A statement such as that predicate ''P'' is satisfied by arbitrarily large values, can be expressed in more formal notation by {{nowrap|∀''x'' : ∃''y'' ≥ ''x'' : ''P''(''y'')}}.  See also ''frequently''. The statement that quantity ''f''(''x'') depending on ''x'' "can be made" arbitrarily large, corresponds to {{nowrap|∀''y'' : ∃''x'' : ''f''(''x'')

definition: Notions which arise mostly in the context of limits, referring to the recurrence of a phenomenon as the limit is approached. A statement such as that predicate P is satisfied by arbitrarily large values, can be expressed in more formal notation by . See also frequently. The statement that quantity f(x) depending on x "can be made" arbitrarily large, corresponds to .

A statement such as that predicate P is satisfied by arbitrarily large values, can be expressed in more formal notation by . See also frequently. The statement that quantity f(x) depending on x "can be made" arbitrarily large, corresponds to .

definition: A shorthand for the universal quantifier. An arbitrary choice is one which is made unrestrictedly, or alternatively, a statement holds of an arbitrary element of a set if it holds of any element of that set. Also much in general-language use among mathematicians: "Of course, this problem can be arbitrarily complicated".
definition_raw: A shorthand for the [[universal quantifier]].  An arbitrary choice is one which is made unrestrictedly, or alternatively, a statement holds of an arbitrary element of a set if it holds of any element of that set. Also much in general-language use among mathematicians: "Of course, this problem can be arbitrarily complicated".

definition: In the context of limits, this is shorthand meaning for sufficiently large arguments; the relevant argument(s) are implicit in the context. As an example, the function log(log(x)) eventually becomes larger than 100"; in this context, "eventually" means "for sufficiently large x."
definition_raw: In the context of limits, this is shorthand meaning ''for sufficiently large arguments''; the relevant argument(s) are implicit in the context.  As an example, the function log(log(''x'')) ''eventually'' becomes larger than 100"; in this context, "eventually" means "for [[#sufficiently large|sufficiently large]] ''x''."

definition: A term in category theory referring to composition of morphisms. If for three objects A, B, and C a map $f \colon A \to C$ can be written as a composition $f = h \circ g$ with $g \colon A \to B$ and $h \colon B \to C$, then f is said to factor through any (and all) of $B$, $g$, and $h$.
definition_raw: A term in [[category theory]] referring to composition of [[morphism]]s.  If for three [[Object (category theory)|objects]] ''A'', ''B'', and ''C'' a map <math>f \colon A \to C</math> can be written as a composition <math>f = h \circ g</math> with <math>g \colon A \to B</math> and <math>h \colon B \to C</math>, then ''f'' is said to ''factor through'' any (and all) of <math>B</math>, <math>g</math>, and <math>h</math>.

definition: When said of the value of a variable assuming values from the non-negative extended reals $\R_{\geq 0}\cup\{\infty\},$ the meaning is usually "not infinite". For example, if the variance of a random variable is said to be finite, this implies it is a non-negative real number, possibly zero. In some contexts though, for example in "a small but finite amplitude", zero and infinitesimals are meant to be excluded. When said of the value of a variable assuming values from the extended natural numbers $\N\cup\{\infty\},$ the meaning is simply "not infinite". When said of a set or a mathematical object whose main component is a set, it means that the cardinality of the set is less than $\aleph_0$.
definition_raw: When said of the value of a variable assuming values from the non-negative [[extended reals]] <math>\R_{\geq 0}\cup\{\infty\},</math> the meaning is usually "not infinite". For example, if the [[variance]] of a [[random variable]] is said to be finite, this implies it is a non-negative real number, possibly zero. In some contexts though, for example in "a small but finite amplitude", zero and infinitesimals are meant to be excluded. When said of the value of a variable assuming values from the e

definition: In the context of limits, this is shorthand for arbitrarily large arguments and its relatives; as with eventually, the intended variant is implicit. As an example, the sequence $(-1)^n$ is frequently in the interval (1/2, 3/2), because there are arbitrarily large n for which the value of the sequence is in the interval.
definition_raw: In the context of limits, this is shorthand for ''[[#arbitrarily large|arbitrarily large]] arguments'' and its relatives; as with ''eventually'', the intended variant is implicit.  As an example, the [[sequence]] <math>(-1)^n</math> is frequently in the [[interval (mathematics)|interval]] (1/2, 3/2), because there are arbitrarily large ''n'' for which the value of the sequence is in the interval.

definition: Qualifies anything that is sufficiently precise to be translated straightforwardly in a formal system. For example. a formal proof, a formal definition.
definition_raw: Qualifies anything that is sufficiently precise to be translated straightforwardly in a [[formal system]]. For example. a [[formal proof]], a '''formal definition'''.

definition: Qualifies anything that is sufficiently precise to be translated straightforwardly in a formal system. For example. a formal proof, a formal definition.
definition_raw: Qualifies anything that is sufficiently precise to be translated straightforwardly in a [[formal system]]. For example. a [[formal proof]], a '''formal definition'''.

definition: This term has similar connotations as almost all but is used particularly for concepts outside the purview of measure theory. A property holds "generically" on a set if the set satisfies some (context-dependent) notion of density, or perhaps if its complement satisfies some (context-dependent) notion of smallness. For example, a property which holds on a dense Gδ (intersection of countably many open sets) is said to hold generically. In algebraic geometry, one says that a property of points on an algebraic variety that holds on a dense Zariski open set is true generically; however, it is usually not said that a property which holds merely on a dense set (which is not Zariski open) is generic in this situation.
definition_raw: This term has similar connotations as ''almost all'' but is used particularly for concepts outside the purview of [[measure theory]].  A property holds "generically" on a set if the set satisfies some (context-dependent) notion of density, or perhaps if its [[complement (set theory)|complement]] satisfies some (context-dependent) notion of smallness.  For example, a property which holds on a [[Dense set|dense]] '''G'''<sub>δ</sub> ([[intersection (set theory)|intersection]] of countably many [[o

definition: In a descriptive context, this phrase introduces a simple characterization of a broad class of objects, with an eye towards identifying a unifying principle. This term introduces an "elegant" description which holds for "arbitrary" objects. Exceptions to this description may be mentioned explicitly, as "pathological" cases.
definition_raw: In a descriptive context, this phrase introduces a simple characterization of a broad class of {{gli|object|objects}}, with an eye towards identifying a unifying principle.  This term introduces an "elegant" description which holds for "[[#arbitrary|arbitrary]]" objects.  Exceptions to this description may be mentioned explicitly, as "[[#pathological|pathological]]" cases.

definition: Most often, these refer simply to the left-hand or the right-hand side of an equation; for example, $x = y + 1$ has $x$ on the LHS and $y + 1$ on the RHS. Occasionally, these are used in the sense of lvalue and rvalue: an RHS is primitive, and an LHS is derivative.
definition_raw: Most often, these refer simply to the left-hand or the right-hand side of an [[equation]]; for example, <math>x = y + 1</math> has <math>x</math> on the LHS and <math>y + 1</math> on the RHS.  Occasionally, these are used in the sense of [[Value (computer science)#Assignment:_l-values_and_r-values|lvalue]] and rvalue: an RHS is primitive, and an LHS is derivative.

definition: Most often, these refer simply to the left-hand or the right-hand side of an equation; for example, $x = y + 1$ has $x$ on the LHS and $y + 1$ on the RHS. Occasionally, these are used in the sense of lvalue and rvalue: an RHS is primitive, and an LHS is derivative.
definition_raw: Most often, these refer simply to the left-hand or the right-hand side of an [[equation]]; for example, <math>x = y + 1</math> has <math>x</math> on the LHS and <math>y + 1</math> on the RHS.  Occasionally, these are used in the sense of [[Value (computer science)#Assignment:_l-values_and_r-values|lvalue]] and rvalue: an RHS is primitive, and an LHS is derivative.

definition: A mathematical object is colloquially called nice or sufficiently nice if it satisfies hypotheses or properties, sometimes unspecified or even unknown, that are especially desirable in a given context. It is an informal antonym for pathological. For example, one might conjecture that a differential operator ought to satisfy a certain boundedness condition "for nice test functions," or one might state that some interesting topological invariant should be computable "for nice spaces X."
definition_raw: A mathematical {{gli|object}} is colloquially called ''nice'' or ''sufficiently nice'' if it satisfies hypotheses or properties, sometimes unspecified or even unknown, that are especially desirable in a given context. It is an informal antonym for [[Mathematical jargon#pathological|pathological]]. For example, one might conjecture that a [[differential operator]] ought to satisfy a certain boundedness condition "for nice test functions," or one might state that some interesting [[topological inv

definition: A function (which in mathematics is generally defined as mapping the elements of one set A to elements of another B) is called "A onto B" (instead of "A to B" or "A into B") only if it is surjective; it may even be said that "f is onto" (i. e. surjective). Not translatable (without circumlocutions) to some languages other than English.
definition_raw: A function (which in mathematics is generally defined as mapping the elements of one set ''A'' to elements of another ''B'') is called "''A'' onto ''B''" (instead of "''A'' to ''B''" or "''A'' into ''B''") only if it is [[surjective]]; it may even be said that "''f'' is onto" (i. e. surjective). Not translatable (without circumlocutions) to some languages other than English.

definition: If, for some notion of substructure, objects are substructures of themselves (that is, the relationship is reflexive), then the qualification proper requires the objects to be different. For example, a proper subset of a set S is a subset of S that is different from S, and a proper divisor of a number n is a divisor of n that is different from n. This overloaded word is also non-jargon for a proper morphism.
definition_raw: If, for some notion of substructure, {{gli|object|objects}} are substructures of themselves (that is, the relationship is [[reflexive relation|reflexive]]), then the qualification ''proper'' requires the objects to be different. For example, a ''proper'' subset of a set ''S'' is a subset of ''S'' that is different from ''S'', and a ''proper'' [[divisor]] of a number ''n'' is a divisor of ''n'' that is different from ''n''.  This [[Operator overloading|overloaded]] word is also non-jargon for a [

definition: A function is called regular if it satisfies satisfactory continuity and differentiability properties, which are often context-dependent. These properties might include possessing a specified number of derivatives, with the function and its derivatives exhibiting some nice property (see nice above), such as Hölder continuity. Informally, this term is sometimes used synonymously with smooth, below. These imprecise uses of the word regular are not to be confused with the notion of a regular topological space, which is rigorously defined.
definition_raw: A function is called ''regular'' if it satisfies satisfactory continuity and differentiability properties, which are often context-dependent. These properties might include possessing a specified number of [[derivative]]s, with the function and its derivatives exhibiting some ''nice'' property (see ''nice'' above), such as [[Hölder continuity]]. Informally, this term is sometimes used synonymously with ''smooth'', below. These imprecise uses of the word ''regular'' are not to be confused with th

definition: (Respectively) A convention to shorten parallel expositions. "A (resp. B) [has some relationship to] X (resp. Y)" means that A [has some relationship to] X and also that B [has (the same) relationship to] Y. For example, squares (resp. triangles) have 4 sides (resp. 3 sides); or compact (resp. Lindelöf) spaces are ones where every open cover has a finite (resp. countable) open subcover.
definition_raw: (Respectively) A convention to shorten parallel expositions. "''A'' (resp. ''B'') [has some relationship to] ''X'' (resp. ''Y'')" means that ''A'' [has some relationship to] ''X'' and also that ''B'' [has (the same) relationship to] ''Y''. For example, [[square]]s (resp. triangles) have 4 sides (resp. 3 sides); or [[compact space|compact]] (resp. [[Lindelöf space|Lindelöf]]) spaces are ones where every open [[cover (topology)|cover]] has a finite (resp. countable) open subcover.

definition: Often, a mathematical theorem will establish constraints on the behavior of some object; for example, a function will be shown to have an upper or lower bound. The constraint is sharp (sometimes optimal) if it cannot be made more restrictive without failing in some cases. For example, for arbitrary non-negative real numbers x, the exponential function ex, where e = 2.7182818..., gives an upper bound on the values of the quadratic function x2. This is not sharp; the gap between the functions is everywhere at least 1. Among the exponential functions of the form αx, setting α = e2/e = 2.0870652... results in a sharp upper bound; the slightly smaller choice α = 2 fails to produce an upper bound, since then α3 = 8 < 32. In applied fields the word "tight" is often used with the same meaning.
definition_raw: Often, a mathematical theorem will establish constraints on the behavior of some {{gli|object}}; for example, a function will be shown to have an [[Upper and lower bounds|upper or lower bound]].  The constraint is ''sharp'' (sometimes ''optimal'') if it cannot be made more restrictive without failing in some cases. For example, for [[#arbitrary|arbitrary]] non-negative real numbers ''x'', the [[exponential function]] ''e<sup>x</sup>'', where [[Euler's number|''e'']] = 2.7182818..., giv

definition: Smoothness is a concept which mathematics has endowed with many meanings, from simple differentiability to infinite differentiability to analyticity, and still others which are more complicated. Each such usage attempts to invoke the physically intuitive notion of smoothness.
definition_raw: ''Smoothness'' is a concept which mathematics has endowed with many meanings, from simple differentiability to infinite differentiability to [[analytic function|analyticity]], and still others which are more complicated.  Each such usage attempts to invoke the physically intuitive notion of smoothness.

definition: A theorem is said to be strong if it deduces restrictive results from general hypotheses. One celebrated example is Donaldson's theorem, which puts tight restraints on what would otherwise appear to be a large class of manifolds. This (informal) usage reflects the opinion of the mathematical community: not only should such a theorem be strong in the descriptive sense (below) but it should also be definitive in its area. A theorem, result, or condition is further called stronger than another one if a proof of the second can be easily obtained from the first but not conversely. An example is the sequence of theorems: Fermat's little theorem, Euler's theorem, Lagrange's theorem, each of which is stronger than the last; another is that a sharp upper bound (see sharp above) is a stronger result than a non-sharp one. Finally, the adjective strong or the adverb strongly may be added to a mathematical notion to indicate a related stronger notion; for example, a strong antichain is an antichain satisfying certain additional conditions, and likewise a strongly regular graph is a regular graph meeting stronger conditions. When used in this way, the stronger notion (such as "strong antichain") is a technical term with a precisely defined meaning; the nature of the extra conditions cannot be derived from the definition of the weaker notion (such as "antichain").

term: sufficiently large, suitably small, sufficiently close
definition: In the context of limits, these terms refer to some (unspecified, even unknown) point at which a phenomenon prevails as the limit is approached. A statement such as that predicate P holds for sufficiently large values, can be expressed in more formal notation by ∃x : ∀y ≥ x : P(y). See also eventually.
definition_raw: In the context of limits, these terms refer to some (unspecified, even unknown) point at which a phenomenon prevails as the limit is approached. A statement such as that predicate ''P'' holds for sufficiently large values, can be expressed in more formal notation by ∃''x'' : ∀''y'' ≥ ''x'' : ''P''(''y'').  See also ''eventually''.

definition: In the context of limits, these terms refer to some (unspecified, even unknown) point at which a phenomenon prevails as the limit is approached. A statement such as that predicate P holds for sufficiently large values, can be expressed in more formal notation by ∃x : ∀y ≥ x : P(y). See also eventually.
definition_raw: In the context of limits, these terms refer to some (unspecified, even unknown) point at which a phenomenon prevails as the limit is approached. A statement such as that predicate ''P'' holds for sufficiently large values, can be expressed in more formal notation by ∃''x'' : ∀''y'' ≥ ''x'' : ''P''(''y'').  See also ''eventually''.

definition: A descriptive term referring to notation in which two objects are written one above the other; the upper one is upstairs and the lower, downstairs. For example, in a fiber bundle, the total space is often said to be upstairs, with the base space downstairs. In a fraction, the numerator is occasionally referred to as upstairs and the denominator downstairs, as in "bringing a term upstairs".
definition_raw: A descriptive term referring to notation in which two {{gli|object|objects}} are written one above the other; the upper one is ''upstairs'' and the lower, ''downstairs''.  For example, in a [[fiber bundle]], the total space is often said to be ''upstairs'', with the base space ''downstairs''.  In a [[fraction]], the [[fraction|numerator]] is occasionally referred to as ''upstairs'' and the [[fraction|denominator]] ''downstairs'', as in "bringing a term upstairs".

term: up to, modulo, mod out by
definition: An extension to mathematical discourse of the notions of modular arithmetic. A statement is true up to a condition if the establishment of that condition is the only impediment to the truth of the statement. Also used when working with members of equivalence classes, especially in category theory, where the equivalence relation is (categorical) isomorphism; for example, "The tensor product in a weak monoidal category is associative and unital up to a natural isomorphism."
definition_raw: An extension to mathematical discourse of the notions of [[modular arithmetic]]. definition: To assume the value 0. For example, "The function sin(x) vanishes for those values of x that are integer multiples of π." This can also apply to limits: see Vanish at infinity.

definition: Accurately and precisely described or specified. For example, sometimes a definition relies on a choice of some object; the result of the definition must then be independent of this choice.
definition_raw: Accurately and precisely described or specified. For example, sometimes a definition relies on a choice of some {{gli|object}}; the result of the definition must then be independent of this choice.

definition: An obsolescent term which is used to announce to the reader an alternative method, or proof of a result. In a proof, it therefore flags a piece of reasoning that is superfluous from a logical point of view, but has some other interest.
definition_raw: An obsolescent term which is used to announce to the reader an alternative method, or proof of a result. In a proof, it therefore flags a piece of reasoning that is superfluous from a logical point of view, but has some other interest.

definition: The rhetorical prelude to a proof by contradiction, preceding the negation of the statement to be proved.

definition: The rhetorical prelude to a proof by contradiction, preceding the negation of the statement to be proved.

definition: A minor variant on "if and only if"; "A is necessary (and sufficient) for B" means "A if (only if) B". For example, "For a field K to be algebraically closed it is necessary and sufficient that it have no finite field extensions" means "K is algebraically closed if and only if it has no finite extensions". Often used in lists, as in "The following conditions are necessary and sufficient for a field to be algebraically closed...".

For example, "For a [[field (mathematics)|field]] ''K'' to be [[algebraically closed field|algebraically closed]] it is necessary and sufficient that it have no finite [[field extension]]s" means "''K'' is algebraically closed if and only if it has no finite extensions". term: need to show (NTS), required to prove (RTP), wish to show, want to show (WTS)
definition: Proofs sometimes proceed by enumerating several conditions whose satisfaction will together imply the desired theorem; thus, one needs to show just these statements.
definition_raw: Proofs sometimes proceed by enumerating several conditions whose satisfaction will together imply the desired theorem; thus, one ''needs to show'' just these statements.

definition: Proofs sometimes proceed by enumerating several conditions whose satisfaction will together imply the desired theorem; thus, one needs to show just these statements.
definition_raw: Proofs sometimes proceed by enumerating several conditions whose satisfaction will together imply the desired theorem; thus, one ''needs to show'' just these statements.

definition: A statement of the existence and uniqueness of an object; the object exists, and furthermore, no other such object exists.
definition_raw: A statement of the [[uniqueness quantification|existence and uniqueness]] of an {{gli|object}}; the object exists, and furthermore, no other such object exists.

definition: (Quod erat demonstrandum): A Latin abbreviation, meaning "which was to be demonstrated", historically placed at the end of proofs, but less common currently, having been supplanted by the Halmos end-of-proof mark, a square sign ∎.
definition_raw: (''Quod erat demonstrandum''): A Latin abbreviation, meaning "which was to be demonstrated", historically placed at the end of proofs, but less common currently, having been supplanted by the [[Tombstone (typography)|Halmos end-of-proof mark]], a square sign ∎.

definition: A condition on objects in the scope of the discussion, to be specified later, that will guarantee that some stated property holds for them. When working out a theorem, the use of this expression in the statement of the theorem indicates that the conditions involved may be not yet known to the speaker, and that the intent is to collect the conditions that will be found to be needed in order for the proof of the theorem to go through.

definition: Often several equivalent conditions (especially for a definition, such as normal subgroup) are equally useful in practice; one introduces a theorem stating an equivalence of more than two statements with TFAE.

definition: It is often the case that two objects are shown to be equivalent in some way, and that one of them is endowed with additional structure. Using the equivalence, we may define such a structure on the second object as well, via transport of structure. For example, any two vector spaces of the same dimension are isomorphic; if one of them is given an inner product and if we fix a particular isomorphism, then we may define an inner product on the other space by factoring through the isomorphism.

term: without (any) loss of generality (WLOG, WOLOG, WALOG), we may assume (WMA)
definition: Sometimes a proposition can be more easily proved with additional assumptions on the objects it concerns. If the proposition as stated follows from this modified one with a simple and minimal explanation (for example, if the remaining special cases are identical but for notation), then the modified assumptions are introduced with this phrase and the altered proposition is proved.

definition: Used to describe a geometrical proof that involves finding relationships between the various angles in a diagram.<ref>{{Citation |title=Elementary Geometry|first=John|last=Roe
definition_raw: Used to describe a geometrical proof that involves finding relationships between the various angles in a diagram.<ref>{{Citation |title=Elementary Geometry|first=John|last=Roe

definition: An informal computation omitting much rigor without sacrificing correctness. Often this computation is "proof of concept" and treats only an accessible special case.
definition_raw: An informal computation omitting much rigor without sacrificing correctness.  Often this computation is "[[proof of concept]]" and treats only an accessible special case.

definition: Rather than finding underlying principles or patterns, this is a method where one would evaluate as many cases as needed to sufficiently prove or provide convincing evidence that the thing in question is true. Sometimes this involves evaluating every possible case (where it is also known as proof by exhaustion).
definition_raw: Rather than finding underlying principles or patterns, this is a method where one would evaluate as many cases as needed to sufficiently prove or provide convincing evidence that the thing in question is true. Sometimes this involves evaluating every possible case (where it is also known as [[proof by exhaustion]]).

definition: A proof by example is an argument whereby a statement is not proved but instead illustrated by an example. If done well, the specific example would easily generalize to a general proof.
definition_raw: A ''proof by example'' is an argument whereby a statement is not proved but instead illustrated by an example. If done well, the specific example would easily generalize to a general proof.

definition: A rhetorical shortcut made by authors who invite the reader to verify, at a glance, the correctness of a proposed expression or deduction. If an expression can be evaluated by straightforward application of simple techniques and without recourse to extended calculation or general theory, then it can be evaluated by inspection. It is also applied to solving equations; for example to find roots of a quadratic equation by inspection is to 'notice' them, or mentally check them. 'By inspection' can play a kind of gestalt role: the answer or solution simply clicks into place.

definition: Style of proof where claims believed by the author to be easily verifiable are labelled as 'obvious' or 'trivial', which often results in the reader being confused.

definition: A term which shortcuts around calculation the mathematician perceives to be tedious or routine, accessible to any member of the audience with the necessary expertise in the field; Laplace used obvious (French: évident).

definition: Given a commutative diagram of objects and morphisms between them, if one wishes to prove some property of the morphisms (such as injectivity) which can be stated in terms of elements, then the proof can proceed by tracing the path of elements of various objects around the diagram as successive morphisms are applied to it. That is, one chases elements around the diagram, or does a diagram chase.

definition: A non-technique of proof mostly employed in lectures, where formal argument is not strictly necessary. It proceeds by omission of details or even significant ingredients, and is merely a plausibility argument.
definition_raw: A non-technique of proof mostly employed in lectures, where formal argument is not strictly necessary.  It proceeds by omission of details or even significant ingredients, and is merely a plausibility argument.

definition: In a context not requiring rigor, this phrase often appears as a labor-saving device when the technical details of a complete argument would outweigh the conceptual benefits. The author gives a proof in a simple enough case that the computations are reasonable, and then indicates that "in general" the proof is similar.

definition: For proofs involving objects with multiple indices which can be solved by going to the bottom (if anyone wishes to take up the effort). Similar to diagram chasing.
definition_raw: For proofs involving objects with multiple indices which can be solved by going to the bottom (if anyone wishes to take up the effort). Similar to diagram chasing.

definition: Used to indicate that the speaker believes a statement should be true, given their mathematical experience, even though a proof has not yet been put forward. As a variation, the statement may in fact be false, but instead provide a slogan for or illustration of a correct principle. Hasse's local-global principle is a particularly influential example of this.
definition_raw: Used to indicate that the speaker believes a statement ''should'' be true, given their mathematical experience, even though a proof has not yet been put forward.  As a variation, the statement may in fact be false, but instead provide a slogan for or illustration of a correct principle.  [[Helmut Hasse|Hasse]]'s [[local-global principle]] is a particularly influential example of this.{{Citation needed|date=April 2010}}

definition_raw: See ''[[#clearly|clearly]]''.

term: the proof is left as an exercise to the reader
definition: Usually applied to a claim within a larger proof when the proof of that claim can be produced routinely by any member of the audience with the necessary expertise, but is not so simple as to be obvious.
definition_raw: Usually applied to a claim within a larger proof when the proof of that claim can be produced routinely by any member of the audience with the necessary expertise, but is not so simple as to be ''obvious''.

definition: Similar to clearly. A concept is trivial if it holds by definition, is an immediate corollary to a known statement, or is a simple special case of a more general concept.
definition_raw: Similar to ''clearly''.  A concept is trivial if it holds by definition, is an immediate [[corollary]] to a known statement, or is a simple special case of a more general concept.

definition: A binary relation is a set of ordered pairs; an element x is said to be related to another element y if and only if (x, are in the set.

In other words, it is a special kind of correspondence where given an element a of A, there is a unique element b of B that corresponds to it.

definition: An invariant of an object or a space is a property or number of the object or a space that remains unchanged under some transformations.
definition_raw: An [[invariant (mathematics)|invariant]] of an object or a space is a property or number of the object or a space that remains unchanged under some transformations.

definition: A synonym for a function between sets or a morphism in a category. Depending on authors, the term "maps" or the term "functions" may be reserved for specific kinds of functions or morphisms (e.g., function as an analytic term and map as a general term).
definition_raw:
A synonym for a [[function (mathematics)|function]] between sets or a [[morphism]] in a category. Depending on authors, the term "maps" 
or the term "functions" may be reserved for specific kinds of functions or morphisms (e.g., function as an analytic term and map as a general term).{{explain|date=November 2025|reason=using jargon to explain other jargon explains nothing}}

definition: See mathematics.
definition_raw: See [[mathematics]].

definition: A "multivalued function” from a set A to a set B is a function from A to the subsets of B. It has typically the property that, for almost all points x of B, there is a neighbourhood of x such that the restriction of the function to the neighbourhood can be considered as a set of functions from the neighbourhood to B.
definition_raw: A "[[multivalued function]]” from a set {{mvar|A}} to a set {{mvar|B}} is a function from {{mvar|A}} to the subsets of {{mvar|B}}. It has typically the property that, for almost all points {{mvar|x}} of {{mvar|B}}, there is a neighbourhood of {{mvar|x}} such that the restriction of the function to the neighbourhood can be considered as a set of functions from the neighbourhood to {{mvar|B}}.{{explain|date=November 2025|reason=using jargon to explain other jargon explains nothing}}

definition: A projection is, roughly, a map from some space or object to another that omits some information on the object or space. For example, $\mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}, (x, y) \mapsto x$ is a projection and its restriction to a graph of a function, say, is also a projection. The terms “idempotent operator” and “forgetful map” are also synonyms for a projection.
definition_raw: A [[projection (mathematics)|projection]] is, roughly, a map from some space or object to another that omits some information on the object or space. For example, <math>\mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R}, (x, y) \mapsto x</math> is a projection and its restriction to a graph of a function, say, is also a projection. The terms “[[idempotent operator]]” and “[[forgetful map]]” are also synonyms for a projection.{{explain|date=November 2025|reason=using jargon to explain other jargon explains nothing}}

definition: A mathematical structure on an object is an additional set of objects or data attached to the object (e.g., relation, operation, metric, topology).
definition_raw: A [[mathematical structure]] on an object is an additional set of objects or data attached to the object (e.g., relation, operation, metric, topology).

definition: algebraically compact module (also called pure injective module) is a module in which all systems of equations can be decided by finitary means. Alternatively, those modules which leave pure-exact sequence exact after applying Hom.
definition_raw: [[algebraically compact module]] (also called [[pure injective module]]) is a module in which all systems of equations can be decided by finitary means. Alternatively, those modules which leave pure-exact sequence exact after applying Hom.

definition: The annihilator of a left $R$-module $M$ is the set $\textrm{Ann}(M) := \{ r \in R ~|~ rm = 0 \, \forall m \in M \}$ . It is a (two-sided) ideal of $R$.

definition: An Artinian module is a module in which every decreasing chain of submodules becomes stationary after finitely many steps.
definition_raw: An [[Artinian module]] is a module in which every decreasing chain of submodules becomes stationary after finitely many steps.

definition: A countably generated module is a module that admits a generating set whose cardinality is at most countable.

definition: A module is called a cyclic module if it is generated by one element.
definition_raw: A module is called a [[cyclic module]] if it is generated by one element.

definition: The determinant of a finite free module over a commutative ring is the r-th exterior power of the module when r is the rank of the module.

definition: A differential graded module or dg-module is a graded module with a differential.
definition_raw: A [[differential graded module]] or dg-module is a graded module with a differential.

definition: A direct sum of modules is a module that is the direct sum of the underlying abelian group together with component-wise scalar multiplication.
definition_raw: A [[direct sum of modules]] is a module that is the direct sum of the underlying abelian group together with component-wise scalar multiplication.

definition: An indecomposable module M is directing if there is no cycle of module homomorphisms $f_0: M \to M_1, f_1: M_1 \to M_2, ..., f_{n-1}: M_{n-1} \to M$ such that for all i, $f_i$ is neither zero nor an isomorphism.
definition_raw: An indecomposable module ''M'' is directing if there is no cycle of module homomorphisms <math>f_0: M \to M_1, f_1: M_1 \to M_2, ..., f_{n-1}: M_{n-1} \to M</math> such that for all ''i'', <math>f_i</math> is neither zero nor an isomorphism.

definition: The dual module of a module M over a commutative ring R is the module $\operatorname{Hom}_R(M, R)$.
definition_raw: The dual module of a module ''M'' over a commutative ring ''R'' is the module <math>\operatorname{Hom}_R(M, R)</math>.

definition: A Drinfeld module is a module over a ring of functions on algebraic curve with coefficients from a finite field.
definition_raw: A [[Drinfeld module]] is a module over a ring of functions on algebraic curve with coefficients from a finite field.

definition: Given a module M, an essential submodule N of M is a submodule that every nonzero submodule of M intersects non-trivially.

definition: Extension of scalars uses a ring homomorphism from R to S to convert R-modules to S-modules.

definition: A faithful module $M$ is one where the action of each nonzero $r \in R$ on $M$ is nontrivial (i.e. $rx \ne 0$ for some $x$ in $M$). Equivalently, $\textrm{Ann}(M)$ is the zero ideal.
definition_raw: A [[faithful module]] <math>M</math> is one where the action of each nonzero <math>r \in R</math> on <math>M</math> is nontrivial (i.e. <math>rx \ne 0</math> for some <math>x</math> in <math>M</math>). Equivalently, <math>\textrm{Ann}(M)</math> is the zero ideal.

definition: The term "finite module" is another name for a finitely generated module.
definition_raw: The term "[[finite module]]" is another name for a [[finitely generated module]].

definition: A module of finite length is a module that admits a (finite) composition series.
definition_raw: A module of finite [[length of a module|length]] is a module that admits a (finite) composition series.

definition: A finite free presentation of a module M is an exact sequence $F_1 \to F_0 \to M$ where $F_i$ are finitely generated free modules.
definition_raw: A [[finite free presentation]] of a module ''M'' is an exact sequence <math>F_1 \to F_0 \to M</math> where <math>F_i</math> are finitely generated free modules.

definition: A module $M$ is finitely generated if there exist finitely many elements $x_1,...,x_n$ in $M$ such that every element of $M$ is a finite linear combination of those elements with coefficients from the scalar ring $R$.
definition_raw: A module <math>M</math> is [[finitely generated module|finitely generated]] if there exist finitely many elements <math>x_1,...,x_n</math> in <math>M</math> such that every element of <math>M</math> is a finite linear combination of those elements with coefficients from the scalar ring <math>R</math>.

definition: A $R$-module $F$ is called a flat module if the tensor product functor $- \otimes_R F$ is exact.In particular, every projective module is flat.
definition_raw: A <math>R</math>-module <math>F</math> is called a [[flat module]] if the [[Tensor product of modules|tensor product]] functor <math>- \otimes_R F</math> is [[exact functor|exact]].{{br}}In particular, every projective module is flat.

definition: A free module is a module that has a basis, or equivalently, one that is isomorphic to a direct sum of copies of the scalar ring $R$.
definition_raw: A [[free module]] is a module that has a basis, or equivalently, one that is isomorphic to a direct sum of copies of the scalar ring <math>R</math>.

definition_raw: [[Frobenius reciprocity]].

definition: A Galois module is a module over the group ring of a Galois group.
definition_raw: A [[Galois module]] is a module over the group ring of a Galois group.

definition: A subset of a module is called a generating set of the module if the submodule generated by the set (i.e., the smallest subset containing the set) is the entire module itself.
definition_raw: A subset of a module is called a [[generating set of a module|generating set]] of the module if the submodule generated by the set (i.e., the smallest subset containing the set) is the entire module itself.

definition: A module $M$ over a graded ring $A = \bigoplus_{n\in \mathbb N}A_n$ is a graded module if $M$ can be expressed as a direct sum $\bigoplus_{i\in \mathbb N}M_i$ and $A_i M_j \subseteq M_{i+j}$.
definition_raw: A module <math>M</math> over a graded ring <math>A = \bigoplus_{n\in \mathbb N}A_n</math> is a [[graded module]] if <math>M</math> can be expressed as a direct sum <math>\bigoplus_{i\in \mathbb N}M_i</math> and <math>A_i M_j \subseteq M_{i+j}</math>.

definition: For two left $R$-modules $M_1, M_2$, a group homomorphism $\phi: M_1 \to M_2$ is called homomorphism of $R$-modules if $r \phi(m) = \phi (r m) \, \forall r \in R, m \in M_1$ .
definition_raw: For two left <math>R</math>-modules <math>M_1, M_2</math>, a group homomorphism <math>\phi: M_1 \to M_2</math> is called [[module homomorphism|homomorphism of <math>R</math>-modules]] if <math>r \phi(m) = \phi (r m) \, \forall r \in R, m \in M_1</math> .

definition: An idempotent is an endomorphism whose square is itself.

definition: An indecomposable module is a non-zero module that cannot be written as a direct sum of two non-zero submodules. Every simple module is indecomposable (but not conversely).
definition_raw: An [[indecomposable module]] is a non-zero module that cannot be written as a direct sum of two non-zero submodules. Every simple module is indecomposable (but not conversely).

definition: The index of an endomorphism $f : M \to M$ is the difference $\operatorname{length}(\operatorname{coker}(f)) - \operatorname{length}(\operatorname{ker}(f))$, when the cokernel and kernel of $f$ have finite length.
definition_raw: The [[index of an endomorphism]] <math>f : M \to M</math> is the difference <math>\operatorname{length}(\operatorname{coker}(f)) - \operatorname{length}(\operatorname{ker}(f))</math>, when the cokernel and kernel of <math>f</math> have finite length.

definition: A $R$-module $Q$ is called an injective module if given a $R$-module homomorphism $g: X \to Q$, and an injective $R$-module homomorphism $f: X \to Y$, there exists a $R$-module homomorphism $h : Y \to Q$ such that $f \circ h = g$. right|frame|The module Q is injective if the diagram commutes : The following conditions are equivalent: :* The contravariant functor $\textrm{Hom}_R( - , I)$ is exact. :* $I$ is a injective module. :* Every short exact sequence $0 \to I \to L \to L' \to 0$ is split.

A [[left module]] <math>M</math> over the [[ring (mathematics)|ring]] <math>R</math> is an [[abelian group]] <math>(M, +)</math> with an operation <math>R \times M \to M</math> (called scalar multipliction) satisfies the following condition:
:: <math>\forall r,s \in R, \forall m,n \in M</math>,
:# <math>r (m + n) = rm + rn</math>

definition: right|frame|The characteristic property of projective modules is called lift (mathematics)|lifting.A $R$-module $P$ is called a projective module if given a $R$-module homomorphism $g: P \to M$, and a surjective $R$-module homomorphism $f: N \to M$, there exists a $R$-module homomorphism $h : P \to N$ such that $f \circ h = g$. : The following conditions are equivalent: :* The covariant functor $\textrm{Hom}_R(P, - )$ is exact. :* $M$ is a projective module. :* Every short exact sequence $0 \to L \to L' \to P \to 0$ is split. :* $M$ is a direct summand of free modules. : In particular, every free module is projective.

It is called a [[quotient module]] or '''factor module'''.

definition: Euclid's lemma states that if a prime p divides the product of two integers ab, then p must divide at least one of a or b.

definition: Let p is an odd prime and a is an integer not divisible by p. Euler's criterion provides a slick way to determine whether a is a quadratic residue mod p. It says that <math>a^{\tfrac{p-1}{2

definition: Euler's theorem states that if n and a are coprime positive integers, then aφ(n) is congruent to 1 mod n. Euler's theorem generalizes Fermat's little theorem.
definition_raw: [[Euler's theorem]] states that if {{math|''n''}} and {{math|''a''}} are coprime positive integers, then {{math|''a''<sup>''φ''(''n'')</sup>}} is congruent to {{math|1}} mod {{math|''n''}}. Euler's theorem generalizes Fermat's little theorem.

definition: For a positive integer n, Euler's totient function of n, denoted φ(n), is the number of integers coprime to n between 1 and n inclusive. For example, φ(4) = 2 and φ(p) = p - 1 for any prime p.
definition_raw: For a positive integer {{math|''n''}}, [[Euler's totient function]] of {{math|''n''}}, denoted {{math|''φ''(''n'')}}, is the number of integers coprime to {{math|''n''}} between {{math|1}} and {{math|''n''}} inclusive. For example, {{math|1=''φ''(4) = 2}} and {{math|1=''φ''(''p'') = ''p'' - 1}} for any prime {{math|''p''}}.

definition: Factorization is the process of splitting a mathematical object, often integers or polynomials, into a product of factors.
definition_raw: [[Factorization]] is the process of splitting a mathematical object, often integers or polynomials, into a product of factors.

definition: Fermat's Last Theorem, one of the most famous and difficult to prove theorems in number theory, states that for any integer n > 2, the equation an + bn = cn has no positive integer solutions.
definition_raw: [[Fermat's Last Theorem]], one of the most famous and difficult to prove theorems in number theory, states that for any integer {{math|''n'' > 2}}, the equation {{math|1=''a''<sup>''n''</sup> + ''b''<sup>''n''</sup> = ''c''<sup>''n''</sup>}} has no positive integer solutions.

definition: A field extension L/K is a pair of fields K and L such that K is a subfield of L. Given a field extension L/K, the field L is a K-vector space.

definition: The fundamental theorem of arithmetic states that every integer greater than 1 can be written uniquely (up to reordering) as a product of primes.
definition_raw: The [[fundamental theorem of arithmetic]] states that every integer greater than 1 can be written uniquely (up to reordering) as a product of primes.

definition: A Galois extension is a finite field extension L/K such that one of the following equivalent conditions are satisfied: *L/K is a normal extension and a separable extension, *L is a splitting field of a separable polynomial with coefficients in K, *, that is, the number of automorphisms of the extension equals its degree. The automorphism group Aut(L/K) of a Galois extension is called its Galois group and it is denoted Gal(L/K).

A [[Galois extension]] is a finite field extension {{math|''L''/''K''}} such that one of the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:
*{{math|''L''/''K''}} is a [[normal extension]] and a [[separable extension]],
*{{math|''L''}} is a [[splitting field]] of a [[separable polynomial]] with coefficients in {{math|''K''}},
*{{math|1={{!}}Aut(''L''/''K''){{!}} = [''L'':''K'']}}, that is, the number of automorphisms of the extension equals its [[degree (field theory)|degree]].

definition: Goldbach's conjecture is a conjecture that states that every even natural number greater than 2 is the sum of two primes.
definition_raw: [[Goldbach's conjecture]] is a conjecture that states that every even natural number greater than 2 is the sum of two primes.

definition: The greatest common divisor of a finite list of integers is the largest positive number that is a divisor of every integer in the list.
definition_raw: The [[greatest common divisor]] of a finite list of integers is the largest positive number that is a divisor of every integer in the list.

definition: The ideal class group of a number field is the group of fractional ideals in the ring of integers in the field modulo principal ideals. The cardinality of the group is called the class number of the number field. It measures the extent of the failure of unique factorization.
definition_raw: The [[ideal class group]] of a number field is the group of fractional ideals in the ring of integers in the field modulo principal ideals. The cardinality of the group is called the class number of the number field. It measures the extent of the failure of unique factorization.

definition: The integers are the numbers …, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ….

definition: The least common multiple of a finite list of integers is the smallest positive number that is a multiple of every integer in the list.

definition: Let p be an odd prime and let a be an integer. The Legendre symbol of a and p is :$\left(\frac{a}{p}\right) = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } a \text{ is a quadratic residue mod } p \text{ and } a \not\equiv 0\pmod p, \\ -1 & \text{if } a \text{ is not a quadratic residue mod } p \text{ and } a \not\equiv 0\pmod p, \\ 0 & \text{if } a \equiv 0 \pmod p. \end{cases}$ The Legendre symbol provides a convenient notational package for several results, including the law of quadratic reciprocity and Euler's criterion.

-1 & \text{if } a \text{ is not a quadratic residue mod } p \text{ and } a \not\equiv 0\pmod p, \\
 0 & \text{if } a \equiv 0 \pmod p.
\end{cases}</math>

definition: A local field in number theory is the completion of a number field at a finite place.
definition_raw: A [[local field]] in number theory is the completion of a number field at a finite place.

definition: A Mersenne prime is a prime number one less than a power of 2.
definition_raw: A [[Mersenne prime]] is a prime number one less than a power of 2.

definition: A number field, also called an algebraic number field, is a finite-degree field extension of the field of rational numbers.
definition_raw: A [[Algebraic number field|number field]], also called an algebraic number field, is a finite-degree field extension of the field of rational numbers.

definition: The non-abelian class field theory is an extension of the class field theory (which is about abelian extensions of number fields) to non-abelian extensions; or at least the idea of such a theory. The non-abelian theory does not exist in a definitive form today.

definition: A place is an equivalence class of non-Archimedean valuations (finite place) or absolute values (infinite place).
definition_raw: A [[place (number theory)|place]] is an equivalence class of non-Archimedean valuations (finite place) or absolute values (infinite place).

definition: A prime number is a positive integer with no divisors other than itself and 1.
definition_raw: A [[prime number]] is a positive integer with no divisors other than itself and 1.

definition: A profinite integer is an element in the profinite completion $\widehat{\mathbb Z}$ of $\mathbb{Z}$ along all integers.
definition_raw: A [[profinite integer]] is an element in the profinite completion <math>\widehat{\mathbb Z}</math> of <math>\mathbb{Z}</math> along all integers.

definition: A Pythagorean triple is three positive integers a, b, c such that a.

definition_raw: The [[ramification theory]].

definition: The ring of integers in a number field is the ring consisting of all algebraic numbers contained in the field.
definition_raw: The [[ring of integers]] in a number field is the ring consisting of all [[algebraic number]]s contained in the field.

definition: An integer q is called a quadratic residue mod n if it is congruent to a perfect square mod n, i.e., if there exists an integer x such that $x^2\equiv q \pmod{n}.$
definition_raw: An integer {{math|''q''}} is called a [[quadratic residue]] mod {{math|''n''}} if it is congruent to a perfect square mod {{math|''n''}}, i.e., if there exists an integer {{math|''x''}} such that <math>x^2\equiv q \pmod{n}.</math>

definition_raw: [[Sieve of Eratosthenes]]

definition: A square-free integer is an integer that is not divisible by any square other than 1.
definition_raw: A [[square-free integer]] is an integer that is not divisible by any square other than 1.

definition: A square number is an integer that is the square of an integer. For example, 4 and 9 are squares, but 10 is not a square.
definition_raw: A [[square number]] is an integer that is the square of an integer. For example, 4 and 9 are squares, but 10 is not a square.

definition: Szpiro's conjecture is, in a modified form, equivalent to the abc conjecture.
definition_raw: [[Szpiro's conjecture]] is, in a modified form, equivalent to the abc conjecture.

definition: Takagi existence theorem is a theorem in class field theory.
definition_raw: [[Takagi existence theorem]] is a theorem in class field theory.

definition: See Euler's totient function.
definition_raw: See Euler's totient function.

definition: A twin prime is a prime number that is 2 less or 2 more than another prime number. For example, 7 is a twin prime, since it is prime and 5 is also prime.
definition_raw: A [[twin prime]] is a prime number that is 2 less or 2 more than another prime number. For example, 7 is a twin prime, since it is prime and 5 is also prime.

definition: A valued field is a field with a valuation on it.
definition_raw: A [[valued field]] is a field with a valuation on it.
