definition: Wilson's theorem states that n > 1 is prime if and only if (n-1)! is congruent to -1 mod n.
definition_raw: [[Wilson's theorem]] states that {{math|1=''n'' > 1}} is prime if and only if {{math|(''n''-1)!}} is congruent to {{math|-1}} mod {{math|''n''}}.

definition: A binary relation is acyclic if it contains no "cycles": equivalently, its transitive closure is antisymmetric.
definition_raw: A [[binary relation]] is acyclic if it contains no "cycles": equivalently, its [[transitive closure]] is [[Antisymmetric relation|antisymmetric]].<ref name=BosSuz/>

definition: See Galois connection.
definition_raw: See ''Galois connection''.

definition: For a preordered set P, any upper set O is Alexandrov-open. Inversely, a topology is Alexandrov if any intersection of open sets is open.
definition_raw: For a preordered set ''P'', any upper set ''O'' is '''Alexandrov-open'''. Inversely, a topology is Alexandrov if any intersection of open sets is open.

definition: A poset is algebraic if it has a base of compact elements.
definition_raw: A poset is algebraic if it has a base of compact elements.

definition: An antichain is a poset in which no two elements are comparable, i.e., there are no two distinct elements x and y such that x ≤ y. In other words, the order relation of an antichain is just the identity relation.
definition_raw: An antichain is a poset in which no two elements are comparable, i.e., there are no two distinct elements ''x'' and ''y'' such that ''x'' ≤ ''y''. In other words, the order relation of an antichain is just the identity relation.

definition: See way-below relation.
definition_raw: See ''way-below relation''.

definition: A homogeneous relation R on a set X is antisymmetric, if x R y and y R x implies x = y, for all elements x, y in X.
definition_raw: A [[homogeneous relation]] ''R'' on a set ''X'' is '''[[Antisymmetric relation|antisymmetric]]''', if ''x R y'' and ''y R x'' implies ''x = y'', for all elements ''x'', ''y'' in ''X''.

definition: An antitone function f between posets P and Q is a function for which, for all elements x, y of P, x ≤ y (in P) implies f(y) ≤ f(x) (in Q). Another name for this property is order-reversing. In analysis, in the presence of total orders, such functions are often called monotonically decreasing, but this is not a very convenient description when dealing with non-total orders. The dual notion is called monotone or order-preserving.
definition_raw: An [[antitone]] function ''f'' between posets ''P'' and ''Q'' is a function for which, for all elements ''x'', ''y'' of ''P'', ''x'' ≤ ''y'' (in ''P'') implies ''f''(''y'') ≤ ''f''(''x'') (in ''Q''). Another name for this property is ''order-reversing''. In [[Mathematical analysis|analysis]], in the presence of [[total order]]s, such functions are often called '''monotonically decreasing''', but this is not a very convenient description when dealing with non-total orders. The dual notion is call

definition: A homogeneous relation R on a set X is asymmetric, if x R y implies not y R x, for all elements x, y in X.
definition_raw: A [[homogeneous relation]] ''R'' on a set ''X'' is asymmetric, if ''x R y'' implies ''not y R x'', for all elements ''x'', ''y'' in ''X''.

definition: An atom in a poset P with least element 0, is an element that is minimal among all elements that are unequal to 0.
definition_raw: An atom in a poset ''P'' with least element 0, is an element that is minimal among all elements that are unequal to 0.

definition: An atomic poset P with least element 0 is one in which, for every non-zero element x of P, there is an atom a of P with a ≤ x.
definition_raw: An atomic poset ''P'' with least element 0 is one in which, for every non-zero element ''x'' of ''P'', there is an atom ''a'' of ''P'' with ''a'' ≤ ''x''.

definition: See continuous poset.
definition_raw: See ''continuous poset''.

definition: A binary relation over two sets $X \text{ and } Y$ is a subset of their Cartesian product $X \times Y.$
definition_raw: A binary relation over two sets <math>X \text{ and } Y</math> is a subset of their [[Cartesian product]] <math>X \times Y.</math>

definition: A Boolean algebra is a distributive lattice with least element 0 and greatest element 1, in which every element x has a complement ¬x, such that x ∧ ¬x = 0 and x ∨ ¬x = 1.
definition_raw: A Boolean algebra is a distributive lattice with least element 0 and greatest element 1, in which every element ''x'' has a complement ¬''x'', such that ''x'' ∧ ¬''x'' = 0 and ''x'' ∨ ¬''x'' = 1.

definition: A bounded poset is one that has a least element and a greatest element.
definition_raw: A [[Bounded poset|bounded]] poset is one that has a least element and a greatest element.

definition: A poset is bounded complete if every of its subsets with some upper bound also has a least such upper bound. The dual notion is not common.
definition_raw: A poset is [[bounded complete]] if every of its subsets with some upper bound also has a least such upper bound. The dual notion is not common.

definition: A chain is a totally ordered set or a totally ordered subset of a poset. See also .
definition_raw: A chain is a totally ordered set or a totally ordered subset of a poset. See also {{em|total order}}.

definition: A partially ordered set in which every chain has a least upper bound.
definition_raw: A [[partially ordered set]] in which every [[Total order#Chains|chain]] has a [[least upper bound]].

definition: A closure operator on the poset P is a function C : P → P that is monotone, idempotent, and satisfies C(x) ≥ x for all x in P.
definition_raw: A closure operator on the poset ''P'' is a function ''C'' : ''P'' → ''P'' that is monotone, [[idempotent]], and satisfies ''C''(''x'') ≥ ''x'' for all ''x'' in ''P''.

definition: An element x of a poset is compact if it is way below itself, i.e. x<<x. One also says that such an x is .
definition_raw: An element ''x'' of a poset is compact if it is ''[[Way-below relation|way below]]'' itself, i.e. ''x''<<''x''. One also says that such an ''x'' is {{em|finite}}.

definition: Two elements x and y of a poset P are comparable if either x ≤ y or y ≤ x.
definition_raw: Two elements ''x'' and ''y'' of a poset ''P'' are comparable if either ''x'' ≤ ''y'' or ''y'' ≤ ''x''.

definition: The comparability graph of a poset (P, ≤) is the graph with vertex set P in which the edges are those pairs of distinct elements of P that are comparable under ≤ (and, in particular, under its reflexive reduction <).
definition_raw: The comparability graph of a poset (''P'', ≤) is the [[Graph (discrete mathematics)|graph]] with vertex set ''P'' in which the edges are those pairs of distinct elements of ''P'' that are comparable under ≤ (and, in particular, under its reflexive reduction <).

definition: A Boolean algebra that is a complete lattice.
definition_raw: A [[Boolean algebra (structure)|Boolean algebra]] that is a complete lattice.

definition: A Heyting algebra that is a complete lattice is called a complete Heyting algebra. This notion coincides with the concepts frame and locale.
definition_raw: A [[Heyting algebra]] that is a complete lattice is called a complete Heyting algebra. This notion coincides with the concepts ''frame'' and ''locale''.

definition: A complete lattice is a poset in which arbitrary (possibly infinite) joins (suprema) and meets (infima) exist.
definition_raw: A complete [[Lattice (order)|lattice]] is a poset in which arbitrary (possibly infinite) joins (suprema) and meets (infima) exist.

definition: A complete partial order, or cpo, is a directed complete partial order (q.v.) with least element.
definition_raw: A complete partial order, or '''cpo''', is a [[directed complete partial order]] (q.v.) with least element.

definition: Synonym for Connected relation.
definition_raw: Synonym for ''[[Connected relation]]''.

definition: The notion of a complete semilattice is defined in different ways. As explained in the article on completeness (order theory), any poset for which either all suprema or all infima exist is already a complete lattice. Hence the notion of a complete semilattice is sometimes used to coincide with the one of a complete lattice. In other cases, complete (meet-) semilattices are defined to be bounded complete cpos, which is arguably the most complete class of posets that are not already complete lattices.
definition_raw: The notion of a ''complete semilattice'' is defined in different ways. As explained in the article on [[completeness (order theory)]], any poset for which either all suprema or all infima exist is already a complete lattice. Hence the notion of a complete semilattice is sometimes used to coincide with the one of a complete lattice. In other cases, complete (meet-) semilattices are defined to be [[bounded complete]] [[complete partial order|cpos]], which is arguably the most complete class of pos

definition: A complete lattice is completely distributive if arbitrary joins distribute over arbitrary meets.
definition_raw: A complete lattice is completely distributive if arbitrary joins distribute over arbitrary meets.

definition: A completion of a poset is an order-embedding of the poset in a complete lattice.
definition_raw: A completion of a poset is an [[order-embedding]] of the poset in a complete lattice.

definition: Synonym of Dedekind–MacNeille completion.
definition_raw: Synonym of [[Dedekind–MacNeille completion]].

definition: A total or complete relation R on a set X has the property that for all elements x, y of X, at least one of x R y or y R x holds.
definition_raw: A total or complete relation ''R'' on a set ''X'' has the property that for all elements ''x'', ''y'' of ''X'', at least one of ''x R y'' or ''y R x'' holds.

definition: A poset is continuous if it has a base, i.e. a subset B of P such that every element x of P is the supremum of a directed set contained in {y in B | y<<x}.
definition_raw: A poset is continuous if it has a '''base''', i.e. a subset ''B'' of ''P'' such that every element ''x'' of ''P'' is the supremum of a directed set contained in {''y'' in ''B'' | ''y''<<''x''}.

definition: See Scott-continuous.
definition_raw: See ''Scott-continuous''.

definition: The converse <° of an order < is that in which x <° y whenever y < x.
definition_raw: The converse <° of an order < is that in which x <° y whenever y < x.

definition: An element y of a poset P is said to cover an element x of P (and is called a cover of x) if x < y and there is no element z of P such that x < z < y.
definition_raw: An element ''y'' of a poset ''P'' is said to cover an element ''x'' of ''P'' (and is called a cover of ''x'') if ''x'' < ''y'' and there is no element ''z'' of ''P'' such that ''x'' < ''z'' < ''y''.

definition: See complete partial order.
definition_raw: See ''complete partial order''.

definition: See directed complete partial order.
definition_raw: See ''[[directed complete partial order]]''.

definition: The Dedekind–MacNeille completion of a partially ordered set is the smallest complete lattice that contains it.
definition_raw: The Dedekind–MacNeille completion of a [[partially ordered set]] is the smallest [[complete lattice]] that contains it.

definition: A dense poset P is one in which, for all elements x and y in P with x < y, there is an element z in P, such that x < z < y. A subset Q of P is dense in P if for any elements x < y in P, there is an element z in Q such that x < z < y.
definition_raw: A '''[[Dense order|dense]]''' poset ''P'' is one in which, for all elements ''x'' and ''y'' in ''P'' with ''x'' < ''y'', there is an element ''z'' in ''P'', such that ''x'' < ''z'' < ''y''.  A subset ''Q'' of ''P'' is '''dense in''' ''P'' if for any elements ''x'' < ''y'' in ''P'', there is an element ''z'' in ''Q'' such that ''x'' < ''z'' < ''y''.

definition: A permutation of the elements of a set, such that no element appears in its original position.
definition_raw: A permutation of the elements of a set, such that no element appears in its original position.

definition: A non-empty subset X of a poset P is called directed, if, for all elements x and y of X, there is an element z of X such that x ≤ z and y ≤ z. The dual notion is called filtered.
definition_raw: A [[non-empty]] subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' is called directed, if, for all elements ''x'' and ''y'' of ''X'', there is an element ''z'' of ''X'' such that ''x'' ≤ ''z'' and ''y'' ≤ ''z''. The dual notion is called ''filtered''.

definition: A poset D is said to be a directed complete poset, or dcpo, if every directed subset of D has a supremum.
definition_raw: A poset ''D'' is said to be a directed complete poset, or '''dcpo''', if every directed subset of ''D'' has a supremum.

definition: A lattice L is called distributive if, for all x, y, and z in L, we find that x ∧ (y ∨ z) = (x ∧ y) ∨ (x ∧ z). This condition is known to be equivalent to its order dual. A meet-semilattice is distributive if for all elements a, b and x, a ∧ b ≤ x implies the existence of elements a' ≥ a and b' ≥ b such that a' ∧ b' = x. See also completely distributive.
definition_raw: A lattice ''L'' is called distributive if, for all ''x'', ''y'', and ''z'' in ''L'', we find that ''x'' ∧ (''y'' ∨ ''z'') = (''x'' ∧ ''y'') ∨ (''x'' ∧ ''z''). This condition is known to be equivalent to its order dual. A meet-[[semilattice]] is distributive if for all elements ''a'', ''b'' and ''x'', ''a'' ∧ ''b'' ≤ ''x'' implies the existence of elements ''a' '' ≥ ''a'' and ''b' '' ≥ ''b'' such that ''a' '' ∧ ''b' '' = ''x''. See also ''completely distributive''.

definition: Domain is a general term for objects like those that are studied in domain theory. If used, it requires further definition.
definition_raw: Domain is a general term for objects like those that are studied in [[domain theory]]. If used, it requires further definition.

definition: See lower set.
definition_raw: See ''lower set''.

definition: For a poset (P, ≤), the dual order Pd = (P, ≥) is defined by setting x ≥ y if and only if y ≤ x. The dual order of P is sometimes denoted by Pop, and is also called opposite or converse order. Any order theoretic notion induces a dual notion, defined by applying the original statement to the order dual of a given set. This exchanges ≤ and ≥, meets and joins, zero and unit.
definition_raw: For a poset (''P'', ≤), the dual order ''P''<sup>''d''</sup> = (''P'', ≥) is defined by setting ''x ≥ y'' [[if and only if]] ''y ≤ x''. The dual order of ''P'' is sometimes denoted by ''P''<sup>op</sup>, and is also called ''opposite'' or ''converse'' order. Any order theoretic notion induces a dual notion, defined by applying the original statement to the order dual of a given set. This exchanges ≤ and ≥, meets and joins, zero and unit.

definition: For partial orders ≤ and ≤′ on a set X, ≤′ is an extension of ≤ provided that for all elements x and y of X, x ≤ y implies that x ≤′ y.
definition_raw: For partial orders ≤ and ≤′ on a set ''X'', ≤′ is an extension of ≤ provided that for all elements ''x'' and ''y'' of ''X'', ''x'' ≤ ''y'' implies that ''x'' ≤′ ''y''.

definition: A subset X of a poset P is called a filter if it is a filtered upper set. The dual notion is called ideal.
definition_raw: A subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' is called a filter if it is a filtered upper set. The dual notion is called ''ideal''.

definition: A non-empty subset X of a poset P is called filtered, if, for all elements x and y of X, there is an element z of X such that z ≤ x and z ≤ y. The dual notion is called directed.
definition_raw: A [[non-empty]] subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' is called filtered, if, for all elements ''x'' and ''y'' of ''X'', there is an element ''z'' of ''X'' such that ''z'' ≤ ''x'' and ''z'' ≤ ''y''. The dual notion is called ''directed''.

definition: See compact.
definition_raw: See ''compact''.

definition: A frame F is a complete lattice, in which, for every x in F and every subset Y of F, the infinite distributive law x ∧ $\bigvee$Y = $\bigvee${x ∧ y | y in Y} holds. Frames are also known as locales and as complete Heyting algebras.
definition_raw: A frame ''F'' is a complete lattice, in which, for every ''x'' in ''F'' and every subset ''Y'' of ''F'', the infinite distributive law ''x'' ∧ <math>\bigvee</math>''Y'' = <math>\bigvee</math>{''x'' ∧ ''y'' | ''y'' in ''Y''} holds. Frames are also known as ''locales'' and as complete [[Heyting algebra]]s.

definition: Given two posets P and Q, a pair of monotone functions F:P → Q and G:Q → P is called a Galois connection, if F(x) ≤ y is equivalent to x ≤ G(y), for all x in P and y in Q. F is called the lower adjoint of G and G is called the upper adjoint of F.
definition_raw: Given two posets ''P'' and ''Q'', a pair of monotone functions ''F'':''P'' → ''Q'' and ''G'':''Q'' → ''P'' is called a Galois connection, if ''F''(''x'') ≤ ''y'' is equivalent to ''x'' ≤ ''G''(''y''), for all ''x'' in ''P'' and ''y'' in ''Q''. ''F'' is called the '''lower adjoint''' of ''G'' and ''G'' is called the '''upper adjoint''' of ''F''.

definition: For a subset X of a poset P, an element a of X is called the greatest element of X, if x ≤ a for every element x in X. The dual notion is called least element.
definition_raw: For a subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'', an element ''a'' of ''X'' is called the greatest element of ''X'', if ''x'' ≤ ''a'' for every element ''x'' in ''X''. The dual notion is called ''least element''.

definition: The ground set of a poset (X, ≤) is the set X on which the partial order ≤ is defined.
definition_raw: The ground set of a poset (''X'', ≤) is the set ''X'' on which the partial order ≤ is defined.

definition: A Heyting algebra H is a bounded lattice in which the function fa: H → H, given by fa(x) = a ∧ x is the lower adjoint of a Galois connection, for every element a of H. The upper adjoint of fa is then denoted by ga, with ga(x) = a ⇒; x. Every Boolean algebra is a Heyting algebra.
definition_raw: A Heyting algebra ''H'' is a bounded lattice in which the function ''f''<sub>''a''</sub>: ''H'' → ''H'', given by ''f''<sub>''a''</sub>(''x'') = ''a'' ∧ ''x'' is the lower adjoint of a [[Galois connection]], for every element ''a'' of ''H''. The upper adjoint of ''f''<sub>''a''</sub> is then denoted by ''g''<sub>''a''</sub>, with ''g''<sub>''a''</sub>(''x'') = ''a'' ⇒; ''x''. Every [[Boolean algebra (structure)|Boolean algebra]] is a Heyting algebra.

definition: A Hasse diagram is a type of mathematical diagram used to represent a finite partially ordered set, in the form of a drawing of its transitive reduction.
definition_raw: A Hasse diagram is a type of mathematical diagram used to represent a finite partially ordered set, in the form of a drawing of its [[transitive reduction]].

definition: A homogeneous relation on a set $X$ is a subset of $X \times X.$ Said differently, it is a binary relation over $X$ and itself.
definition_raw: A [[homogeneous relation]] on a set <math>X</math> is a subset of <math>X \times X.</math> Said differently, it is a [[binary relation]] over <math>X</math> and itself.

definition: An ideal is a subset X of a poset P that is a directed lower set. The dual notion is called filter.
definition_raw: An '''[[Ideal (order theory)|ideal]]''' is a subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' that is a directed lower set. The dual notion is called ''filter''.

definition: The incidence algebra of a poset is the associative algebra of all scalar-valued functions on intervals, with addition and scalar multiplication defined pointwise, and multiplication defined as a certain convolution; see incidence algebra for the details.
definition_raw: The '''[[incidence algebra]]''' of a poset is the [[associative algebra]] of all scalar-valued functions on intervals, with addition and scalar multiplication defined pointwise, and multiplication defined as a certain convolution; see [[incidence algebra]] for the details.

definition: For a poset P and a subset X of P, the greatest element in the set of lower bounds of X (if it exists, which it may not) is called the infimum, meet, or greatest lower bound of X. It is denoted by inf X or $\bigwedge$X. The infimum of two elements may be written as inf{x,y} or x ∧ y. If the set X is finite, one speaks of a finite infimum. The dual notion is called supremum.
definition_raw: For a poset ''P'' and a subset ''X'' of ''P'', the greatest element in the set of lower bounds of ''X'' (if it exists, which it may not) is called the '''infimum''', '''meet''', or '''greatest lower bound''' of ''X''. It is denoted by inf ''X'' or <math>\bigwedge</math>''X''. The infimum of two elements may be written as inf{''x'',''y''} or ''x'' ∧ ''y''. If the set ''X'' is finite, one speaks of a '''finite infimum'''.  The dual notion is called ''supremum''.

definition: For two elements a, b of a partially ordered set P, the interval [a,b] is the subset {x in P | a ≤ x ≤ b} of P. If a ≤ b does not hold the interval will be empty.
definition_raw: For two elements ''a'', ''b'' of a partially ordered set ''P'', the ''interval'' [''a'',''b''] is the subset {''x'' in ''P'' | ''a'' ≤ ''x'' ≤ ''b''} of ''P''. If ''a'' ≤ ''b'' does not hold the interval will be empty.

definition: See converse.
definition_raw: See ''converse''.

definition: A relation R on a set X is irreflexive, if there is no element x in X such that x R x.
definition_raw: A [[Relation (mathematics)|relation]] ''R'' on a set ''X'' is irreflexive, if there is no element ''x'' in ''X'' such that ''x R x''.

definition: See monotone.
definition_raw: See ''monotone''.

definition: See supremum.
definition_raw: See ''supremum''.

definition: A lattice is a poset in which all non-empty finite joins (suprema) and meets (infima) exist.
definition_raw: A lattice is a poset in which all non-empty finite joins (suprema) and meets (infima) exist.

definition: For a subset X of a poset P, an element a of X is called the least element of X, if a ≤ x for every element x in X. The dual notion is called greatest element.
definition_raw: For a subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'', an element ''a'' of ''X'' is called the least element of ''X'', if ''a'' ≤ ''x'' for every element ''x'' in ''X''. The dual notion is called ''greatest element''.

definition: See total order.
definition_raw: See ''total order''.

definition: A linear extension of a partial order is an extension that is a linear order, or total order.
definition_raw: A linear extension of a partial order is an extension that is a linear order, or total order.

definition: A locale is a complete Heyting algebra. Locales are also called frames and appear in Stone duality and pointless topology.
definition_raw: A locale is a ''complete Heyting algebra''. Locales are also called ''frames'' and appear in [[Stone duality]] and [[pointless topology]].

definition: A partially ordered set P is locally finite if every interval [a, b] = {x in P | a ≤ x ≤ b} is a finite set.
definition_raw: A partially ordered set ''P'' is ''locally finite'' if every interval [''a'', ''b''] = {''x'' in ''P'' | ''a'' ≤ ''x'' ≤ ''b''} is a finite set.

definition: A lower bound of a subset X of a poset P is an element b of P, such that b ≤ x, for all x in X. The dual notion is called upper bound.
definition_raw: A lower bound of a subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' is an element ''b'' of ''P'', such that ''b'' ≤ ''x'', for all ''x'' in ''X''. The dual notion is called ''upper bound''.

definition: A subset X of a poset P is called a lower set if, for all elements x in X and p in P, p ≤ x implies that p is contained in X. The dual notion is called upper set.
definition_raw: A subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' is called a lower set if, for all elements ''x'' in ''X'' and ''p'' in ''P'', ''p'' ≤ ''x'' implies that ''p'' is contained in ''X''. The dual notion is called ''upper set''.

definition: A chain in a poset to which no element can be added without losing the property of being totally ordered. This is stronger than being a saturated chain, as it also excludes the existence of elements either less than all elements of the chain or greater than all its elements. A finite saturated chain is maximal if and only if it contains both a minimal and a maximal element of the poset.
definition_raw: A [[Total order#Chains|chain]] in a poset to which no element can be added without losing the property of being totally ordered. This is stronger than being a saturated chain, as it also excludes the existence of elements either less than all elements of the chain or greater than all its elements. A finite saturated chain is maximal if and only if it contains both a minimal and a maximal element of the poset.

definition: A maximal element of a subset X of a poset P is an element m of X, such that m ≤ x implies m = x, for all x in X. The dual notion is called minimal element.
definition_raw: A maximal element of a subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' is an element ''m'' of ''X'', such that ''m'' ≤ ''x'' implies ''m'' = ''x'', for all ''x'' in ''X''. The dual notion is called ''minimal element''.

definition: Synonym of greatest element. For a subset X of a poset P, an element a of X is called the maximum element of X if x ≤ a for every element x in X. A maximum element is necessarily maximal, but the converse need not hold.
definition_raw: Synonym of greatest element. For a subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'', an element ''a'' of ''X'' is called the maximum element of ''X'' if ''x'' ≤ ''a'' for every element ''x'' in ''X''. A maxim''um'' element is necessarily maxim''al'', but the converse need not hold.

definition: See infimum.
definition_raw: See ''infimum''.

definition: A minimal element of a subset X of a poset P is an element m of X, such that x ≤ m implies m = x, for all x in X. The dual notion is called maximal element.
definition_raw: A minimal element of a subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' is an element ''m'' of ''X'', such that ''x'' ≤ ''m'' implies ''m'' = ''x'', for all ''x'' in ''X''. The dual notion is called ''maximal element''.

definition: Synonym of least element. For a subset X of a poset P, an element a of X is called the minimum element of X if x ≥ a for every element x in X. A minimum element is necessarily minimal, but the converse need not hold.
definition_raw: Synonym of least element. For a subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'', an element ''a'' of ''X'' is called the minimum element of ''X'' if ''x'' ≥ ''a'' for every element ''x'' in ''X''. A minim''um'' element is necessarily minim''al'', but the converse need not hold.

definition: A function f between posets P and Q is monotone if, for all elements x, y of P, x ≤ y (in P) implies f(x) ≤ f(y) (in Q). Other names for this property are isotone and order-preserving. In analysis, in the presence of total orders, such functions are often called monotonically increasing, but this is not a very convenient description when dealing with non-total orders. The dual notion is called antitone or order reversing.
definition_raw: A function ''f'' between posets ''P'' and ''Q'' is monotone if, for all elements ''x'', ''y'' of ''P'', ''x'' ≤ ''y'' (in ''P'') implies ''f''(''x'') ≤ ''f''(''y'') (in ''Q''). Other names for this property are ''isotone'' and ''order-preserving''. In [[Mathematical analysis|analysis]], in the presence of [[total order]]s, such functions are often called '''monotonically increasing''', but this is not a very convenient description when dealing with non-total orders. The dual notion is called ''a

definition: The order dual of a partially ordered set is the same set with the partial order relation replaced by its converse.
definition_raw: The order dual of a partially ordered set is the same set with the partial order relation replaced by its converse.

definition: A function f between posets P and Q is an order-embedding if, for all elements x, y of P, x ≤ y (in P) is equivalent to f(x) ≤ f(y) (in Q).
definition_raw: A function ''f'' between posets ''P'' and ''Q'' is an order-embedding if, for all elements ''x'', ''y'' of ''P'', ''x'' ≤ ''y'' (in ''P'') is equivalent to ''f''(''x'') ≤ ''f''(''y'') (in ''Q'').

definition: A mapping f: P → Q between two posets P and Q is called an order isomorphism, if it is bijective and both f and f−1 are monotone functions. Equivalently, an order isomorphism is a surjective order embedding.
definition_raw: A mapping ''f'': ''P'' → ''Q'' between two posets ''P'' and ''Q'' is called an order isomorphism, if it is [[bijective]] and both ''f'' and ''f''<sup>−1</sup> are [[monotone function]]s. Equivalently, an order isomorphism is a surjective ''order embedding''.

definition: See monotone.
definition_raw: See ''monotone''.

definition: See antitone.
definition_raw: See ''antitone''.

definition: A partial order is a binary relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive. In a slight abuse of terminology, the term is sometimes also used to refer not to such a relation, but to its corresponding partially ordered set.
definition_raw: A partial order is a [[binary relation]] that is [[Reflexive relation|reflexive]], [[Antisymmetric relation|antisymmetric]], and [[Transitive relation|transitive]]. In a slight abuse of terminology, the term is sometimes also used to refer not to such a relation, but to its corresponding partially ordered set.

definition: A partially ordered set $(P, \leq),$ or for short, is a set $P$ together with a partial order $\,\leq\,$ on $P.$
definition_raw: A partially ordered set <math>(P, \leq),</math> or {{em|poset}} for short, is a set <math>P</math> together with a partial order <math>\,\leq\,</math> on <math>P.</math>

definition: A partially ordered set.
definition_raw: A partially ordered set.

definition: A preorder is a binary relation that is reflexive and transitive. Such orders may also be called or . The term is also used to denote an acyclic binary relation (also called an ).
definition_raw: A preorder is a [[binary relation]] that is [[Reflexive relation|reflexive]] and [[Transitive relation|transitive]]. Such orders may also be called {{em|quasiorders}} or {{em|non-strict preorder}}. The term {{em|preorder}} is also used to denote an [[#A|acyclic]] [[binary relation]] (also called an {{em|acyclic digraph}}).

definition: A preordered set $(P, \leq)$ is a set $P$ together with a preorder $\,\leq\,$ on $P.$
definition_raw: A preordered set <math>(P, \leq)</math> is a set <math>P</math> together with a preorder <math>\,\leq\,</math> on <math>P.</math>

definition: A function f between posets P and Q is said to preserve suprema (joins), if, for all subsets X of P that have a supremum sup X in P, we find that sup{f(x): x in X} exists and is equal to f(sup X). Such a function is also called join-preserving. Analogously, one says that f preserves finite, non-empty, directed, or arbitrary joins (or meets). The converse property is called join-reflecting.
definition_raw: A function ''f'' between posets ''P'' and ''Q'' is said to preserve suprema (joins), if, for all subsets ''X'' of ''P'' that have a supremum sup ''X'' in ''P'', we find that sup{''f''(''x''): ''x'' in ''X''} exists and is equal to ''f''(sup ''X''). Such a function is also called '''join-preserving'''. Analogously, one says that ''f'' preserves finite, non-empty, directed, or arbitrary joins (or meets). The converse property is called ''join-reflecting''.

definition: An I in a lattice L is said to be prime, if, for all elements x and y in L, x ∧ y in I implies x in I or y in I. The dual notion is called a prime filter. Equivalently, a set is a prime filter if and only if its complement is a prime ideal.
definition_raw: An {{em|ideal}} ''I'' in a lattice ''L'' is said to be prime, if, for all elements ''x'' and ''y'' in ''L'', ''x'' ∧ ''y'' in ''I'' implies ''x'' in ''I'' or ''y'' in ''I''. The dual notion is called a ''prime filter''. Equivalently, a set is a prime filter [[if and only if]] its complement is a prime ideal.

definition: A filter is called principal filter if it has a least element. Dually, a principal ideal is an ideal with a greatest element. The least or greatest elements may also be called principal elements in these situations.
definition_raw: A filter is called ''principal filter'' if it has a least element. Dually, a ''principal ideal'' is an ideal with a greatest element. The least or greatest elements may also be called ''principal elements'' in these situations.

definition: A self-map on a partially ordered set that is monotone and idempotent under function composition. Projections play an important role in domain theory.
definition_raw: A self-map on a [[partially ordered set]] that is [[Monotonic function|monotone]] and [[idempotent]] under [[function composition]]. Projections play an important role in [[domain theory]].

definition: In a Heyting algebra, the element x ⇒; 0 is called the pseudo-complement of x. It is also given by sup{y : y ∧ x = 0}, i.e. as the least upper bound of all elements y with y ∧ x = 0.
definition_raw: In a [[Heyting algebra]], the element ''x'' ⇒; ''0'' is called the pseudo-complement of ''x''. It is also given by sup{''y'' :  ''y'' ∧ ''x'' = 0}, i.e. as the least upper bound of all elements ''y'' with ''y'' ∧ ''x'' = 0.

definition: See preorder.
definition_raw: See ''preorder''.

definition: A relation is quasitransitive if the relation on distinct elements is transitive. Transitive implies quasitransitive and quasitransitive implies acyclic.
definition_raw: A relation is quasitransitive if the relation on distinct elements is transitive.  Transitive implies quasitransitive and quasitransitive implies acyclic.<ref name=BosSuz>{{cite book | title=Consistency, choice and rationality | first1=Walter | last1=Bossert | first2=Kōtarō | last2=Suzumura | publisher=Harvard University Press | year=2010 | isbn=978-0674052994 }}</ref>

definition: A function f between posets P and Q is said to reflect suprema (joins), if, for all subsets X of P for which the supremum sup{f(x): x in X} exists and is of the form f(s) for some s in P, then we find that sup X exists and that sup X = s . Analogously, one says that f reflects finite, non-empty, directed, or arbitrary joins (or meets). The converse property is called join-preserving.
definition_raw: A function ''f'' between posets ''P'' and ''Q'' is said to reflect suprema (joins), if, for all subsets ''X'' of ''P'' for which the supremum sup{''f''(''x''): ''x'' in ''X''} exists and is of the form ''f''(''s'') for some ''s'' in ''P'', then we find that sup ''X'' exists and that sup ''X'' = ''s'' . Analogously, one says that ''f'' reflects finite, non-empty, directed, or arbitrary joins (or meets). The converse property is called ''join-preserving''.

definition: A binary relation R on a set X is reflexive, if x R x holds for every element x in X.
definition_raw: A [[binary relation]] ''R'' on a set ''X'' is reflexive, if ''x R x'' holds for every element ''x'' in ''X''.

definition: A dual map attached to a residuated mapping.
definition_raw: A dual map attached to a [[residuated mapping]].

definition: A monotone map for which the preimage of a principal down-set is again principal. Equivalently, one component of a Galois connection.
definition_raw: A monotone map for which the preimage of a principal down-set is again principal.  Equivalently, one component of a Galois connection.

definition: A chain in a poset such that no element can be added between two of its elements without losing the property of being totally ordered. If the chain is finite, this means that in every pair of successive elements the larger one covers the smaller one. See also maximal chain.
definition_raw: A [[Total order#Chains|chain]] in a poset such that no element can be added ''between two of its elements'' without losing the property of being totally ordered. If the chain is finite, this means that in every pair of successive elements the larger one covers the smaller one. See also maximal chain.

definition: A total order is scattered if it has no densely ordered subset.
definition_raw: A total order is scattered if it has no densely ordered subset.

definition: A monotone function f : P → Q between posets P and Q is Scott-continuous if, for every directed set D that has a supremum sup D in P, the set {fx | x in D} has the supremum f(sup D) in Q. Stated differently, a Scott-continuous function is one that preserves all directed suprema. This is in fact equivalent to being continuous with respect to the Scott topology on the respective posets.
definition_raw: A monotone function ''f'' : ''P'' → ''Q'' between posets ''P'' and ''Q'' is Scott-continuous if, for every directed set ''D'' that has a supremum sup ''D'' in ''P'', the set {''fx'' | ''x'' in ''D''} has the supremum ''f''(sup ''D'') in ''Q''. Stated differently, a Scott-continuous function is one that preserves all directed suprema. This is in fact equivalent to being [[Continuity (topology)|continuous]] with respect to the [[Scott topology]] on the respective posets.

definition: A Scott domain is a partially ordered set which is a bounded complete algebraic cpo.
definition_raw: A Scott domain is a partially ordered set which is a [[bounded complete]] [[Algebraic poset|algebraic]] [[Complete partial order|cpo]].

definition: See Scott topology.
definition_raw: See ''Scott topology''.

definition: For a poset P, a subset O is Scott-open if it is an upper set and all directed sets D that have a supremum in O have non-empty intersection with O. The set of all Scott-open sets forms a topology, the Scott topology.
definition_raw: For a poset ''P'', a subset ''O'' is '''Scott-open''' if it is an [[upper set]] and all directed sets ''D'' that have a supremum in ''O'' have non-empty intersection with ''O''. The set of all Scott-open sets forms a [[topology]], the '''Scott topology'''.

definition: A semilattice is a poset in which either all finite non-empty joins (suprema) or all finite non-empty meets (infima) exist. Accordingly, one speaks of a join-semilattice or meet-semilattice.
definition_raw: A semilattice is a poset in which either all finite non-empty joins (suprema) or all finite non-empty meets (infima) exist. Accordingly, one speaks of a '''join-semilattice''' or '''meet-semilattice'''.

definition: See least element.
definition_raw: See ''least element''.

definition: See .
definition_raw: See {{em|strict partial order}}.

definition: A strict partial order is a homogeneous binary relation that is transitive, irreflexive, and antisymmetric.
definition_raw: A strict partial order is a [[Homogeneous relation|homogeneous binary relation]] that is [[Transitive relation|transitive]], [[Irreflexive relation|irreflexive]], and [[Antisymmetric relation|antisymmetric]].

definition: See .
definition_raw: See {{em|strict partial order}}.

definition: For a poset P and a subset X of P, the least element in the set of upper bounds of X (if it exists, which it may not) is called the supremum, join, or least upper bound of X. It is denoted by sup X or $\bigvee$X. The supremum of two elements may be written as sup{x,y} or x ∨ y. If the set X is finite, one speaks of a finite supremum. The dual notion is called infimum.
definition_raw: For a poset ''P'' and a subset ''X'' of ''P'', the [[least element]] in the set of [[upper bound]]s of ''X'' (if it exists, which it may not) is called the '''supremum''', '''join''', or '''least upper bound''' of ''X''. It is denoted by sup ''X'' or <math>\bigvee</math>''X''. The supremum of two elements may be written as sup{''x'',''y''} or ''x'' ∨ ''y''. If the set ''X'' is finite, one speaks of a '''finite supremum'''. The dual notion is called ''infimum''.

definition: A binary relation R is Suzumura consistent if x R∗ y implies that x R y or not y R x.
definition_raw: A binary relation R is Suzumura consistent if ''x'' R<sup>∗</sup> ''y'' implies that ''x'' R ''y'' or not ''y'' R ''x''.<ref name=BosSuz/>

definition: A homogeneous relation R on a set X is symmetric, if x R y implies y R x, for all elements x, y in X.
definition_raw: A [[homogeneous relation]] ''R'' on a set ''X'' is symmetric, if ''x R y'' implies ''y R x'', for all elements ''x'', ''y'' in ''X''.

definition: See unit.
definition_raw: See ''unit''.

definition: A total order T is a partial order in which, for each x and y in T, we have x ≤ y or y ≤ x. Total orders are also called linear orders or chains.
definition_raw: A total order ''T'' is a partial order in which, for each ''x'' and ''y'' in ''T'', we have ''x'' ≤ ''y'' or ''y'' ≤ ''x''. Total orders are also called ''linear orders'' or ''chains''.

definition: Synonym for Connected relation.
definition_raw: Synonym for ''[[Connected relation]]''.

definition: A relation R on a set X is transitive, if x R y and y R z imply x R z, for all elements x, y, z in X.
definition_raw: A [[Homogeneous relation|relation]] ''R'' on a set ''X'' is transitive, if ''x R y'' and ''y R z'' imply ''x R z'', for all elements ''x'', ''y'', ''z'' in ''X''.

definition: The transitive closure R∗ of a relation R consists of all pairs x,y for which there exists a finite chain x R a, a R b, ..., z R y.
definition_raw: The transitive closure R<sup>∗</sup> of a relation R consists of all pairs ''x'',''y'' for which there exists a finite chain ''x'' R ''a'', ''a'' R ''b'', ..., ''z'' R ''y''.<ref name=BosSuz/>

definition: The greatest element of a poset P can be called unit or just 1 (if it exists). Another common term for this element is top. It is the infimum of the empty set and the supremum of P. The dual notion is called zero.
definition_raw: The [[greatest element]] of a poset ''P'' can be called ''unit'' or just ''1'' (if it exists). Another common term for this element is '''top'''. It is the infimum of the empty set and the supremum of ''P''. The dual notion is called ''zero''.

definition: See upper set.
definition_raw: See ''upper set''.

definition: An upper bound of a subset X of a poset P is an element b of P, such that x ≤ b, for all x in X. The dual notion is called lower bound.
definition_raw: An upper bound of a subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' is an element ''b'' of ''P'', such that ''x'' ≤ ''b'', for all ''x'' in ''X''. The dual notion is called ''lower bound''.

definition: A subset X of a poset P is called an upper set if, for all elements x in X and p in P, x ≤ p implies that p is contained in X. The dual notion is called lower set.
definition_raw: A subset ''X'' of a poset ''P'' is called an upper set if, for all elements ''x'' in ''X'' and ''p'' in ''P'', ''x'' ≤ ''p'' implies that ''p'' is contained in ''X''. The dual notion is called ''lower set''.

definition:
Given a lattice $X$, a valuation $
u : X 	o [0,1]$ is strict (that is, $
u(\varnothing) = 0$), monotone, modular (that is, $
u(U) + 
u(V) = 
u(U \cup V) + 
u(U \cap V)$) and positive. Continuous valuations are a generalization of measures.
definition_raw:
Given a lattice <math>X</math>, a valuation <math>
u : X 	o [0,1]</math> is strict (that is, <math>
u(\varnothing) = 0</math>), monotone, modular (that is, <math>
u(U) + 
u(V) = 
u(U \cup V) + 
u(U \cap V)</math>) and positive.  Continuous valuations are a generalization of measures.

definition: In a poset P, some element x is way below y, written x<<y, if for all directed subsets D of P which have a supremum, y ≤ sup D implies x ≤ d for some d in D. One also says that x approximates y. See also domain theory.
definition_raw: In a poset ''P'', some element ''x'' is ''way below'' ''y'', written ''x''<<''y'', if for all directed subsets ''D'' of ''P'' which have a supremum, ''y'' ≤ ''sup D'' implies ''x'' ≤ ''d'' for some ''d'' in ''D''. One also says that ''x'' '''approximates''' ''y''. See also [[domain theory]].

definition: A partial order ≤ on a set X is a weak order provided that the poset (X, ≤) is isomorphic to a countable collection of sets ordered by comparison of cardinality.
definition_raw: A partial order ≤ on a set ''X'' is a weak order provided that the poset (X, ≤) is [[isomorphic]] to a countable collection of sets ordered by comparison of [[cardinality]].

definition: The least element of a poset P can be called zero or just 0 (if it exists). Another common term for this element is bottom. Zero is the supremum of the empty set and the infimum of P. The dual notion is called unit.
definition_raw: The [[least element]] of a poset ''P'' can be called ''zero'' or just ''0'' (if it exists). Another common term for this element is '''bottom'''. Zero is the supremum of the empty set and the infimum of ''P''. The dual notion is called ''unit''.

definition: Another name for elementary event.
definition_raw: Another name for {{gli|elementary event}}.

definition: Any feature of a sample that is not representative of the larger population.
definition_raw: Any feature of a {{gli|sample}} that is not representative of the larger {{gli|population}}.

definition: Data that can take only two values, usually represented by the binary digits 0 and 1.
definition_raw: Data that can take only two values, usually represented by the [[binary digit]]s 0 and 1.

definition: A type of quantitative statistical analysis in which exactly two variables are analyzed, for the purpose of determining the empirical relationship between them. Contrast multivariate analysis.
definition_raw: A type of quantitative statistical analysis in which exactly two {{gli|variable|variables}} are analyzed, for the purpose of determining the empirical relationship between them. Contrast ''{{gli|multivariate analysis}}''.

definition: In experimental design, the arranging of experimental units in groups ("blocks") that are similar to one another. Blocking is often used to manage the problem of pseudoreplication.
definition_raw: In experimental design, the arranging of experimental units in groups ("blocks") that are similar to one another. Blocking is often used to manage the problem of {{gli|pseudoreplication}}.

definition: A statistical study in which the objective is to measure the effect of some variable on the outcome of a different variable. For example, a causal study might ask the question: "How will my headache feel if I take aspirin, versus if I do not take aspirin?" Causal studies may be either experimental or observational.
definition_raw: A statistical study in which the objective is to measure the effect of some {{gli|variable}} on the outcome of a different variable. For example, a causal study might ask the question: "How will my headache feel if I take aspirin, versus if I do not take aspirin?" Causal studies may be either experimental or observational.<ref name=Reiter>{{cite journal|last1=Reiter|first1=Jerome|title=Using Statistics to Determine Causal Relationships|journal=American Mathematical Monthly|volume=107|issue=1|pag

definition: The study of statistical methods that are enabled by using computational methods, at the interface of statistics and computer science.
definition_raw: The study of statistical methods that are enabled by using computational methods, at the interface of statistics and [[computer science]].

definition: In a statistical study, any variables whose values are unaffected by experimental treatments, such as a unit’s age, gender, and cholesterol level before starting an experimental diet.
definition_raw: In a statistical study, any variables whose values are unaffected by experimental treatments, such as a unit’s age, gender, and cholesterol level before starting an experimental diet.<ref name=Reiter/>

definition: Given two jointly distributed random variables X and Y, the conditional probability distribution of Y given X (written "Y | X") is the probability distribution of Y when X is known to be a particular value.
definition_raw: Given two jointly distributed random variables ''X'' and ''Y'', the conditional probability distribution of ''Y'' given ''X'' (written "''Y'' <nowiki>|</nowiki> ''X''") is the probability distribution of ''Y'' when ''X'' is known to be a particular value.

definition: The probability of some event A, assuming the occurrence of event B. In mathematical notation, conditional probability is written P(A|B), and is read "the probability of A, given B".
definition_raw: The {{gli|probability}} of some event A, assuming the occurrence of event B. In mathematical notation, conditional probability is written P(''A''<nowiki>|</nowiki>''B''), and is read "the probability of ''A'', given ''B''".

definition: In inferential statistics, a range of plausible values for some unknown parameter, such as a population mean, defined as an interval with a lower bound and an upper bound. The precise values of these bounds are calculated from a pre-determined confidence level, chosen by the researcher. The confidence level represents the frequency of intervals that, over the long run, capture the true value of the unknown parameter; i.e. 95% of confidence intervals computed at the 95% confidence level contain the true value, and likewise for other confidence levels. For example, based on a study of sleep habits among a random sample of 100 people, a researcher may estimate at the 95% confidence level that the overall population sleeps somewhere between 5 and 9 hours per night. There is a 95% chance that the true population mean falls within this interval, because 95% of random samples taken from this same population will yield 95% confidence intervals that contain the true mean.
definition_raw: In inferential statistics, a range of plausible values for some unknown {{gli|parameter}}, such as a {{gli|population mean}}, defined as an interval with a lower bound and an upper bound.<ref name="Kalinowski">Pav Kalinowski. Understanding Confidence Intervals (CIs) and Effect Size Estimation. Association for Psychological Science Observer April 10, 2010. http://www.psychologicalscience.org/index.php/publications/observer/2010/april-10/understanding-confidence-intervals-cis-and-effect-size-estim

definition: A number indicating the probability that the confidence interval (range) captures the true population mean. For example, a confidence interval with a 95% confidence level has a 95% chance of capturing the population mean. Technically, this means that, if the experiment were repeated many times, 95% of the CIs computed at this level would contain the true population mean.
definition_raw: A number indicating the probability that the {{gli|confidence interval}} (range) captures the true {{gli|population mean}}. For example, a confidence interval with a 95% confidence level has a 95% chance of capturing the population mean. Technically, this means that, if the experiment were repeated many times, 95% of the CIs computed at this level would contain the true population mean.<ref name="Kalinowski"/>

definition: A variable that influences both the dependent variable and the independent variable, causing a spurious association. The existence of hidden confounding variables is an important quantitative explanation why correlation does not imply causation: if changes in two variables appear to be correlated, it is risky to presume that one change causes the other because it is possible that one or more unidentified confounders has in fact caused the changes in both variables. A classic example is the correlation between increased consumption of ice cream and increased crime in the summer. It is irrational to assume that eating more ice cream makes people commit more crime, or vice versa; it is more likely that one or more additional variables, e.g. warmer weather, increase both ice cream consumption and crime simultaneously. In this example, warmer weather is the confounder.
definition_raw: A {{gli|variable}} that influences both the {{gli|dependent variable}} and the {{gli|independent variable}}, causing a spurious association. The existence of hidden confounding variables is an important quantitative explanation why {{gli|correlation}} does not imply causation: if changes in two variables appear to be correlated, it is risky to presume that one change causes the other because it is possible that one or more unidentified confounders has in fact caused the changes in both variables

definition: A numeric measure of the strength of a linear relationship between two random variables (one can use it to quantify, for example, how shoe size and height are correlated in the population). An example is the Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient, which is found by dividing the covariance of the two variables by the product of their standard deviations. Independent variables, by definition, have a correlation of 0. A population correlation is often represented by the symbol $\rho$, and a sample correlation by $r$.
definition_raw: A numeric measure of the strength of a linear relationship between two random variables (one can use it to quantify, for example, how shoe size and height are correlated in the population). An example is the [[Pearson product-moment correlation coefficient]], which is found by dividing the {{gli|covariance}} of the two variables by the product of their {{gli|standard deviation|standard deviations}}. {{gli|independent variable|Independent variables}}, by definition, have a correlation of 0. A pop

definition: Data arising from counting, and which can therefore take only non-negative integer values.
definition_raw: Data arising from [[counting]], and which can therefore take only non-negative [[integer]] values.

definition: Given two random variables X and Y, with expected values $E(X)=\mu$ and $E(Y)=\nu$, the expected value of random variable $(X - \mu) (Y - \nu)$, written in statistical notation as $\operatorname{cov}(X, Y)$. The covariance is used for measuring correlation; it can be interpreted as the degree to which the two variables change simultaneously with each other or "co-vary".
definition_raw: Given two random variables ''X'' and ''Y'', with expected values <math>E(X)=\mu</math> and <math>E(Y)=\nu</math>, the expected value of random variable <math>(X - \mu) (Y - \nu)</math>, written in statistical notation as <math>\operatorname{cov}(X, Y)</math>. The covariance is used for measuring {{gli|correlation}}; it can be interpreted as the degree to which the two variables change simultaneously with each other or "co-vary".

definition: A sample and the associated data points.
definition_raw: A sample and the associated {{gli|data point|data points}}.

definition: A typed measurement — it can be a Boolean value, a real number, a vector (in which case it is also called a data vector), etc.
definition_raw: A typed measurement — it can be a [[Boolean data type|Boolean]] value, a real number, a vector (in which case it is also called a ''data vector''), etc.

definition: An event which contains only a single outcome in the sample space; in a set of possibilities, a possibility that can occur in precisely one way. For example, when pulling a card from a standard deck of playing cards, 'pulling the jack of spades' is an elementary event (because there is only one jack of spades in the entire deck), while 'pulling a king or an ace' is not (because there are a combined four kings and four aces in the deck).
definition_raw: An event which contains only a single outcome in the {{gli|sample space}}; in a set of possibilities, a possibility that can occur in precisely one way. For example, when pulling a card from a standard deck of [[playing cards]], 'pulling the jack of spades' is an elementary event (because there is only one jack of spades in the entire deck), while 'pulling a king or an ace' is not (because there are a combined four kings and four aces in the deck).

definition: The branch of statistics concerned with estimating the values of parameters based on measured empirical data with a random component. The parameters describe an underlying physical setting in such a way that their values affect the distribution of the measured data; an estimator attempts to use the measurements to approximate the unknown parameters.
definition_raw: The branch of statistics concerned with estimating the values of {{gli|parameter|parameters}} based on measured empirical data with a {{gli|random}} component. The parameters describe an underlying physical setting in such a way that their values affect the distribution of the measured data; an {{gli|estimator}} attempts to use the measurements to approximate the unknown parameters.

definition: A function of the known data that is used to estimate an unknown parameter; an estimate is the result of the actual application of the function to a particular set of data. For example, the mean can be used as an estimator.
definition_raw: A function of the known data that is used to estimate an unknown {{gli|parameter}}; an estimate is the result of the actual application of the function to a particular set of data. For example, the {{gli|mean}} can be used as an estimator.

definition: The sum of the probabilities of each possible outcome of an experiment multiplied by their corresponding payoff or "value". Thus, it represents the average amount one "expects" to win per bet if bets with identical odds are repeated many times. For example, the expected value of rolling a fair six-sided die is 3.5. The concept is, intuitively, a generalization of the weighted average of all possible outcomes of a particular procedure or experiment, and can be viewed as the arithmetic mean of a large number of independent realizations of the experiment. The expected value of random variable X is typically written as E(X) for the expectation operator, and $\mu$ (mu) for the parameter.
definition_raw: The sum of the probabilities of each possible outcome of an {{gli|experiment}} multiplied by their corresponding {{gli|payoff}} or "value". Thus, it represents the average amount one "expects" to win per bet if bets with identical odds are repeated many times. For example, the expected value of rolling a fair six-sided die is 3.5. The concept is, intuitively, a generalization of the weighted average of all possible outcomes of a particular procedure or experiment, and can be viewed as the [[arit

definition: Any procedure which can be infinitely repeated and which has a well-defined set of outcomes.
definition_raw: Any procedure which can be infinitely repeated and which has a well-defined set of outcomes.

definition: A subset of the sample space of a procedure or experiment (i.e. a possible outcome) to which a probability can be assigned. For example, on rolling a die, "getting a three" is an event (with a probability of if the die is fair), as is "getting a five or a six" (with a probability of ).
definition_raw: A subset of the {{gli|sample space}} of a procedure or {{gli|experiment}} (i.e. a possible outcome) to which a probability can be assigned. For example, on rolling a die, "getting a three" is an event (with a probability of {{frac|1|6}} if the die is fair), as is "getting a five or a six" (with a probability of {{frac|1|3}}).

definition: An approximate graphical representation of the distribution of numerical data. A histogram displays this distribution by dividing the entire range of values into a series of consecutive, non-overlapping intervals and then counting how many instances of the dataset fall into each interval.
definition_raw: An approximate graphical representation of the distribution of numerical data. A histogram displays this distribution by dividing the entire {{gli|range}} of values into a series of consecutive, non-overlapping intervals and then counting how many instances of the dataset fall into each interval.

A histogram displays this distribution by dividing the entire range of values into a series of consecutive, non-overlapping intervals and then counting how many instances of the dataset fall into each interval.
