A histogram displays this distribution by dividing the entire {{gli|range}} of values into a series of consecutive, non-overlapping intervals and then counting how many instances of the dataset fall into each interval.

definition: A measure of the statistical dispersion or spread of a dataset, defined as the difference between the 25th and 75th percentiles of the data. To calculate the IQR, the dataset is divided into four rank-ordered even parts or quartiles, the boundaries between which, at the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles, are denoted $Q$1, $Q$2, and $Q$3, respectively; the IQR = $Q$3$-$$Q$1.
definition_raw: A measure of the statistical dispersion or spread of a dataset, defined as the difference between the 25th and 75th {{gli|percentile|percentiles}} of the data. To calculate the IQR, the dataset is divided into four rank-ordered even parts or {{gli|quartile|quartiles}}, the boundaries between which, at the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles, are denoted <math>Q</math><sub>1</sub>, <math>Q</math><sub>2</sub>, and <math>Q</math><sub>3</sub>, respectively; the IQR = <math>Q</math><sub>3</sub><math>-</

definition: Given two random variables X and Y, the joint distribution of X and Y is the probability distribution of X and Y together.
definition_raw: Given two random variables ''X'' and ''Y'', the joint distribution of ''X'' and ''Y'' is the {{gli|probability distribution}} of X and Y together.

definition: The probability of two events occurring together. The joint probability of A and B is written $P(A \cap B)$ or $P(A, \ B)$.
definition_raw: The {{gli|probability}} of two {{gli|event|events}} occurring together. The joint probability of ''A'' and ''B'' is written <math>P(A \cap B)</math> or <math>P(A, \ B)</math>.

definition: A measure of the "tailedness" of the probability distribution of a real-valued random variable. There are different ways of quantifying, estimating, and interpreting kurtosis, but a common interpretation is that kurtosis represents the degree to which the shape of the distribution is influenced by infrequent extreme observations (outliers); in this case, higher kurtosis means more of the variance is due to infrequent extreme deviations, as opposed to frequent modestly sized deviations.
definition_raw: A measure of the "tailedness" of the {{gli|probability distribution}} of a real-valued random variable. There are different ways of quantifying, estimating, and interpreting kurtosis, but a common interpretation is that kurtosis represents the degree to which the shape of the distribution is influenced by infrequent extreme observations ({{gli|outlier|outliers}}); in this case, higher kurtosis means more of the {{gli|variance}} is due to infrequent extreme deviations, as opposed to frequent mode

definition: A theorem according to which the average of the results obtained from performing the same experiment a large number of times should be close to the experiment's expected value, and tends to become closer to the expected value as more trials are performed. The law suggests that a sufficiently large number of trials is necessary for the results of any experiment to be considered reliable, and by extension that performing only a small number of trials may produce an incomplete or misleading interpretation of the experiment's outcomes.
definition_raw: A theorem according to which the average of the results obtained from performing the same experiment a large number of times should be close to the experiment's {{gli|expected value}}, and tends to become closer to the expected value as more trials are performed. The law suggests that a sufficiently large number of trials is necessary for the results of any experiment to be considered reliable, and by extension that performing only a small number of trials may produce an incomplete or misleading

definition: A conditional probability function considered a function of its second argument with its first argument held fixed. For example, imagine pulling a numbered ball with a number k from a bag of n balls, numbered 1 to n; a likelihood function for the random variable N could be described as the probability of pulling k given that there are n balls: the likelihood will be 1/n for n greater than or equal to k, and 0 for n smaller than k. Unlike a probability distribution function, this likelihood function will not sum up to 1 on the sample space.
definition_raw: A conditional probability function considered a function of its second argument with its first argument held fixed. For example, imagine pulling a numbered ball with a number ''k'' from a bag of ''n'' balls, numbered 1 to ''n''; a likelihood function for the random variable ''N'' could be described as the probability of pulling ''k'' given that there are ''n'' balls: the likelihood will be 1/''n'' for n greater than or equal to ''k'', and 0 for ''n'' smaller than ''k''. Unlike a probability dist

definition: Given two jointly distributed random variables X and Y, the marginal distribution of X is simply the probability distribution of X when information about Y is ignored.
definition_raw: Given two {{gli|joint distribution|jointly distributed}} random variables ''X'' and ''Y'', the marginal distribution of ''X'' is simply the {{gli|probability distribution}} of ''X'' when information about ''Y'' is ignored.

definition: The probability of a given event, ignoring any information about other events. The marginal probability of A is written P(A). Contrast conditional probability.
definition_raw: The probability of a given event, ignoring any information about other events. The marginal probability of ''A'' is written ''P''(''A''). Contrast ''{{gli|conditional probability}}''.

definition: The expected value of a random variable.
definition_raw: The {{gli|expected value}} of a random variable.

definition: A series of mathematical averages or means of different subsets of a larger data set, usually computed so as to understand trends in the data set over time.
definition_raw: A series of mathematical averages or {{gli|mean|means}} of different subsets of a larger data set, usually computed so as to understand trends in the data set over time.

definition: A vector whose components are random variables on the same probability space.
definition_raw: A vector whose components are random variables on the same probability space.

definition: A collection of events is said to be mutually independent if for any subset of the collection, the joint probability of all events occurring is equal to the product of the joint probabilities of the individual events. Think of the result of a series of coin-flips. This is a stronger condition than pairwise independence.
definition_raw: A collection of events is said to be ''mutually independent'' if for any subset of the collection, the joint probability of all events occurring is equal to the product of the joint probabilities of the individual events. Think of the result of a series of coin-flips. This is a stronger condition than pairwise independence.

definition: The statement being tested in a test of statistical significance; usually a statement of 'no effect' or 'no difference'. For example, in a test of whether light has an effect on sleep, the null hypothesis would be that light has no effect on sleep (i.e. sleep patterns are the same regardless of the lighting conditions). The null hypothesis is an expression of the expectation that the dependent variable will not change significantly as the independent variable is modified; statistical significance is measured and reported according to the degree to which this expectation is met. Contrast alternative hypothesis.
definition_raw: The statement being tested in a test of {{gli|statistical significance}}; usually a statement of 'no effect' or 'no difference'.<ref name=moore>{{cite book|last1=Moore|first1=David|author-link1=David S. Moore|last2=McCabe|first2=George|author-link2=George P. McCabe|title=Introduction to the Practice of Statistics|publisher=W.H. Freeman and Co|location=New York|year=2003|page=438|edition=4|isbn=9780716796572}}</ref> For example, in a test of whether light has an effect on sleep, the null hypothes

definition: A set of random variables, any two of which are independent.
definition_raw: A set of random variables, any two of which are independent.

definition: Any measured quantity of a statistical population that summarizes or describes an aspect of the population, e.g. a mean or a standard deviation; often a quantity to be estimated based on the corresponding quantity calculated by drawing random samples from the population. Can be a population parameter, a distribution parameter, or an unobserved parameter.
definition_raw: Any measured quantity of a statistical {{gli|population}} that summarizes or describes an aspect of the population, e.g. a {{gli|mean}} or a {{gli|standard deviation}}; often a quantity to be estimated based on the corresponding quantity calculated by drawing {{gli|random sample|random samples}} from the population. Can be a population parameter, a distribution parameter, or an unobserved parameter.

definition: In Bayesian inference, prior beliefs or other information that is available before new data or observations are taken into account.
definition_raw: In [[Bayesian inference]], prior beliefs or other information that is available before new data or observations are taken into account.

definition: See parameter.
definition_raw: See ''{{gli|parameter}}''.

definition: The result of a Bayesian analysis that encapsulates the combination of prior beliefs or information (the prior probability) with observed data.
definition_raw: The result of a [[Bayesian analysis]] that encapsulates the combination of prior beliefs or information (the {{gli|prior probability}}) with observed data.

definition: The probability in a continuous probability distribution. For example, you can't say that the probability of a man being six feet tall is 20%, but you can say he has 20% of chances of being between five and six feet tall. Probability density is given by a probability density function. Contrast probability mass.
definition_raw: The probability in a continuous probability distribution. For example, you can't say that the probability of a man being six feet tall is 20%, but you can say he has 20% of chances of being between five and six feet tall. Probability density is given by a {{gli|probability density function}}. Contrast ''{{gli|probability mass}}''.

definition: The probability distribution for a continuous random variable.
definition_raw: The probability distribution for a continuous random variable.

definition: A function that gives the probability of all elements in a given space; see List of probability distributions.
definition_raw: A function that gives the probability of all elements in a given space; see [[List of probability distributions]].

definition: The probability of events in a probability space.
definition_raw: The {{gli|probability}} of events in a {{gli|probability space}}.

definition: A sample space over which a probability measure has been defined.
definition_raw: A {{gli|sample space}} over which a {{gli|probability measure}} has been defined.

definition: A particular point or value at which the range of a probability distribution is divided into continuous intervals with equal probabilities, or at which the observations in a sample are divided in the same way. The number of groups into which the range is divided is always one greater than the number of quantiles dividing them. Commonly used quantiles include quartiles (which divide a range into four groups), deciles (ten groups), and percentiles (one hundred groups). The groups themselves are termed halves, thirds, quarters, etc., though the terms for the quantiles are sometimes used to refer to the groups, rather than to the cut points.
definition_raw: A particular point or value at which the range of a probability distribution is divided into continuous intervals with equal probabilities, or at which the observations in a sample are divided in the same way. The number of groups into which the range is divided is always one greater than the number of quantiles dividing them. Commonly used quantiles include {{gli|quartile|quartiles}} (which divide a range into four groups), {{gli|decile|deciles}} (ten groups), and {{gli|percentile|percentiles}}

definition: A type of quantile which divides a range of data points into four groups, termed quarters, of equal size. For any quartile-divided dataset, there are exactly three quartiles or cut points that create the four groups. The first quartile ($Q$1) is defined as the middle data point or value that is halfway between the smallest value (minimum) and the median of the dataset, such that 25 percent of the data lies below this quartile. The second quartile ($Q$2) is the median itself, with 50 percent of the data below this point. The third quartile ($Q$3) is defined as the middle value halfway between the median and the largest value (maximum) of the dataset, such that 75 percent of the data lies below this quartile. Because the data must be ordered from smallest to largest in order to compute them, quartiles are a type of order statistic.
definition_raw: A type of {{gli|quantile}} which divides a range of data points into four groups, termed ''quarters'', of equal size. For any quartile-divided dataset, there are exactly three quartiles or cut points that create the four groups. The first quartile (<math>Q</math><sub>1</sub>) is defined as the middle data point or value that is halfway between the smallest value (minimum) and the {{gli|median}} of the dataset, such that 25 percent of the data lies below this quartile. The second quartile (<math>

definition: A measurable function on a probability space, often real-valued. The distribution function of a random variable gives the probability of the different values of the variable. The mean and variance of a random variable can also be derived. See also discrete random variable and continuous random variable.
definition_raw: A measurable function on a probability space, often real-valued. The distribution function of a random variable gives the probability of the different values of the variable. The mean and variance of a random variable can also be derived. See also ''{{gli|discrete random variable}}'' and ''{{gli|continuous random variable}}''.

definition: The length of the smallest interval which contains all of the data in a dataset, calculated as the arithmetic difference between the largest and smallest values contained in the dataset and expressed in the same units used for the data. The range provides a measure of the statistical dispersion of the dataset.
definition_raw: The length of the smallest interval which contains all of the data in a dataset, calculated as the arithmetic difference between the largest and smallest values contained in the dataset and expressed in the same units used for the data. The range provides a measure of the statistical dispersion of the dataset.

definition: A data analysis or statistical model that employs a set of statistical methods which estimate the relationships between a dependent variable and one or more error-free independent variables. The most common example of such a model is a linear regression, in which the equation of a line that most closely fits a set of data points according to a specific mathematical criterion is calculated and plotted on a graph of the data points; other forms of regression use related methods to estimate alternative parameters or to estimate conditional expectations from various non-linear models.
definition_raw: A data analysis or statistical model that employs a set of statistical methods which estimate the relationships between a dependent variable and one or more error-free independent variables. The most common example of such a model is a {{gli|linear regression}}, in which the equation of a line that most closely fits a set of data points according to a specific mathematical criterion is calculated and plotted on a graph of the data points; other forms of regression use related methods to estimate

definition: Any variable whose value is or is expected to be affected by an experimental treatment, or by changes in one or more other variables; e.g. cholesterol levels after following a particular diet for six months. Response variables are those that change or respond to some phenomenon under study. The term is often used interchangeably with dependent variable.
definition_raw: Any {{gli|variable}} whose value is or is expected to be affected by an experimental treatment, or by changes in one or more other variables; e.g. cholesterol levels after following a particular diet for six months. Response variables are those that change or respond to some phenomenon under study. The term is often used interchangeably with {{gli|dependent variable}}.<ref name=Reiter/>

definition: That part of a population which is actually observed or measured.
definition_raw: That part of a {{gli|population}} which is actually observed or measured.

definition: The arithmetic mean of a sample of values drawn from a population, commonly denoted by $\overline{x}$. An example is the average test score of a subset of 10 students from a class. On the assumption that the sample is representative of the larger population, the sample mean is often used as an estimator of the population mean, which in this example would be the average test score of all of the students in the class.
definition_raw: The {{gli|arithmetic mean}} of a {{gli|sample}} of values drawn from a population, commonly denoted by <math>\overline{x}</math>. An example is the average test score of a subset of 10 students from a class. On the assumption that the sample is representative of the larger population, the sample mean is often used as an estimator of the {{gli|population mean}}, which in this example would be the average test score of all of the students in the class.

definition: The set of possible outcomes of an experiment. For example, the sample space for rolling a six-sided die is {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}.
definition_raw: The set of possible outcomes of an experiment. For example, the sample space for rolling a six-sided die is <nowiki>{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6}</nowiki>.

definition: The process of using a fraction of observations or samples selected from a larger population in order to study and estimate characteristics of the larger population. Sampling relies on the assumption that a sufficiently sized subset of data points chosen from a larger set of data points, when chosen according to appropriate criteria, is representative of the larger set as a whole – i.e. that the statistical properties of the subset are nearly the same as the properties of the larger set – and therefore that researchers are justified in using the samples to make inferences about the larger population without having to actually observe or measure every single data point in the population, which may be prohibitively expensive, difficult, or impossible to do in reality.
definition_raw: The process of using a fraction of observations or {{gli|samples}} selected from a larger {{gli|population}} in order to study and estimate characteristics of the larger population. Sampling relies on the assumption that a {{gli|sample size|sufficiently sized}} subset of data points chosen from a larger set of data points, when chosen according to appropriate criteria, is representative of the larger set as a whole – i.e. that the statistical properties of the subset are nearly the same as the p

definition: The probability distribution, obtained by repeated sampling of the population, of a given statistic.
definition_raw: The {{gli|probability distribution}}, obtained by repeated {{gli|sampling}} of the {{gli|population}}, of a given statistic.

definition: A measure of the asymmetry of the probability distribution of a real-valued random variable about its mean. Roughly speaking, a distribution has positive skew (right-skewed) if the higher tail is longer, and negative skew (left-skewed) if the lower tail is longer. Perfectly symmetrical distributions always have zero skewness, though zero skewness does not necessarily imply a symmetrical distribution.
definition_raw: A measure of the asymmetry of the {{gli|probability distribution}} of a real-valued random variable about its {{gli|mean}}. Roughly speaking, a distribution has positive skew (right-skewed) if the higher tail is longer, and negative skew (left-skewed) if the lower tail is longer. Perfectly symmetrical distributions always have zero skewness, though zero skewness does not necessarily imply a symmetrical distribution.

definition: The most commonly used measure of statistical dispersion. It is the square root of the variance, and is generally denoted with the lowercase Greek letter $\sigma$ (sigma).
definition_raw: The most commonly used measure of statistical dispersion. It is the [[square root]] of the {{gli|variance}}, and is generally denoted with the lowercase Greek letter <math>\sigma</math> ([[Sigma (letter)|sigma]]).

definition: The result of applying a statistical algorithm to a data set. It can also be described as an observable random variable.
definition_raw: The result of applying a statistical algorithm to a data set. It can also be described as an observable random variable.

definition: Two events are independent if the outcome of one does not affect that of the other (for example, getting a 1 on a single die roll does not affect the probability of getting a 1 on a second roll). Similarly, when we assert that two random variables are independent, we intuitively mean that knowing something about the value of one of them does not yield any information about the value of the other.
definition_raw: Two events are independent if the outcome of one does not affect that of the other (for example, getting a 1 on a single die roll does not affect the probability of getting a 1 on a second roll). Similarly, when we assert that two random variables are independent, we intuitively mean that knowing something about the value of one of them does not yield any information about the value of the other.

definition: Inference about a population based on a random sample drawn from that population or, more generally, about a random process from its observed behavior during a finite period of time.
definition_raw: Inference about a population based on a random sample drawn from that population or, more generally, about a random process from its observed behavior during a finite period of time.

definition: A set of entities about which statistical inferences are to be drawn, often based on random sampling. One can also talk about a population of measurements or values.
definition_raw: A set of entities about which statistical inferences are to be drawn, often based on random sampling. One can also talk about a population of measurements or values.

definition: A parameter that indexes a family of probability distributions.
definition_raw: A {{gli|parameter}} that indexes a family of probability distributions.

definition: Standard for structuring data such that "each variable is a column, each observation is a row, and each type of observational unit is a table". It is equivalent to Codd's third normal form.
definition_raw: Standard for structuring data such that "each variable is a column, each observation is a row, and each type of observational unit is a table". It is equivalent to Codd's [[third normal form]].<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Wickham|first=Hadley|authorlink=Hadley Wickham|date=2014|title=Tidy Data|url=https://www.jstatsoft.org/index.php/jss/article/view/v059i10/v59i10.pdf|journal=Journal of Statistical Software|volume=59|issue=10|doi=10.18637/jss.v059.i10 |doi-access=free }}</ref>

definition: Variables in a statistical study that are conceptually manipulable. For example, in a health study, following a certain diet is a treatment whereas age is not.
definition_raw: Variables in a statistical study that are conceptually manipulable. For example, in a health study, following a certain diet is a treatment whereas age is not.<ref name=Reiter/>

definition: Can refer to each individual repetition when talking about an experiment composed of any fixed number of them. As an example, one can think of an experiment being any number from one to n coin tosses, say 17. In this case, one toss can be called a trial to avoid confusion, since the whole experiment is composed of 17 ones.
definition_raw: Can refer to each individual repetition when talking about an experiment composed of any fixed number of them. As an example, one can think of an experiment being any number from one to ''n'' coin tosses, say 17. In this case, one toss can be called a trial to avoid confusion, since the whole experiment is composed of 17 ones.

term: type I and type II errors
term_wikilink: type I and type II errors

definition: In a statistical study, the objects to which treatments are assigned. For example, in a study examining the effects of smoking cigarettes, the units would be people.
definition_raw: In a statistical study, the objects to which treatments are assigned. For example, in a study examining the effects of smoking cigarettes, the units would be people.<ref name=Reiter/>

definition: A measure of its statistical dispersion of a random variable, indicating how far from the expected value its values typically are. The variance of random variable X is typically designated as $\operatorname{var}(X)$, $\sigma_X^2$, or simply $\sigma^2$
definition_raw: A measure of its statistical dispersion of a random variable, indicating how far from the expected value its values typically are. The variance of random variable ''X'' is typically designated as <math>\operatorname{var}(X)</math>, <math>\sigma_X^2</math>, or simply <math>\sigma^2</math>

term: Yates's correction for continuity, yules correction

definition: Abel sum
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Abel sum]]

definition: analytic capacity.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[analytic capacity]].

definition: An analytic continuation of a holomorphic function is a unique holomorphic extension of the function (on a connected open subset of $\mathbb{C}$).
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: An [[analytic continuation]] of a holomorphic function is a unique holomorphic extension of the function (on a connected open subset of <math>\mathbb{C}</math>).

definition: argument principle
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[argument principle]]

definition: Ascoli's theorem says that an equicontinous bounded sequence of functions on a compact subset of $\mathbb{R}^n$ has a convergent subsequence with respect to the sup norm.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Ascoli's theorem]] says that an equicontinous bounded sequence of functions on a compact subset of <math>\mathbb{R}^n</math> has a convergent subsequence with respect to the sup norm.

definition: Bargmann transform
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Bargmann transform]]

definition: Berezin integral
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Berezin integral]]

definition: A Borel measure is a measure whose domain is the Borel σ-algebra.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A [[Borel measure]] is a measure whose domain is the Borel σ-algebra.

definition: A subset $A$ of a metric space $(X,d)$ is bounded if there is some $C > 0$ such that $d(a,b) < C$ for all $a,b \in A$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A subset <math>A</math> of a metric space <math>(X,d)</math> is bounded if there is some <math>C > 0</math> such that <math>d(a,b) < C</math> for all <math>a,b \in A</math>.

definition: A bump function is a nonzero compactly-supported smooth function, usually constructed using the exponential function.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A [[bump function]] is a nonzero compactly-supported smooth function, usually constructed using the exponential function.

definition: A BV-function or a bounded variation is a function with bounded total variation.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A [[BV-function]] or a [[bounded variation]] is a function with bounded total variation.

definition: Calderón–Zygmund lemma
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Calderón–Zygmund lemma]]

definition: Capacity of a set is a notion in potential theory.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Capacity of a set]] is a notion in [[potential theory]].

definition: Carathéodory's extension theorem
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Carathéodory's extension theorem]]

definition: Cartan's theorems A and B.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Cartan's theorems A and B]].

definition: Cartwright's theorem gives a bounded for a p-valent entire function.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Cartwright's theorem]] gives a bounded for a ''p''-valent entire function.

definition: The Cauchy–Riemann equations are a system of differential equations such that a function satisfying it (in the distribution sense) is a holomorphic function.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: The [[Cauchy–Riemann equations]] are a system of differential equations such that a function satisfying it (in the distribution sense) is a [[holomorphic function]].

definition: Cesàro summation is one way to compute a divergent series.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Cesàro summation]] is one way to compute a [[divergent series]].

definition: Clarke generalized derivative.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Clarke generalized derivative]].

definition: A function $f : X \to Y$ between metric spaces $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ is continuous if for any convergent sequence $x_n \to x$ in $X$, we have $f(x_n) \to f(x)$ in $Y$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A function <math>f : X \to Y</math> between metric spaces <math>(X,d_X)</math> and <math>(Y,d_Y)</math> is continuous if for any convergent sequence <math>x_n \to x</math> in <math>X</math>, we have <math>f(x_n) \to f(x)</math> in <math>Y</math>.

definition: The contour integral of a measurable function $f$ over a piece-wise smooth curve $\gamma : [0, 1] \to \mathbb{C}$ is $\int_{\gamma} f \, dz := \int_0^1 \gamma^*(f \, dz)$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: The [[contour integral]] of a measurable function <math>f</math> over a piece-wise smooth curve <math>\gamma : [0, 1] \to \mathbb{C}</math> is <math>\int_{\gamma} f \, dz := \int_0^1 \gamma^*(f \, dz)</math>.

definition: A sequence $x_n$ in a topological space is said to converge to a point $x$ if for each open neighborhood $U$ of $x$, the set $\{ n \mid x_n \not\in U \}$ is finite.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A sequence <math>x_n</math> in a topological space is said to [[converge (mathematics)|converge]] to a point <math>x</math> if for each open neighborhood <math>U</math> of <math>x</math>, the set <math>\{ n \mid x_n \not\in U \}</math> is finite.

definition: The convolution $f * g$ of two functions on a convex set is given by :$(f * g)(x) = \int f(y - x)g(y) \, dy,$ provided the integration converges.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw:
The [[convolution]] <math>f * g</math> of two functions on a convex set is given by
:<math>(f * g)(x) = \int f(y - x)g(y) \, dy,</math>
provided the integration converges.

definition: Cousin problems.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Cousin problems]].

definition: For sets $F \subset U$, $F$ closed, $U$ open, a cutoff function is a function that is $1$ on $F$ and has support contained in $U$. It’s usually required to be continuous or smooth.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: For sets <math>F \subset U</math>, <math>F</math> closed, <math>U</math> open, a [[cutoff function]] is a function that is <math>1</math> on <math>F</math> and has support contained in <math>U</math>. It’s usually required to be continuous or smooth.

definition: A Dedekind cut is one way to construct real numbers.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A [[Dedekind cut]] is one way to construct real numbers.

definition: Given a map $f : E \to F$ between normed spaces, the derivative of $f$ at a point x is a (unique) linear map $T : E \to F$ such that $\lim_{h \to 0} \| f(x + h) - f(x) - Th \|/\|h\| = 0$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: Given a map <math>f : E \to F</math> between normed spaces, the [[derivative]] of <math>f</math> at a point ''x'' is a (unique) linear map <math>T : E \to F</math> such that <math>\lim_{h \to 0} \| f(x + h) - f(x) - Th \|/\|h\| = 0</math>.

definition: A map between normed space is differentiable at a point x if the derivative at x exists.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A map between normed space is differentiable at a point ''x'' if the derivative at ''x'' exists.

definition: Lebesgue's differentiation theorem says: $f(x) = \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{1}{\operatorname{vol}(B(x, r))} \int_{B(x, r)} f \, d\mu$ for almost all x.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Lebesgue's differentiation theorem]] says: <math>f(x) = \lim_{r \to 0} \frac{1}{\operatorname{vol}(B(x, r))} \int_{B(x, r)} f \, d\mu</math> for almost all ''x''.

definition: Dini's theorem.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Dini's theorem]].

definition: The Dirac delta function $\delta_0$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$ is a distribution (so not exactly a function) given as $\langle \delta_0, \varphi \rangle = \varphi(0).$
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: The [[Dirac delta function]] <math>\delta_0</math> on <math>\mathbb{R}^n</math> is a distribution (so not exactly a function) given as <math>\langle \delta_0, \varphi \rangle = \varphi(0).</math>

definition: A distribution is a type of a generalized function; precisely, it is a continuous linear functional on the space of test functions.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A [[distribution (mathematics)|distribution]] is a type of a generalized function; precisely, it is a continuous linear functional on the space of test functions.

definition: A divergent series is a series whose partial sum does not converge. For example, $\sum_1^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}$ is divergent.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A [[divergent series]] is a series whose partial sum does not converge. For example, <math>\sum_1^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}</math> is divergent.

definition: The division conjecture of L. Schwartz (now a theorem) says a distribution divided by a real analytic function is again a distribution.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: The [[division conjecture of L. Schwartz]] (now a theorem) says a distribution divided by a real analytic function is again a distribution.

definition: Lebesgue's dominated convergence theorem says $\int f_n \, d\mu$ converges to $\int f \, d\mu$ if $f_n$ is a sequence of measurable functions such that $f_n$ converges to $f$ pointwise and $|f_n| \le g$ for some integrable function $g$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Lebesgue's dominated convergence theorem]] says <math>\int f_n \, d\mu</math> converges to <math>\int f \, d\mu</math> if <math>f_n</math> is a sequence of measurable functions such that <math>f_n</math> converges to <math>f</math> pointwise and <math>|f_n| \le g</math> for some integrable function <math>g</math>.

definition: Edge-of-the-wedge theorem.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Edge-of-the-wedge theorem]].

definition: Egoroff's theorem.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Egoroff's theorem]].

definition: An entire function is a holomorphic function whose domain is the entire complex plane.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: An [[entire function]] is a holomorphic function whose domain is the entire complex plane.

definition: A set $S$ of maps between fixed metric spaces is said to be equicontinuous if for each $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a $\delta > 0$ such that $\sup_{f \in S} d(f(x), f(y)) < \epsilon$ for all $x, y$ with $d(x, y) < \delta$. A map $f$ is uniformly continuous if and only if $\{ f \}$ is equicontinuous.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A set <math>S</math> of maps between fixed metric spaces is said to be [[equicontinuous]] if for each <math>\epsilon > 0</math>, there exists a <math>\delta > 0</math> such that <math>\sup_{f \in S} d(f(x), f(y)) < \epsilon</math> for all <math>x, y</math> with <math>d(x, y) < \delta</math>. A map <math>f</math> is uniformly continuous if and only if <math>\{ f \}</math> is equicontinuous.

definition: Fatou's lemma
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Fatou's lemma]]

definition: Fock space
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Fock space]]

definition: The Fourier transform of a function $f$ on $\mathbb{R}^n$ is: (provided it makes sense) :$\widehat{f}(\xi) = \int f(x) e^{-2\pi i x \cdot \xi} \, dx.$
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw:
The Fourier transform of a function <math>f</math> on <math>\mathbb{R}^n</math> is: (provided it makes sense)
:<math>\widehat{f}(\xi) = \int f(x) e^{-2\pi i x \cdot \xi} \, dx.</math>

definition: The Gauss–Green formula
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: The [[Gauss–Green formula]]

definition: A generalized function is an element of some function space that contains the space of ordinary (e.g., locally integrable) functions. Examples are Schwartz's distributions and Sato's hyperfunctions.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A [[generalized function]] is an element of some function space that contains the space of ordinary (e.g., locally integrable) functions. Examples are [[Schwartz's distribution]]s and [[Sato's hyperfunction]]s.

definition: The Hardy-Littlewood maximal function of $f \in L^1(\mathbb R^n)$ is :$Hf(x) := \sup_{r>0} \frac{1}{m(B_r(x))} \int_{B_r(x)} |f|.$ The Hardy-Littlewood maximal inequality states that there is some constant $C$ such that for all $f \in L^1(\mathbb R^n)$ and all $\alpha > 0$, :$m\left(\{ x : Hf(x) > \alpha \}\right) < \frac{C}{\alpha} \int_{\mathbb R^n} |f|.$
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw:
The [[Hardy-Littlewood maximal function]] of <math>f \in L^1(\mathbb R^n)</math> is
:<math>Hf(x) := \sup_{r>0} \frac{1}{m(B_r(x))} \int_{B_r(x)} |f|.</math>
The [[Hardy-Littlewood maximal inequality]] states that there is some constant <math>C</math> such that for all <math>f \in L^1(\mathbb R^n)</math> and all <math>\alpha > 0</math>,
:<math>m\left(\{ x : Hf(x) > \alpha \}\right) < \frac{C}{\alpha} \int_{\mathbb R^n} |f|.</math>

definition: Hardy space
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Hardy space]]

definition: Hartogs extension theorem
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Hartogs extension theorem]]

definition: A function is harmonic if it satisfies the Laplace equation (in the distribution sense if the function is not twice differentiable).
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A function is [[harmonic function|harmonic]] if it satisfies the Laplace equation (in the distribution sense if the function is not twice differentiable).

definition: The Hausdorff–Young inequality says that the Fourier transformation $\widehat{\cdot} : L^p(\mathbb{R}^n) \to L^{p'}(\mathbb{R}^n)$ is a well-defined bounded operator when $1/p + 1/p' = 1$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: The [[Hausdorff–Young inequality]] says that the Fourier transformation <math>\widehat{\cdot} : L^p(\mathbb{R}^n) \to L^{p'}(\mathbb{R}^n)</math> is a well-defined bounded operator when <math>1/p + 1/p' = 1</math>.

definition: The Heaviside function is the function H on $\mathbb{R}$ such that $H(x) = 1, \, x \ge 0$ and $H(x) = 0, \, x < 0$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: The [[Heaviside function]] is the function ''H'' on <math>\mathbb{R}</math> such that <math>H(x) = 1, \, x \ge 0</math> and <math>H(x) = 0, \, x < 0</math>.

definition: Hermite polynomial
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Hermite polynomial]]

definition: A Hilbert space is a real or complex inner product space that is a complete metric space with the metric induced by the inner product.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A [[Hilbert space]] is a real or complex inner product space that is a complete metric space with the metric induced by the inner product.

definition: A function defined on an open subset of $\mathbb C^n$ is holomorphic if it is complex differentiable. Equivalently, a function is holomorphic if it satisfies the Cauchy–Riemann equations (in the distribution sense if the function is not differentiable).
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A function defined on an open subset of <math>\mathbb C^n</math> is [[holomorphic]] if it is [[complex differentiable]]. Equivalently, a function is holomorphic if it satisfies the Cauchy–Riemann equations (in the distribution sense if the function is not differentiable).

definition: A hypoelliptic operator is an operator for which the elliptic regularity holds.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A [[hypoelliptic operator]] is an operator for which the elliptic regularity holds.

definition: A measurable function $f$ is said to be integrable if $\int |f| \, d\mu < \infty$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: A measurable function <math>f</math> is said to be [[integrable function|integrable]] if <math>\int |f| \, d\mu < \infty</math>.

definition: The integral of the indicator function on a measurable set is the measure (volume) of the set.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: The [[integral]] of the [[indicator function]] on a measurable set is the measure (volume) of the set.

definition: An isometry between metric spaces $(X,d_X)$ and $(Y,d_Y)$ is a bijection $f : X \to Y$ that preserves the metric: $d_X(x,x') = d_Y(f(x),f(x'))$ for all $x,x' \in X$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: An isometry between metric spaces <math>(X,d_X)</math> and <math>(Y,d_Y)</math> is a bijection <math>f : X \to Y</math> that preserves the metric: <math>d_X(x,x') = d_Y(f(x),f(x'))</math> for all <math>x,x' \in X</math>.

definition: The Lebesgue differentiation theorem states that for locally integrable <math>f \in L^1_{\text{loc
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: The [[Lebesgue differentiation theorem]] states that for locally integrable <math>f \in L^1_{\text{loc

definition: Lebesgue integral.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Lebesgue integral]].

definition: Legendre transformation.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Legendre transformation]].

definition: Lelong number.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Lelong number]].

definition: Levi's problem asks to show a pseudoconvex set is a domain of holomorphy.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Levi's problem]] asks to show a pseudoconvex set is a domain of holomorphy.

definition: Line integral.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Line integral]].

definition: Liouville's theorem says a bounded entire function is a constant function.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis
definition_raw: [[Liouville's theorem (complex analysis)|Liouville's theorem]] says a bounded entire function is a constant function.

definition: A map $f$ between metric spaces is said to be Lipschitz continuous if $\sup_{x \ne y} \frac{d(f(x), f(y))}{d(x, y)} < \infty$.

definition: A measure is a function on a measurable space that assigns to each measurable set a number representing its measure or size. Specifically, if X is a set and Σ is a σ-algebra on X, then a set-function μ from Σ to the extended real number line is called a measure if the following conditions hold: *Non-negativity: For all $E \in \Sigma, \ \ \mu(E) \geq 0.$ *$\mu(\varnothing) = 0.$ *Countable additivity (or σ-additivity): For all countable collections $\{ E_k \}_{k=1}^\infty$ of pairwise disjoint sets in Σ, $\mu\left(\bigcup_{k=1}^\infty E_k\right)=\sum_{k=1}^\infty \mu(E_k).$
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

definition: A metric space is a set X equipped with a function $d : X \times X \to \mathbb R_{\geq 0}$, called a metric, such that (1) $d(x,y) = 0$ iff $x=y$, (2) $d(x,y) \leq d(x,z) + d(z,y)$ for all $x,y,z \in X$, (3) $d(x,y) = d(y,x)$ for all $x,y \in X$.

definition: A normed vector space, also called a normed space, is a real or complex vector space V on which a norm is defined. A norm is a map $\lVert\cdot \rVert : V \to \mathbb R$ satisfying four axioms: # Non-negativity: for every $x\in V$,$\; \lVert x \rVert \ge 0$. # Positive definiteness: for every $x \in V$, $\; \lVert x\rVert=0$ if and only if $x$ is the zero vector. # Absolute homogeneity: for every scalar $\lambda$ and $x\in V$,$\lVert \lambda x \rVert = |\lambda|\, \lVert x\rVert $ # Triangle inequality: for every $x\in V$ and $y\in V$,$\|x+y\| \leq \|x\| + \|y\|.$

definition: Let $X$ be a locally compact Hausdorff space and let $I$ be a positive linear functional on the space of continuous functions with compact support $C_c(X)$. Positivity means that $I(f) \geq 0$ if $f \geq 0$. There exist Borel measures $\mu$ on $X$ such that $I(f) = \int f \, d\mu$ for all $f \in C_c(X)$. A Radon measure on $X$ is a Borel measure that is finite on all compact sets, outer regular on all Borel sets, and inner regular on all open sets. These conditions guarantee that there exists a unique Radon measure $\mu$ on $X$ such that $I(f) = \int f \, d\mu$ for all $f \in C_c(X)$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

definition: The Stone–Weierstrass theorem is any one of a number of related generalizations of the Weierstrass approximation theorem, which states that any continuous real-valued function defined on a closed interval can be uniformly approximated by polynomials. Let $X$ be a compact Hausdorff space and let $C(X,\mathbb R)$ have the uniform metric. One version of the Stone–Weierstrass theorem states that if $\mathcal A$ is a closed subalgebra of $C(X,\mathbb R)$ that separates points and contains a nonzero constant function, then in fact $\mathcal A = C(X,\mathbb R)$. If a subalgebra is not closed, taking the closure and applying the previous version of the Stone–Weierstrass theorem reveals a different version of the theorem: if $\mathcal A$ is a subalgebra of $C(X,\mathbb R)$ that separates points and contains a nonzero constant function, then $\mathcal A$ is dense in $C(X,\mathbb R)$.
source_glossary: Glossary of real and complex analysis

definition: Given a field extension $K/F$, a representation V of a group G over K is said to be defined over F if $V \simeq V_0 \otimes_F K$ for some representation $V_0$ over F such that $g: V \to V$ is induced by $g: V_0 \to V_0$; i.e., $ g \cdot (v \otimes \lambda) = g v \otimes \lambda$. Here, $V_0$ is called an F-form of V (and is not necessarily unique).
definition_raw: Given a field extension <math>K/F</math>, a representation ''V'' of a group ''G'' over ''K'' is said to be [[field of definition|defined over ''F'']] if <math>V \simeq V_0 \otimes_F K</math> for some representation <math>V_0</math> over ''F'' such that <math>g: V \to V</math> is induced by <math>g: V_0 \to V_0</math>; i.e., <math> g \cdot (v \otimes \lambda) = g v \otimes \lambda</math>. Here, <math>V_0</math> is called an ''F''-form of ''V'' (and is not necessarily unique).

definition: Demazure's character formula
definition_raw: [[Demazure's character formula]]

definition: The direct sum of representations V, W is a representation that is the direct sum $V \oplus W$ of the vector spaces together with the linear group action $\pi_{V \oplus W}(g)(v + w) = \pi_V(g)v + \pi_W(g)w$.
definition_raw: The [[direct sum of representations]] ''V'', ''W'' is a representation that is the direct sum <math>V \oplus W</math> of the vector spaces together with the linear group action <math>\pi_{V \oplus W}(g)(v + w) = \pi_V(g)v + \pi_W(g)w</math>.

definition: An irreducible representation of a Lie group G is said to be in the discrete series if the matrix coefficients of it are all square integrable. For example, if G is compact, then every irreducible representation of it is in the discrete series.
definition_raw: An irreducible representation of a Lie group ''G'' is said to be in the [[discrete series]] if the matrix coefficients of it are all square integrable. For example, if ''G'' is compact, then every irreducible representation of it is in the discrete series.

definition: The irreducible representations of a simply-connected compact Lie group are indexed by their highest weight. These dominant weights form the lattice points in an orthant in the weight lattice of the Lie group.
definition_raw: The irreducible representations of a simply-connected compact Lie group are indexed by their highest weight. These ''dominant weights'' form the lattice points in an orthant in the weight lattice of the Lie group.

definition: The dual representation (or the contragredient representation) of a representation V is a representation that is the dual vector space $V^* = \operatorname{Hom}(V, k)$ together with the linear group action that preserves the natural pairing $V^* \times V \to k$
definition_raw: The [[dual representation]] (or the contragredient representation) of a representation ''V'' is a representation that is the dual vector space <math>V^* = \operatorname{Hom}(V, k)</math> together with the linear group action that preserves the natural pairing <math>V^* \times V \to k</math>

definition: Eisenstein series
definition_raw: [[Eisenstein series]]

definition: The term “G-equivariant” is another term for “G-linear”.
definition_raw: The term “''G''-equivariant” is another term for “''G''-linear”.

definition: An exterior power of a representation V is a representation $\wedge^n(V)$ with the group action induced by $V^{\otimes n} \to \wedge^n(V)$.
definition_raw: An [[exterior power of a representation]] ''V'' is a representation <math>\wedge^n(V)</math> with the group action induced by <math>V^{\otimes n} \to \wedge^n(V)</math>.

definition: A faithful representation $\pi: G \to GL(V)$ is a representation such that $\pi$ is injective as a function.
definition_raw: A [[faithful representation]] <math>\pi: G \to GL(V)</math> is a representation such that <math>\pi</math> is [[injective function|injective]] as a function.

definition: fiber functor.
definition_raw: [[fiber functor]].

definition: The Frobenius reciprocity states that for each representation $\sigma$ of H and representation $\pi$ of G there is a bijection :$\operatorname{Hom}_G(\pi, \operatorname{Ind}_H^G \sigma) = \operatorname{Hom}_H(\pi|_H, \sigma)$ that is natural in the sense that $\operatorname{Ind}_H^G$ is the right adjoint functor to the restriction functor $\pi \mapsto \pi|_H$.
definition_raw:
The [[Frobenius reciprocity]] states that for each representation <math>\sigma</math> of ''H'' and representation <math>\pi</math> of ''G'' there is a bijection
:<math>\operatorname{Hom}_G(\pi, \operatorname{Ind}_H^G \sigma) = \operatorname{Hom}_H(\pi|_H, \sigma)</math>
that is natural in the sense that <math>\operatorname{Ind}_H^G</math> is the right [[adjoint functor]] to the restriction functor <math>\pi \mapsto \pi|_H</math>.

definition: Fundamental representation: For the irreducible representations of a simply-connected compact Lie group there exists a set of fundamental weights, indexed by the vertices of the Dynkin diagram of G, such that dominant weights are simply non-negative integer linear combinations of the fundamental weights. The corresponding irreducible representations are the fundamental representations of the Lie group. In particular, from the expansion of a dominant weight in terms of the fundamental weights, one can take a corresponding tensor product of the fundamental representations and extract one copy of the irreducible representation corresponding to that dominant weight. In the case of the special unitary group SU(n), the n − 1 fundamental representations are the wedge products :$Alt^k\ {\mathbb C}^n$ consisting of alternating tensors, for k=1,2,...,n-1.
definition_raw:
[[Fundamental representation]]: For the irreducible representations of a simply-connected [[compact Lie group]] there exists a set of ''fundamental weights'', indexed by the vertices of the [[Dynkin diagram]] of G, such that ''dominant weights'' are simply non-negative integer linear combinations of the fundamental weights.

The corresponding irreducible representations are the ''fundamental representations'' of the Lie group. In particular, from the expansion of a dominant weight in terms of th

definition: A G-linear map $f: V \to W$ between representations is a linear transformation that commutes with the G-actions; i.e., $f \circ \pi_V(g) = \pi_W(g) \circ f$ for every g in G.
definition_raw: A [[equivariant map|''G''-linear map]] <math>f: V \to W</math> between representations is a linear transformation that commutes with the ''G''-actions; i.e., <math>f \circ \pi_V(g) = \pi_W(g) \circ f</math> for every ''g'' in ''G''.

definition: Another name for a representation. It allows for the module-theoretic terminology: e.g., trivial G-module, G-submodules, etc.
definition_raw: Another name for a representation. It allows for the module-theoretic terminology: e.g., trivial ''G''-module, ''G''-submodules, etc.

definition: A G-equivariant vector bundle is a vector bundle $p: E \to X$ on a G-space X together with a G-action on E (say right) such that $g: p^{-1}(x) \to p^{-1}(xg)$ is a well-defined linear map.
definition_raw: A [[equivariant vector bundle|''G''-equivariant vector bundle]] is a vector bundle <math>p: E \to X</math> on a ''G''-space ''X'' together with a ''G''-action on ''E'' (say right) such that <math>g: p^{-1}(x) \to p^{-1}(xg)</math> is a well-defined linear map.

definition: Galois representation.
definition_raw: [[Galois representation]].

definition: A good filtration of a representation of a reductive group G is a filtration such that the quotients are isomorphic to $H^0(\lambda) = \Gamma(G/B, L_\lambda)$ where $L_\lambda$ are the line bundles on the flag variety $G/B$.
definition_raw: A [[good filtration]] of a representation of a [[reductive group]] ''G'' is a filtration such that the quotients are isomorphic to <math>H^0(\lambda) = \Gamma(G/B, L_\lambda)</math> where <math>L_\lambda</math> are the line bundles on the flag variety <math>G/B</math>.

definition: Harish-Chandra (11 October 1923 – 16 October 1983), an Indian American mathematician.
definition_raw: [[Harish-Chandra]] (11 October 1923 – 16 October 1983), an Indian American mathematician.

definition: Given a complex semisimple Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$, Cartan subalgebra $\mathfrak{h}$ and a choice of a positive Weyl chamber, the highest weight of a representation of $\mathfrak{g}$ is the weight of an $\mathfrak{h}$-weight vector v such that $E_{\alpha} v = 0$ for every positive root $\alpha$ (v is called the highest weight vector).
definition_raw: Given a complex semisimple Lie algebra <math>\mathfrak{g}</math>, Cartan subalgebra <math>\mathfrak{h}</math> and a choice of a [[positive Weyl chamber]], the [[highest weight]] of a representation of <math>\mathfrak{g}</math> is the weight of an <math>\mathfrak{h}</math>-weight vector ''v'' such that <math>E_{\alpha} v = 0</math> for every positive root <math>\alpha</math> (''v'' is called the highest weight vector).

definition: The Hom representation $\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)$ of representations V, W is a representation with the group action obtained by the vector space identification $\operatorname{Hom}(V, W) = V^* \otimes W$.
definition_raw: The [[Hom representation]] <math>\operatorname{Hom}(V, W)</math> of representations ''V'', ''W'' is a representation with the group action obtained by the vector space identification <math>\operatorname{Hom}(V, W) = V^* \otimes W</math>.

definition: An indecomposable representation is a representation that is not a direct sum of at least two proper subrepresebtations.
definition_raw: An [[indecomposable representation]] is a representation that is not a direct sum of at least two proper subrepresebtations.

definition: Given a representation $(\sigma, W)$ of a subgroup H of a group G, the induced representation :$\operatorname{Ind}_H^G(\sigma) = \{ f: G \to W | f(hg) = \sigma(h) f(g) \}$ is a representation of G that is induced on the H-linear functions $f: G \to W$; cf. #Frobenius reciprocity.
definition_raw:
Given a representation <math>(\sigma, W)</math> of a subgroup ''H'' of a group ''G'', the [[induced representation]]
:<math>\operatorname{Ind}_H^G(\sigma) = \{ f: G \to W | f(hg) = \sigma(h) f(g) \}</math>
is a representation of ''G'' that is induced on the ''H''-linear functions <math>f: G \to W</math>; cf. [[#Frobenius reciprocity]].

definition: Two admissible representations of a real reductive group are said to be infinitesimally equivalent if their associated Lie algebra representations on the space of K-finite vectors are isomorphic.
definition_raw: Two admissible representations of a real reductive group are said to be [[infinitesimally equivalent]] if their associated Lie algebra representations on the space of ''K''-finite vectors are isomorphic.

definition: A representation of a Kac–Moody algebra is said to be integrable if (1) it is a sum of weight spaces and (2) Chevalley generators $e_i, f_i$ are locally nilpotent.
definition_raw: A representation of a [[Kac–Moody algebra]] is said to be [[integrable module|integrable]] if (1) it is a sum of weight spaces and (2) Chevalley generators <math>e_i, f_i</math> are [[locally nilpotent endomorphism|locally nilpotent]].

definition: The term "intertwining operator" is an old name for a G-linear map between representations.
definition_raw: The term "[[intertwining operator]]" is an old name for a ''G''-linear map between representations.

definition: An involution representation is a representation of a C*-algebra on a Hilbert space that preserves involution.
definition_raw: An [[involution representation]] is a representation of a [[C*-algebra]] on a Hilbert space that preserves involution.

definition: An irreducible representation is a representation whose only subrepresentations are zero and itself. The term "irreducible" is synonymous with "simple".
definition_raw: An [[irreducible representation]] is a representation whose only subrepresentations are zero and itself. The term "irreducible" is synonymous with "simple".

definition: An isomorphism between representations of a group G is an invertible G-linear map between the representations.
definition_raw: An isomorphism between representations of a group ''G'' is an invertible ''G''-linear map between the representations.

definition: Given a representation V and a simple representation W (subrepresebtation or otherwise), the isotypic component of V of type W is the direct sum of all subrepresentations of V that are isomorphic to W. For example, let A be a ring and G a group acting on it as automorphisms. If A is semisimple as a G-module, then the ring of invariants $A^G$ is the isotypic component of A of trivial type.
definition_raw: Given a representation ''V'' and a simple representation ''W'' (subrepresebtation or otherwise), the [[isotypic component]] of ''V'' of type ''W'' is the direct sum of all subrepresentations of ''V'' that are isomorphic to ''W''. For example, let ''A'' be a ring and ''G'' a group acting on it as automorphisms. If ''A'' is [[semisimple representation|semisimple]] as a ''G''-module, then the [[ring of invariants]] <math>A^G</math> is the isotypic component of ''A'' of trivial type.

definition: Jacquet functor
definition_raw: [[Jacquet functor]]

definition: The Kac character formula
definition_raw: The [[Kac character formula]]

definition: A vector v in a representation space of a group K is said to be K-finite if $K \cdot v$ spans a finite-dimensional vector space.
definition_raw: A vector ''v'' in a representation space of a group ''K'' is said to be ''K''-finite if <math>K \cdot v</math> spans a finite-dimensional vector space.

definition: The Kirillov character formula
definition_raw: The [[Kirillov character formula]]

definition: The root lattice is the free abelian group generated by the roots.
definition_raw: The [[root lattice]] is the free abelian group generated by the roots.

definition: Littelmann path model
definition_raw: [[Littelmann path model]]

definition: Maschke's theorem states that a finite-dimensional representation over a field F of a finite group G is a semisimple representation if the characteristic of F does not divide the order of G.
definition_raw: [[Maschke's theorem]] states that a finite-dimensional representation over a field ''F'' of a finite group ''G'' is a [[semisimple representation]] if the characteristic of ''F'' does not divide the order of ''G''.

definition: The Mackey theory may be thought of a tool to answer the question: given a representation W of a subgroup H of a group G, when is the induced representation $\operatorname{Ind}_H^G W$ an irreducible representation of G?
definition_raw: The [[Mackey theory]] may be thought of a tool to answer the question: given a representation ''W'' of a subgroup ''H'' of a group ''G'', when is the induced representation <math>\operatorname{Ind}_H^G W</math> an irreducible representation of ''G''?<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.dpmms.cam.ac.uk/~nd332/Mackey.pdf |title=Induction and Mackey Theory |access-date=2017-11-23 |archive-date=2017-12-01 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20171201032703/https://www.dpmms.cam.ac.uk/~nd332/Mackey.p

definition: Maass–Selberg relations.
definition_raw: [[Maass–Selberg relations]].

definition: A matrix coefficient of a representation $\pi: G \to GL(V)$ is a linear combination of functions on G of the form $g \mapsto \langle \pi(g) v, \alpha \rangle$ for v in V and $\alpha$ in the dual space $V^*$. Note the notion makes sense for any group: if G is a topological group and $\pi$ is continuous, then a matrix matrix coefficient would be a continuous function on G. If G and $\pi$ are algebraic, it would be a regular function on G.
definition_raw: A [[matrix coefficient]] of a representation <math>\pi: G \to GL(V)</math> is a linear combination of functions on ''G'' of the form <math>g \mapsto \langle \pi(g) v, \alpha \rangle</math> for ''v'' in ''V'' and <math>\alpha</math> in the dual space <math>V^*</math>. Note the notion makes sense for any group: if ''G'' is a topological group and <math>\pi</math> is continuous, then a matrix matrix coefficient would be a continuous function on ''G''. If ''G'' and <math>\pi</math> are algebraic, it

definition: The modular representation theory.
definition_raw: The [[modular representation theory]].

definition: Given a finite-dimensional complex representation V of a finite group G, Molien's theorem says that the series $\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} \dim (\mathbb{C}[V]^G)_n t^n$, where $(\mathbb{C}[V]^G)_n$ denotes the space of $G$-invariant homogeneous polynomials on V of degree n, coincides with $(\# G)^{-1} \sum_{g \in G} \det(1 - t g |V)^{-1}$. The theorem is also valid for a reductive group by replacing $(\# G)^{-1} \sum_{g \in G}$ by integration over a maximal compact subgroup.
definition_raw: Given a finite-dimensional complex representation ''V'' of a finite group ''G'', [[Molien's theorem]] says that the series <math>\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} \dim (\mathbb{C}[V]^G)_n t^n</math>, where <math>(\mathbb{C}[V]^G)_n</math> denotes the space of <math>G</math>-invariant homogeneous polynomials on ''V'' of degree ''n'', coincides with <math>(\# G)^{-1} \sum_{g \in G} \det(1 - t g |V)^{-1}</math>. The theorem is also valid for a reductive group by replacing <math>(\# G)^{-1} \sum_{g \in G}</math

definition: Oscillator representation
definition_raw: [[Oscillator representation]]

definition: orbit method, an approach to representation theory that uses tools from symplectic geometry
definition_raw: [[orbit method]], an approach to representation theory that uses tools from symplectic geometry

definition: The Peter–Weyl theorem states that the linear span of the matrix coefficients on a compact group G is dense in $L^2(G)$.
definition_raw: The [[Peter–Weyl theorem]] states that the linear span of the [[matrix coefficient]]s on a compact group ''G'' is dense in <math>L^2(G)</math>.

definition: Given a group G, a G-set X and V the vector space of functions from X to a fixed field, a permutation representation $\pi$ of G on V is a representation given by the induced action of G on V; i.e., $(\pi(g)v)(x) = v(g^{-1} x)$. For example, if X is a finite set and V is viewed as a vector space with a basis parameteized by X, then the symmetric group $G = \operatorname{Sym}(X)$ permutates the elements of the basis and its linear extension is precisely the permutation representation.
definition_raw: Given a group ''G'', a ''G''-set ''X'' and ''V'' the vector space of functions from ''X'' to a fixed field, a [[permutation representation]] <math>\pi</math> of ''G'' on ''V'' is a representation given by the induced action of ''G'' on ''V''; i.e., <math>(\pi(g)v)(x) = v(g^{-1} x)</math>. For example, if ''X'' is a finite set and ''V'' is viewed as a vector space with a basis parameteized by ''X'', then the symmetric group <math>G = \operatorname{Sym}(X)</math> permutates the elements of the bas

definition: Plancherel formula
definition_raw: [[Plancherel formula]]

definition: positive-energy representation.
definition_raw: [[positive-energy representation]].

definition: The term "primitive element" (or a vector) is an old term for a Borel-weight vector.
definition_raw: The term "primitive element" (or a vector) is an old term for a Borel-weight vector.

definition: A projective representation of a group G is a group homomorphism $\pi: G \to PGL(V) = GL(V)/\mathbb{G}_m$. Since $PGL(V) = \operatorname{Aut}(\mathbb{P}(V))$, a projective representation is precisely a group action of G on $\mathbb{P}(V)$ as automorphisms.
definition_raw: A [[projective representation]] of a group ''G'' is a group homomorphism <math>\pi: G \to PGL(V) = GL(V)/\mathbb{G}_m</math>. Since <math>PGL(V) = \operatorname{Aut}(\mathbb{P}(V))</math>, a projective representation is precisely a [[Group action (mathematics)|group action]] of ''G'' on <math>\mathbb{P}(V)</math> as automorphisms.

definition: A proper subrepresentation of a representation V is a subrepresentstion that is not V.
definition_raw: A proper subrepresentation of a representation ''V'' is a subrepresentstion that is not ''V''.

definition: Given a representation V and a subrepresentation $W \subset V$, the quotient representation is the representation $(\pi_{V/W}, V/W)$ given by $\pi_{V/W}(g): V/W \to V/W, \, v + W \mapsto gv + W$.
definition_raw: Given a representation ''V'' and a subrepresentation <math>W \subset V</math>, the quotient representation is the representation <math>(\pi_{V/W}, V/W)</math> given by <math>\pi_{V/W}(g): V/W \to V/W, \, v + W \mapsto gv + W</math>.

definition: A quaternionic representation of a group G is a complex representation equipped with a G-invariant quaternionic structure.
definition_raw: A [[quaternionic representation]] of a group ''G'' is a [[complex representation]] equipped with a ''G''-invariant [[quaternionic structure]].

definition: A quiver, by definition, is a directed graph. But one typically studies representations of a quiver.
definition_raw: A [[quiver (mathematics)|quiver]], by definition, is a directed graph. But one typically studies representations of a quiver.

definition: A representation V is rational if each vector v in V is contained in some finite-dimensional subrepresentation (depending on v.)
definition_raw: A representation ''V'' is [[rational representation|rational]] if each vector ''v'' in ''V'' is contained in some finite-dimensional subrepresentation (depending on ''v''.)

definition: A real representation of a vector space is a representation on a real vector space.
definition_raw: A [[real representation]] of a vector space is a representation on a real vector space.

definition: A regular representation of a finite group G is the induced representation of G on the group algebra over a field of G.
definition_raw: A [[regular representation]] of a finite group ''G'' is the induced representation of ''G'' on the [[group ring|group algebra]] over a field of ''G''.

definition: A linear representation of a group G is a group homomorphism $\pi: G \to GL(V)$ from G to the general linear group $GL(V)$. Depending on the group G, the homomorphism $\pi$ is often implicitly required to be a morphishm in a category to which G belongs; e.g., if G is a topological group, then $\pi$ must be continuous. The adjective “linear” is often omitted.
