|align=right
|width=33%
|quote=Representation theory is simple to define: it is the study of the ways in which a given group may act on vector spaces. It is almost certainly unique, however, among such clearly delineated subjects, in the breadth of its interest to mathematicians. This is not surprising: group actions are ubiquitous in 20th century mathematics, and where the object on which a group acts is not a vector space, we have learned to replace it by one that is (e.g., a cohom

definition: The term "representative function" is another term for a matrix coefficient.
definition_raw: The term "[[representative function]]" is another term for a [[matrix coefficient]].

definition: thumb|Issai SchurIssai Schur
definition_raw: [[File:Schur.jpg|thumb|Issai Schur]][[Issai Schur]]

definition: A semisimple representation (also called a completely reducible representation) is a direct sum of simple representations.
definition_raw: A [[semisimple representation]] (also called a completely reducible representation) is a direct sum of simple representations.

definition: Another term for "irreducible".
definition_raw: Another term for "irreducible".

definition: A smooth representation of a locally profinite group G is a complex representation such that, for each v in V, there is some compact open subgroup K of G that fixes v; i.e., $g \cdot v = v$ for every g in K.
definition_raw: A [[smooth representation]] of a [[locally profinite group]] ''G'' is a complex representation such that, for each ''v'' in ''V'', there is some compact open subgroup  ''K'' of ''G'' that fixes ''v''; i.e., <math>g \cdot v = v</math> for every ''g'' in ''K''.

definition: Specht module
definition_raw: [[Specht module]]

definition: Steinberg representation.
definition_raw: [[Steinberg representation]].

definition: A subrepresentation of a representation $(\pi, V)$ of G is a vector subspace W of V such that $\pi(g): W \to W$ is well-defined for each g in G.
definition_raw: A [[subrepresentation]] of a representation <math>(\pi, V)</math> of ''G'' is a vector subspace ''W'' of ''V'' such that <math>\pi(g): W \to W</math> is well-defined for each ''g'' in ''G''.

definition: The Swan representation is used to define the Swan conductor.
definition_raw: The [[Swan representation]] is used to define the [[Swan conductor]].

definition: A symmetric power of a representation V is a representation $\operatorname{Sym}^n(V)$ with the group action induced by $V^{\otimes n} \to \operatorname{Sym}^n(V)$.
definition_raw: A [[symmetric power of a representation]] ''V'' is a representation <math>\operatorname{Sym}^n(V)</math> with the group action induced by <math>V^{\otimes n} \to \operatorname{Sym}^n(V)</math>.

definition: A concept in the Mackey theory. See system of imprimitivity.
definition_raw: A concept in the [[Mackey theory]]. See [[system of imprimitivity]].

definition: The Tannakian duality is roughly an idea that a group can be recovered from all of its representations.
definition_raw: The [[Tannakian duality]] is roughly an idea that a group can be recovered from all of its representations.

definition: tempered representation
definition_raw: [[tempered representation]]

definition: A tensor representation is roughly a representation obtained from tensor products (of certain representations).
definition_raw: A [[tensor representation]] is roughly a representation obtained from tensor products (of certain representations).

definition: The tensor product of representations V, W is the representation that is the tensor product of vector spaces $V \otimes W$ together with the linear group action $\pi_{V \otimes W}(g)(v \otimes w) = \pi_V(g) v \otimes \pi_W(g) w$.
definition_raw: The [[tensor product of representations]] ''V'', ''W'' is the representation that is the tensor product of vector spaces <math>V \otimes W</math> together with the linear group action <math>\pi_{V \otimes W}(g)(v \otimes w) = \pi_V(g) v \otimes \pi_W(g) w</math>.

definition: A trivial representation of a group G is a representation π such that π(g) is the identity for every g in G.
definition_raw: A [[trivial representation]] of a group ''G'' is a representation π such that π(''g'') is the identity for every ''g'' in ''G''.

definition: A uniformly bounded representation of a locally compact group is a representation in the algebra of bounded operators that is continuous in strong operator topology and that is such that the norm of the operator given by each group element is uniformly bounded.
definition_raw: A [[uniformly bounded representation]] of a [[locally compact group]] is a representation in the algebra of bounded operators that is continuous in [[strong operator topology]] and that is such that the norm of the operator given by each group element is uniformly bounded.

definition: A unitary representation of a group G is a representation π such that π(g) is a unitary operator for every g in G.
definition_raw: A [[unitary representation]] of a group ''G'' is a representation π such that π(''g'') is a [[unitary operator]] for every ''g'' in ''G''.

definition: Given a complex semisimple Lie algebra $\mathfrak{g}$, a Cartan subalgebra $\mathfrak{h}$ and a choice of a positive Weyl chamber, the Verma module $M_{\chi}$ associated to a linear functional $\chi: \mathfrak{h} \to \mathbb{C}$ is the quotient of the enveloping algebra $U(\mathfrak{g})$ by the left ideal generated by $E_{\alpha}$ for all positive roots $\alpha$ as well as $H - \chi(H) 1$ for all $H \in \mathfrak{h}$.
definition_raw: Given a complex semisimple Lie algebra <math>\mathfrak{g}</math>, a Cartan subalgebra <math>\mathfrak{h}</math> and a choice of a [[positive Weyl chamber]], the [[Verma module]] <math>M_{\chi}</math> associated to a linear functional <math>\chi: \mathfrak{h} \to \mathbb{C}</math> is the quotient of the enveloping algebra <math>U(\mathfrak{g})</math> by the left ideal generated by <math>E_{\alpha}</math> for all positive roots <math>\alpha</math> as well as <math>H - \chi(H) 1</math> for all <mat

definition: The term "weight" is another name for a character.
definition_raw: The term "weight" is another name for a character.

definition: Hermann Weyl
definition_raw: [[Hermann Weyl]]

definition: Alfred Young
definition_raw: [[Alfred Young (mathematician)|Alfred Young]]

definition: A zero representation is a zero-dimensional representation. Note: while a zero representation is a trivial representation, a trivial representation need not be zero (since “trivial” mean G acts trivially.)
definition_raw: A [[zero representation]] is a zero-dimensional representation. Note: while a zero representation is a trivial representation, a trivial representation need not be zero (since “trivial” mean ''G'' acts trivially.)

definition: The Amitsur complex of a ring homomorphism is a cochain complex that measures the extent in which the ring homomorphism fails to be faithfully flat.
definition_raw: The [[Amitsur complex]] of a ring homomorphism is a cochain complex that measures the extent in which the ring homomorphism fails to be [[faithfully flat ring homomorphism|faithfully flat]].

definition: A left Artinian ring is a ring satisfying the descending chain condition for left ideals; a right Artinian ring is one satisfying the descending chain condition for right ideals. If a ring is both left and right Artinian, it is called Artinian. Artinian rings are Noetherian rings.
definition_raw: A left [[Artinian ring]] is a ring satisfying the [[descending chain condition]] for left ideals; a right Artinian ring is one satisfying the descending chain condition for right ideals. If a ring is both left and right Artinian, it is called ''Artinian''. Artinian rings are Noetherian rings.

definition: In a commutative ring, an element a is called an associate of an element b if a divides b and b divides a.
definition_raw: In a commutative ring, an element ''a'' is called an [[Divisibility (ring theory)|associate]] of an element ''b'' if ''a'' divides ''b'' and ''b'' divides ''a''.

definition: A ring automorphism is a ring isomorphism between the same ring; in other words, it is a unit element of the endomorphism ring of the ring that is multiplicative and preserves the multiplicative identity.
definition_raw: A [[ring automorphism]] is a ring isomorphism between the same ring; in other words, it is a unit element of the endomorphism ring of the ring that is multiplicative and preserves the multiplicative identity.

definition: An Azumaya algebra is a generalization of a central simple algebra to a non-field base ring.
definition_raw: An [[Azumaya algebra]] is a generalization of a central simple algebra to a non-field base ring.

definition: The bidimension of an associative algebra A over a commutative ring R is the projective dimension of A as an -module. For example, an algebra has bidimension zero if and only if it is separable.
definition_raw: The [[bidimension of an associative algebra]] ''A'' over a commutative ring ''R'' is the projective dimension of ''A'' as an {{nowrap|(''A''<sup>op</sup> ⊗<sub>''R''</sub> ''A'')}}-module. For example, an algebra has bidimension zero if and only if it is separable.

definition: A boolean ring is a ring in which every element is multiplicatively idempotent.
definition_raw: A [[boolean ring]] is a ring in which every element is multiplicatively [[idempotent (ring theory)|idempotent]].

definition: The Brauer group of a field is an abelian group consisting of all equivalence classes of central simple algebras over the field.
definition_raw: The [[Brauer group]] of a field is an abelian group consisting of all equivalence classes of central simple algebras over the field.

definition: The category of rings is a category where the objects are (all) the rings and where the morphisms are (all) the ring homomorphisms.
definition_raw: The [[category of rings]] is a category where the objects are (all) the rings and where the morphisms are (all) the ring homomorphisms.

definition: An element r of a ring R is central if for all x in R. The set of all central elements forms a subring of R, known as the centre of R.
definition_raw: An element ''r'' of a ring ''R'' is ''central'' if {{nowrap|1=''xr'' = ''rx''}} for all ''x'' in ''R''. The set of all central elements forms a [[subring]] of ''R'', known as the [[center (ring theory)|centre]] of ''R''.

definition: The centralizer of a subset S of a ring is the subring of the ring consisting of the elements commuting with the elements of S. For example, the centralizer of the ring itself is the centre of the ring.
definition_raw: The [[centralizer (ring theory)|centralizer]] of a subset ''S'' of a ring is the subring of the ring consisting of the elements commuting with the elements of ''S''. For example, the centralizer of the ring itself is the centre of the ring.

definition: The characteristic of a ring is the smallest positive integer n satisfying nx = 0 for all elements x of the ring, if such an n exists. Otherwise, the characteristic is 0.
definition_raw: The [[Characteristic (algebra)|characteristic]] of a ring is the smallest positive integer ''n'' satisfying ''nx'' = 0 for all elements ''x'' of the ring, if such an ''n'' exists. Otherwise, the characteristic is 0.

definition: A change of rings is a functor (between appropriate categories) induced by a ring homomorphism.
definition_raw: A [[change of rings]] is a functor (between appropriate categories) induced by a ring homomorphism.

definition: A Clifford algebra is a certain associative algebra that is useful in geometry and physics.
definition_raw: A [[Clifford algebra]] is a certain associative algebra that is useful in geometry and physics.

definition: A left coherent ring is a ring such that every finitely generated left ideal of it is a finitely presented module; in other words, it is coherent as a left module over itself.
definition_raw: A left [[coherent ring]] is a ring such that every finitely generated left ideal of it is a finitely presented module; in other words, it is [[coherent module|coherent]] as a left module over itself.

definition: A ring R is commutative if the multiplication is commutative, i.e. for all .
definition_raw: A ring ''R'' is [[commutative ring|commutative]] if the multiplication is commutative, i.e. {{nowrap|1=''rs'' = ''sr''}} for all {{nowrap|''r'',''s'' ∈ ''R''}}.

definition: A derivation of a possibly-non-associative algebra A over a commutative ring R is an R-linear endomorphism that satisfies the Leibniz rule.
definition_raw: A [[derivation of an algebra|derivation]] of a possibly-non-associative algebra ''A'' over a commutative ring ''R'' is an ''R''-linear endomorphism that satisfies the [[Product rule|Leibniz rule]].

definition: A differential algebra is an algebra together with a derivation.
definition_raw: A [[differential algebra]] is an algebra together with a derivation.

definition: A direct product of a family of rings is a ring given by taking the cartesian product of the given rings and defining the algebraic operations component-wise.
definition_raw: A [[direct product ring|direct product]] of a family of rings is a ring given by taking the [[cartesian product]] of the given rings and defining the algebraic operations component-wise.

definition: In an integral domain R, an element a is called a divisor of the element b (and we say a divides b) if there exists an element x in R with .
definition_raw: In an [[integral domain]] ''R'',{{clarify|Do we need to assume ''R'' is an integral domain?|date=January 2020}} an element ''a'' is called a [[Divisibility (ring theory)|divisor]] of the element ''b'' (and we say ''a'' ''divides'' ''b'') if there exists an element ''x'' in ''R'' with {{nowrap|1=''ax'' = ''b''}}.

definition: A division ring or skew field is a ring in which every nonzero element is a unit and .
definition_raw: A [[division ring]] or skew field is a ring in which every nonzero element is a unit and {{nowrap|1 ≠ 0}}.

definition: A domain is a nonzero ring with no zero divisors except 0. For a historical reason, a commutative domain is called an integral domain.
definition_raw: A [[Domain (ring theory)|domain]] is a nonzero ring with no zero divisors except 0. For a historical reason, a commutative domain is called an [[integral domain]].

definition: An endomorphism ring is a ring formed by the endomorphisms of an object with additive structure; the multiplication is taken to be function composition, while its addition is pointwise addition of the images.
definition_raw: An [[endomorphism ring]] is a ring formed by the [[endomorphism]]s of an object with additive structure; the multiplication is taken to be [[function composition]], while its addition is pointwise addition of the images.

definition: The (universal) enveloping algebra E of a not-necessarily-associative algebra A is the associative algebra determined by A in some universal way. The best known example is the universal enveloping algebra of a Lie algebra.
definition_raw: The (universal) [[enveloping algebra (disambiguation)|enveloping algebra]] ''E'' of a not-necessarily-associative algebra ''A'' is the associative algebra determined by ''A'' in some universal way. The best known example is the [[universal enveloping algebra]] of a Lie algebra.

definition: A ring E is a ring extension of a ring R if R is a subring of E.
definition_raw: A ring ''E'' is a [[ring extension]] of a ring ''R'' if ''R'' is a [[subring]] of ''E''.

definition: The exterior algebra of a vector space or a module V is the quotient of the tensor algebra of V by the ideal generated by elements of the form .
definition_raw: The [[exterior algebra]] of a vector space or a module ''V'' is the quotient of the tensor algebra of ''V'' by the ideal generated by elements of the form {{nowrap|''x'' ⊗ ''x''}}.

definition: A field is a commutative division ring; i.e., a nonzero ring in which each nonzero element is invertible.
definition_raw: A [[Field (mathematics)|field]] is a commutative division ring; i.e., a nonzero ring in which each nonzero element is invertible.

definition: A filtered ring is a ring with a filtration.
definition_raw: A [[filtered ring]] is a ring with a filtration.

definition: A left ideal I is finitely generated if there exist finitely many elements such that . A right ideal I is finitely generated if there exist finitely many elements such that . A two-sided ideal I is finitely generated if there exist finitely many elements such that .
definition_raw: A left ideal ''I'' is ''[[finitely generated ideal|finitely generated]]'' if there exist finitely many elements {{nowrap|''a''<sub>1</sub>, ..., ''a''<sub>''n''</sub>}} such that {{nowrap|1=''I'' = ''Ra''<sub>1</sub> + ... + ''Ra''<sub>''n''</sub>}}. A right ideal ''I'' is ''finitely generated'' if there exist finitely many elements {{nowrap|''a''<sub>1</sub>, ..., ''a''<sub>''n''</sub>}} such that {{nowrap|1=''I'' = ''a''<sub>1</sub>''R'' + ... + ''a''<sub>''n''</sub>''R''}}. A two-sided ideal

definition: A finitely presented algebra over a commutative ring R is a (commutative) associative algebra that is a quotient of a polynomial ring over R in finitely many variables by a finitely generated ideal.
definition_raw: A finitely presented algebra over a commutative ring ''R'' is a (commutative) [[associative algebra]] that is a [[quotient ring|quotient]] of a [[polynomial ring]] over ''R'' in finitely many variables by a [[finitely generated ideal]].<ref>{{harvnb|Grothendieck|Dieudonné|1964|loc=§1.4.1}}</ref>

definition: A free ideal ring or a fir is a ring in which every right ideal is a free module of fixed rank.
definition_raw: A [[free ideal ring]] or a fir is a ring in which every right ideal is a free module of fixed rank.

definition: A graded ring is a ring together with a grading or a graduation; i.e, it is a direct sum of additive subgroups with the multiplication that respects the grading. For example, a polynomial ring is a graded ring by degrees of polynomials.
definition_raw: A [[graded ring]] is a ring together with a grading or a graduation; i.e, it is a direct sum of additive subgroups with the multiplication that respects the grading. For example, a polynomial ring is a graded ring by degrees of polynomials.

definition: An associative algebra A over a commutative ring R is said to be generated by a subset S of A if the smallest subalgebra containing S is A itself and S is said to be the generating set of A. If there is a finite generating set, A is said to be a finitely generated algebra.
definition_raw: An associative algebra ''A'' over a commutative ring ''R'' is said to be [[algebra generated by a set|generated]] by a subset ''S'' of ''A'' if the smallest subalgebra containing ''S'' is ''A'' itself and ''S'' is said to be the generating set of ''A''. If there is a finite generating set, ''A'' is said to be a [[finitely generated algebra]].

definition: A ring is left hereditary if its left ideals are all projective modules. Right hereditary rings are defined analogously.
definition_raw: A ring is [[hereditary ring|left hereditary]] if its left ideals are all projective modules. Right hereditary rings are defined analogously.

definition: A left ideal I of R is an additive subgroup of R such that for all . A right ideal is a subgroup of R such that for all . An ideal (sometimes called a two-sided ideal for emphasis) is a subgroup that is both a left ideal and a right ideal.
definition_raw: A [[left ideal]] ''I'' of ''R'' is an additive subgroup of ''R'' such that {{nowrap|''aI'' ⊆ ''I''}} for all {{nowrap|''a'' ∈ ''R''}}. A ''right ideal'' is a subgroup of ''R'' such that {{nowrap|''Ia'' ⊆ ''I''}} for all {{nowrap|''a'' ∈ ''R''}}. An ''ideal'' (sometimes called a ''two-sided ideal'' for emphasis) is a subgroup that is both a left ideal and a right ideal.

definition: An element r of a ring is idempotent if .
definition_raw: An element ''r'' of a ring is [[Idempotent element (ring theory)|idempotent]] if {{nowrap|1=''r''{{i sup|2}} = ''r''}}.

definition: "integral domain" or "entire ring" is another name for a commutative domain; i.e., a nonzero commutative ring with no zero divisors except 0.
definition_raw: "'''[[integral domain]]'''" or "'''entire ring'''" is another name for a [[commutative domain]]; i.e., a nonzero [[commutative ring]] with no [[zero divisor]]s except 0.

definition: A ring R has invariant basis number if Rm isomorphic to Rn as R-modules implies .
definition_raw: A ring ''R'' has [[invariant basis number]] if ''R''<sup>''m''</sup> isomorphic to ''R''<sup>''n''</sup> as [[module (mathematics)|''R''-modules]] implies {{nowrap|1=''m'' = ''n''}}.

definition: An element x of an integral domain is irreducible if it is not a unit and for any elements a and b such that , either a or b is a unit. Note that every prime element is irreducible, but not necessarily vice versa.
definition_raw: An element ''x'' of an integral domain is [[Irreducible element|irreducible]] if it is not a unit and for any elements ''a'' and ''b'' such that {{nowrap|1=''x'' = ''ab''}}, either ''a'' or ''b'' is a unit. Note that every prime element is irreducible, but not necessarily vice versa.

definition: The Jacobson radical of a ring is the intersection of all maximal left ideals.
definition_raw: The [[Jacobson radical]] of a ring is the intersection of all maximal left ideals.

definition: The kernel of a ring homomorphism of a ring homomorphism is the set of all elements x of R such that . Every ideal is the kernel of a ring homomorphism and vice versa.
definition_raw: The [[kernel (algebra)|kernel]] of a ring homomorphism of a ring homomorphism {{nowrap|''f'' : ''R'' → ''S''}} is the set of all elements ''x'' of ''R'' such that {{nowrap|1=''f''(''x'') = 0}}. Every ideal is the kernel of a ring homomorphism and vice versa.

definition: Köthe's conjecture states that if a ring has a nonzero nil right ideal, then it has a nonzero nil ideal.
definition_raw: [[Köthe's conjecture]] states that if a ring has a nonzero nil right ideal, then it has a nonzero nil ideal.

definition: A ring with a unique maximal left ideal is a local ring. These rings also have a unique maximal right ideal, and the left and the right unique maximal ideals coincide. Certain commutative rings can be embedded in local rings via localization at a prime ideal.
definition_raw: A ring with a unique maximal left ideal is a [[local ring]]. These rings also have a unique maximal right ideal, and the left and the right unique maximal ideals coincide. Certain commutative rings can be embedded in local rings via [[localization of a ring|localization]] at a [[prime ideal]].

definition: A left ideal M of the ring R is a maximal left ideal (resp. minimal left ideal) if it is maximal (resp. minimal) among proper (resp. nonzero) left ideals. Maximal (resp. minimal) right ideals are defined similarly.
definition_raw: A left ideal ''M'' of the ring ''R'' is a [[maximal ideal|maximal left ideal]] (resp. minimal left ideal) if it is maximal (resp. minimal) among proper (resp. nonzero) left ideals. Maximal (resp. minimal) right ideals are defined similarly.

definition: A matrix ring over a ring R is a ring whose elements are square matrices of fixed size with the entries in R. The matrix ring or the full matrix ring of matrices over R is the matrix ring consisting of all square matrices of fixed size with the entries in R. When the grammatical construction is not workable, the term "matrix ring" often refers to the "full" matrix ring when the context makes no confusion likely; for example, when one says a semsimple ring is a product of matrix rings of division rings, it is implicitly assumed that "matrix rings" refer to "full matrix rings". Every ring is (isomorphic to) the full matrix ring over itself.
definition_raw: A [[matrix ring]] over a ring ''R'' is a ring whose elements are square matrices of fixed size with the entries in ''R''. The matrix ring or the full matrix ring of matrices over ''R'' is ''the'' matrix ring consisting of all square matrices of fixed size with the entries in ''R''. When the grammatical construction is not workable, the term "matrix ring" often refers to the "full" matrix ring when the context makes no confusion likely; for example, when one says a semsimple ring is a product of

definition: A monoid ring.
definition_raw: A [[monoid ring]].

definition: Two rings are said to be Morita equivalent if the category of modules over the one is equivalent to the category of modules over the other.
definition_raw: Two rings are said to be [[Morita equivalent]] if the [[category of modules]] over the one is equivalent to the category of modules over the other.

definition: A nearring is a structure that is a group under addition, a semigroup under multiplication, and whose multiplication distributes on the right over addition.
definition_raw: A '''[[nearring]]''' is a structure that is a group under addition, a [[semigroup]] under multiplication, and whose multiplication distributes on the right over addition.

definition: A nil ideal is an ideal consisting of nilpotent elements.
definition_raw: A [[nil ideal]] is an ideal consisting of nilpotent elements.

definition: An element r of R is nilpotent if there exists a positive integer n such that .
definition_raw: An element ''r'' of ''R'' is [[nilpotent element|nilpotent]] if there exists a positive integer ''n'' such that {{nowrap|1=''r''{{i sup|''n''}} = 0}}.

definition: A left Noetherian ring is a ring satisfying the ascending chain condition for left ideals. A right Noetherian is defined similarly and a ring that is both left and right Noetherian is Noetherian. A ring is left Noetherian if and only if all its left ideals are finitely generated; analogously for right Noetherian rings.
definition_raw: A left [[Noetherian ring]] is a ring satisfying the [[ascending chain condition]] for left ideals. A ''right Noetherian'' is defined similarly and a ring that is both left and right Noetherian is ''Noetherian''. A ring is left Noetherian if and only if all its left ideals are finitely generated; analogously for right Noetherian rings.

definition: null ring: See rng of square zero.
definition_raw: '''null ring''': See {{gli|rng of square zero}}.

definition: Given a ring R, its opposite ring Rop has the same underlying set as R, the addition operation is defined as in R, but the product of s and r in Rop is rs, while the product is sr in R.
definition_raw: Given a ring ''R'', its [[opposite ring]] ''R''<sup>op</sup> has the same underlying set as ''R'', the addition operation is defined as in ''R'', but the product of ''s'' and ''r'' in ''R''<sup>op</sup> is ''rs'', while the product is ''sr'' in ''R''.

definition: An order of an algebra is (roughly) a subalgebra that is also a full lattice.
definition_raw: An [[order (ring theory)|order]] of an algebra is (roughly) a subalgebra that is also a full lattice.

definition: A left Ore domain is a (non-commutative) domain for which the set of non-zero elements satisfies the left Ore condition. A right Ore domain is defined similarly.
definition_raw: A left [[Ore domain]] is a (non-commutative) domain for which the set of non-zero elements satisfies the left Ore condition. A right Ore domain is defined similarly.

definition: A left perfect ring is one satisfying the descending chain condition on right principal ideals. They are also characterized as rings whose flat left modules are all projective modules. Right perfect rings are defined analogously. Artinian rings are perfect.
definition_raw: A ''left [[perfect ring]]'' is one satisfying the [[descending chain condition]] on ''right'' principal ideals. They are also characterized as rings whose flat left modules are all projective modules. Right perfect rings are defined analogously. Artinian rings are perfect.

definition: A polynomial ring over a commutative ring R is a commutative ring consisting of all the polynomials in the specified variables with coefficients in R.
definition_raw: A [[polynomial ring]] over a commutative ring ''R'' is a commutative ring consisting of all the polynomials in the specified variables with coefficients in ''R''.

definition: An element x of an integral domain is a prime element if it is not zero and not a unit and whenever x divides a product ab, x divides a or x divides b.
definition_raw: An element ''x'' of an integral domain is a [[prime element]] if it is not zero and not a unit and whenever ''x'' divides a product ''ab'', ''x'' divides ''a'' or ''x'' divides ''b''.

definition: A left primitive ring is a ring that has a faithful simple left R-module. Every simple ring is primitive. Primitive rings are prime.
definition_raw: A ''left [[primitive ring]]'' is a ring that has a [[faithful module|faithful]] [[simple module|simple]] [[module (mathematics)|left ''R''-module]]. Every [[simple ring]] is primitive.  Primitive rings are [[prime ring|prime]].

definition: A principal ideal : A principal left ideal in a ring R is a left ideal of the form Ra for some element a of R. A principal right ideal is a right ideal of the form aR for some element a of R. A principal ideal is a two-sided ideal of the form RaR for some element a of R.
definition_raw: A '''[[principal ideal]]''' : A ''principal left ideal'' in a ring ''R'' is a left ideal of the form ''Ra'' for some element ''a'' of ''R''. A ''principal right ideal'' is a right ideal of the form ''aR'' for some element ''a'' of ''R''. A ''principal ideal'' is a two-sided ideal of the form ''RaR'' for some element ''a'' of ''R''.

definition: quasi-Frobenius ring : a special type of Artinian ring that is also a self-injective ring on both sides. Every semisimple ring is quasi-Frobenius.
definition_raw: '''[[quasi-Frobenius ring]]''' : a special type of Artinian ring that is also a [[self-injective ring]] on both sides. Every semisimple ring is quasi-Frobenius.

definition: The radical of an ideal I in a commutative ring consists of all those ring elements a power of which lies in I. It is equal to the intersection of all prime ideals containing I.
definition_raw: The [[radical of an ideal]] ''I'' in a [[commutative ring]] consists of all those ring elements a power of which lies in ''I''. It is equal to the intersection of all prime ideals containing ''I''.

definition: A set R with two binary operations, usually called addition (+) and multiplication (×), such that R is an abelian group under addition, R is a monoid under multiplication, and multiplication is both left and right distributive over addition. Rings are assumed to have multiplicative identities unless otherwise noted. The additive identity is denoted by 0 and the multiplicative identity by 1. (Warning: some books, especially older books, use the term "ring" to mean what here will be called a rng; i.e., they do not require a ring to have a multiplicative identity.)
definition_raw: A [[Set (mathematics)|set]] ''R'' with two [[binary operation]]s, usually called addition (+) and multiplication (×), such that ''R'' is an [[abelian group]] under addition, ''R'' is a [[monoid]] under multiplication, and multiplication is both left and right [[distributive property|distributive]] over addition. Rings are assumed to have multiplicative identities unless otherwise noted. The additive identity is denoted by 0 and the multiplicative identity by 1. (''Warning'': some books, especial

definition: A rng is a set R with two binary operations, usually called addition (+) and multiplication (×), such that is an abelian group, is a semigroup, and multiplication is both left and right distributive over addition. A rng that has an identity element is a "ring".
definition_raw: A '''[[rng (algebra)|rng]]''' is a [[Set (mathematics)|set]] ''R'' with two [[binary operation]]s, usually called addition (+) and multiplication (×), such that {{nowrap|(''R'', +)}} is an [[abelian group]], {{nowrap|(''R'', ×)}} is a [[semigroup]], and multiplication is both left and right [[distributive property|distributive]] over addition.  A rng that has an '''i'''dentity element is a "r'''i'''ng".

definition: A ring R is left self-injective if the module RR is an injective module. While rings with unity are always projective as modules, they are not always injective as modules.
definition_raw: A ring ''R'' is ''left [[self-injective ring|self-injective]]'' if the module <sub>''R''</sub>''R'' is an [[injective module]]. While rings with unity are always projective as modules, they are not always injective as modules.

definition: A semiperfect ring is a ring R such that, for the Jacobson radical J(R) of R, (1) R/J(R) is semisimple and (2) idempotents lift modulo J(R).
definition_raw: A [[semiperfect ring]] is a ring ''R'' such that, for the Jacobson radical J(''R'') of ''R'', (1) ''R''/J(''R'') is semisimple and (2) idempotents lift modulo J(''R'').

definition: A semiprimary ring is a ring R such that, for the Jacobson radical J(R) of R, (1) R/J(R) is semisimple and (2) J(R) is a nilpotent ideal.
definition_raw: A [[semiprimary ring]] is a ring ''R'' such that, for the Jacobson radical J(''R'') of ''R'', (1) ''R''/J(''R'') is semisimple and (2) J(''R'') is a [[nilpotent ideal]].

definition: A semiprime ring is a ring where the only nilpotent ideal is the trivial ideal {0}. A commutative ring is semiprime if and only if it is reduced.
definition_raw: A [[semiprime ring]] is a ring where the only [[nilpotent ideal]] is the trivial ideal {{mset|0}}. A commutative ring is semiprime if and only if it is reduced.

definition: A semiprimitive ring or Jacobson semisimple ring is a ring whose Jacobson radical is zero. Von Neumann regular rings and primitive rings are semiprimitive, however quasi-Frobenius rings and local rings are usually not semiprimitive.
definition_raw: A [[semiprimitive ring]] or Jacobson semisimple ring is a ring whose [[Jacobson radical]] is zero. Von Neumann regular rings and primitive rings are semiprimitive, however quasi-Frobenius rings and local rings are usually not semiprimitive.

definition: A semiring : An algebraic structure satisfying the same properties as a ring, except that addition need only be an abelian monoid operation, rather than an abelian group operation. That is, elements in a semiring need not have additive inverses.
definition_raw: A '''[[semiring]]''' : An algebraic structure satisfying the same properties as a ring, except that addition need only be an abelian [[monoid]] operation, rather than an abelian group operation. That is, elements in a semiring need not have additive inverses.

definition: A semisimple ring is an Artinian ring R that is a finite product of simple Artinian rings; in other words, it is a semisimple left R-module.
definition_raw: A [[semisimple ring]] is an Artinian ring ''R'' that is a finite product of simple Artinian rings; in other words, it is a [[semisimple module|semisimple]] left ''R''-module.

definition: A separable algebra is an associative algebra whose tensor-square admits a separability idempotent.
definition_raw: A [[separable algebra]] is an associative algebra whose tensor-square admits a [[separability idempotent]].

definition: A right serial ring is a ring that is a right serial module over itself.
definition_raw: A right [[serial ring]] is a ring that is a right serial module over itself.

definition: The Severi–Brauer variety is an algebraic variety associated to a given central simple algebra.
definition_raw: The [[Severi–Brauer variety]] is an algebraic variety associated to a given central simple algebra.

definition: A simple ring is a non-zero ring that only has trivial two-sided ideals (the zero ideal, the ring itself, and no more) is a simple ring.
definition_raw: A [[simple ring]] is a non-zero ring that only has trivial two-sided ideals (the zero ideal, the ring itself, and no more) is a ''simple ring''.

definition: The right (resp. left) R-module M has a singular submodule if it consists of elements whose annihilators are essential right (resp. left) ideals in R. In set notation it is usually denoted as {{nowrap|1=(M) = {m ∈ M {{pipe} ann(m) ⊆e R}}}}.
definition_raw: The right (resp. left) ''R''-[[module (mathematics)|module]] ''M'' has a [[singular submodule]] if it consists of elements whose [[annihilator (ring theory)|annihilator]]s are [[essential submodule|essential]] right (resp. left) [[ideal (ring theory)|ideal]]s in ''R''.  In set notation it is usually denoted as {{nowrap|1={{mathcal|{{big|Z}}}}(''M'') = {{mset|''m'' ∈ ''M'' {{pipe}} ann(''m'') ⊆<sub>''e''</sub> ''R''}}}}.

definition: A subring is a subset S of the ring that remains a ring when + and × are restricted to S and contains the multiplicative identity 1 of R.
definition_raw: A [[subring]] is a subset ''S'' of the ring {{nowrap|(''R'', +, ×)}} that remains a ring when + and × are restricted to ''S'' and contains the multiplicative identity 1 of ''R''.

definition: The symmetric algebra of a vector space or a module V is the quotient of the tensor algebra of V by the ideal generated by elements of the form .
definition_raw: The [[symmetric algebra]] of a vector space or a module ''V'' is the quotient of the tensor algebra of ''V'' by the ideal generated by elements of the form {{nowrap|''x'' ⊗ ''y'' − ''y'' ⊗ ''x''}}.

definition: A Sylvester domain is a ring in which Sylvester's law of nullity holds.
definition_raw: A [[Sylvester domain]] is a ring in which [[Sylvester's law of nullity]] holds.

definition: The tensor product algebra of associative algebras is the tensor product of the algebras as the modules with component multiplication
definition_raw: The [[tensor product algebra]] of associative algebras is the tensor product of the algebras as the modules with component multiplication

definition: A trivial ideal is either the zero or the unit ideal.
definition_raw: A trivial ideal is either the zero or the unit ideal.

definition: unit or invertible element : An element r of the ring R is a unit if there exists an element r such that . This element r is uniquely determined by r and is called the multiplicative inverse of r. The set of units forms a group under multiplication.
definition_raw: '''[[Unit (ring theory)|unit]]''' or '''invertible element''' : An element ''r'' of the ring ''R'' is a ''unit'' if there exists an element ''r''{{i sup|−1}} such that {{nowrap|1=''rr''{{i sup|−1}} = ''r''{{i sup|−1}}''r'' = 1}}. This element ''r''{{i sup|−1}} is uniquely determined by ''r'' and is called the ''multiplicative inverse'' of ''r''. The set of units forms a [[group (mathematics)|group]] under multiplication.

definition: The term "unity" is another name for the multiplicative identity.
definition_raw: The term "unity" is another name for the multiplicative identity.

definition: A unique factorization domain or factorial ring is an integral domain R in which every non-zero non-unit element can be written as a product of prime elements of R.
definition_raw: A '''[[unique factorization domain]]''' or '''factorial ring''' is an integral domain ''R'' in which every non-zero non-[[Unit (ring theory)|unit]] element can be written as a product of [[prime element]]s of ''R''.

definition: A right uniserial ring is a ring that is a right uniserial module over itself. A commutative uniserial ring is also called a valuation ring.
definition_raw: A right [[uniserial ring]] is a ring that is a right uniserial module over itself. A commutative uniserial ring is also called a [[valuation ring]].

definition: von Neumann regular element : An element r of a ring R is von Neumann regular if there exists an element x of R such that .
definition_raw: '''[[von Neumann regular element]]''' : An element ''r'' of a ring ''R'' is ''von Neumann regular'' if there exists an element ''x'' of ''R'' such that {{nowrap|1=''r'' = ''rxr''}}.

definition: The Wedderburn–Artin theorem states that a semisimple ring is a finite product of (full) matrix rings over division rings.
definition_raw: The [[Wedderburn–Artin theorem]] states that a semisimple ring is a finite product of (full) matrix rings over division rings.

definition: A zero ring: The ring consisting only of a single element , also called the trivial ring. Sometimes "zero ring" is used in an alternative sense to mean rng of square zero.

definition: The components of a tensor with respect to a basis is an indexed array. The order of a tensor is the number of indices needed. Some texts may refer to the tensor order using the term degree or rank.

definition: The rank of a tensor is the minimum number of rank-one tensor that must be summed to obtain the tensor. A rank-one tensor may be defined as expressible as the outer product of the number of nonzero vectors needed to obtain the correct order.

definition: This notation is based on the understanding that whenever a multidimensional array contains a repeated index letter, the default interpretation is that the product is summed over all permitted values of the index. For example, if aij is a matrix, then under this convention aii is its trace. The Einstein convention is widely used in physics and engineering texts, to the extent that if summation is not to be applied, it is normal to note that explicitly.

definition: The classical interpretation is by components. For example, in the differential form aidxi the components ai are a covariant vector. That means all indices are lower; contravariant means all indices are upper.

definition: Cartesian tensors are widely used in various branches of continuum mechanics, such as fluid mechanics and elasticity. In classical continuum mechanics, the space of interest is usually 3-dimensional Euclidean space, as is the tangent space at each point. If we restrict the local coordinates to be Cartesian coordinates with the same scale centered at the point of interest, the metric tensor is the Kronecker delta. This means that there is no need to distinguish covariant and contravariant components, and furthermore there is no need to distinguish tensors and tensor densities. All Cartesian-tensor indices are written as subscripts. Cartesian tensors achieve considerable computational simplification at the cost of generality and of some theoretical insight.

In classical [[continuum mechanics]], the space of interest is usually 3-dimensional [[Euclidean space]], as is the tangent space at each point. definition: If v and w are vectors in vector spaces V and W respectively, then :$v \otimes w $ is a tensor in :$V \otimes W. $ That is, the ⊗ operation is a binary operation, but it takes values into a fresh space (it is in a strong sense external). The ⊗ operation is a bilinear map; but no other conditions are applied to it.
definition_raw: If ''v'' and ''w'' are vectors in [[vector space]]s ''V'' and ''W'' respectively, then :<math>v \otimes w </math> is a tensor in :<math>V \otimes W. </math> That is, the ⊗ operation is a [[binary operation]], but it takes values into a fresh space (it is in a strong sense ''external''). The ⊗ operation is a [[bilinear map]]; but no other conditions are applied to it.

definition: A pure tensor of V ⊗ W is one that is of the form v ⊗ w. It could be written dyadically aibj, or more accurately aibj ei ⊗ fj, where the ei are a basis for V and the fj a basis for W. Therefore, unless V and W have the same dimension, the array of components need not be square. Such pure tensors are not generic: if both V and W have dimension greater than 1, there will be tensors that are not pure, and there will be non-linear conditions for a tensor to satisfy, to be pure. For more see Segre embedding.
definition_raw: A pure tensor of ''V'' ⊗ ''W'' is one that is of the form ''v'' ⊗ ''w''. definition: In the tensor algebra T(V) of a vector space V, the operation $ \otimes $ becomes a normal (internal) binary operation. A consequence is that T(V) has infinite dimension unless V has dimension 0. The free algebra on a set X is for practical purposes the same as the tensor algebra on the vector space with X as basis.
definition_raw: In the tensor algebra ''T''(''V'') of a vector space ''V'', the operation <math> \otimes </math> becomes a normal (internal) [[binary operation]]. A consequence is that ''T''(''V'') has infinite dimension unless ''V'' has dimension 0. The [[free algebra]] on a set ''X'' is for practical purposes the same as the tensor algebra on the vector space with ''X'' as basis.

definition: A group is a set together with an associative operation that admits an identity element and such that there exists an inverse for every element.

definition: A field is a commutative ring (F, +, *) in which 0 ≠ 1 and every nonzero element has a multiplicative inverse. In a field we thus can perform the operations addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division.

definition: Diophantine geometry in general is the study of algebraic varieties V over fields K that are finitely generated over their prime fields, including as of special interest number fields and finite fields, and over local fields.

definition: Arithmetic geometry can be defined as the study of schemes of finite type over the spectrum of the ring of integers. It has also been defined as the application of the techniques of algebraic geometry to problems in number theory.

definition: A game in normal form is a function from the product of each player's strategy space to the reals for each player, assigning an allocation of payments given the tuple of strategies chosen by the players.
definition_raw: A game in normal form is a function from the product of each player's strategy space to the reals for each player, assigning an allocation of payments given the tuple of strategies chosen by the players.

definition: An extensive form game is given by a tree, where at each vertex of the tree a different player has the choice of choosing an edge. The outcome set of an extensive form game is usually the set of tree leaves.
definition_raw: An extensive form game is given by a tree, where at each vertex of the tree a different player has the choice of choosing an edge. The outcome set of an extensive form game is usually the set of tree leaves.

definition: A cooperative game is a game in which players are allowed to form coalitions and to enforce coalitionary discipline. A cooperative game is given by stating a value for every coalition. It is always assumed that the empty coalition gains nil.
definition_raw: A cooperative game is a game in which players are allowed to form coalitions and to enforce coalitionary discipline. A cooperative game is given by stating a value for every coalition. It is always assumed that the empty coalition gains nil.

definition: A simple game is a simplified form of a cooperative game, where the possible gain is assumed to be either 0 or 1. A simple game is a pair (N, W), where W is the list of winning coalitions capable of gaining the loot, and N is the set of players.
definition_raw: A simple game is a simplified form of a cooperative game, where the possible gain is assumed to be either 0 or 1. A simple game is a pair (N, W), where W is the list of winning coalitions capable of gaining the loot, and N is the set of players.
